page_content:
[Context: Features > FastAPI features]

# Features
## FastAPI features
[BOLD:FastAPI] gives you the following:

metadata.breadcrumb: Features > FastAPI features > Based on open standards

page_content:
[Context: Features > FastAPI features > Automatic docs]

### Automatic docs
Interactive API documentation and exploration web user interfaces. As the framework is based on OpenAPI, there are multiple options, 2 included by default.
[ITALIC: [BOLD:Swagger UI], with interactive exploration, call and test your API directly from the browser.
!Swagger UI interaction
] Alternative API documentation with [BOLD:ReDoc].
!ReDoc

[Context: Features > FastAPI features > Just Modern Python]

### Just Modern Python
It's all based on standard [BOLD:Python type] declarations (thanks to Pydantic). No new syntax to learn. Just standard modern Python.
If you need a 2 minute refresher of how to use Python types (even if you don't use FastAPI), check the short tutorial: Python Types.
You write standard Python with types:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: from datetime import date from pydantic import Bas...]
That can then be used like:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]1: my[ITALIC:user: User = User(id=3, name="John Doe", joined...]
/// info
[CODE: **second]user[ITALIC:data] means:
Pass the keys and values of the [CODE: second]user_data] dict directly as key-value arguments, equivalent to: [CODE: User(id=4, name="Mary", joined="2018-11-30")]
///

metadata.breadcrumb: Features > FastAPI features > Just Modern Python

page_content:
[Context: Features > FastAPI features > Editor support]

### Editor support
All the framework was designed to be easy and intuitive to use, all the decisions were tested on multiple editors even before starting development, to ensure the best development experience.
In the Python developer surveys, it's clear that one of the most used features is "autocompletion".
The whole [BOLD:FastAPI] framework is based to satisfy that. Autocompletion works everywhere.
You will rarely need to come back to the docs.
Here's how your editor might help you:
[ITALIC: in Visual Studio Code:
!editor support
] in PyCharm:
!editor support
You will get completion in code you might even consider impossible before. As for example, the [CODE: price] key inside a JSON body (that could have been nested) that comes from a request.
No more typing the wrong key names, coming back and forth between docs, or scrolling up and down to find if you finally used [CODE: username] or [CODE: user_name].

[Context: Features > FastAPI features > Short]

### Short
It has sensible [BOLD:defaults] for everything, with optional configurations everywhere. All the parameters can be fine-tuned to do what you need and to define the API you need.
But by default, it all [BOLD:"just works"].

[Context: Features > FastAPI features > Security and authentication]

### Security and authentication
Security and authentication integrated. Without any compromise with databases or data models.
All the security schemes defined in OpenAPI, including:
[ITALIC: HTTP Basic.
] [BOLD:OAuth2] (also with [BOLD:JWT tokens]). Check the tutorial on OAuth2 with JWT.
[ITALIC: API keys in:
] Headers.
[ITALIC: Query parameters.
] Cookies, etc.
Plus all the security features from Starlette (including [BOLD:session cookies]).
All built as reusable tools and components that are easy to integrate with your systems, data stores, relational and NoSQL databases, etc.

metadata.breadcrumb: Features > FastAPI features > Security and authentication

page_content:
[Context: Features > FastAPI features > Unlimited "plug-ins"]

### Unlimited "plug-ins"
Or in other way, no need for them, import and use the code you need.
Any integration is designed to be so simple to use (with dependencies) that you can create a "plug-in" for your application in 2 lines of code using the same structure and syntax used for your [ITALIC:path operations].

[Context: Features > FastAPI features > Tested]

### Tested
[ITALIC: 100% test coverage.
] 100% type annotated code base.
- Used in production applications.

[Context: Features > Starlette features]

## Starlette features
[BOLD:FastAPI] is fully compatible with (and based on) [BOLD:Starlette]. So, any additional Starlette code you have, will also work.
[CODE: FastAPI] is actually a sub-class of [CODE: Starlette]. So, if you already know or use Starlette, most of the functionality will work the same way.
With [BOLD:FastAPI] you get all of [BOLD:Starlette]'s features (as FastAPI is just Starlette on steroids):
[ITALIC: Seriously impressive performance. It is one of the fastest Python frameworks available, on par with [BOLD:NodeJS] and [BOLD:Go].
] [BOLD:WebSocket] support.
[ITALIC: In-process background tasks.
] Startup and shutdown events.
[ITALIC: Test client built on HTTPX.
] [BOLD:CORS], GZip, Static Files, Streaming responses.
[ITALIC: [BOLD:Session and Cookie] support.
] 100% test coverage.
- 100% type annotated codebase.

page_content:
[Context: Help FastAPI - Get Help > Help others with questions in GitHub]

## Help others with questions in GitHub
You can try and help others with their questions in:
[ITALIC: GitHub Discussions
] GitHub Issues
In many cases you might already know the answer for those questions. 🤓
If you are helping a lot of people with their questions, you will become an official FastAPI Expert.
Just remember, the most important point is: try to be kind. People come with their frustrations and in many cases don't ask in the best way, but try as best as you can to be kind. 🤗
The idea is for the [BOLD:FastAPI] community to be kind and welcoming. At the same time, don't accept bullying or disrespectful behavior towards others. We have to take care of each other.
---
Here's how to help others with questions (in discussions or issues):

hierarchy: Help others with questions in GitHub
metadata.breadcrumb: Help FastAPI - Get Help > Help others with questions in GitHub

hierarchy: Help others with questions in GitHub
metadata.breadcrumb: Help FastAPI - Get Help > Help others with questions in GitHub > Understand the question

page_content:
[Context: Help FastAPI - Get Help > Help others with questions in GitHub > Reproduce the problem]

### Reproduce the problem
For most of the cases and most of the questions there's something related to the person's [BOLD:original code].
In many cases they will only copy a fragment of the code, but that's not enough to [BOLD:reproduce the problem].
[ITALIC: You can ask them to provide a minimal, reproducible, example, that you can [BOLD:copy-paste] and run locally to see the same error or behavior they are seeing, or to understand their use case better.
] If you are feeling too generous, you can try to [BOLD:create an example] like that yourself, just based on the description of the problem. Just keep in mind that this might take a lot of time and it might be better to ask them to clarify the problem first.

hierarchy: Help others with questions in GitHub
metadata.breadcrumb: Help FastAPI - Get Help > Help others with questions in GitHub > Reproduce the problem

page_content:
[Context: Help FastAPI - Get Help > Help others with questions in GitHub > Suggest solutions]

### Suggest solutions
[ITALIC: After being able to understand the question, you can give them a possible [BOLD:answer].
] In many cases, it's better to understand their [BOLD:underlying problem or use case], because there might be a better way to solve it than what they are trying to do.

hierarchy: Help others with questions in GitHub
metadata.breadcrumb: Help FastAPI - Get Help > Help others with questions in GitHub > Suggest solutions

page_content:
[Context: Help FastAPI - Get Help > Help others with questions in GitHub > Ask to close]

### Ask to close
If they reply, there's a high chance you would have solved their problem, congrats, [BOLD:you're a hero]! 🦸
[ITALIC: Now, if that solved their problem, you can ask them to:
] In GitHub Discussions: mark the comment as the [BOLD:answer].
- In GitHub Issues: [BOLD:close] the issue.

hierarchy: Help others with questions in GitHub
metadata.breadcrumb: Help FastAPI - Get Help > Help others with questions in GitHub > Ask to close

page_content:

## Watch the GitHub repository
You can "watch" FastAPI in GitHub (clicking the "watch" button at the top right): https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi.
If you select "Watching" instead of "Releases only" you will receive notifications when someone creates a new issue or question. You can also specify that you only want to be notified about new issues, or discussions, or PRs, etc.
Then you can try and help them solve those questions.

[Context: Help FastAPI - Get Help > Ask Questions]

## Ask Questions
You can create a new question in the GitHub repository, for example to:
[ITALIC: Ask a [BOLD:question] or ask about a [BOLD:problem].
] Suggest a new [BOLD:feature].
[BOLD:Note]: if you do it, then I'm going to ask you to also help others.

metadata.breadcrumb: Help FastAPI - Get Help > Ask Questions

page_content:
[Context: Help FastAPI - Get Help > Review Pull Requests]

## Review Pull Requests
You can help me review pull requests from others.
Again, please try your best to be kind. 🤗
---
Here's what to keep in mind and how to review a pull request:

metadata.breadcrumb: Help FastAPI - Get Help > Review Pull Requests

page_content:
[Context: Help FastAPI - Get Help > Review Pull Requests > Understand the problem]

### Understand the problem
[ITALIC: First, make sure you [BOLD:understand the problem] that the pull request is trying to solve. It might have a longer discussion in a GitHub Discussion or issue.
] There's also a good chance that the pull request is not actually needed because the problem can be solved in a [BOLD:different way]. Then you can suggest or ask about that.

metadata.breadcrumb: Help FastAPI - Get Help > Review Pull Requests > Understand the problem

page_content:
[Context: Help FastAPI - Get Help > Review Pull Requests > Don't worry about style]

### Don't worry about style
[ITALIC: Don't worry too much about things like commit message styles, I will squash and merge customizing the commit manually.
] Also don't worry about style rules, there are already automatized tools checking that.
And if there's any other style or consistency need, I'll ask directly for that, or I'll add commits on top with the needed changes.

metadata.breadcrumb: Help FastAPI - Get Help > Review Pull Requests > Don't worry about style
Help FastAPI - Get Help > Review Pull Requests > Check the code

page_content:
[Context: Help FastAPI - Get Help > Review Pull Requests > Tests]

### Tests
[ITALIC: Help me check that the PR has [BOLD:tests].
] Check that the tests [BOLD:fail] before the PR.
[ITALIC: Then check that the tests [BOLD:pass] after the PR.
] Many PRs don't have tests, you can [BOLD:remind] them to add tests, or you can even [BOLD:suggest] some tests yourself. That's one of the things that consume most time and you can help a lot with that.
- Then also comment what you tried, that way I'll know that you checked it. 🤓

metadata.breadcrumb: Help FastAPI - Get Help > Review Pull Requests > Tests
Help FastAPI - Get Help > Create a Pull Request

page_content:
[Context: Help FastAPI - Get Help > Help Maintain FastAPI]

## Help Maintain FastAPI
Help me maintain [BOLD:FastAPI]! 🤓
There's a lot of work to do, and for most of it, [BOLD:YOU] can do it.
The main tasks that you can do right now are:
[ITALIC: Help others with questions in GitHub (see the section above).
] Review Pull Requests (see the section above).
Those two tasks are what [BOLD:consume time the most]. That's the main work of maintaining FastAPI.
If you can help me with that, [BOLD:you are helping me maintain FastAPI] and making sure it keeps [BOLD:advancing faster and better].

metadata.breadcrumb: Help FastAPI - Get Help > Help Maintain FastAPI

page_content:
[Context: Help FastAPI - Get Help > Join the chat]

## Join the chat
Join the Discord chat server and hang out with others in the FastAPI community.
/// tip
For questions, ask them in GitHub Discussions, there's a much better chance you will receive help by the FastAPI Experts.
Use the chat only for other general conversations.
///

metadata.breadcrumb: Help FastAPI - Get Help > Join the chat

page_content:
[Context: Help FastAPI - Get Help > Join the chat > Don't use the chat for questions]

### Don't use the chat for questions
Keep in mind that as chats allow more "free conversation", it's easy to ask questions that are too general and more difficult to answer, so, you might not receive answers.
In GitHub, the template will guide you to write the right question so that you can more easily get a good answer, or even solve the problem yourself even before asking. And in GitHub I can make sure I always answer everything, even if it takes some time. I can't personally do that with the chat systems.
Conversations in the chat systems are also not as easily searchable as in GitHub, so questions and answers might get lost in the conversation. And only the ones in GitHub count to become a FastAPI Expert, so you will most probably receive more attention in GitHub.
On the other side, there are thousands of users in the chat systems, so there's a high chance you'll find someone to talk to there, almost all the time.

### Don't use the chat for questions
Keep in mind that as chats allow more "free conversation", it's easy to ask questions that are too general and more difficult to answer, so, you might not receive answers.
In GitHub, the template will guide you to write the right question so that you can more easily get a good answer, or even solve the problem yourself even before asking. And in GitHub I can make sure I always answer everything, even if it takes some time. I can't personally do that with the chat systems.
Conversations in the chat systems are also not as easily searchable as in GitHub, so questions and answers might get lost in the conversation. And only the ones in GitHub count to become a FastAPI Expert, so you will most probably receive more attention in GitHub.

page_content:
[Context: History, Design and Future]

# History, Design and Future
Some time ago, a [BOLD:FastAPI] user asked:
> What’s the history of this project? It seems to have come from nowhere to awesome in a few weeks [...]
Here's a little bit of that history.

[Context: History, Design and Future > Alternatives]

## Alternatives
I have been creating APIs with complex requirements for several years (Machine Learning, distributed systems, asynchronous jobs, NoSQL databases, etc), leading several teams of developers.
As part of that, I needed to investigate, test and use many alternatives.
The history of [BOLD:FastAPI] is in great part the history of its predecessors.
As said in the section Alternatives:

[BOLD:FastAPI] wouldn't exist if not for the previous work of others.
There have been many tools created before that have helped inspire its creation.
I have been avoiding the creation of a new framework for several years. First I tried to solve all the features covered by [BOLD:FastAPI] using many different frameworks, plug-ins, and tools.
But at some point, there was no other option than creating something that provided all these features, taking the best ideas from previous tools, and combining them in the best way possible, using language features that weren't even available before (Python 3.6+ type hints).

[Context: History, Design and Future > Alternatives]

## Alternatives
I have been creating APIs with complex requirements for several years (Machine Learning, distributed systems, asynchronous jobs, NoSQL databases, etc), leading several teams of developers.
As part of that, I needed to investigate, test and use many alternatives.
The history of [BOLD:FastAPI] is in great part the history of its predecessors.
As said in the section Alternatives:

[BOLD:FastAPI] wouldn't exist if not for the previous work of others.
There have been many tools created before that have helped inspire its creation.
I have been avoiding the creation of a new framework for several years. First I tried to solve all the features covered by [BOLD:FastAPI] using many different frameworks, plug-ins, and tools.
But at some point, there was no other option than creating something that provided all these features, taking the best ideas from previous tools, and combining them in the best way possible, using language features that weren't even available before (Python 3.6+ type hints).

[Context: History, Design and Future > Investigation]

## Investigation
By using all the previous alternatives I had the chance to learn from all of them, take ideas, and combine them in the best way I could find for myself and the teams of developers I have worked with.
For example, it was clear that ideally it should be based on standard Python type hints.
Also, the best approach was to use already existing standards.
So, before even starting to code [BOLD:FastAPI], I spent several months studying the specs for OpenAPI, JSON Schema, OAuth2, etc. Understanding their relationship, overlap, and differences.

[Context: History, Design and Future > Design]

## Design
Then I spent some time designing the developer "API" I wanted to have as a user (as a developer using FastAPI).
I tested several ideas in the most popular Python editors: PyCharm, VS Code, Jedi based editors.
By the last Python Developer Survey, that covers about 80% of the users.
It means that [BOLD:FastAPI] was specifically tested with the editors used by 80% of the Python developers. And as most of the other editors tend to work similarly, all its benefits should work for virtually all editors.
That way I could find the best ways to reduce code duplication as much as possible, to have completion everywhere, type and error checks, etc.
All in a way that provided the best development experience for all the developers.

[Context: History, Design and Future > Requirements]

## Requirements
After testing several alternatives, I decided that I was going to use [BOLD:Pydantic] for its advantages.
Then I contributed to it, to make it fully compliant with JSON Schema, to support different ways to define constraint declarations, and to improve editor support (type checks, autocompletion) based on the tests in several editors.
During the development, I also contributed to [BOLD:Starlette], the other key requirement.

[Context: History, Design and Future > Development]

## Development
By the time I started creating [BOLD:FastAPI] itself, most of the pieces were already in place, the design was defined, the requirements and tools were ready, and the knowledge about the standards and specifications was clear and fresh.

[Context: History, Design and Future > Future]

## Future
By this point, it's already clear that [BOLD:FastAPI] with its ideas is being useful for many people.
It is being chosen over previous alternatives for suiting many use cases better.
Many developers and teams already depend on [BOLD:FastAPI] for their projects (including me and my team).
But still, there are many improvements and features to come.
[BOLD:FastAPI] has a great future ahead.
And your help is greatly appreciated.

[Context: FastAPI > FastAPI mini documentary]

## FastAPI mini documentary
There's a FastAPI mini documentary released at the end of 2025, you can watch it online:

[Context: FastAPI > **Typer**, the FastAPI of CLIs]

## [BOLD:Typer], the FastAPI of CLIs

If you are building a CLI app to be used in the terminal instead of a web API, check out [BOLD:Typer].
[BOLD:Typer] is FastAPI's little sibling. And it's intended to be the [BOLD:FastAPI of CLIs]. ⌨

[Context: FastAPI > Requirements]

## Requirements
FastAPI stands on the shoulders of giants:
[ITALIC: Starlette for the web parts.
] Pydantic for the data parts.

[Context: FastAPI > Installation]

## Installation
Create and activate a virtual environment and then install FastAPI:

[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: $ pip install "fastapi[standard]" ---> 100%...]

[BOLD:Note]: Make sure you put [CODE: "fastapi[standard]"] in quotes to ensure it works in all terminals.

[Context: FastAPI > Example > Create it]

## Example
### Create it
Create a file [CODE: main.py] with:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: from fastapi import FastAPI app = FastAPI() @app.g...]

Or use async def...
If your code uses [CODE: async] / [CODE: await], use [CODE: async def]:
```Python hl[ITALIC:lines="7 12"
from fastapi import FastAPI

app = FastAPI()

@app.get("/")
async def read]root():
return {"Hello": "World"}

@app.get("/items/{item[ITALIC:id}")
async def read]item(item[ITALIC:id: int, q: str | None = None):
return {"item]id": item[ITALIC:id, "q": q}
```
[BOLD:Note]:
If you don't know, check the ]"In a hurry?"_ section about [[CODE: async] and [CODE: await] in the docs](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/async/#in-a-hurry).

[Context: FastAPI > Example > Run it]

### Run it
Run the server with:

[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: $ fastapi dev FastAPI CLI - Developmen...]


About the command fastapi dev...
The command [CODE: fastapi dev] reads your [CODE: main.py] file automatically, detects the [BOLD:FastAPI] app in it, and starts a server using Uvicorn.
By default, [CODE: fastapi dev] will start with auto-reload enabled for local development.
You can read more about it in the FastAPI CLI docs.

[Context: FastAPI > Example > Check it]

### Check it
Open your browser at http://127.0.0.1:8000/items/5?q=somequery.
You will see the JSON response as:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: {"item[ITALIC:id": 5, "q": "somequery"}...]
You already created an API that:
[ITALIC: Receives HTTP requests in the ]paths[ITALIC: [CODE: /] and [CODE: /items/{item]id}].
] Both [ITALIC:paths] take [CODE: GET] operations (also known as HTTP [ITALIC:methods]).
[ITALIC: The [ITALIC:path] [CODE: /items/{item[ITALIC:id}] has a ]path parameter[ITALIC: [CODE: item]id] that should be an [CODE: int].
] The [ITALIC:path] [CODE: /items/{item[ITALIC:id}] has an optional [CODE: str] ]query parameter_ [CODE: q].

[Context: FastAPI > Example > Interactive API docs]

### Interactive API docs
Now go to http://127.0.0.1:8000/docs.
You will see the automatic interactive API documentation (provided by Swagger UI):
!Swagger UI

[Context: FastAPI > Example > Alternative API docs]

### Alternative API docs
And now, go to http://127.0.0.1:8000/redoc.
You will see the alternative automatic documentation (provided by ReDoc):
!ReDoc

[Context: FastAPI > Example upgrade > Interactive API docs upgrade]

### Interactive API docs upgrade
Now go to http://127.0.0.1:8000/docs.
[ITALIC: The interactive API documentation will be automatically updated, including the new body:
!Swagger UI
] Click on the button "Try it out", it allows you to fill the parameters and directly interact with the API:
!Swagger UI interaction
- Then click on the "Execute" button, the user interface will communicate with your API, send the parameters, get the results and show them on the screen:
!Swagger UI interaction

page_content:
[Context: FastAPI > Example upgrade > Alternative API docs upgrade]

### Alternative API docs upgrade
And now, go to http://127.0.0.1:8000/redoc.
- The alternative documentation will also reflect the new query parameter and body:
!ReDoc

page_content:
[Context: FastAPI > Example upgrade > Deploy your app (optional)]

### Deploy your app (optional)
You can optionally deploy your FastAPI app to FastAPI Cloud, go and join the waiting list if you haven't.
If you already have a [BOLD:FastAPI Cloud] account (we invited you from the waiting list ), you can deploy your application with one command.

[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: $ fastapi deploy Deploying to FastAPI Cloud... D...]

That's it! Now you can access your app at that URL.
#### About FastAPI Cloud
[BOLD:FastAPI Cloud] is built by the same author and team behind [BOLD:FastAPI].
It streamlines the process of [BOLD:building], [BOLD:deploying], and [BOLD:accessing] an API with minimal effort.
It brings the same [BOLD:developer experience] of building apps with FastAPI to [BOLD:deploying] them to the cloud.
FastAPI Cloud is the primary sponsor and funding provider for the [ITALIC:FastAPI and friends] open source projects.
#### Deploy to other cloud providers
FastAPI is open source and based on standards. You can deploy FastAPI apps to any cloud provider you choose.
Follow your cloud provider's guides to deploy FastAPI apps with them. 🤓

metadata.breadcrumb: FastAPI > Example upgrade > Deploy your app (optional)

page_content:
[Context: FastAPI > Performance]

## Performance
Independent TechEmpower benchmarks show [BOLD:FastAPI] applications running under Uvicorn as one of the fastest Python frameworks available, only below Starlette and Uvicorn themselves (used internally by FastAPI). (*)
To understand more about it, see the section Benchmarks.

[Context: FastAPI > Dependencies]

## Dependencies
FastAPI depends on Pydantic and Starlette.

[Context: FastAPI > Dependencies > `standard` Dependencies]

### [CODE: standard] Dependencies
When you install FastAPI with [CODE: pip install "fastapi[standard]"] it comes with the [CODE: standard] group of optional dependencies:
Used by Pydantic:
[ITALIC: [[CODE: email-validator]](https://github.com/JoshData/python-email-validator) - for email validation.
Used by Starlette:
] [[CODE: httpx]](https://www.python-httpx.org) - Required if you want to use the [CODE: TestClient].
[ITALIC: [[CODE: jinja2]](https://jinja.palletsprojects.com) - Required if you want to use the default template configuration.
] [[CODE: python-multipart]](https://github.com/Kludex/python-multipart) - Required if you want to support form "parsing", with [CODE: request.form()].
Used by FastAPI:
[ITALIC: [[CODE: uvicorn]](https://www.uvicorn.dev) - for the server that loads and serves your application. This includes [CODE: uvicorn[standard]], which includes some dependencies (e.g. [CODE: uvloop]) needed for high performance serving.
] [CODE: fastapi-cli[standard]] - to provide the [CODE: fastapi] command.
- This includes [CODE: fastapi-cloud-cli], which allows you to deploy your FastAPI application to FastAPI Cloud.

[Context: FastAPI > Dependencies > `standard` Dependencies]

### [CODE: standard] Dependencies
When you install FastAPI with [CODE: pip install "fastapi[standard]"] it comes with the [CODE: standard] group of optional dependencies:
Used by Pydantic:
[ITALIC: [[CODE: email-validator]](https://github.com/JoshData/python-email-validator) - for email validation.
Used by Starlette:
] [[CODE: httpx]](https://www.python-httpx.org) - Required if you want to use the [CODE: TestClient].
[ITALIC: [[CODE: jinja2]](https://jinja.palletsprojects.com) - Required if you want to use the default template configuration.
] [[CODE: python-multipart]](https://github.com/Kludex/python-multipart) - Required if you want to support form "parsing", with [CODE: request.form()].
Used by FastAPI:
[ITALIC: [[CODE: uvicorn]](https://www.uvicorn.dev) - for the server that loads and serves your application. This includes [CODE: uvicorn[standard]], which includes some dependencies (e.g. [CODE: uvloop]) needed for high performance serving.
] [CODE: fastapi-cli[standard]] - to provide the [CODE: fastapi] command.
- This includes [CODE: fastapi-cloud-cli], which allows you to deploy your FastAPI application to FastAPI Cloud.

[Context: FastAPI > Dependencies > Without `standard` Dependencies]

### Without [CODE: standard] Dependencies
If you don't want to include the [CODE: standard] optional dependencies, you can install with [CODE: pip install fastapi] instead of [CODE: pip install "fastapi[standard]"].

[Context: FastAPI > Dependencies > Without `fastapi-cloud-cli`]

### Without [CODE: fastapi-cloud-cli]
If you want to install FastAPI with the standard dependencies but without the [CODE: fastapi-cloud-cli], you can install with [CODE: pip install "fastapi[standard-no-fastapi-cloud-cli]"].

[Context: FastAPI > Dependencies > Additional Optional Dependencies]

### Additional Optional Dependencies
There are some additional dependencies you might want to install.
Additional optional Pydantic dependencies:
[ITALIC: [[CODE: pydantic-settings]](https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/usage/pydantic[ITALIC:settings/) - for settings management.
] [[CODE: pydantic-extra-types]](https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/usage/types/extra]types/extra_types/) - for extra types to be used with Pydantic.
Additional optional FastAPI dependencies:
[ITALIC: [[CODE: orjson]](https://github.com/ijl/orjson) - Required if you want to use [CODE: ORJSONResponse].
] [[CODE: ujson]](https://github.com/esnme/ultrajson) - Required if you want to use [CODE: UJSONResponse].

[Context: FastAPI > License]

## License
This project is licensed under the terms of the MIT license.

[Context: Repository Management Tasks]

# Repository Management Tasks
These are the tasks that can be performed to manage the FastAPI repository by team members.
/// tip
This section is useful only to a handful of people, team members with permissions to manage the repository. You can probably skip it.
///
...so, you are a team member of FastAPI? Wow, you are so cool!
You can help with everything on Help FastAPI - Get Help the same ways as external contributors. But additionally, there are some tasks that only you (as part of the team) can perform.
Here are the general instructions for the tasks you can perform.
Thanks a lot for your help.

[Context: Repository Management Tasks > Be Nice]

## Be Nice
First of all, be nice.
You probably are super nice if you were added to the team, but it's worth mentioning it. 🤓

[Context: Repository Management Tasks > Be Nice > When Things are Difficult]

### When Things are Difficult
When things are great, everything is easier, so that doesn't need much instructions. But when things are difficult, here are some guidelines.
Try to find the good side. In general, if people are not being unfriendly, try to thank their effort and interest, even if you disagree with the main subject (discussion, PR), just thank them for being interested in the project, or for having dedicated some time to try to do something.
It's difficult to convey emotion in text, use emojis to help.
In discussions and PRs, in many cases, people bring their frustration and show it without filter, in many cases exaggerating, complaining, being entitled, etc. That's really not nice, and when it happens, it lowers our priority to solve their problems. But still, try to breath, and be gentle with your answers.
Try to avoid using bitter sarcasm or potentially passive-aggressive comments. If something is wrong, it's better to be direct (try to be gentle) than sarcastic.
Try to be as specific and objective as possible, avoid generalizations.
For conversations that are more difficult, for example to reject a PR, you can ask me (@tiangolo) to handle it directly.

metadata.breadcrumb: Repository Management Tasks > Be Nice > When Things are Difficult

page_content:
[Context: Repository Management Tasks > Add Labels to Translation PRs]

## Add Labels to Translation PRs
When there's a PR for a translation, apart from adding the [CODE: lang-all] label, also add a label for the language.
There will be a label for each language using the language code, like [CODE: lang-{lang code}], for example, [CODE: lang-es] for Spanish, [CODE: lang-fr] for French, etc.
[ITALIC: Add the specific language label.
] Add the label [CODE: awaiting-review].
The label [CODE: awaiting-review] is special, only used for translations. A GitHub Action will detect it, then it will read the language label, and it will update the GitHub Discussions managing the translations for that language to notify people that there's a new translation to review.
Once a native speaker comes, reviews the PR, and approves it, the GitHub Action will come and remove the [CODE: awaiting-review] label, and add the [CODE: approved-1] label.
This way, we can notice when there are new translations ready, because they have the [CODE: approved-1] label.

metadata.breadcrumb: Repository Management Tasks > Add Labels to Translation PRs

page_content:
[Context: Repository Management Tasks > Merge Translation PRs]

## Merge Translation PRs
Translations are generated automatically with LLMs and scripts.
There's one GitHub Action that can be manually run to add or update translations for a language: [[CODE: translate.yml]](https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/actions/workflows/translate.yml).
For these language translation PRs, confirm that:
[ITALIC: The PR was automated (authored by @tiangolo), not made by another user.
] It has the labels [CODE: lang-all] and [CODE: lang-{lang code}].
- If the PR is approved by at least one native speaker, you can merge it.

metadata.breadcrumb: Repository Management Tasks > Merge Translation PRs

page_content:
[Context: Repository Management Tasks > FastAPI People PRs]

## FastAPI People PRs
Every month, a GitHub Action updates the FastAPI People data. Those PRs look like this one: Update FastAPI People.
If the tests are passing, you can merge it right away.

metadata.breadcrumb: Repository Management Tasks > FastAPI People PRs

page_content:
[Context: Repository Management Tasks > Dependabot PRs]

## Dependabot PRs
Dependabot will create PRs to update dependencies for several things, and those PRs all look similar, but some are way more delicate than others.
[ITALIC: If the PR is for a direct dependency, so, Dependabot is modifying [CODE: pyproject.toml] in the main dependencies, [BOLD:don't merge it]. Let me check it first. There's a good chance that some additional tweaks or updates are needed.
] If the PR updates one of the internal dependencies, for example the group [CODE: dev] in [CODE: pyproject.toml], or GitHub Action versions, if the tests are passing, the release notes (shown in a summary in the PR) don't show any obvious potential breaking change, you can merge it.

[Context: Repository Management Tasks > Mark GitHub Discussions Answers]

## Mark GitHub Discussions Answers
When a question in GitHub Discussions has been answered, mark the answer by clicking "Mark as answer".
You can filter discussions by [[CODE: Questions] that are [CODE: Unanswered]](https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/discussions/categories/questions?discussions_q=category:Questions+is:open+is:unanswered).

metadata.breadcrumb: Repository Management Tasks > Mark GitHub Discussions Answers

page_content:
[Context: Repository Management]

# Repository Management
Here's a short description of how the FastAPI repository is managed and maintained.

[Context: Repository Management > Owner]

## Owner
I, @tiangolo, am the creator and owner of the FastAPI repository. 🤓
I normally give the final review to each PR before merging them. I make the final decisions on the project, I'm the BDFL.

[Context: Repository Management > Team]

## Team
There's a team of people that help manage and maintain the project.
They have different levels of permissions and specific instructions.
Some of the tasks they can perform include:
[ITALIC: Adding labels to PRs.
] Editing PR titles.
[ITALIC: Adding commits on top of PRs to tweak them.
] Mark answers in GitHub Discussions questions, etc.
- Merge some specific types of PRs.
You can see the current team members in FastAPI People - Team.
Joining the team is by invitation only, and I could update or remove permissions, instructions, or membership.

[Context: Repository Management > FastAPI Experts]

## FastAPI Experts
The people that help others the most in GitHub Discussions can become [BOLD:FastAPI Experts].
This is normally the best way to contribute to the project.

[Context: Repository Management > External Contributions]

## External Contributions
External contributions are very welcome and appreciated, including answering questions, submitting PRs, etc.
There are many ways to help maintain FastAPI.

[Context: FastAPI and friends newsletter]

# FastAPI and friends newsletter

[Context: Full Stack FastAPI Template]

# Full Stack FastAPI Template
Templates, while typically come with a specific setup, are designed to be flexible and customizable. This allows you to modify and adapt them to your project's requirements, making them an excellent starting point.
You can use this template to get started, as it includes a lot of the initial set up, security, database and some API endpoints already done for you.
GitHub Repository: Full Stack FastAPI Template

[Context: Full Stack FastAPI Template > Full Stack FastAPI Template - Technology Stack and Features]

## Full Stack FastAPI Template - Technology Stack and Features
- [BOLD:FastAPI] for the Python backend API.
- 🧰 SQLModel for the Python SQL database interactions (ORM).
- Pydantic, used by FastAPI, for the data validation and settings management.
- PostgreSQL as the SQL database.
- React for the frontend.
- Using TypeScript, hooks, Vite, and other parts of a modern frontend stack.
- Tailwind CSS and shadcn/ui for the frontend components.
- 🤖 An automatically generated frontend client.
- 🧪 Playwright for End-to-End testing.
- 🦇 Dark mode support.
- Docker Compose for development and production.
- Secure password hashing by default.
- JWT (JSON Web Token) authentication.
- Email based password recovery.
- Tests with Pytest.
- Traefik as a reverse proxy / load balancer.
- Deployment instructions using Docker Compose, including how to set up a frontend Traefik proxy to handle automatic HTTPS certificates.
- CI (continuous integration) and CD (continuous deployment) based on GitHub Actions.

hierarchy: Full Stack FastAPI Template - Technology Stack and Features
metadata.breadcrumb: Full Stack FastAPI Template > Full Stack FastAPI Template - Technology Stack and Features

page_content:
[Context: Python Types Intro]

# Python Types Intro
Python has support for optional "type hints" (also called "type annotations").
These [BOLD:"type hints"] or annotations are a special syntax that allow declaring the type of a variable.
By declaring types for your variables, editors and tools can give you better support.
This is just a [BOLD:quick tutorial / refresher] about Python type hints. It covers only the minimum necessary to use them with [BOLD:FastAPI]... which is actually very little.
[BOLD:FastAPI] is all based on these type hints, they give it many advantages and benefits.
But even if you never use [BOLD:FastAPI], you would benefit from learning a bit about them.
/// note
If you are a Python expert, and you already know everything about type hints, skip to the next chapter.
///

[Context: Python Types Intro > Motivation]

## Motivation
Let's start with a simple example:
{[ITALIC: ../../docs[ITALIC:src/python]types/tutorial001[ITALIC:py310.py ]}
Calling this program outputs:
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:0: John Doe...]
The function does the following:
[ITALIC: Takes a [CODE: first]name] and [CODE: last[ITALIC:name].
] Converts the first letter of each one to upper case with [CODE: title()].
- Concatenates them with a space in the middle.
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:1: full]name = first[ITALIC:name.title() + " " + last]name.t...]

[Context: Python Types Intro > Motivation > Edit it]

### Edit it
It's a very simple program.
But now imagine that you were writing it from scratch.
At some point you would have started the definition of the function, you had the parameters ready...
But then you have to call "that method that converts the first letter to upper case".
Was it [CODE: upper]? Was it [CODE: uppercase]? [CODE: first[ITALIC:uppercase]? [CODE: capitalize]?
Then, you try with the old programmer's friend, editor autocompletion.
You type the first parameter of the function, [CODE: first]name], then a dot ([CODE: .]) and then hit [CODE: Ctrl+Space] to trigger the completion.
But, sadly, you get nothing useful:

metadata.breadcrumb: Python Types Intro > Motivation > Edit it

page_content:
[Context: Python Types Intro > Motivation > Add types]

### Add types
Let's modify a single line from the previous version.
We will change exactly this fragment, the parameters of the function, from:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: first[ITALIC:name, last]name...]
to:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]1: first[ITALIC:name: str, last]name: str...]
That's it.
Those are the "type hints":
```python
def get[ITALIC:full]name(first[ITALIC:name: str, last]name: str):
full[ITALIC:name = first]name.title() + " " + last[ITALIC:name.title()
```
That is not the same as declaring default values like would be with:
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:2: first]name="john", last_name="doe"...]
It's a different thing.
We are using colons ([CODE: :]), not equals ([CODE: =]).
And adding type hints normally doesn't change what happens from what would happen without them.
But now, imagine you are again in the middle of creating that function, but with type hints.
At the same point, you try to trigger the autocomplete with [CODE: Ctrl+Space] and you see:

With that, you can scroll, seeing the options, until you find the one that "rings a bell":

metadata.breadcrumb: Python Types Intro > Motivation > Add types


page_content:
[Context: Python Types Intro > More motivation]

## More motivation
Check this function, it already has type hints:
```python
def get[ITALIC:name]with[ITALIC:age(name: str, age: int):
name]with[ITALIC:age = name + " is this old: " + age
```
Because the editor knows the types of the variables, you don't only get completion, you also get error checks:

Now you know that you have to fix it, convert [CODE: age] to a string with [CODE: str(age)]:
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:0: name]with_age = name + " is this old: " + str(age)...]

[Context: Python Types Intro > Declaring types]

## Declaring types
You just saw the main place to declare type hints. As function parameters.
This is also the main place you would use them with [BOLD:FastAPI].

[Context: Python Types Intro > Declaring types > Simple types]

### Simple types
You can declare all the standard Python types, not only [CODE: str].
You can use, for example:
[ITALIC: [CODE: int]
] [CODE: float]
[ITALIC: [CODE: bool]
] [CODE: bytes]
```python
def get[ITALIC:items(item]a: str, item[ITALIC:b: int, item]c: float, item[ITALIC:d: bool, item]e: bytes):
return item[ITALIC:a, item]b, item[ITALIC:c, item]d, item_e
```

metadata.breadcrumb: Python Types Intro > Declaring types > Simple types

page_content:
[Context: Python Types Intro > Declaring types > `typing` module]

### [CODE: typing] module
For some additional use cases, you might need to import some things from the standard library [CODE: typing] module, for example when you want to declare that something has "any type", you can use [CODE: Any] from [CODE: typing]:
```python
from typing import Any

def some_function(data: Any):
print(data)
```

metadata.breadcrumb: Python Types Intro > Declaring types > `typing` module

page_content:
[Context: Python Types Intro > Declaring types > Generic types]

### Generic types
Some types can take "type parameters" in square brackets, to define their internal types, for example a "list of strings" would be declared [CODE: list[str]].
These types that can take type parameters are called [BOLD:Generic types] or [BOLD:Generics].
You can use the same builtin types as generics (with square brackets and types inside):
[ITALIC: [CODE: list]
] [CODE: tuple]
[ITALIC: [CODE: set]
] [CODE: dict]
#### List
For example, let's define a variable to be a [CODE: list] of [CODE: str].
Declare the variable, with the same colon ([CODE: :]) syntax.
As the type, put [CODE: list].
As the list is a type that contains some internal types, you put them in square brackets:
```python
def process[ITALIC:items(items: list[str]):
for item in items:
```
/// info
Those internal types in the square brackets are called "type parameters".
In this case, [CODE: str] is the type parameter passed to [CODE: list].
///
That means: "the variable [CODE: items] is a [CODE: list], and each of the items in this list is a [CODE: str]".
By doing that, your editor can provide support even while processing items from the list:

Without types, that's almost impossible to achieve.
Notice that the variable [CODE: item] is one of the elements in the list [CODE: items].
And still, the editor knows it is a [CODE: str], and provides support for that.
#### Tuple and Set
You would do the same to declare [CODE: tuple]s and [CODE: set]s:
```python
def process]items(items[ITALIC:t: tuple[int, int, str], items]s: set[bytes]):
return items[ITALIC:t, items]s
```
This means:
[ITALIC: The variable [CODE: items[ITALIC:t] is a [CODE: tuple] with 3 items, an [CODE: int], another [CODE: int], and a [CODE: str].
] The variable [CODE: items]s] is a [CODE: set], and each of its items is of type [CODE: bytes].
#### Dict
To define a [CODE: dict], you pass 2 type parameters, separated by commas.
The first type parameter is for the keys of the [CODE: dict].
The second type parameter is for the values of the [CODE: dict]:
```python
def process[ITALIC:items(prices: dict[str, float]):
for item]name, item[ITALIC:price in prices.items():
```
This means:
[ITALIC: The variable [CODE: prices] is a [CODE: dict]:
] The keys of this [CODE: dict] are of type [CODE: str] (let's say, the name of each item).
- The values of this [CODE: dict] are of type [CODE: float] (let's say, the price of each item).
#### Union
You can declare that a variable can be any of [BOLD:several types], for example, an [CODE: int] or a [CODE: str].
To define it you use the vertical bar ([CODE: |]) to separate both types.
This is called a "union", because the variable can be anything in the union of those two sets of types.
```Python hl]lines="1"
{!> ../../docs[ITALIC:src/python]types/tutorial008b[ITALIC:py310.py!}
```
This means that [CODE: item] could be an [CODE: int] or a [CODE: str].
#### Possibly [CODE: None]
You can declare that a value could have a type, like [CODE: str], but that it could also be [CODE: None].
//// tab | Python 3.10+
```Python hl]lines="1"
{!> ../../docs[ITALIC:src/python]types/tutorial009_py310.py!}
```
////
Using [CODE: str | None] instead of just [CODE: str] will let the editor help you detect errors where you could be assuming that a value is always a [CODE: str], when it could actually be [CODE: None] too.

metadata.breadcrumb: Python Types Intro > Declaring types > Generic types

page_content:
[Context: Python Types Intro > Declaring types > Classes as types]

### Classes as types
You can also declare a class as the type of a variable.
Let's say you have a class [CODE: Person], with a name:
```python
class Person:
def [BOLD:init](self, name: str):
self.name = name
```
Then you can declare a variable to be of type [CODE: Person]:
```python
def get[ITALIC:person]name(one[ITALIC:person: Person):
return one]person.name
```
And then, again, you get all the editor support:

Notice that this means "[CODE: one[ITALIC:person] is an [BOLD:instance] of the class [CODE: Person]".
It doesn't mean "[CODE: one]person] is the [BOLD:class] called [CODE: Person]".

metadata.breadcrumb: Python Types Intro > Declaring types > Classes as types

page_content:
[Context: Python Types Intro > Pydantic models]

## Pydantic models
Pydantic is a Python library to perform data validation.
You declare the "shape" of the data as classes with attributes.
And each attribute has a type.
Then you create an instance of that class with some values and it will validate the values, convert them to the appropriate type (if that's the case) and give you an object with all the data.
And you get all the editor support with that resulting object.
An example from the official Pydantic docs:
{[ITALIC: ../../docs[ITALIC:src/python]types/tutorial011_py310.py ]}
/// info
To learn more about Pydantic, check its docs.
///
[BOLD:FastAPI] is all based on Pydantic.
You will see a lot more of all this in practice in the Tutorial - User Guide.

[Context: Python Types Intro > Type Hints with Metadata Annotations]

## Type Hints with Metadata Annotations
Python also has a feature that allows putting [BOLD:additional metadata] in these type hints using [CODE: Annotated].
You can import [CODE: Annotated] from [CODE: typing].
```python
from typing import Annotated

def say_hello(name: Annotated[str, "this is just metadata"]) -> str:
return f"Hello {name}"
```
Python itself doesn't do anything with this [CODE: Annotated]. And for editors and other tools, the type is still [CODE: str].
But you can use this space in [CODE: Annotated] to provide [BOLD:FastAPI] with additional metadata about how you want your application to behave.
The important thing to remember is that *[ITALIC:the first ]type parameter[ITALIC:[BOLD: you pass to [CODE: Annotated] is the ]actual type[BOLD:. The rest, is just metadata for other tools.
For now, you just need to know that [CODE: Annotated] exists, and that it's standard Python.
Later you will see how ]powerful[BOLD: it can be.
/// tip
The fact that this is ]standard Python[BOLD: means that you will still get the ]best possible developer experience]* in your editor, with the tools you use to analyze and refactor your code, etc.
And also that your code will be very compatible with many other Python tools and libraries.
///

metadata.breadcrumb: Python Types Intro > Type Hints with Metadata Annotations

## 0.120.0 (2025-10-23)
There are no major nor breaking changes in this release.
The internal reference documentation now uses [CODE: annotated[ITALIC:doc.Doc] instead of [CODE: typing]extensions.Doc], this adds a new (very small) dependency on [[CODE: annotated-doc]](https://github.com/fastapi/annotated-doc), a package made just to provide that [CODE: Doc] documentation utility class.
I would expect [CODE: typing[ITALIC:extensions.Doc] to be deprecated and then removed at some point from [CODE: typing]extensions], for that reason there's the new [CODE: annotated-doc] micro-package. If you are curious about this, you can read more in the repo for [[CODE: annotated-doc]](https://github.com/fastapi/annotated-doc).
This new version [CODE: 0.120.0] only contains that transition to the new home package for that utility class [CODE: Doc].

## 0.119.0 (2025-10-11)
FastAPI now (temporarily) supports both Pydantic v2 models and [CODE: pydantic.v1] models at the same time in the same app, to make it easier for any FastAPI apps still using Pydantic v1 to gradually but quickly [BOLD:migrate to Pydantic v2].
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: from fastapi import FastAPI from pydantic import B...]
Adding this feature was a big effort with the main objective of making it easier for the few applications still stuck in Pydantic v1 to migrate to Pydantic v2.
And with this, support for [BOLD:Pydantic v1 is now deprecated] and will be [BOLD:removed] from FastAPI in a future version soon.
[BOLD:Note]: have in mind that the Pydantic team already stopped supporting Pydantic v1 for recent versions of Python, starting with Python 3.14.
You can read in the docs more about how to Migrate from Pydantic v1 to Pydantic v2.

[Context: Release Notes > 0.119.0 (2025-10-11) > Features]

### Features
- Add support for [CODE: from pydantic.v1 import BaseModel], mixed Pydantic v1 and v2 models in the same app. PR #14168 by @tiangolo.

[Context: Release Notes > 0.118.0 (2025-09-29) > Fixes]

## 0.118.0 (2025-09-29)
### Fixes
[ITALIC: Fix support for [CODE: StreamingResponse]s with dependencies with [CODE: yield] or [CODE: UploadFile]s, close after the response is done. PR #14099 by @tiangolo.
Before FastAPI 0.118.0, if you used a dependency with [CODE: yield], it would run the exit code after the ]path operation function* returned but right before sending the response.
This change also meant that if you returned a [CODE: StreamingResponse], the exit code of the dependency with [CODE: yield] would have been already run.
For example, if you had a database session in a dependency with [CODE: yield], the [CODE: StreamingResponse] would not be able to use that session while streaming data because the session would have already been closed in the exit code after [CODE: yield].
This behavior was reverted in 0.118.0, to make the exit code after [CODE: yield] be executed after the response is sent.
You can read more about it in the docs for [Advanced Dependencies - Dependencies with [CODE: yield], [CODE: HTTPException], [CODE: except] and Background Tasks](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/advanced/advanced-dependencies#dependencies-with-yield-httpexception-except-and-background-tasks). Including what you could do if you wanted to close a database session earlier, before returning the response to the client.

[Context: Release Notes > 0.118.0 (2025-09-29) > Fixes]

## 0.115.0 (2024-09-17)
### Highlights
Now you can declare [CODE: Query], [CODE: Header], and [CODE: Cookie] parameters with Pydantic models.
#### [CODE: Query] Parameter Models
Use Pydantic models for [CODE: Query] parameters:
```python
from typing import Annotated, Literal

from fastapi import FastAPI, Query
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field

app = FastAPI()

class FilterParams(BaseModel):
limit: int = Field(100, gt=0, le=100)
offset: int = Field(0, ge=0)
order[ITALIC:by: Literal["created]at", "updated[ITALIC:at"] = "created]at"
tags: list[str] = []

@app.get("/items/")
async def read[ITALIC:items(filter]query: Annotated[FilterParams, Query()]):
return filter[ITALIC:query
```
Read the new docs: Query Parameter Models.
#### [CODE: Header] Parameter Models
Use Pydantic models for [CODE: Header] parameters:
```python
from typing import Annotated

from fastapi import FastAPI, Header
from pydantic import BaseModel

app = FastAPI()

class CommonHeaders(BaseModel):
host: str
save]data: bool
if[ITALIC:modified]since: str | None = None
traceparent: str | None = None
x[ITALIC:tag: list[str] = []

@app.get("/items/")
async def read]items(headers: Annotated[CommonHeaders, Header()]):
return headers
```
Read the new docs: Header Parameter Models.
#### [CODE: Cookie] Parameter Models
Use Pydantic models for [CODE: Cookie] parameters:
```python
from typing import Annotated

from fastapi import Cookie, FastAPI
from pydantic import BaseModel

app = FastAPI()

class Cookies(BaseModel):
session[ITALIC:id: str
fatebook]tracker: str | None = None
googall[ITALIC:tracker: str | None = None

@app.get("/items/")
async def read]items(cookies: Annotated[Cookies, Cookie()]):
return cookies
```
Read the new docs: Cookie Parameter Models.
#### Forbid Extra Query (Cookie, Header) Parameters
Use Pydantic models to restrict extra values for [CODE: Query] parameters (also applies to [CODE: Header] and [CODE: Cookie] parameters).
To achieve it, use Pydantic's [CODE: model[ITALIC:config = {"extra": "forbid"}]:
```python
from typing import Annotated, Literal

from fastapi import FastAPI, Query
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field

app = FastAPI()

class FilterParams(BaseModel):
model]config = {"extra": "forbid"}

limit: int = Field(100, gt=0, le=100)
offset: int = Field(0, ge=0)
order[ITALIC:by: Literal["created]at", "updated[ITALIC:at"] = "created]at"
tags: list[str] = []

@app.get("/items/")
async def read[ITALIC:items(filter]query: Annotated[FilterParams, Query()]):
return filter_query
```
This applies to [CODE: Query], [CODE: Header], and [CODE: Cookie] parameters, read the new docs:
[ITALIC: Forbid Extra Query Parameters
] Forbid Extra Headers
- Forbid Extra Cookies

## 0.114.0 (2024-09-06)
You can restrict form fields to only include those declared in a Pydantic model and forbid any extra field sent in the request using Pydantic's [CODE: model[ITALIC:config = {"extra": "forbid"}]:
```python
from typing import Annotated

from fastapi import FastAPI, Form
from pydantic import BaseModel

app = FastAPI()

class FormData(BaseModel):
username: str
password: str
model]config = {"extra": "forbid"}

@app.post("/login/")
async def login(data: Annotated[FormData, Form()]):
return data
```
Read the new docs: Form Models - Forbid Extra Form Fields.

## 0.112.0 (2024-08-02)
### Breaking Changes
[ITALIC: Add support for [CODE: pip install "fastapi[standard]"] with standard dependencies and [CODE: python -m fastapi]. PR #11935 by @tiangolo.
#### Summary
Install with:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: pip install "fastapi[standard]"...]
#### Other Changes
] This adds support for calling the CLI as:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]1: python -m fastapi...]
- And it upgrades [CODE: fastapi-cli[standard] >=0.0.5].
#### Technical Details
Before this, [CODE: fastapi] would include the standard dependencies, with Uvicorn and the [CODE: fastapi-cli], etc.
And [CODE: fastapi-slim] would not include those standard dependencies.
Now [CODE: fastapi] doesn't include those standard dependencies unless you install with [CODE: pip install "fastapi[standard]"].
Before, you would install [CODE: pip install fastapi], now you should include the [CODE: standard] optional dependencies (unless you want to exclude one of those): [CODE: pip install "fastapi[standard]"].
This change is because having the standard optional dependencies installed by default was being inconvenient to several users, and having to install instead [CODE: fastapi-slim] was not being a feasible solution.
Discussed here: #11522 and here: #11525

[Context: Release Notes > 0.112.0 (2024-08-02) > Breaking Changes]

## 0.110.0 (2024-02-24)
### Breaking Changes
[ITALIC: Fix unhandled growing memory for internal server errors, refactor dependencies with [CODE: yield] and [CODE: except] to require raising again as in regular Python. PR #11191 by @tiangolo.
] This is a breaking change (and only slightly) if you used dependencies with [CODE: yield], used [CODE: except] in those dependencies, and didn't raise again.
[ITALIC: This was reported internally by @rushilsrivastava as a memory leak when the server had unhandled exceptions that would produce internal server errors, the memory allocated before that point would not be released.
] Read the new docs: [Dependencies with [CODE: yield] and [CODE: except]](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/dependencies/dependencies-with-yield/#dependencies-with-yield-and-except).
In short, if you had dependencies that looked like:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: def my[ITALIC:dep(): try: yield except SomeException: pas...]
Now you need to make sure you raise again after [CODE: except], just as you would in regular Python:
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:1: def my]dep(): try: yield except SomeException: rai...]

[Context: Release Notes > 0.110.0 (2024-02-24) > Breaking Changes]

## 0.106.0 (2023-12-25)
### Breaking Changes
Using resources from dependencies with [CODE: yield] in background tasks is no longer supported.
This change is what supports the new features, read below. 🤓

[Context: Release Notes > 0.106.0 (2023-12-25) > Dependencies with `yield`, `HTTPException` and Background Tasks]

### Dependencies with [CODE: yield], [CODE: HTTPException] and Background Tasks
Dependencies with [CODE: yield] now can raise [CODE: HTTPException] and other exceptions after [CODE: yield].
Read the new docs here: [Dependencies with [CODE: yield] and [CODE: HTTPException]](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/dependencies/dependencies-with-yield/#dependencies-with-yield-and-httpexception).
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: from fastapi import Depends, FastAPI, HTTPExceptio...]
---
Before FastAPI 0.106.0, raising exceptions after [CODE: yield] was not possible, the exit code in dependencies with [CODE: yield] was executed [ITALIC:after] the response was sent, so Exception Handlers would have already run.
This was designed this way mainly to allow using the same objects "yielded" by dependencies inside of background tasks, because the exit code would be executed after the background tasks were finished.
Nevertheless, as this would mean waiting for the response to travel through the network while unnecessarily holding a resource in a dependency with yield (for example a database connection), this was changed in FastAPI 0.106.0.
Additionally, a background task is normally an independent set of logic that should be handled separately, with its own resources (e.g. its own database connection).
If you used to rely on this behavior, now you should create the resources for background tasks inside the background task itself, and use internally only data that doesn't depend on the resources of dependencies with [CODE: yield].
For example, instead of using the same database session, you would create a new database session inside of the background task, and you would obtain the objects from the database using this new session. And then instead of passing the object from the database as a parameter to the background task function, you would pass the ID of that object and then obtain the object again inside the background task function.
The sequence of execution before FastAPI 0.106.0 was like this diagram:
Time flows from top to bottom. And each column is one of the parts interacting or executing code.
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]1: sequenceDiagram participant client as Client parti...]
The new execution flow can be found in the docs: [Execution of dependencies with [CODE: yield]](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/dependencies/dependencies-with-yield/#execution-of-dependencies-with-yield).

[Context: Release Notes > 0.100.0 (2023-07-07)]

## 0.100.0 (2023-07-07)
Support for [BOLD:Pydantic v2]
Pydantic version 2 has the [BOLD:core] re-written in [BOLD:Rust] and includes a lot of improvements and features, for example:
[ITALIC: Improved [BOLD:correctness] in corner cases.
] [BOLD:Safer] types.
[ITALIC: Better [BOLD:performance] and [BOLD:less energy] consumption.
] Better [BOLD:extensibility].
- etc.
...all this while keeping the [BOLD:same Python API]. In most of the cases, for simple models, you can simply upgrade the Pydantic version and get all the benefits.
In some cases, for pure data validation and processing, you can get performance improvements of [BOLD:20x] or more. This means 2,000% or more. 🤯
When you use [BOLD:FastAPI], there's a lot more going on, processing the request and response, handling dependencies, executing [BOLD:your own code], and particularly, [BOLD:waiting for the network]. But you will probably still get some nice performance improvements just from the upgrade.
The focus of this release is [BOLD:compatibility] with Pydantic v1 and v2, to make sure your current apps keep working. Later there will be more focus on refactors, correctness, code improvements, and then [BOLD:performance] improvements. Some third-party early beta testers that ran benchmarks on the beta releases of FastAPI reported improvements of [BOLD:2x - 3x]. Which is not bad for just doing [CODE: pip install --upgrade fastapi pydantic]. This was not an official benchmark and I didn't check it myself, but it's a good sign.

[Context: Release Notes > 0.100.0 (2023-07-07)]

## 0.100.0 (2023-07-07)

Pydantic version 2 has the [BOLD:core] re-written in [BOLD:Rust] and includes a lot of improvements and features, for example:
[ITALIC: Improved [BOLD:correctness] in corner cases.
] [BOLD:Safer] types.
[ITALIC: Better [BOLD:performance] and [BOLD:less energy] consumption.
] Better [BOLD:extensibility].
- etc.
...all this while keeping the [BOLD:same Python API]. In most of the cases, for simple models, you can simply upgrade the Pydantic version and get all the benefits.

The focus of this release is [BOLD:compatibility] with Pydantic v1 and v2, to make sure your current apps keep working. Later there will be more focus on refactors, correctness, code improvements, and then [BOLD:performance] improvements. [Context: Release Notes > 0.100.0 (2023-07-07) > Migration]

### Migration
Check out the Pydantic migration guide.
For the things that need changes in your Pydantic models, the Pydantic team built [[CODE: bump-pydantic]](https://github.com/pydantic/bump-pydantic).
A command line tool that will [BOLD:process your code] and update most of the things [BOLD:automatically] for you. Make sure you have your code in git first, and review each of the changes to make sure everything is correct before committing the changes.

[Context: Release Notes > 0.100.0 (2023-07-07) > Pydantic v1]

### Pydantic v1
[BOLD:This version of FastAPI still supports Pydantic v1]. And although Pydantic v1 will be deprecated at some point, it will still be supported for a while.
This means that you can install the new Pydantic v2, and if something fails, you can install Pydantic v1 while you fix any problems you might have, but having the latest FastAPI.
There are [BOLD:tests for both Pydantic v1 and v2], and test [BOLD:coverage] is kept at [BOLD:100%].

[Context: Release Notes > 0.95.0 (2023-03-18) > Highlights]

## 0.95.0 (2023-03-18)
### Highlights
This release adds support for dependencies and parameters using [CODE: Annotated] and recommends its usage.
This has [BOLD:several benefits], one of the main ones is that now the parameters of your functions with [CODE: Annotated] would [BOLD:not be affected] at all.
If you call those functions in [BOLD:other places in your code], the actual [BOLD:default values] will be kept, your editor will help you notice missing [BOLD:required arguments], Python will require you to pass required arguments at [BOLD:runtime], you will be able to [BOLD:use the same functions] for different things and with different libraries (e.g. [BOLD:Typer] will soon support [CODE: Annotated] too, then you could use the same function for an API and a CLI), etc.
Because [CODE: Annotated] is [BOLD:standard Python], you still get all the [BOLD:benefits] from editors and tools, like [BOLD:autocompletion], [BOLD:inline errors], etc.
One of the [BOLD:biggest benefits] is that now you can create [CODE: Annotated] dependencies that are then shared by multiple [ITALIC:path operation functions], this will allow you to [BOLD:reduce] a lot of [BOLD:code duplication] in your codebase, while keeping all the support from editors and tools.
For example, you could have code like this:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: def get[ITALIC:current]user(token: str): # authenticate u...]
There's a bit of code duplication for the dependency:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]1: user: User = Depends(get[ITALIC:current]user)...]
...the bigger the codebase, the more noticeable it is.
Now you can create an annotated dependency once, like this:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]2: CurrentUser = Annotated[User, Depends(get[ITALIC:current]...]
And then you can reuse this [CODE: Annotated] dependency:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]3: CurrentUser = Annotated[User, Depends(get[ITALIC:current]...]
...and [CODE: CurrentUser] has all the typing information as [CODE: User], so your editor will work as expected (autocompletion and everything), and [BOLD:FastAPI] will be able to understand the dependency defined in [CODE: Annotated].
Roughly [BOLD:all the docs] have been rewritten to use [CODE: Annotated] as the main way to declare [BOLD:parameters] and [BOLD:dependencies]. All the [BOLD:examples] in the docs now include a version with [CODE: Annotated] and a version without it, for each of the specific Python versions (when there are small differences/improvements in more recent versions). There were around 23K new lines added between docs, examples, and tests.
The key updated docs are:
[ITALIC: Python Types Intro:
] Type Hints with Metadata Annotations.
[ITALIC: Tutorial:
] Query Parameters and String Validations - Additional validation
[ITALIC: [Advantages of [CODE: Annotated]](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/query-params-str-validations/#advantages-of-annotated)
] Path Parameters and Numeric Validations - Order the parameters as you need, tricks
[ITALIC: [Better with [CODE: Annotated]](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/path-params-numeric-validations/#better-with-annotated)
] [Dependencies - First Steps - Share [CODE: Annotated] dependencies](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/dependencies/#share-annotated-dependencies)
Special thanks to @nzig for the core implementation and to @adriangb for the inspiration and idea with Xpresso!

## 0.93.0 (2023-03-07)
### Features
- Add support for [CODE: lifespan] async context managers (superseding [CODE: startup] and [CODE: shutdown] events). Initial PR #2944 by @uSpike.
Now, instead of using independent [CODE: startup] and [CODE: shutdown] events, you can define that logic in a single function with [CODE: yield] decorated with [CODE: @asynccontextmanager] (an async context manager).
For example:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: from contextlib import asynccontextmanager from fa...]
[BOLD:Note]: This is the recommended way going forward, instead of using [CODE: startup] and [CODE: shutdown] events.
Read more about it in the new docs: Advanced User Guide: Lifespan Events.

## 0.91.0 (2023-02-10)
### Upgrades
[ITALIC: Upgrade Starlette version to [CODE: 0.24.0] and refactor internals for compatibility. PR #5985 by @tiangolo.
] This can solve nuanced errors when using middlewares. Before Starlette [CODE: 0.24.0], a new instance of each middleware class would be created when a new middleware was added. That normally was not a problem, unless the middleware class expected to be created only once, with only one instance, that happened in some cases. This upgrade would solve those cases (thanks @adriangb! Starlette PR #2017). Now the middleware class instances are created once, right before the first request (the first time the app is called).
- If you depended on that previous behavior, you might need to update your code. As always, make sure your tests pass before merging the upgrade.

## 0.89.0 (2023-01-07)
### Features
[ITALIC: Add support for function return type annotations to declare the [CODE: response[ITALIC:model]. Initial PR #1436 by @uriyyo.
Now you can declare the return type / [CODE: response]model] in the function return type annotation:
```python
from fastapi import FastAPI
from pydantic import BaseModel

app = FastAPI()

class Item(BaseModel):
name: str
price: float

@app.get("/items/")
async def read[ITALIC:items() -> list[Item]:
return [
Item(name="Portal Gun", price=42.0),
Item(name="Plumbus", price=32.0),
]
```
FastAPI will use the return type annotation to perform:
] Data validation
[ITALIC: Automatic documentation
] It could power automatic client generators
- [BOLD:Data filtering]
Before this version it was only supported via the [CODE: response]model] parameter.
Read more about it in the new docs: Response Model - Return Type.

## 0.80.0 (2022-08-23)
### Breaking Changes - Fixes
[ITALIC: Fix [CODE: response[ITALIC:model] not invalidating [CODE: None]. PR #2725 by @hukkin.
If you are using [CODE: response]model] with some type that doesn't include [CODE: None] but the function is returning [CODE: None], it will now raise an internal server error, because you are returning invalid data that violates the contract in [CODE: response[ITALIC:model]. Before this release it would allow breaking that contract returning [CODE: None].
For example, if you have an app like this:
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:0: from fastapi import FastAPI from pydantic import B...]
...calling the path [CODE: /items/invalidnone] will raise an error, because [CODE: None] is not a valid type for the [CODE: response]model] declared with [CODE: Item].
You could also be implicitly returning [CODE: None] without realizing, for example:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]1: from fastapi import FastAPI from pydantic import B...]
If you have ]path operations[ITALIC: using [CODE: response[ITALIC:model] that need to be allowed to return [CODE: None], make it explicit in [CODE: response]model] using [CODE: Union[Something, None]]:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]2: from typing import Union from fastapi import FastA...]
This way the data will be correctly validated, you won't have an internal server error, and the documentation will also reflect that this ]path operation* could return [CODE: None] (or [CODE: null] in JSON).

[Context: Release Notes > 0.80.0 (2022-08-23) > Breaking Changes - Fixes]

## 0.78.0 (2022-05-14)
### Features
[ITALIC: Add support for omitting [CODE: ...] as default value when declaring required parameters with:
] [CODE: Path()]
[ITALIC: [CODE: Query()]
] [CODE: Header()]
[ITALIC: [CODE: Cookie()]
] [CODE: Body()]
[ITALIC: [CODE: Form()]
] [CODE: File()]
New docs at Tutorial - Query Parameters and String Validations - Make it required. PR #4906 by @tiangolo.
Up to now, declaring a required parameter while adding additional validation or metadata needed using [CODE: ...] (Ellipsis).
For example:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: from fastapi import Cookie, FastAPI, Header, Path,...]
...all these parameters are required because the default value is [CODE: ...] (Ellipsis).
But now it's possible and supported to just omit the default value, as would be done with Pydantic fields, and the parameters would still be required.
For example, this is now supported:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]1: from fastapi import Cookie, FastAPI, Header, Path,...]
To declare parameters as optional (not required), you can set a default value as always, for example using [CODE: None]:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]2: from typing import Union from fastapi import Cooki...]

[Context: Release Notes > 0.78.0 (2022-05-14) > Features]

## 0.74.0 (2022-02-17)
### Breaking Changes
- Update internal [CODE: AsyncExitStack] to fix context for dependencies with [CODE: yield]. PR #4575 by @tiangolo.
Dependencies with [CODE: yield] can now catch [CODE: HTTPException] and custom exceptions. For example:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: async def get[ITALIC:database(): with Session() as sessio...]
After the dependency with [CODE: yield] handles the exception (or not) the exception is raised again. So that any exception handlers can catch it, or ultimately the default internal [CODE: ServerErrorMiddleware].
If you depended on exceptions not being received by dependencies with [CODE: yield], and receiving an exception breaks the code after [CODE: yield], you can use a block with [CODE: try] and [CODE: finally]:
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:1: async def do]something(): try: yield something fin...]
...that way the [CODE: finally] block is run regardless of any exception that might happen.

[Context: Release Notes > 0.74.0 (2022-02-17) > Features]

### Features
- The same PR #4575 from above also fixes the [CODE: contextvars] context for the code before and after [CODE: yield]. This was the main objective of that PR.
This means that now, if you set a value in a context variable before [CODE: yield], the value would still be available after [CODE: yield] (as you would intuitively expect). And it also means that you can reset the context variable with a token afterwards.
For example, this works correctly now:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: from contextvars import ContextVar from typing imp...]
...before this change it would raise an error when resetting the context variable, because the [CODE: contextvars] context was different, because of the way it was implemented.
[BOLD:Note]: You probably don't need [CODE: contextvars], and you should probably avoid using them. But they are powerful and useful in some advanced scenarios, for example, migrating from code that used Flask's [CODE: g] semi-global variable.
[BOLD:Technical Details]: If you want to know more of the technical details you can check out the PR description #4575.

[Context: Release Notes > 0.74.0 (2022-02-17) > Features]

...before this change it would raise an error when resetting the context variable, because the [CODE: contextvars] context was different, because of the way it was implemented.

## 0.70.0 (2021-10-07)
This release just upgrades Starlette to the latest version, [CODE: 0.16.0], which includes several bug fixes and some small breaking changes.
These last [BOLD:three consecutive releases] are independent so that you can [BOLD:migrate gradually]:
[ITALIC: First to FastAPI [CODE: 0.68.2], with no breaking changes, but upgrading all the sub-dependencies.
] Next to FastAPI [CODE: 0.69.0], which upgrades Starlette to [CODE: 0.15.0], with AnyIO support, and a higher chance of having breaking changes in your code.
- Finally to FastAPI [CODE: 0.70.0], just upgrading Starlette to the latest version [CODE: 0.16.0] with additional bug fixes.
This way, in case there was a breaking change for your code in one of the releases, you can still benefit from the previous upgrades.

[Context: Release Notes > 0.70.0 (2021-10-07) > Breaking Changes - Upgrade]

### Breaking Changes - Upgrade
[ITALIC: Upgrade Starlette to 0.16.0. PR #4016 by @tiangolo.
Also upgrades the ranges of optional dependencies:
] [CODE: "jinja2 >=2.11.2,=1.1.0,<3.0.0"]

[Context: Release Notes > 0.69.0 (2021-10-07) > Breaking Changes - Upgrade]

## 0.69.0 (2021-10-07)
### Breaking Changes - Upgrade
This release adds support for Trio.
It upgrades the version of Starlette to [CODE: 0.15.0], now based on AnyIO, and the internal async components in [BOLD:FastAPI] are now based on AnyIO as well, making it compatible with both [BOLD:asyncio] and [BOLD:Trio].
You can read the docs about running FastAPI with Trio using Hypercorn.
This release also removes [CODE: graphene] as an optional dependency for GraphQL. If you need to work with GraphQL, the recommended library now is Strawberry. You can read the new FastAPI with GraphQL docs.

[Context: Release Notes > 0.65.2 (2021-06-09) > Security fixes]

## 0.65.2 (2021-06-09)
### Security fixes
- Check Content-Type request header before assuming JSON. Initial PR #2118 by @patrickkwang.
This change fixes a CSRF security vulnerability when using cookies for authentication in path operations with JSON payloads sent by browsers.
In versions lower than [CODE: 0.65.2], FastAPI would try to read the request payload as JSON even if the [CODE: content-type] header sent was not set to [CODE: application/json] or a compatible JSON media type (e.g. [CODE: application/geo+json]).
So, a request with a content type of [CODE: text/plain] containing JSON data would be accepted and the JSON data would be extracted.
But requests with content type [CODE: text/plain] are exempt from CORS preflights, for being considered Simple requests. So, the browser would execute them right away including cookies, and the text content could be a JSON string that would be parsed and accepted by the FastAPI application.
See CVE-2021-32677 for more details.
Thanks to Dima Boger for the security report!

[Context: Release Notes > 0.65.2 (2021-06-09) > Security fixes]

This change fixes a CSRF security vulnerability when using cookies for authentication in path operations with JSON payloads sent by browsers.

So, a request with a content type of [CODE: text/plain] containing JSON data would be accepted and the JSON data would be extracted.
But requests with content type [CODE: text/plain] are exempt from CORS preflights, for being considered Simple requests. So, the browser would execute them right away including cookies, and the text content could be a JSON string that would be parsed and accepted by the FastAPI application.
See CVE-2021-32677 for more details.
Thanks to Dima Boger for the security report!
