## 0.62.0 (2020-11-29)
### Features
[ITALIC: Add support for shared/top-level parameters (dependencies, tags, etc). PR #2434 by @tiangolo.
Up to now, for several options, the only way to apply them to a group of ]path operations[ITALIC: was in [CODE: include[ITALIC:router]. That works well, but the call to [CODE: app.include]router()] or [CODE: router.include[ITALIC:router()] is normally done in another file.
That means that, for example, to apply authentication to all the ]path operations[ITALIC: in a router it would end up being done in a different file, instead of keeping related logic together.
Setting options in [CODE: include]router] still makes sense in some cases, for example, to override or increase configurations from a third party router included in an app. But in a router that is part of a bigger application, it would probably make more sense to add those settings when creating the [CODE: APIRouter].
[BOLD:In [CODE: FastAPI]]
This allows setting the (mostly new) parameters (additionally to the already existing parameters):
] [CODE: default[ITALIC:response]class]: updated to handle defaults in [CODE: APIRouter] and [CODE: include[ITALIC:router].
[ITALIC: [CODE: dependencies]: to include top-level dependencies that apply to the whole application. E.g. to add global authentication.
] [CODE: callbacks]: OpenAPI callbacks that apply to all the [ITALIC:path operations].
[ITALIC: [CODE: deprecated]: to mark all the ]path operations[ITALIC: as deprecated. 🤷
] [CODE: include]in[ITALIC:schema]: to allow excluding all the [ITALIC:path operations] from the OpenAPI schema.
[ITALIC: [CODE: responses]: OpenAPI responses that apply to all the ]path operations[ITALIC:.
For example:
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:0: from fastapi import FastAPI, Depends async def som...]
[BOLD:In [CODE: APIRouter]]
This allows setting the (mostly new) parameters (additionally to the already existing parameters):
] [CODE: default]response[ITALIC:class]: updated to handle defaults in [CODE: APIRouter] and [CODE: include]router]. For example, it's not needed to set it explicitly when creating callbacks.
[ITALIC: [CODE: dependencies]: to include router-level dependencies that apply to all the ]path operations[ITALIC: in a router. Up to now, this was only possible with [CODE: include[ITALIC:router].
] [CODE: callbacks]: OpenAPI callbacks that apply to all the [ITALIC:path operations] in this router.
[ITALIC: [CODE: deprecated]: to mark all the ]path operations[ITALIC: in a router as deprecated.
] [CODE: include]in[ITALIC:schema]: to allow excluding all the [ITALIC:path operations] in a router from the OpenAPI schema.
[ITALIC: [CODE: responses]: OpenAPI responses that apply to all the ]path operations[ITALIC: in a router.
] [CODE: prefix]: to set the path prefix for a router. Up to now, this was only possible when calling [CODE: include]router].
[ITALIC: [CODE: tags]: OpenAPI tags to apply to all the ]path operations[ITALIC: in this router.
For example:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]1: from fastapi import APIRouter, Depends async def s...]
[BOLD:In [CODE: include[ITALIC:router]]
Most of these settings are now supported in [CODE: APIRouter], which normally lives closer to the related code, so it is recommended to use [CODE: APIRouter] when possible.
But [CODE: include]router] is still useful to, for example, adding options (like [CODE: dependencies], [CODE: prefix], and [CODE: tags]) when including a third party router, or a generic router that is shared between several projects.
This PR allows setting the (mostly new) parameters (additionally to the already existing parameters):
] [CODE: default[ITALIC:response]class]: updated to handle defaults in [CODE: APIRouter] and [CODE: FastAPI].
[ITALIC: [CODE: deprecated]: to mark all the ]path operations[ITALIC: in a router as deprecated in OpenAPI.
] [CODE: include[ITALIC:in]schema]: to allow disabling all the [ITALIC:path operations] from showing in the OpenAPI schema.
[ITALIC: [CODE: callbacks]: OpenAPI callbacks that apply to all the ]path operations* in this router.
Note: all the previous parameters are still there, so it's still possible to declare [CODE: dependencies] in [CODE: include_router].

[Context: Release Notes > 0.62.0 (2020-11-29) > Features]

[Context: Release Notes > 0.62.0 (2020-11-29) > Breaking Changes]

### Breaking Changes
[ITALIC: PR #2434 includes several improvements that shouldn't affect normal use cases, but could affect in advanced scenarios:
] If you are testing the generated OpenAPI (you shouldn't, FastAPI already tests it extensively for you): the order for [CODE: tags] in [CODE: include[ITALIC:router] and [ITALIC:path operations] was updated for consistency, but it's a simple order change.
- If you have advanced custom logic to access each route's [CODE: route.response]class], or the [CODE: router.default[ITALIC:response]class], or the [CODE: app.default[ITALIC:response]class]: the default value for [CODE: response[ITALIC:class] in [CODE: APIRoute] and for [CODE: default]response[ITALIC:class] in [CODE: APIRouter] and [CODE: FastAPI] is now a [CODE: DefaultPlaceholder] used internally to handle and solve default values and overrides. The actual response class inside the [CODE: DefaultPlaceholder] is available at [CODE: route.response]class.value].

[Context: Release Notes > 0.62.0 (2020-11-29) > Breaking Changes]

[Context: Virtual Environments > Install Packages]

## Install Packages
After activating the environment, you can install packages in it.
/// tip
Do this [BOLD:once] when installing or upgrading the packages your project needs.
If you need to upgrade a version or add a new package you would [BOLD:do this again].
///

metadata.breadcrumb: Virtual Environments > Install Packages > Install Packages Directly
Virtual Environments > Install Packages > Install from `requirements.txt`

page_content:
[Context: Virtual Environments > Run Your Program]

## Run Your Program
After you activated the virtual environment, you can run your program, and it will use the Python inside of your virtual environment with the packages you installed there.

[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: $ python main.py Hello World...]

[Context: Virtual Environments > Deactivate the Virtual Environment]

## Deactivate the Virtual Environment
Once you are done working on your project you can [BOLD:deactivate] the virtual environment.

[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: $ deactivate...]

This way, when you run [CODE: python] it won't try to run it from that virtual environment with the packages installed there.

metadata.breadcrumb: Virtual Environments > Deactivate the Virtual Environment

page_content:
[Context: Virtual Environments > Ready to Work]

## Ready to Work
Now you're ready to start working on your project.
/// tip
Do you want to understand what's all that above?
Continue reading. 🤓
///

[Context: Virtual Environments > Why Virtual Environments]

## Why Virtual Environments
To work with FastAPI you need to install Python.
After that, you would need to [BOLD:install] FastAPI and any other [BOLD:packages] you want to use.
To install packages you would normally use the [CODE: pip] command that comes with Python (or similar alternatives).
Nevertheless, if you just use [CODE: pip] directly, the packages would be installed in your [BOLD:global Python environment] (the global installation of Python).

[Context: Virtual Environments > Why Virtual Environments > The Problem]

### The Problem
So, what's the problem with installing packages in the global Python environment?
At some point, you will probably end up writing many different programs that depend on [BOLD:different packages]. And some of these projects you work on will depend on [BOLD:different versions] of the same package.
For example, you could create a project called [CODE: philosophers-stone], this program depends on another package called [BOLD:[CODE: harry], using the version [CODE: 1]]. So, you need to install [CODE: harry].
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: flowchart LR stone(philosophers-stone) -->|require...]
Then, at some point later, you create another project called [CODE: prisoner-of-azkaban], and this project also depends on [CODE: harry], but this project needs [BOLD:[CODE: harry] version [CODE: 3]].
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]1: flowchart LR azkaban(prisoner-of-azkaban) --> |req...]
But now the problem is, if you install the packages globally (in the global environment) instead of in a local [BOLD:virtual environment], you will have to choose which version of [CODE: harry] to install.
If you want to run [CODE: philosophers-stone] you will need to first install [CODE: harry] version [CODE: 1], for example with:

[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]2: $ pip install "harry==1"...]

And then you would end up with [CODE: harry] version [CODE: 1] installed in your global Python environment.
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]3: flowchart LR subgraph global[global env] harry-1[h...]
But then if you want to run [CODE: prisoner-of-azkaban], you will need to uninstall [CODE: harry] version [CODE: 1] and install [CODE: harry] version [CODE: 3] (or just installing version [CODE: 3] would automatically uninstall version [CODE: 1]).

[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]4: $ pip install "harry==3"...]

And then you would end up with [CODE: harry] version [CODE: 3] installed in your global Python environment.
And if you try to run [CODE: philosophers-stone] again, there's a chance it would [BOLD:not work] because it needs [CODE: harry] version [CODE: 1].
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]5: flowchart LR subgraph global[global env] harry-1[<...]
/// tip
It's very common in Python packages to try the best to [BOLD:avoid breaking changes] in [BOLD:new versions], but it's better to be safe, and install newer versions intentionally and when you can run the tests to check everything is working correctly.
///
Now, imagine that with [BOLD:many] other [BOLD:packages] that all your [BOLD:projects depend on]. That's very difficult to manage. And you would probably end up running some projects with some [BOLD:incompatible versions] of the packages, and not knowing why something isn't working.
Also, depending on your operating system (e.g. Linux, Windows, macOS), it could have come with Python already installed. And in that case it probably had some packages pre-installed with some specific versions [BOLD:needed by your system]. If you install packages in the global Python environment, you could end up [BOLD:breaking] some of the programs that came with your operating system.

metadata.breadcrumb: Virtual Environments > Why Virtual Environments > The Problem

page_content:
[Context: Virtual Environments > Where are Packages Installed]

## Where are Packages Installed
When you install Python, it creates some directories with some files in your computer.
Some of these directories are the ones in charge of having all the packages you install.
When you run:

[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: // Don't run this now, it's just an example 🤓 $ pi...]

That will download a compressed file with the FastAPI code, normally from PyPI.
It will also [BOLD:download] files for other packages that FastAPI depends on.
Then it will [BOLD:extract] all those files and put them in a directory in your computer.
By default, it will put those files downloaded and extracted in the directory that comes with your Python installation, that's the [BOLD:global environment].

metadata.breadcrumb: Virtual Environments > Where are Packages Installed

page_content:
[Context: Virtual Environments > What are Virtual Environments]

## What are Virtual Environments
The solution to the problems of having all the packages in the global environment is to use a [BOLD:virtual environment for each project] you work on.
A virtual environment is a [BOLD:directory], very similar to the global one, where you can install the packages for a project.
This way, each project will have its own virtual environment ([CODE: .venv] directory) with its own packages.
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: flowchart TB subgraph stone-project[philosophers-s...]

page_content:
[Context: Virtual Environments > Why Deactivate a Virtual Environment]

## Why Deactivate a Virtual Environment
For example, you could be working on a project [CODE: philosophers-stone], [BOLD:activate that virtual environment], install packages and work with that environment.
And then you want to work on [BOLD:another project] [CODE: prisoner-of-azkaban].
You go to that project:

[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: $ cd ~/code/prisoner-of-azkaban...]

If you don't deactivate the virtual environment for [CODE: philosophers-stone], when you run [CODE: python] in the terminal, it will try to use the Python from [CODE: philosophers-stone].

[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]1: $ cd ~/code/prisoner-of-azkaban $ python main.py /...]

But if you deactivate the virtual environment and activate the new one for [CODE: prisoner-of-askaban] then when you run [CODE: python] it will use the Python from the virtual environment in [CODE: prisoner-of-azkaban].

[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]2: $ cd ~/code/prisoner-of-azkaban // You don't need ...]

metadata.breadcrumb: Virtual Environments > Why Deactivate a Virtual Environment

page_content:
[Context: Virtual Environments > Alternatives]

## Alternatives
This is a simple guide to get you started and teach you how everything works [BOLD:underneath].
There are many [BOLD:alternatives] to managing virtual environments, package dependencies (requirements), projects.
Once you are ready and want to use a tool to [BOLD:manage the entire project], packages dependencies, virtual environments, etc. I would suggest you try uv.
[CODE: uv] can do a lot of things, it can:
[ITALIC: [BOLD:Install Python] for you, including different versions
] Manage the [BOLD:virtual environment] for your projects
[ITALIC: Install [BOLD:packages]
] Manage package [BOLD:dependencies and versions] for your project
[ITALIC: Make sure you have an [BOLD:exact] set of packages and versions to install, including their dependencies, so that you can be sure that you can run your project in production exactly the same as in your computer while developing, this is called [BOLD:locking]
] And many other things

[Context: Virtual Environments > Conclusion]

## Conclusion
If you read and understood all this, now [BOLD:you know much more] about virtual environments than many developers out there. 🤓
Knowing these details will most probably be useful in a future time when you are debugging something that seems complex, but you will know [BOLD:how it all works underneath].

Tests added here will be seen by all designers of language specific prompts.
Use as follows:
[ITALIC: Have a language specific prompt - [CODE: docs/{language code}/llm-prompt.md].
] Do a fresh translation of this document into your desired target language (see e.g. the [CODE: translate-page] command of the [CODE: translate.py]). This will create the translation under [CODE: docs/{language code}/docs/_llm-test.md].
[ITALIC: Check if things are okay in the translation.
] If necessary, improve your language specific prompt, the general prompt, or the English document.
[ITALIC: Then manually fix the remaining issues in the translation, so that it is a good translation.
] Retranslate, having the good translation in place. The ideal result would be that the LLM makes no changes anymore to the translation. That means that the general prompt and your language specific prompt are as good as they can be (It will sometimes make a few seemingly random changes, the reason is that LLMs are not deterministic algorithms).

[Context: Additional Responses in OpenAPI]

# Additional Responses in OpenAPI
/// warning
This is a rather advanced topic.
If you are starting with [BOLD:FastAPI], you might not need this.
///
You can declare additional responses, with additional status codes, media types, descriptions, etc.
Those additional responses will be included in the OpenAPI schema, so they will also appear in the API docs.
But for those additional responses you have to make sure you return a [CODE: Response] like [CODE: JSONResponse] directly, with your status code and content.

page_content:
[Context: Additional Responses in OpenAPI > Combining information]

## Combining information
You can also combine response information from multiple places, including the [CODE: response[ITALIC:model], [CODE: status]code], and [CODE: responses] parameters.
You can declare a [CODE: response[ITALIC:model], using the default status code [CODE: 200] (or a custom one if you need), and then declare additional information for that same response in [CODE: responses], directly in the OpenAPI schema.
[BOLD:FastAPI] will keep the additional information from [CODE: responses], and combine it with the JSON Schema from your model.
For example, you can declare a response with a status code [CODE: 404] that uses a Pydantic model and has a custom [CODE: description].
And a response with a status code [CODE: 200] that uses your [CODE: response]model], but includes a custom [CODE: example]:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: response_model=Item, responses={ 404: {"model": Me...]
It will all be combined and included in your OpenAPI, and shown in the API docs:

metadata.breadcrumb: Additional Responses in OpenAPI > Combining information
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/additional-responses.md

page_content:
[Context: Additional Responses in OpenAPI > Combine predefined responses and custom ones]

## Combine predefined responses and custom ones
You might want to have some predefined responses that apply to many [ITALIC:path operations], but you want to combine them with custom responses needed by each [ITALIC:path operation].
For those cases, you can use the Python technique of "unpacking" a [CODE: dict] with [CODE: *[ITALIC:dict[ITALIC:to]unpack]:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: old[ITALIC:dict = { "old key": "old value", "second old k...]
Here, [CODE: new]dict] will contain all the key-value pairs from [CODE: old[ITALIC:dict] plus the new key-value pair:
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:1: { "old key": "old value", "second old key": "secon...]
You can use that technique to reuse some predefined responses in your ]path operations* and combine them with additional custom ones.
For example:
[CODE]BLOCK_2: responses = { 404: {"description": "Item not found...]

hierarchy: Combine predefined responses and custom ones

page_content:
[Context: Additional Responses in OpenAPI > More information about OpenAPI responses]

## More information about OpenAPI responses
To see what exactly you can include in the responses, you can check these sections in the OpenAPI specification:
[ITALIC: OpenAPI Responses Object, it includes the [CODE: Response Object].
] OpenAPI Response Object, you can include anything from this directly in each response inside your [CODE: responses] parameter. Including [CODE: description], [CODE: headers], [CODE: content] (inside of this is that you declare different media types and JSON Schemas), and [CODE: links].

page_content:
[Context: Additional Status Codes]

# Additional Status Codes
By default, [BOLD:FastAPI] will return the responses using a [CODE: JSONResponse], putting the content you return from your [ITALIC:path operation] inside of that [CODE: JSONResponse].
It will use the default status code or the one you set in your [ITALIC:path operation].

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/additional-status-codes.md

page_content:
[Context: Additional Status Codes > Additional status codes]

## Additional status codes
If you want to return additional status codes apart from the main one, you can do that by returning a [CODE: Response] directly, like a [CODE: JSONResponse], and set the additional status code directly.
For example, let's say that you want to have a [ITALIC:path operation] that allows to update items, and returns HTTP status codes of 200 "OK" when successful.
But you also want it to accept new items. And when the items didn't exist before, it creates them, and returns an HTTP status code of 201 "Created".
To achieve that, import [CODE: JSONResponse], and return your content there directly, setting the [CODE: status]code] that you want:
```python
from fastapi.responses import JSONResponse

return JSONResponse(status[ITALIC:code=status.HTTP]201_CREATED, content=item)
```
/// warning
When you return a [CODE: Response] directly, like in the example above, it will be returned directly.
It won't be serialized with a model, etc.
Make sure it has the data you want it to have, and that the values are valid JSON (if you are using [CODE: JSONResponse]).
///
/// note | Technical Details
You could also use [CODE: from starlette.responses import JSONResponse].
[BOLD:FastAPI] provides the same [CODE: starlette.responses] as [CODE: fastapi.responses] just as a convenience for you, the developer. But most of the available responses come directly from Starlette. The same with [CODE: status].
///

metadata.breadcrumb: Additional Status Codes > Additional status codes
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/additional-status-codes.md

page_content:
[Context: Additional Status Codes > OpenAPI and API docs]

## OpenAPI and API docs
If you return additional status codes and responses directly, they won't be included in the OpenAPI schema (the API docs), because FastAPI doesn't have a way to know beforehand what you are going to return.
But you can document that in your code, using: Additional Responses.

metadata.breadcrumb: Additional Status Codes > OpenAPI and API docs

page_content:
[Context: Advanced Dependencies > Parameterized dependencies]

# Advanced Dependencies
## Parameterized dependencies
All the dependencies we have seen are a fixed function or class.
But there could be cases where you want to be able to set parameters on the dependency, without having to declare many different functions or classes.
Let's imagine that we want to have a dependency that checks if the query parameter [CODE: q] contains some fixed content.
But we want to be able to parameterize that fixed content.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/advanced-dependencies.md

page_content:
[Context: Advanced Dependencies > A "callable" instance]

## A "callable" instance
In Python there's a way to make an instance of a class a "callable".
Not the class itself (which is already a callable), but an instance of that class.
To do that, we declare a method [CODE: [BOLD:call]]:
```python
def [BOLD:call](self, q: str = ""):
if q:
```
In this case, this [CODE: [BOLD:call]] is what [BOLD:FastAPI] will use to check for additional parameters and sub-dependencies, and this is what will be called to pass a value to the parameter in your [ITALIC:path operation function] later.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/advanced-dependencies.md

page_content:
[Context: Advanced Dependencies > Parameterize the instance]

## Parameterize the instance
And now, we can use [CODE: [BOLD:init]] to declare the parameters of the instance that we can use to "parameterize" the dependency:
```python
def [BOLD:init](self, fixed[ITALIC:content: str):
self.fixed]content = fixed_content
```
In this case, [BOLD:FastAPI] won't ever touch or care about [CODE: [BOLD:init]], we will use it directly in our code.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/advanced-dependencies.md

page_content:
[Context: Advanced Dependencies > Create an instance]

## Create an instance
We could create an instance of this class with:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: checker = FixedContentQueryChecker("bar")...]
And that way we are able to "parameterize" our dependency, that now has [CODE: "bar"] inside of it, as the attribute [CODE: checker.fixed_content].

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/advanced-dependencies.md

page_content:
[Context: Advanced Dependencies > Use the instance as a dependency]

## Use the instance as a dependency
Then, we could use this [CODE: checker] in a [CODE: Depends(checker)], instead of [CODE: Depends(FixedContentQueryChecker)], because the dependency is the instance, [CODE: checker], not the class itself.
And when solving the dependency, [BOLD:FastAPI] will call this [CODE: checker] like:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: checker(q="somequery")...]
...and pass whatever that returns as the value of the dependency in our [ITALIC:path operation function] as the parameter [CODE: fixed[ITALIC:content]included]:
```python
async def read[ITALIC:query]check(fixed[ITALIC:content]included: Annotated[bool, Depends(checker)]):
return {"fixed[ITALIC:content]in[ITALIC:query": fixed]content_included}
```
/// tip
All this might seem contrived. And it might not be very clear how is it useful yet.
These examples are intentionally simple, but show how it all works.
In the chapters about security, there are utility functions that are implemented in this same way.
If you understood all this, you already know how those utility tools for security work underneath.
///

hierarchy: Use the instance as a dependency
metadata.breadcrumb: Advanced Dependencies > Use the instance as a dependency
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/advanced-dependencies.md

page_content:
[Context: Advanced Dependencies > Dependencies with `yield`, `HTTPException`, `except` and Background Tasks]

## Dependencies with [CODE: yield], [CODE: HTTPException], [CODE: except] and Background Tasks
/// warning
You most probably don't need these technical details.
These details are useful mainly if you had a FastAPI application older than 0.121.0 and you are facing issues with dependencies with [CODE: yield].
///
Dependencies with [CODE: yield] have evolved over time to account for the different use cases and to fix some issues, here's a summary of what has changed.

metadata.breadcrumb: Advanced Dependencies > Dependencies with `yield`, `HTTPException`, `except` and Background Tasks
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/advanced-dependencies.md

page_content:
[Context: Advanced Dependencies > Dependencies with `yield`, `HTTPException`, `except` and Background Tasks > Dependencies with `yield` and `scope`]

### Dependencies with [CODE: yield] and [CODE: scope]
In version 0.121.0, FastAPI added support for [CODE: Depends(scope="function")] for dependencies with [CODE: yield].
Using [CODE: Depends(scope="function")], the exit code after [CODE: yield] is executed right after the [ITALIC:path operation function] is finished, before the response is sent back to the client.
And when using [CODE: Depends(scope="request")] (the default), the exit code after [CODE: yield] is executed after the response is sent.
You can read more about it in the docs for [Dependencies with [CODE: yield] - Early exit and [CODE: scope]](../tutorial/dependencies/dependencies-with-yield.md#early-exit-and-scope).

metadata.breadcrumb: Advanced Dependencies > Dependencies with `yield`, `HTTPException`, `except` and Background Tasks > Dependencies with `yield` and `scope`
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/advanced-dependencies.md

page_content:
[Context: Advanced Dependencies > Dependencies with `yield`, `HTTPException`, `except` and Background Tasks > Dependencies with `yield` and `StreamingResponse`, Technical Details]

### Dependencies with [CODE: yield] and [CODE: StreamingResponse], Technical Details
Before FastAPI 0.118.0, if you used a dependency with [CODE: yield], it would run the exit code after the [ITALIC:path operation function] returned but right before sending the response.
The intention was to avoid holding resources for longer than necessary, waiting for the response to travel through the network.
This change also meant that if you returned a [CODE: StreamingResponse], the exit code of the dependency with [CODE: yield] would have been already run.
For example, if you had a database session in a dependency with [CODE: yield], the [CODE: StreamingResponse] would not be able to use that session while streaming data because the session would have already been closed in the exit code after [CODE: yield].
This behavior was reverted in 0.118.0, to make the exit code after [CODE: yield] be executed after the response is sent.
/// info
As you will see below, this is very similar to the behavior before version 0.106.0, but with several improvements and bug fixes for corner cases.
///
#### Use Cases with Early Exit Code
There are some use cases with specific conditions that could benefit from the old behavior of running the exit code of dependencies with [CODE: yield] before sending the response.
For example, imagine you have code that uses a database session in a dependency with [CODE: yield] only to verify a user, but the database session is never used again in the [ITALIC:path operation function], only in the dependency, [BOLD:and] the response takes a long time to be sent, like a [CODE: StreamingResponse] that sends data slowly, but for some reason doesn't use the database.
In this case, the database session would be held until the response is finished being sent, but if you don't use it, then it wouldn't be necessary to hold it.
Here's how it could look like:
{[ITALIC: ../../docs[ITALIC:src/dependencies/tutorial013]an[ITALIC:py310.py ]}
The exit code, the automatic closing of the [CODE: Session] in:
{[ITALIC: ../../docs]src/dependencies/tutorial013[ITALIC:an]py310.py ln[19:21] ]}
...would be run after the response finishes sending the slow data:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: for ch in query: yield ch time.sleep(0.1)...]
But as [CODE: generate[ITALIC:stream()] doesn't use the database session, it is not really necessary to keep the session open while sending the response.
If you have this specific use case using SQLModel (or SQLAlchemy), you could explicitly close the session after you don't need it anymore:
[CODE]BLOCK_1: session.close()...]
That way the session would release the database connection, so other requests could use it.
If you have a different use case that needs to exit early from a dependency with [CODE: yield], please create a GitHub Discussion Question with your specific use case and why you would benefit from having early closing for dependencies with [CODE: yield].
If there are compelling use cases for early closing in dependencies with [CODE: yield], I would consider adding a new way to opt in to early closing.

metadata.breadcrumb: Advanced Dependencies > Dependencies with `yield`, `HTTPException`, `except` and Background Tasks > Dependencies with `yield` and `StreamingResponse`, Technical Details
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/advanced-dependencies.md

page_content:
[Context: Advanced Dependencies > Dependencies with `yield`, `HTTPException`, `except` and Background Tasks > Dependencies with `yield` and `except`, Technical Details]

### Dependencies with [CODE: yield] and [CODE: except], Technical Details
Before FastAPI 0.110.0, if you used a dependency with [CODE: yield], and then you captured an exception with [CODE: except] in that dependency, and you didn't raise the exception again, the exception would be automatically raised/forwarded to any exception handlers or the internal server error handler.
This was changed in version 0.110.0 to fix unhandled memory consumption from forwarded exceptions without a handler (internal server errors), and to make it consistent with the behavior of regular Python code.

metadata.breadcrumb: Advanced Dependencies > Dependencies with `yield`, `HTTPException`, `except` and Background Tasks > Dependencies with `yield` and `except`, Technical Details
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/advanced-dependencies.md

page_content:
[Context: Advanced Dependencies > Dependencies with `yield`, `HTTPException`, `except` and Background Tasks > Background Tasks and Dependencies with `yield`, Technical Details]

### Background Tasks and Dependencies with [CODE: yield], Technical Details
Before FastAPI 0.106.0, raising exceptions after [CODE: yield] was not possible, the exit code in dependencies with [CODE: yield] was executed [ITALIC:after] the response was sent, so Exception Handlers would have already run.
This was designed this way mainly to allow using the same objects "yielded" by dependencies inside of background tasks, because the exit code would be executed after the background tasks were finished.
This was changed in FastAPI 0.106.0 with the intention to not hold resources while waiting for the response to travel through the network.
/// tip
Additionally, a background task is normally an independent set of logic that should be handled separately, with its own resources (e.g. its own database connection).
So, this way you will probably have cleaner code.
///
If you used to rely on this behavior, now you should create the resources for background tasks inside the background task itself, and use internally only data that doesn't depend on the resources of dependencies with [CODE: yield].
For example, instead of using the same database session, you would create a new database session inside of the background task, and you would obtain the objects from the database using this new session. And then instead of passing the object from the database as a parameter to the background task function, you would pass the ID of that object and then obtain the object again inside the background task function.

hierarchy: Background Tasks and Dependencies with `yield`, Technical Details

page_content:
[Context: Advanced Dependencies > Dependencies with `yield`, `HTTPException`, `except` and Background Tasks > Background Tasks and Dependencies with `yield`, Technical Details]

### Background Tasks and Dependencies with [CODE: yield], Technical Details
Before FastAPI 0.106.0, raising exceptions after [CODE: yield] was not possible, the exit code in dependencies with [CODE: yield] was executed [ITALIC:after] the response was sent, so Exception Handlers would have already run.
This was designed this way mainly to allow using the same objects "yielded" by dependencies inside of background tasks, because the exit code would be executed after the background tasks were finished.
This was changed in FastAPI 0.106.0 with the intention to not hold resources while waiting for the response to travel through the network.
/// tip
Additionally, a background task is normally an independent set of logic that should be handled separately, with its own resources (e.g. its own database connection).
So, this way you will probably have cleaner code.
///
If you used to rely on this behavior, now you should create the resources for background tasks inside the background task itself, and use internally only data that doesn't depend on the resources of dependencies with [CODE: yield].
For example, instead of using the same database session, you would create a new database session inside of the background task, and you would obtain the objects from the database using this new session. And then instead of passing the object from the database as a parameter to the background task function, you would pass the ID of that object and then obtain the object again inside the background task function.

hierarchy: Background Tasks and Dependencies with `yield`, Technical Details

page_content:
[Context: Advanced Python Types]

# Advanced Python Types
Here are some additional ideas that might be useful when working with Python types.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/advanced-python-types.md

page_content:
[Context: Advanced Python Types > Using `Union` or `Optional`]

## Using [CODE: Union] or [CODE: Optional]
If your code for some reason can't use [CODE: |], for example if it's not in a type annotation but in something like [CODE: response[ITALIC:model=], instead of using the vertical bar ([CODE: |]) you can use [CODE: Union] from [CODE: typing].
For example, you could declare that something could be a [CODE: str] or [CODE: None]:
```python
from typing import Union

def say]hi(name: Union[str, None]):
print(f"Hi {name}!")
```
[CODE: typing] also has a shortcut to declare that something could be [CODE: None], with [CODE: Optional].
Here's a tip from my very [BOLD:subjective] point of view:
[ITALIC: Avoid using [CODE: Optional[SomeType]]
] Instead [BOLD:use [CODE: Union[SomeType, None]]] .
Both are equivalent and underneath they are the same, but I would recommend [CODE: Union] instead of [CODE: Optional] because the word "[BOLD:optional]" would seem to imply that the value is optional, and it actually means "it can be [CODE: None]", even if it's not optional and is still required.
I think [CODE: Union[SomeType, None]] is more explicit about what it means.
It's just about the words and names. But those words can affect how you and your teammates think about the code.
As an example, let's take this function:
```python
from typing import Optional

def say[ITALIC:hi(name: Optional[str]):
print(f"Hey {name}!")
```
The parameter [CODE: name] is defined as [CODE: Optional[str]], but it is [BOLD:not optional], you cannot call the function without the parameter:
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:0: say]hi() # Oh, no, this throws an error! ...]
The [CODE: name] parameter is [BOLD:still required] (not [ITALIC:optional]) because it doesn't have a default value. Still, [CODE: name] accepts [CODE: None] as the value:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]1: say[ITALIC:hi(name=None) # This works, None is valid ...]
The good news is, in most cases, you will be able to simply use [CODE: |] to define unions of types:
```python
def say]hi(name: str | None):
print(f"Hey {name}!")
```
So, normally you don't have to worry about names like [CODE: Optional] and [CODE: Union].

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/advanced-python-types.md

page_content:
[Context: Async Tests]

# Async Tests
You have already seen how to test your [BOLD:FastAPI] applications using the provided [CODE: TestClient]. Up to now, you have only seen how to write synchronous tests, without using [CODE: async] functions.
Being able to use asynchronous functions in your tests could be useful, for example, when you're querying your database asynchronously. Imagine you want to test sending requests to your FastAPI application and then verify that your backend successfully wrote the correct data in the database, while using an async database library.
Let's look at how we can make that work.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/async-tests.md

page_content:
[Context: Async Tests > pytest.mark.anyio]

## pytest.mark.anyio
If we want to call asynchronous functions in our tests, our test functions have to be asynchronous. AnyIO provides a neat plugin for this, that allows us to specify that some test functions are to be called asynchronously.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/async-tests.md

page_content:
[Context: Async Tests > HTTPX]

## HTTPX
Even if your [BOLD:FastAPI] application uses normal [CODE: def] functions instead of [CODE: async def], it is still an [CODE: async] application underneath.
The [CODE: TestClient] does some magic inside to call the asynchronous FastAPI application in your normal [CODE: def] test functions, using standard pytest. But that magic doesn't work anymore when we're using it inside asynchronous functions. By running our tests asynchronously, we can no longer use the [CODE: TestClient] inside our test functions.
The [CODE: TestClient] is based on HTTPX, and luckily, we can use it directly to test the API.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/async-tests.md

page_content:
[Context: Async Tests > Example]

## Example
For a simple example, let's consider a file structure similar to the one described in Bigger Applications and Testing:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: . app [BOLD:init].py main.py te...]
The file [CODE: main.py] would have:
{[ITALIC: ../../docs[ITALIC:src/async]tests/app[ITALIC:a]py310/main.py ]}
The file [CODE: test[ITALIC:main.py] would have the tests for [CODE: main.py], it could look like this now:
{[ITALIC: ../../docs]src/async[ITALIC:tests/app]a[ITALIC:py310/test]main.py ]}

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/async-tests.md

page_content:
[Context: Async Tests > Run it]

## Run it
You can run your tests as usual via:

[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: $ pytest ---> 100%...]

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/async-tests.md
Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/async-tests.md

metadata.breadcrumb: Async Tests > Other Asynchronous Function Calls
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/async-tests.md

page_content:
[Context: Behind a Proxy]

# Behind a Proxy
In many situations, you would use a [BOLD:proxy] like Traefik or Nginx in front of your FastAPI app.
These proxies could handle HTTPS certificates and other things.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/behind-a-proxy.md

page_content:
[Context: Behind a Proxy > Proxy Forwarded Headers]

## Proxy Forwarded Headers
A [BOLD:proxy] in front of your application would normally set some headers on the fly before sending the requests to your [BOLD:server] to let the server know that the request was [BOLD:forwarded] by the proxy, letting it know the original (public) URL, including the domain, that it is using HTTPS, etc.
The [BOLD:server] program (for example [BOLD:Uvicorn] via [BOLD:FastAPI CLI]) is capable of interpreting these headers, and then passing that information to your application.
But for security, as the server doesn't know it is behind a trusted proxy, it won't interpret those headers.
/// note | Technical Details
The proxy headers are:
[ITALIC: X-Forwarded-For
] X-Forwarded-Proto
- X-Forwarded-Host
///

metadata.breadcrumb: Behind a Proxy > Proxy Forwarded Headers
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/behind-a-proxy.md

page_content:
[Context: Behind a Proxy > Proxy Forwarded Headers > Enable Proxy Forwarded Headers]

### Enable Proxy Forwarded Headers
You can start FastAPI CLI with the [ITALIC:CLI Option] [CODE: --forwarded-allow-ips] and pass the IP addresses that should be trusted to read those forwarded headers.
If you set it to [CODE: --forwarded-allow-ips="[ITALIC:"] it would trust all the incoming IPs.
If your [BOLD:server] is behind a trusted [BOLD:proxy] and only the proxy talks to it, this would make it accept whatever is the IP of that [BOLD:proxy].

[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: $ fastapi run --forwarded-allow-ips="]"

metadata.breadcrumb: Behind a Proxy > Proxy Forwarded Headers > Enable Proxy Forwarded Headers
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/behind-a-proxy.md

page_content:
[Context: Behind a Proxy > Proxy Forwarded Headers > Redirects with HTTPS]

### Redirects with HTTPS
For example, let's say you define a [ITALIC:path operation] [CODE: /items/]:
```python
@app.get("/items/")
def read[ITALIC:items():
```
If the client tries to go to [CODE: /items], by default, it would be redirected to [CODE: /items/].
But before setting the [ITALIC:CLI Option] [CODE: --forwarded-allow-ips] it could redirect to [CODE: http://localhost:8000/items/].
But maybe your application is hosted at [CODE: https://mysuperapp.com], and the redirection should be to [CODE: https://mysuperapp.com/items/].
By setting [CODE: --proxy-headers] now FastAPI would be able to redirect to the right location.
[CODE]BLOCK_0: https://mysuperapp.com/items/...]
/// tip
If you want to learn more about HTTPS, check the guide About HTTPS.
///

metadata.breadcrumb: Behind a Proxy > Proxy Forwarded Headers > Redirects with HTTPS
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/behind-a-proxy.md

page_content:
[Context: Behind a Proxy > Proxy Forwarded Headers > How Proxy Forwarded Headers Work]

### How Proxy Forwarded Headers Work
Here's a visual representation of how the [BOLD:proxy] adds forwarded headers between the client and the [BOLD:application server]:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: sequenceDiagram participant Client participant Pro...]
The [BOLD:proxy] intercepts the original client request and adds the special [ITALIC:forwarded] headers ([CODE: X-Forwarded-[ITALIC:]) before passing the request to the [BOLD:application server].
These headers preserve information about the original request that would otherwise be lost:
] [BOLD:X-Forwarded-For]: The original client's IP address
[ITALIC: [BOLD:X-Forwarded-Proto]: The original protocol ([CODE: https])
] [BOLD:X-Forwarded-Host]: The original host ([CODE: mysuperapp.com])
When [BOLD:FastAPI CLI] is configured with [CODE: --forwarded-allow-ips], it trusts these headers and uses them, for example to generate the correct URLs in redirects.

metadata.breadcrumb: Behind a Proxy > Proxy Forwarded Headers > How Proxy Forwarded Headers Work
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/behind-a-proxy.md

metadata.breadcrumb: Behind a Proxy > Proxy with a stripped path prefix
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/behind-a-proxy.md

hierarchy: Proxy with a stripped path prefix
metadata.breadcrumb: Behind a Proxy > Proxy with a stripped path prefix
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/behind-a-proxy.md

hierarchy: Proxy with a stripped path prefix
metadata.breadcrumb: Behind a Proxy > Proxy with a stripped path prefix
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/behind-a-proxy.md

page_content:
[Context: Behind a Proxy > Proxy with a stripped path prefix > Providing the `root_path`]

### Providing the [CODE: root[ITALIC:path]
To achieve this, you can use the command line option [CODE: --root-path] like:

[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:0: $ fastapi run main.py --forwarded-allow-ips="*" --...]

If you use Hypercorn, it also has the option [CODE: --root-path].
/// note | Technical Details
The ASGI specification defines a [CODE: root]path] for this use case.
And the [CODE: --root-path] command line option provides that [CODE: root_path].
///

hierarchy: Proxy with a stripped path prefix
metadata.breadcrumb: Behind a Proxy > Proxy with a stripped path prefix > Providing the `root_path`
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/behind-a-proxy.md

page_content:
[Context: Behind a Proxy > Proxy with a stripped path prefix > Checking the current `root_path`]

### Checking the current [CODE: root[ITALIC:path]
You can get the current [CODE: root]path] used by your application for each request, it is part of the [CODE: scope] dictionary (that's part of the ASGI spec).
Here we are including it in the message just for demonstration purposes.
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: return {"message": "Hello World", "root[ITALIC:path": req...]
Then, if you start Uvicorn with:

[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:1: $ fastapi run main.py --forwarded-allow-ips="*" --...]

The response would be something like:
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:2: { "message": "Hello World", "root]path": "/api/v1"...]

hierarchy: Proxy with a stripped path prefix
metadata.breadcrumb: Behind a Proxy > Proxy with a stripped path prefix > Checking the current `root_path`
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/behind-a-proxy.md

page_content:
[Context: Behind a Proxy > Proxy with a stripped path prefix > Setting the `root_path` in the FastAPI app]

### Setting the [CODE: root[ITALIC:path] in the FastAPI app
Alternatively, if you don't have a way to provide a command line option like [CODE: --root-path] or equivalent, you can set the [CODE: root]path] parameter when creating your FastAPI app:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: app = FastAPI(root[ITALIC:path="/api/v1")...]
Passing the [CODE: root]path] to [CODE: FastAPI] would be the equivalent of passing the [CODE: --root-path] command line option to Uvicorn or Hypercorn.

hierarchy: Proxy with a stripped path prefix
Setting the `root_path` in the FastAPI app
metadata.breadcrumb: Behind a Proxy > Proxy with a stripped path prefix > Setting the `root_path` in the FastAPI app
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/behind-a-proxy.md

page_content:
[Context: Behind a Proxy > Proxy with a stripped path prefix > About `root_path`]

### About [CODE: root[ITALIC:path]
Keep in mind that the server (Uvicorn) won't use that [CODE: root]path] for anything else than passing it to the app.
But if you go with your browser to http://127.0.0.1:8000/app you will see the normal response:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: { "message": "Hello World", "root_path": "/api/v1"...]
So, it won't expect to be accessed at [CODE: http://127.0.0.1:8000/api/v1/app].
Uvicorn will expect the proxy to access Uvicorn at [CODE: http://127.0.0.1:8000/app], and then it would be the proxy's responsibility to add the extra [CODE: /api/v1] prefix on top.

hierarchy: Proxy with a stripped path prefix
metadata.breadcrumb: Behind a Proxy > Proxy with a stripped path prefix > About `root_path`
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/behind-a-proxy.md

page_content:
[Context: Behind a Proxy > About proxies with a stripped path prefix]

## About proxies with a stripped path prefix
Keep in mind that a proxy with stripped path prefix is only one of the ways to configure it.
Probably in many cases the default will be that the proxy doesn't have a stripped path prefix.
In a case like that (without a stripped path prefix), the proxy would listen on something like [CODE: https://myawesomeapp.com], and then if the browser goes to [CODE: https://myawesomeapp.com/api/v1/app] and your server (e.g. Uvicorn) listens on [CODE: http://127.0.0.1:8000] the proxy (without a stripped path prefix) would access Uvicorn at the same path: [CODE: http://127.0.0.1:8000/api/v1/app].

hierarchy: About proxies with a stripped path prefix
metadata.breadcrumb: Behind a Proxy > About proxies with a stripped path prefix
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/behind-a-proxy.md

page_content:
[Context: Behind a Proxy > Testing locally with Traefik]

## Testing locally with Traefik
You can easily run the experiment locally with a stripped path prefix using Traefik.
Download Traefik, it's a single binary, you can extract the compressed file and run it directly from the terminal.
Then create a file [CODE: traefik.toml] with:
```TOML hl[ITALIC:lines="3"
[entryPoints]
[entryPoints.http]
address = ":9999"

[providers]
[providers.file]
filename = "routes.toml"
```
This tells Traefik to listen on port 9999 and to use another file [CODE: routes.toml].
/// tip
We are using port 9999 instead of the standard HTTP port 80 so that you don't have to run it with admin ([CODE: sudo]) privileges.
///
Now create that other file [CODE: routes.toml]:
```TOML hl]lines="5 12 20"
[http]
[http.middlewares]

[http.middlewares.api-stripprefix.stripPrefix]
prefixes = ["/api/v1"]

[http.routers]

[http.routers.app-http]
entryPoints = ["http"]
service = "app"
rule = "PathPrefix([CODE: /api/v1])"
middlewares = ["api-stripprefix"]

[http.services]

[http.services.app]
[http.services.app.loadBalancer]
[[http.services.app.loadBalancer.servers]]
url = "http://127.0.0.1:8000"
```
This file configures Traefik to use the path prefix [CODE: /api/v1].
And then Traefik will redirect its requests to your Uvicorn running on [CODE: http://127.0.0.1:8000].
Now start Traefik:

[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: $ ./traefik --configFile=traefik.toml INFO[0000] C...]

And now start your app, using the [CODE: --root-path] option:

[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]1: $ fastapi run main.py --forwarded-allow-ips="*" --...]

metadata.breadcrumb: Behind a Proxy > Testing locally with Traefik
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/behind-a-proxy.md

page_content:
[Context: Behind a Proxy > Testing locally with Traefik > Check the responses]

### Check the responses
Now, if you go to the URL with the port for Uvicorn: http://127.0.0.1:8000/app, you will see the normal response:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: { "message": "Hello World", "root[ITALIC:path": "/api/v1"...]
/// tip
Notice that even though you are accessing it at [CODE: http://127.0.0.1:8000/app] it shows the [CODE: root]path] of [CODE: /api/v1], taken from the option [CODE: --root-path].
///
And now open the URL with the port for Traefik, including the path prefix: http://127.0.0.1:9999/api/v1/app.
We get the same response:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]1: { "message": "Hello World", "root[ITALIC:path": "/api/v1"...]
but this time at the URL with the prefix path provided by the proxy: [CODE: /api/v1].
Of course, the idea here is that everyone would access the app through the proxy, so the version with the path prefix [CODE: /api/v1] is the "correct" one.
And the version without the path prefix ([CODE: http://127.0.0.1:8000/app]), provided by Uvicorn directly, would be exclusively for the ]proxy[ITALIC: (Traefik) to access it.
That demonstrates how the Proxy (Traefik) uses the path prefix and how the server (Uvicorn) uses the [CODE: root]path] from the option [CODE: --root-path].

metadata.breadcrumb: Behind a Proxy > Testing locally with Traefik > Check the responses
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/behind-a-proxy.md

page_content:
[Context: Behind a Proxy > Testing locally with Traefik > Check the responses]

### Check the responses
Now, if you go to the URL with the port for Uvicorn: http://127.0.0.1:8000/app, you will see the normal response:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: { "message": "Hello World", "root[ITALIC:path": "/api/v1"...]
/// tip
Notice that even though you are accessing it at [CODE: http://127.0.0.1:8000/app] it shows the [CODE: root]path] of [CODE: /api/v1], taken from the option [CODE: --root-path].
///
And now open the URL with the port for Traefik, including the path prefix: http://127.0.0.1:9999/api/v1/app.
We get the same response:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]1: { "message": "Hello World", "root[ITALIC:path": "/api/v1"...]
but this time at the URL with the prefix path provided by the proxy: [CODE: /api/v1].
Of course, the idea here is that everyone would access the app through the proxy, so the version with the path prefix [CODE: /api/v1] is the "correct" one.
And the version without the path prefix ([CODE: http://127.0.0.1:8000/app]), provided by Uvicorn directly, would be exclusively for the ]proxy[ITALIC: (Traefik) to access it.
That demonstrates how the Proxy (Traefik) uses the path prefix and how the server (Uvicorn) uses the [CODE: root]path] from the option [CODE: --root-path].

metadata.breadcrumb: Behind a Proxy > Testing locally with Traefik > Check the responses
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/behind-a-proxy.md

page_content:
[Context: Behind a Proxy > Testing locally with Traefik > Check the docs UI]

### Check the docs UI
But here's the fun part.
The "official" way to access the app would be through the proxy with the path prefix that we defined. So, as we would expect, if you try the docs UI served by Uvicorn directly, without the path prefix in the URL, it won't work, because it expects to be accessed through the proxy.
You can check it at http://127.0.0.1:8000/docs:

But if we access the docs UI at the "official" URL using the proxy with port [CODE: 9999], at [CODE: /api/v1/docs], it works correctly!
You can check it at http://127.0.0.1:9999/api/v1/docs:

Right as we wanted it.
This is because FastAPI uses this [CODE: root[ITALIC:path] to create the default [CODE: server] in OpenAPI with the URL provided by [CODE: root]path].

### Disable automatic server from [CODE: root[ITALIC:path]
If you don't want [BOLD:FastAPI] to include an automatic server using the [CODE: root]path], you can use the parameter [CODE: root[ITALIC:path]in[ITALIC:servers=False]:
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:0: root]path[ITALIC:in]servers=False, )...]
and then it won't include it in the OpenAPI schema.

## Mounting a sub-application
If you need to mount a sub-application (as described in Sub Applications - Mounts) while also using a proxy with [CODE: root[ITALIC:path], you can do it normally, as you would expect.
FastAPI will internally use the [CODE: root]path] smartly, so it will just work.

page_content:
[Context: Custom Response - HTML, Stream, File, others]

# Custom Response - HTML, Stream, File, others
By default, [BOLD:FastAPI] will return JSON responses.
You can override it by returning a [CODE: Response] directly as seen in Return a Response directly.
But if you return a [CODE: Response] directly (or any subclass, like [CODE: JSONResponse]), the data won't be automatically converted (even if you declare a [CODE: response[ITALIC:model]), and the documentation won't be automatically generated (for example, including the specific "media type", in the HTTP header [CODE: Content-Type] as part of the generated OpenAPI).
But you can also declare the [CODE: Response] that you want to be used (e.g. any [CODE: Response] subclass), in the [ITALIC:path operation decorator] using the [CODE: response]class] parameter.
The contents that you return from your [ITALIC:path operation function] will be put inside of that [CODE: Response].
/// note
If you use a response class with no media type, FastAPI will expect your response to have no content, so it will not document the response format in its generated OpenAPI docs.
///

hierarchy: Custom Response - HTML, Stream, File, others
metadata.breadcrumb: Custom Response - HTML, Stream, File, others
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/custom-response.md

# Custom Response - HTML, Stream, File, others
By default, [BOLD:FastAPI] will return JSON responses.
You can override it by returning a [CODE: Response] directly as seen in Return a Response directly.
But if you return a [CODE: Response] directly (or any subclass, like [CODE: JSONResponse]), the data won't be automatically converted (even if you declare a [CODE: response[ITALIC:model]), and the documentation won't be automatically generated (for example, including the specific "media type", in the HTTP header [CODE: Content-Type] as part of the generated OpenAPI).
But you can also declare the [CODE: Response] that you want to be used (e.g. any [CODE: Response] subclass), in the [ITALIC:path operation decorator] using the [CODE: response]class] parameter.
The contents that you return from your [ITALIC:path operation function] will be put inside of that [CODE: Response].
/// note
If you use a response class with no media type, FastAPI will expect your response to have no content, so it will not document the response format in its generated OpenAPI docs.
///

page_content:
[Context: Custom Response - HTML, Stream, File, others > JSON Responses]

## JSON Responses
By default FastAPI returns JSON responses.
If you declare a Response Model FastAPI will use it to serialize the data to JSON, using Pydantic.
If you don't declare a response model, FastAPI will use the [CODE: jsonable[ITALIC:encoder] explained in JSON Compatible Encoder and put it in a [CODE: JSONResponse].
If you declare a [CODE: response]class] with a JSON media type ([CODE: application/json]), like is the case with the [CODE: JSONResponse], the data you return will be automatically converted (and filtered) with any Pydantic [CODE: response[ITALIC:model] that you declared in the [ITALIC:path operation decorator]. But the data won't be serialized to JSON bytes with Pydantic, instead it will be converted with the [CODE: jsonable]encoder] and then passed to the [CODE: JSONResponse] class, which will serialize it to bytes using the standard JSON library in Python.

hierarchy: Custom Response - HTML, Stream, File, others
metadata.breadcrumb: Custom Response - HTML, Stream, File, others > JSON Responses
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/custom-response.md

page_content:
[Context: Custom Response - HTML, Stream, File, others > JSON Responses > JSON Performance]

### JSON Performance
In short, if you want the maximum performance, use a Response Model and don't declare a [CODE: response[ITALIC:class] in the [ITALIC:path operation decorator].
```python
async def create]item(item: Item) -> Item:
return item
```

hierarchy: Custom Response - HTML, Stream, File, others
metadata.breadcrumb: Custom Response - HTML, Stream, File, others > JSON Responses > JSON Performance
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/custom-response.md

page_content:
[Context: Custom Response - HTML, Stream, File, others > HTML Response]

## HTML Response
To return a response with HTML directly from [BOLD:FastAPI], use [CODE: HTMLResponse].
[ITALIC: Import [CODE: HTMLResponse].
] Pass [CODE: HTMLResponse] as the parameter [CODE: response[ITALIC:class] of your [ITALIC:path operation decorator].
```python
from fastapi.responses import HTMLResponse

@app.get("/items/", response]class=HTMLResponse)
async def read[ITALIC:items():
```
/// info
The parameter [CODE: response]class] will also be used to define the "media type" of the response.
In this case, the HTTP header [CODE: Content-Type] will be set to [CODE: text/html].
And it will be documented as such in OpenAPI.
///

hierarchy: Custom Response - HTML, Stream, File, others
metadata.breadcrumb: Custom Response - HTML, Stream, File, others > HTML Response
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/custom-response.md

page_content:
[Context: Custom Response - HTML, Stream, File, others > HTML Response > Document in OpenAPI and override `Response`]

### Document in OpenAPI and override [CODE: Response]
If you want to override the response from inside of the function but at the same time document the "media type" in OpenAPI, you can use the [CODE: response[ITALIC:class] parameter AND return a [CODE: Response] object.
The [CODE: response]class] will then be used only to document the OpenAPI [ITALIC:path operation], but your [CODE: Response] will be used as is.
#### Return an [CODE: HTMLResponse] directly
For example, it could be something like:
```python
def generate[ITALIC:html]response():
html[ITALIC:content = """
@app.get("/items/", response]class=HTMLResponse)
async def read[ITALIC:items():
return generate]html[ITALIC:response()
```
In this example, the function [CODE: generate]html[ITALIC:response()] already generates and returns a [CODE: Response] instead of returning the HTML in a [CODE: str].
By returning the result of calling [CODE: generate]html[ITALIC:response()], you are already returning a [CODE: Response] that will override the default [BOLD:FastAPI] behavior.
But as you passed the [CODE: HTMLResponse] in the [CODE: response]class] too, [BOLD:FastAPI] will know how to document it in OpenAPI and the interactive docs as HTML with [CODE: text/html]:

page_content:
[Context: Custom Response - HTML, Stream, File, others > Available responses]

## Available responses
Here are some of the available responses.
Keep in mind that you can use [CODE: Response] to return anything else, or even create a custom sub-class.
/// note | Technical Details
You could also use [CODE: from starlette.responses import HTMLResponse].
[BOLD:FastAPI] provides the same [CODE: starlette.responses] as [CODE: fastapi.responses] just as a convenience for you, the developer. But most of the available responses come directly from Starlette.
///

hierarchy: Custom Response - HTML, Stream, File, others
metadata.breadcrumb: Custom Response - HTML, Stream, File, others > Available responses
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/custom-response.md

page_content:
[Context: Custom Response - HTML, Stream, File, others > Available responses > `Response`]

### [CODE: Response]
The main [CODE: Response] class, all the other responses inherit from it.
You can return it directly.
It accepts the following parameters:
[ITALIC: [CODE: content] - A [CODE: str] or [CODE: bytes].
] [CODE: status[ITALIC:code] - An [CODE: int] HTTP status code.
[ITALIC: [CODE: headers] - A [CODE: dict] of strings.
] [CODE: media]type] - A [CODE: str] giving the media type. E.g. [CODE: "text/html"].
FastAPI (actually Starlette) will automatically include a Content-Length header. It will also include a Content-Type header, based on the [CODE: media[ITALIC:type] and appending a charset for text types.
```python
from fastapi import FastAPI, Response

return Response(content=data, media]type="application/xml")
```

hierarchy: Custom Response - HTML, Stream, File, others
metadata.breadcrumb: Custom Response - HTML, Stream, File, others > Available responses > `Response`
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/custom-response.md

page_content:
[Context: Custom Response - HTML, Stream, File, others > Available responses > `HTMLResponse`]

### [CODE: HTMLResponse]
Takes some text or bytes and returns an HTML response, as you read above.

hierarchy: Custom Response - HTML, Stream, File, others

page_content:
[Context: Custom Response - HTML, Stream, File, others > Available responses > `PlainTextResponse`]

### [CODE: PlainTextResponse]
Takes some text or bytes and returns a plain text response.
```python
from fastapi.responses import PlainTextResponse

@app.get("/", response_class=PlainTextResponse)
async def main():
return "Hello World"
```

hierarchy: Custom Response - HTML, Stream, File, others
metadata.breadcrumb: Custom Response - HTML, Stream, File, others > Available responses > `PlainTextResponse`
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/custom-response.md

page_content:
[Context: Custom Response - HTML, Stream, File, others > Available responses > `JSONResponse`]

### [CODE: JSONResponse]
Takes some data and returns an [CODE: application/json] encoded response.
This is the default response used in [BOLD:FastAPI], as you read above.
/// note | Technical Details
But if you declare a response model or return type, that will be used directly to serialize the data to JSON, and a response with the right media type for JSON will be returned directly, without using the [CODE: JSONResponse] class.
This is the ideal way to get the best performance.
///

hierarchy: Custom Response - HTML, Stream, File, others

page_content:
[Context: Custom Response - HTML, Stream, File, others > Available responses > `RedirectResponse`]

### [CODE: RedirectResponse]
Returns an HTTP redirect. Uses a 307 status code (Temporary Redirect) by default.
You can return a [CODE: RedirectResponse] directly:
```python
from fastapi.responses import RedirectResponse

return RedirectResponse("https://typer.tiangolo.com")
```
---
Or you can use it in the [CODE: response[ITALIC:class] parameter:
```python
from fastapi.responses import RedirectResponse

@app.get("/fastapi", response]class=RedirectResponse)
async def redirect[ITALIC:fastapi():
return "https://fastapi.tiangolo.com"
```
If you do that, then you can return the URL directly from your [ITALIC:path operation] function.
In this case, the [CODE: status]code] used will be the default one for the [CODE: RedirectResponse], which is [CODE: 307].
---
You can also use the [CODE: status[ITALIC:code] parameter combined with the [CODE: response]class] parameter:
```python
from fastapi.responses import RedirectResponse

@app.get("/pydantic", response[ITALIC:class=RedirectResponse, status]code=302)
async def redirect_pydantic():
return "https://docs.pydantic.dev/"
```

hierarchy: Custom Response - HTML, Stream, File, others
metadata.breadcrumb: Custom Response - HTML, Stream, File, others > Available responses > `RedirectResponse`
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/custom-response.md

page_content:
[Context: Custom Response - HTML, Stream, File, others > Available responses > `FileResponse`]

### [CODE: FileResponse]
Asynchronously streams a file as the response.
Takes a different set of arguments to instantiate than the other response types:
[ITALIC: [CODE: path] - The file path to the file to stream.
] [CODE: headers] - Any custom headers to include, as a dictionary.
[ITALIC: [CODE: media[ITALIC:type] - A string giving the media type. If unset, the filename or path will be used to infer a media type.
] [CODE: filename] - If set, this will be included in the response [CODE: Content-Disposition].
File responses will include appropriate [CODE: Content-Length], [CODE: Last-Modified] and [CODE: ETag] headers.
```python
from fastapi.responses import FileResponse

return FileResponse(some]file[ITALIC:path)
```
You can also use the [CODE: response]class] parameter:
```python
from fastapi.responses import FileResponse

@app.get("/", response[ITALIC:class=FileResponse)
async def main():
return some]file_path
```
In this case, you can return the file path directly from your [ITALIC:path operation] function.

page_content:
[Context: Custom Response - HTML, Stream, File, others > Custom response class]

## Custom response class
You can create your own custom response class, inheriting from [CODE: Response] and using it.
For example, let's say that you want to use [[CODE: orjson]](https://github.com/ijl/orjson) with some settings.
Let's say you want it to return indented and formatted JSON, so you want to use the orjson option [CODE: orjson.OPT[ITALIC:INDENT]2].
You could create a [CODE: CustomORJSONResponse]. The main thing you have to do is create a [CODE: Response.render(content)] method that returns the content as [CODE: bytes]:
```python
class CustomORJSONResponse(Response):
media[ITALIC:type = "application/json"

def render(self, content: Any) -> bytes:
assert orjson is not None, "orjson must be installed"
return orjson.dumps(content, option=orjson.OPT]INDENT[ITALIC:2)
@app.get("/", response]class=CustomORJSONResponse)
async def main():
```
Now instead of returning:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: {"message": "Hello World"}...]
...this response will return:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]1: { "message": "Hello World" }...]
Of course, you will probably find much better ways to take advantage of this than formatting JSON.

hierarchy: Custom Response - HTML, Stream, File, others
metadata.breadcrumb: Custom Response - HTML, Stream, File, others > Custom response class
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/custom-response.md

page_content:
[Context: Custom Response - HTML, Stream, File, others > Custom response class]

## Custom response class
You can create your own custom response class, inheriting from [CODE: Response] and using it.
For example, let's say that you want to use [[CODE: orjson]](https://github.com/ijl/orjson) with some settings.
Let's say you want it to return indented and formatted JSON, so you want to use the orjson option [CODE: orjson.OPT[ITALIC:INDENT]2].
You could create a [CODE: CustomORJSONResponse]. The main thing you have to do is create a [CODE: Response.render(content)] method that returns the content as [CODE: bytes]:
```python
class CustomORJSONResponse(Response):
media[ITALIC:type = "application/json"

def render(self, content: Any) -> bytes:
assert orjson is not None, "orjson must be installed"
return orjson.dumps(content, option=orjson.OPT]INDENT[ITALIC:2)
@app.get("/", response]class=CustomORJSONResponse)
async def main():
```
Now instead of returning:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: {"message": "Hello World"}...]
...this response will return:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]1: { "message": "Hello World" }...]
Of course, you will probably find much better ways to take advantage of this than formatting JSON.

hierarchy: Custom Response - HTML, Stream, File, others

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/custom-response.md

page_content:
[Context: Custom Response - HTML, Stream, File, others > Custom response class > `orjson` or Response Model]

### [CODE: orjson] or Response Model
If what you are looking for is performance, you are probably better off using a Response Model than an [CODE: orjson] response.
With a response model, FastAPI will use Pydantic to serialize the data to JSON, without using intermediate steps, like converting it with [CODE: jsonable_encoder], which would happen in any other case.
And under the hood, Pydantic uses the same underlying Rust mechanisms as [CODE: orjson] to serialize to JSON, so you will already get the best performance with a response model.

hierarchy: Custom Response - HTML, Stream, File, others
metadata.breadcrumb: Custom Response - HTML, Stream, File, others > Custom response class > `orjson` or Response Model
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/custom-response.md

page_content:
[Context: Custom Response - HTML, Stream, File, others > Default response class]

## Default response class
When creating a [BOLD:FastAPI] class instance or an [CODE: APIRouter] you can specify which response class to use by default.
The parameter that defines this is [CODE: default[ITALIC:response]class].
In the example below, [BOLD:FastAPI] will use [CODE: HTMLResponse] by default, in all [ITALIC:path operations], instead of JSON.
```python
from fastapi.responses import HTMLResponse

app = FastAPI(default[ITALIC:response]class=HTMLResponse)

```
/// tip
You can still override [CODE: response_class] in [ITALIC:path operations] as before.

page_content:
[Context: Using Dataclasses]

# Using Dataclasses
FastAPI is built on top of [BOLD:Pydantic], and I have been showing you how to use Pydantic models to declare requests and responses.
But FastAPI also supports using [[CODE: dataclasses]](https://docs.python.org/3/library/dataclasses.html) the same way:
```python
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class Item:
name: str
price: float
description: str | None = None
tax: float | None = None
async def create_item(item: Item):
return item
```
This is still supported thanks to [BOLD:Pydantic], as it has [internal support for [CODE: dataclasses]](https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/concepts/dataclasses/#use-of-stdlib-dataclasses-with-basemodel).
So, even with the code above that doesn't use Pydantic explicitly, FastAPI is using Pydantic to convert those standard dataclasses to Pydantic's own flavor of dataclasses.
And of course, it supports the same:
[ITALIC: data validation
] data serialization
- data documentation, etc.
This works the same way as with Pydantic models. And it is actually achieved in the same way underneath, using Pydantic.
/// info
Keep in mind that dataclasses can't do everything Pydantic models can do.
So, you might still need to use Pydantic models.
But if you have a bunch of dataclasses laying around, this is a nice trick to use them to power a web API using FastAPI. 🤓
///

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/dataclasses.md

page_content:
[Context: Using Dataclasses > Dataclasses in `response_model`]

## Dataclasses in [CODE: response[ITALIC:model]
You can also use [CODE: dataclasses] in the [CODE: response]model] parameter:
```python
from dataclasses import dataclass, field

@dataclass
class Item:
name: str
price: float
tags: list[str] = field(default[ITALIC:factory=list)
description: str | None = None
tax: float | None = None
@app.get("/items/next", response]model=Item)
async def read[ITALIC:next]item():
```
The dataclass will be automatically converted to a Pydantic dataclass.
This way, its schema will show up in the API docs user interface:

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/dataclasses.md

page_content:
[Context: Using Dataclasses > Dataclasses in Nested Data Structures]

## Dataclasses in Nested Data Structures
You can also combine [CODE: dataclasses] with other type annotations to make nested data structures.
In some cases, you might still have to use Pydantic's version of [CODE: dataclasses]. For example, if you have errors with the automatically generated API documentation.
In that case, you can simply swap the standard [CODE: dataclasses] with [CODE: pydantic.dataclasses], which is a drop-in replacement:
```python
from dataclasses import field # (1)

from pydantic.dataclasses import dataclass # (2)

@dataclass
class Item:
name: str
description: str | None = None
@dataclass
class Author:
name: str
items: list[Item] = field(default[ITALIC:factory=list) # (3)
@app.post("/authors/{author]id}/items/", response[ITALIC:model=Author) # (4)
async def create]author[ITALIC:items(author]id: str, items: list[Item]): # (5)
return {"name": author[ITALIC:id, "items": items} # (6)
@app.get("/authors/", response]model=list[Author]) # (7)
def get[ITALIC:authors(): # (8)
```
- We still import [CODE: field] from standard [CODE: dataclasses].
- [CODE: pydantic.dataclasses] is a drop-in replacement for [CODE: dataclasses].
- The [CODE: Author] dataclass includes a list of [CODE: Item] dataclasses.
- The [CODE: Author] dataclass is used as the [CODE: response]model] parameter.
- You can use other standard type annotations with dataclasses as the request body.
In this case, it's a list of [CODE: Item] dataclasses.
- Here we are returning a dictionary that contains [CODE: items] which is a list of dataclasses.
FastAPI is still capable of serializing the data to JSON.
- Here the [CODE: response[ITALIC:model] is using a type annotation of a list of [CODE: Author] dataclasses.
Again, you can combine [CODE: dataclasses] with standard type annotations.
- Notice that this [ITALIC:path operation function] uses regular [CODE: def] instead of [CODE: async def].
As always, in FastAPI you can combine [CODE: def] and [CODE: async def] as needed.
If you need a refresher about when to use which, check out the section ]"In a hurry?"[ITALIC: in the docs about [[CODE: async] and [CODE: await]](../async.md#in-a-hurry).
- This [ITALIC:path operation function] is not returning dataclasses (although it could), but a list of dictionaries with internal data.
FastAPI will use the [CODE: response]model] parameter (that includes dataclasses) to convert the response.
You can combine [CODE: dataclasses] with other type annotations in many different combinations to form complex data structures.
Check the in-code annotation tips above to see more specific details.

metadata.breadcrumb: Using Dataclasses > Dataclasses in Nested Data Structures
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/dataclasses.md
