page_content:
[Context: Lifespan Events]

# Lifespan Events
You can define logic (code) that should be executed before the application [BOLD:starts up]. This means that this code will be executed [BOLD:once], [BOLD:before] the application [BOLD:starts receiving requests].
The same way, you can define logic (code) that should be executed when the application is [BOLD:shutting down]. In this case, this code will be executed [BOLD:once], [BOLD:after] having handled possibly [BOLD:many requests].
Because this code is executed before the application [BOLD:starts] taking requests, and right after it [BOLD:finishes] handling requests, it covers the whole application [BOLD:lifespan] (the word "lifespan" will be important in a second ).
This can be very useful for setting up [BOLD:resources] that you need to use for the whole app, and that are [BOLD:shared] among requests, and/or that you need to [BOLD:clean up] afterwards. For example, a database connection pool, or loading a shared machine learning model.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/events.md

page_content:
[Context: Lifespan Events > Use Case]

## Use Case
Let's start with an example [BOLD:use case] and then see how to solve it with this.
Let's imagine that you have some [BOLD:machine learning models] that you want to use to handle requests. 🤖
The same models are shared among requests, so, it's not one model per request, or one per user or something similar.
Let's imagine that loading the model can [BOLD:take quite some time], because it has to read a lot of [BOLD:data from disk]. So you don't want to do it for every request.
You could load it at the top level of the module/file, but that would also mean that it would [BOLD:load the model] even if you are just running a simple automated test, then that test would be [BOLD:slow] because it would have to wait for the model to load before being able to run an independent part of the code.
That's what we'll solve, let's load the model before the requests are handled, but only right before the application starts receiving requests, not while the code is being loaded.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/events.md

page_content:
[Context: Lifespan Events > Use Case]

## Use Case
Let's start with an example [BOLD:use case] and then see how to solve it with this.
Let's imagine that you have some [BOLD:machine learning models] that you want to use to handle requests. 🤖
The same models are shared among requests, so, it's not one model per request, or one per user or something similar.
Let's imagine that loading the model can [BOLD:take quite some time], because it has to read a lot of [BOLD:data from disk]. So you don't want to do it for every request.
You could load it at the top level of the module/file, but that would also mean that it would [BOLD:load the model] even if you are just running a simple automated test, then that test would be [BOLD:slow] because it would have to wait for the model to load before being able to run an independent part of the code.
That's what we'll solve, let's load the model before the requests are handled, but only right before the application starts receiving requests, not while the code is being loaded.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/events.md

page_content:
[Context: Lifespan Events > Lifespan]

## Lifespan
You can define this [ITALIC:startup] and [ITALIC:shutdown] logic using the [CODE: lifespan] parameter of the [CODE: FastAPI] app, and a "context manager" (I'll show you what that is in a second).
Let's start with an example and then see it in detail.
We create an async function [CODE: lifespan()] with [CODE: yield] like this:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: ml[ITALIC:models["answer]to[ITALIC:everything"] = fake]answer_to...]
Here we are simulating the expensive [ITALIC:startup] operation of loading the model by putting the (fake) model function in the dictionary with machine learning models before the [CODE: yield]. This code will be executed [BOLD:before] the application [BOLD:starts taking requests], during the [ITALIC:startup].
And then, right after the [CODE: yield], we unload the model. This code will be executed [BOLD:after] the application [BOLD:finishes handling requests], right before the [ITALIC:shutdown]. This could, for example, release resources like memory or a GPU.
/// tip
The [CODE: shutdown] would happen when you are [BOLD:stopping] the application.
Maybe you need to start a new version, or you just got tired of running it. 🤷
///

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/events.md

page_content:
[Context: Lifespan Events > Lifespan]

## Lifespan
You can define this [ITALIC:startup] and [ITALIC:shutdown] logic using the [CODE: lifespan] parameter of the [CODE: FastAPI] app, and a "context manager" (I'll show you what that is in a second).
Let's start with an example and then see it in detail.
We create an async function [CODE: lifespan()] with [CODE: yield] like this:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: ml[ITALIC:models["answer]to[ITALIC:everything"] = fake]answer_to...]
Here we are simulating the expensive [ITALIC:startup] operation of loading the model by putting the (fake) model function in the dictionary with machine learning models before the [CODE: yield]. This code will be executed [BOLD:before] the application [BOLD:starts taking requests], during the [ITALIC:startup].
And then, right after the [CODE: yield], we unload the model. This code will be executed [BOLD:after] the application [BOLD:finishes handling requests], right before the [ITALIC:shutdown]. This could, for example, release resources like memory or a GPU.
/// tip
The [CODE: shutdown] would happen when you are [BOLD:stopping] the application.
Maybe you need to start a new version, or you just got tired of running it. 🤷
///

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/events.md

page_content:
[Context: Lifespan Events > Lifespan > Lifespan function]

### Lifespan function
The first thing to notice, is that we are defining an async function with [CODE: yield]. This is very similar to Dependencies with [CODE: yield].
```python
async def lifespan(app: FastAPI):
# Load the ML model
ml[ITALIC:models["answer]to[ITALIC:everything"] = fake]answer[ITALIC:to]everything[ITALIC:ml]model
yield
# Clean up the ML models and release the resources
ml_models.clear()
```
The first part of the function, before the [CODE: yield], will be executed [BOLD:before] the application starts.
And the part after the [CODE: yield] will be executed [BOLD:after] the application has finished.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/events.md

page_content:
[Context: Lifespan Events > Lifespan > Async Context Manager]

### Async Context Manager
If you check, the function is decorated with an [CODE: @asynccontextmanager].
That converts the function into something called an "[BOLD:async context manager]".
```python
from contextlib import asynccontextmanager

@asynccontextmanager
async def lifespan(app: FastAPI):
```
A [BOLD:context manager] in Python is something that you can use in a [CODE: with] statement, for example, [CODE: open()] can be used as a context manager:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: with open("file.txt") as file: file.read()...]
In recent versions of Python, there's also an [BOLD:async context manager]. You would use it with [CODE: async with]:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]1: async with lifespan(app): await do[ITALIC:stuff()...]
When you create a context manager or an async context manager like above, what it does is that, before entering the [CODE: with] block, it will execute the code before the [CODE: yield], and after exiting the [CODE: with] block, it will execute the code after the [CODE: yield].
In our code example above, we don't use it directly, but we pass it to FastAPI for it to use it.
The [CODE: lifespan] parameter of the [CODE: FastAPI] app takes an [BOLD:async context manager], so we can pass our new [CODE: lifespan] async context manager to it.
[CODE]BLOCK_2: app = FastAPI(lifespan=lifespan)...]

metadata.breadcrumb: Lifespan Events > Lifespan > Async Context Manager
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/events.md

page_content:
[Context: Lifespan Events > Lifespan > Async Context Manager]

### Async Context Manager
If you check, the function is decorated with an [CODE: @asynccontextmanager].
That converts the function into something called an "[BOLD:async context manager]".
```python

@asynccontextmanager
async def lifespan(app: FastAPI):
```
A [BOLD:context manager] in Python is something that you can use in a [CODE: with] statement, for example, [CODE: open()] can be used as a context manager:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: with open("file.txt") as file: file.read()...]

page_content:
[Context: Lifespan Events > Alternative Events (deprecated)]

## Alternative Events (deprecated)
/// warning
The recommended way to handle the [ITALIC:startup] and [ITALIC:shutdown] is using the [CODE: lifespan] parameter of the [CODE: FastAPI] app as described above. If you provide a [CODE: lifespan] parameter, [CODE: startup] and [CODE: shutdown] event handlers will no longer be called. It's all [CODE: lifespan] or all events, not both.
You can probably skip this part.
///
There's an alternative way to define this logic to be executed during [ITALIC:startup] and during [ITALIC:shutdown].
You can define event handlers (functions) that need to be executed before the application starts up, or when the application is shutting down.
These functions can be declared with [CODE: async def] or normal [CODE: def].

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/events.md

page_content:
[Context: Lifespan Events > Alternative Events (deprecated) > `startup` event]

### [CODE: startup] event
To add a function that should be run before the application starts, declare it with the event [CODE: "startup"]:
```python
@app.on[ITALIC:event("startup")
async def startup]event():
```
In this case, the [CODE: startup] event handler function will initialize the items "database" (just a [CODE: dict]) with some values.
You can add more than one event handler function.
And your application won't start receiving requests until all the [CODE: startup] event handlers have completed.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/events.md
Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/events.md

page_content:
[Context: Lifespan Events > Alternative Events (deprecated) > `startup` and `shutdown` together]

### [CODE: startup] and [CODE: shutdown] together
There's a high chance that the logic for your [ITALIC:startup] and [ITALIC:shutdown] is connected, you might want to start something and then finish it, acquire a resource and then release it, etc.
Doing that in separated functions that don't share logic or variables together is more difficult as you would need to store values in global variables or similar tricks.
Because of that, it's now recommended to instead use the [CODE: lifespan] as explained above.

metadata.breadcrumb: Lifespan Events > Alternative Events (deprecated) > `startup` and `shutdown` together
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/events.md

page_content:
[Context: Lifespan Events > Technical Details]

## Technical Details
Just a technical detail for the curious nerds. 🤓
Underneath, in the ASGI technical specification, this is part of the Lifespan Protocol, and it defines events called [CODE: startup] and [CODE: shutdown].
/// info
You can read more about the Starlette [CODE: lifespan] handlers in Starlette's Lifespan' docs.
Including how to handle lifespan state that can be used in other areas of your code.
///

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/events.md

page_content:
[Context: Lifespan Events > Sub Applications]

## Sub Applications
Keep in mind that these lifespan events (startup and shutdown) will only be executed for the main application, not for Sub Applications - Mounts.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/events.md

page_content:
[Context: Generating SDKs]

# Generating SDKs
Because [BOLD:FastAPI] is based on the [BOLD:OpenAPI] specification, its APIs can be described in a standard format that many tools understand.
This makes it easy to generate up-to-date [BOLD:documentation], client libraries ([BOLD:SDKs]) in multiple languages, and [BOLD:testing] or [BOLD:automation workflows] that stay in sync with your code.
In this guide, you'll learn how to generate a [BOLD:TypeScript SDK] for your FastAPI backend.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/generate-clients.md

page_content:
[Context: Generating SDKs > Open Source SDK Generators]

## Open Source SDK Generators
A versatile option is the OpenAPI Generator, which supports [BOLD:many programming languages] and can generate SDKs from your OpenAPI specification.
For [BOLD:TypeScript clients], Hey API is a purpose-built solution, providing an optimized experience for the TypeScript ecosystem.
You can discover more SDK generators on OpenAPI.Tools.
/// tip
FastAPI automatically generates [BOLD:OpenAPI 3.1] specifications, so any tool you use must support this version.
///

metadata.breadcrumb: Generating SDKs > Open Source SDK Generators
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/generate-clients.md

page_content:
[Context: Generating SDKs > SDK Generators from FastAPI Sponsors]

## SDK Generators from FastAPI Sponsors
This section highlights [BOLD:venture-backed] and [BOLD:company-supported] solutions from companies that sponsor FastAPI. These products provide [BOLD:additional features] and [BOLD:integrations] on top of high-quality generated SDKs.
By [BOLD:sponsoring FastAPI] , these companies help ensure the framework and its [BOLD:ecosystem] remain healthy and [BOLD:sustainable].
Their sponsorship also demonstrates a strong commitment to the FastAPI [BOLD:community] (you), showing that they care not only about offering a [BOLD:great service] but also about supporting a [BOLD:robust and thriving framework], FastAPI.
For example, you might want to try:
[ITALIC: Speakeasy
] Stainless
- liblab
Some of these solutions may also be open source or offer free tiers, so you can try them without a financial commitment. Other commercial SDK generators are available and can be found online. 🤓

metadata.breadcrumb: Generating SDKs > SDK Generators from FastAPI Sponsors
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/generate-clients.md

page_content:
[Context: Generating SDKs > Create a TypeScript SDK]

## Create a TypeScript SDK
Let's start with a simple FastAPI application:
```python
class Item(BaseModel):
name: str
price: float
class ResponseMessage(BaseModel):
message: str
@app.post("/items/", response[ITALIC:model=ResponseMessage)
async def create]item(item: Item):
@app.get("/items/", response[ITALIC:model=list[Item])
async def get]items():
```
Notice that the [ITALIC:path operations] define the models they use for request payload and response payload, using the models [CODE: Item] and [CODE: ResponseMessage].

metadata.breadcrumb: Generating SDKs > Create a TypeScript SDK
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/generate-clients.md

page_content:
[Context: Generating SDKs > Create a TypeScript SDK > API Docs]

### API Docs
If you go to [CODE: /docs], you will see that it has the [BOLD:schemas] for the data to be sent in requests and received in responses:

You can see those schemas because they were declared with the models in the app.
That information is available in the app's [BOLD:OpenAPI schema], and then shown in the API docs.
That same information from the models that is included in OpenAPI is what can be used to [BOLD:generate the client code].

metadata.breadcrumb: Generating SDKs > Create a TypeScript SDK > API Docs
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/generate-clients.md

page_content:
[Context: Generating SDKs > Create a TypeScript SDK > Hey API]

### Hey API
Once we have a FastAPI app with the models, we can use Hey API to generate a TypeScript client. The fastest way to do that is via npx.
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: npx @hey-api/openapi-ts -i http://localhost:8000/o...]
This will generate a TypeScript SDK in [CODE: ./src/client].
You can learn how to [install [CODE: @hey-api/openapi-ts]](https://heyapi.dev/openapi-ts/get-started) and read about the generated output on their website.

metadata.breadcrumb: Generating SDKs > Create a TypeScript SDK > Hey API
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/generate-clients.md

page_content:
[Context: Generating SDKs > Create a TypeScript SDK > Using the SDK]

### Using the SDK
Now you can import and use the client code. It could look like this, notice that you get autocompletion for the methods:

You will also get autocompletion for the payload to send:

/// tip
Notice the autocompletion for [CODE: name] and [CODE: price], that was defined in the FastAPI application, in the [CODE: Item] model.
///
You will have inline errors for the data that you send:

The response object will also have autocompletion:

metadata.breadcrumb: Generating SDKs > Create a TypeScript SDK > Using the SDK
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/generate-clients.md

page_content:
[Context: Generating SDKs > FastAPI App with Tags]

## FastAPI App with Tags
In many cases, your FastAPI app will be bigger, and you will probably use tags to separate different groups of [ITALIC:path operations].
For example, you could have a section for [BOLD:items] and another section for [BOLD:users], and they could be separated by tags:
```python
@app.post("/items/", response[ITALIC:model=ResponseMessage, tags=["items"])
async def create]item(item: Item):
@app.get("/items/", response[ITALIC:model=list[Item], tags=["items"])
async def get]items():
@app.post("/users/", response[ITALIC:model=ResponseMessage, tags=["users"])
async def create]user(user: User):
```

metadata.breadcrumb: Generating SDKs > FastAPI App with Tags
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/generate-clients.md

page_content:
[Context: Generating SDKs > FastAPI App with Tags > Generate a TypeScript Client with Tags]

### Generate a TypeScript Client with Tags
If you generate a client for a FastAPI app using tags, it will normally also separate the client code based on the tags.
This way, you will be able to have things ordered and grouped correctly for the client code:

In this case, you have:
[ITALIC: [CODE: ItemsService]
] [CODE: UsersService]

hierarchy: Generate a TypeScript Client with Tags
metadata.breadcrumb: Generating SDKs > FastAPI App with Tags > Generate a TypeScript Client with Tags
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/generate-clients.md

page_content:
[Context: Generating SDKs > FastAPI App with Tags > Client Method Names]

### Client Method Names
Right now, the generated method names like [CODE: createItemItemsPost] don't look very clean:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: ItemsService.createItemItemsPost({name: "Plumbus",...]
...that's because the client generator uses the OpenAPI internal [BOLD:operation ID] for each [ITALIC:path operation].
OpenAPI requires that each operation ID is unique across all the [ITALIC:path operations], so FastAPI uses the [BOLD:function name], the [BOLD:path], and the [BOLD:HTTP method/operation] to generate that operation ID, because that way it can make sure that the operation IDs are unique.
But I'll show you how to improve that next. 🤓

metadata.breadcrumb: Generating SDKs > FastAPI App with Tags > Client Method Names
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/generate-clients.md

page_content:
[Context: Generating SDKs > Custom Operation IDs and Better Method Names]

## Custom Operation IDs and Better Method Names
You can [BOLD:modify] the way these operation IDs are [BOLD:generated] to make them simpler and have [BOLD:simpler method names] in the clients.
In this case, you will have to ensure that each operation ID is [BOLD:unique] in some other way.
For example, you could make sure that each [ITALIC:path operation] has a tag, and then generate the operation ID based on the [BOLD:tag] and the [ITALIC:path operation] [BOLD:name] (the function name).

hierarchy: Custom Operation IDs and Better Method Names
metadata.breadcrumb: Generating SDKs > Custom Operation IDs and Better Method Names
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/generate-clients.md

page_content:
[Context: Generating SDKs > Custom Operation IDs and Better Method Names > Custom Generate Unique ID Function]

### Custom Generate Unique ID Function
FastAPI uses a [BOLD:unique ID] for each [ITALIC:path operation], which is used for the [BOLD:operation ID] and also for the names of any needed custom models, for requests or responses.
You can customize that function. It takes an [CODE: APIRoute] and outputs a string.
For example, here it is using the first tag (you will probably have only one tag) and the [ITALIC:path operation] name (the function name).
You can then pass that custom function to [BOLD:FastAPI] as the [CODE: generate[ITALIC:unique]id[ITALIC:function] parameter:
```python
def custom]generate[ITALIC:unique]id(route: APIRoute):
return f"{route.tags[0]}-{route.name}"
app = FastAPI(generate[ITALIC:unique]id[ITALIC:function=custom]generate[ITALIC:unique]id)

```

hierarchy: Custom Operation IDs and Better Method Names
metadata.breadcrumb: Generating SDKs > Custom Operation IDs and Better Method Names > Custom Generate Unique ID Function
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/generate-clients.md

page_content:
[Context: Generating SDKs > Custom Operation IDs and Better Method Names > Generate a TypeScript Client with Custom Operation IDs]

### Generate a TypeScript Client with Custom Operation IDs
Now, if you generate the client again, you will see that it has the improved method names:

As you see, the method names now have the tag and then the function name, now they don't include information from the URL path and the HTTP operation.

hierarchy: Custom Operation IDs and Better Method Names
Generate a TypeScript Client with Custom Operation IDs
metadata.breadcrumb: Generating SDKs > Custom Operation IDs and Better Method Names > Generate a TypeScript Client with Custom Operation IDs
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/generate-clients.md

page_content:
[Context: Generating SDKs > Custom Operation IDs and Better Method Names > Preprocess the OpenAPI Specification for the Client Generator]

### Preprocess the OpenAPI Specification for the Client Generator
The generated code still has some [BOLD:duplicated information].
We already know that this method is related to the [BOLD:items] because that word is in the [CODE: ItemsService] (taken from the tag), but we still have the tag name prefixed in the method name too.
We will probably still want to keep it for OpenAPI in general, as that will ensure that the operation IDs are [BOLD:unique].
But for the generated client, we could [BOLD:modify] the OpenAPI operation IDs right before generating the clients, just to make those method names nicer and [BOLD:cleaner].
We could download the OpenAPI JSON to a file [CODE: openapi.json] and then we could [BOLD:remove that prefixed tag] with a script like this:
{[ITALIC: ../../docs[ITALIC:src/generate]clients/tutorial004[ITALIC:py310.py ]}
//// tab | Node.js
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:0: {!> ../../docs]src/generate[ITALIC:clients/tutorial004.js...]
////
With that, the operation IDs would be renamed from things like [CODE: items-get]items] to just [CODE: get_items], that way the client generator can generate simpler method names.

hierarchy: Custom Operation IDs and Better Method Names
Preprocess the OpenAPI Specification for the Client Generator
metadata.breadcrumb: Generating SDKs > Custom Operation IDs and Better Method Names > Preprocess the OpenAPI Specification for the Client Generator
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/generate-clients.md

page_content:
[Context: Generating SDKs > Custom Operation IDs and Better Method Names > Preprocess the OpenAPI Specification for the Client Generator]

### Preprocess the OpenAPI Specification for the Client Generator
The generated code still has some [BOLD:duplicated information].
We already know that this method is related to the [BOLD:items] because that word is in the [CODE: ItemsService] (taken from the tag), but we still have the tag name prefixed in the method name too.
We will probably still want to keep it for OpenAPI in general, as that will ensure that the operation IDs are [BOLD:unique].
But for the generated client, we could [BOLD:modify] the OpenAPI operation IDs right before generating the clients, just to make those method names nicer and [BOLD:cleaner].
We could download the OpenAPI JSON to a file [CODE: openapi.json] and then we could [BOLD:remove that prefixed tag] with a script like this:
{[ITALIC: ../../docs[ITALIC:src/generate]clients/tutorial004[ITALIC:py310.py ]}
//// tab | Node.js
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:0: {!> ../../docs]src/generate[ITALIC:clients/tutorial004.js...]
////
With that, the operation IDs would be renamed from things like [CODE: items-get]items] to just [CODE: get_items], that way the client generator can generate simpler method names.

hierarchy: Custom Operation IDs and Better Method Names
Preprocess the OpenAPI Specification for the Client Generator
metadata.breadcrumb: Generating SDKs > Custom Operation IDs and Better Method Names > Preprocess the OpenAPI Specification for the Client Generator
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/generate-clients.md

page_content:
[Context: Generating SDKs > Custom Operation IDs and Better Method Names > Generate a TypeScript Client with the Preprocessed OpenAPI]

### Generate a TypeScript Client with the Preprocessed OpenAPI
Since the end result is now in an [CODE: openapi.json] file, you need to update your input location:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: npx @hey-api/openapi-ts -i ./openapi.json -o src/c...]
After generating the new client, you would now have [BOLD:clean method names], with all the [BOLD:autocompletion], [BOLD:inline errors], etc:

hierarchy: Custom Operation IDs and Better Method Names
Generate a TypeScript Client with the Preprocessed OpenAPI
metadata.breadcrumb: Generating SDKs > Custom Operation IDs and Better Method Names > Generate a TypeScript Client with the Preprocessed OpenAPI
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/generate-clients.md

page_content:
[Context: Generating SDKs > Benefits]

## Benefits
When using the automatically generated clients, you would get [BOLD:autocompletion] for:
[ITALIC: Methods.
] Request payloads in the body, query parameters, etc.
[ITALIC: Response payloads.
You would also have [BOLD:inline errors] for everything.
And whenever you update the backend code, and [BOLD:regenerate] the frontend, it would have any new ]path operations* available as methods, the old ones removed, and any other change would be reflected on the generated code. 🤓
This also means that if something changed, it will be [BOLD:reflected] on the client code automatically. And if you [BOLD:build] the client, it will error out if you have any [BOLD:mismatch] in the data used.
So, you would [BOLD:detect many errors] very early in the development cycle instead of having to wait for the errors to show up to your final users in production and then trying to debug where the problem is.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/generate-clients.md

And it's possible that for your use case, the solution is in one of them.

You could still use most of the features in [BOLD:FastAPI] with the knowledge from the main Tutorial - User Guide.

page_content:
[Context: JSON with Bytes as Base64]

# JSON with Bytes as Base64
If your app needs to receive and send JSON data, but you need to include binary data in it, you can encode it as base64.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/json-base64-bytes.md

page_content:
[Context: JSON with Bytes as Base64 > Base64 vs Files]

## Base64 vs Files
Consider first if you can use Request Files for uploading binary data and Custom Response - FileResponse for sending binary data, instead of encoding it in JSON.
JSON can only contain UTF-8 encoded strings, so it can't contain raw bytes.
Base64 can encode binary data in strings, but to do it, it needs to use more characters than the original binary data, so it would normally be less efficient than regular files.
Use base64 only if you definitely need to include binary data in JSON, and you can't use files for that.

metadata.breadcrumb: JSON with Bytes as Base64 > Base64 vs Files
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/json-base64-bytes.md

page_content:
[Context: JSON with Bytes as Base64 > Pydantic `bytes`]

## Pydantic [CODE: bytes]
You can declare a Pydantic model with [CODE: bytes] fields, and then use [CODE: val[ITALIC:json]bytes] in the model config to tell it to use base64 to [ITALIC:validate] input JSON data, as part of that validation it will decode the base64 string into bytes.
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: model[ITALIC:config = {"val]json[ITALIC:bytes": "base64"}...]
If you check the [CODE: /docs], they will show that the field [CODE: data] expects base64 encoded bytes:


You could send a request like:
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:1: { "description": "Some data", "data": "aGVsbG8=" }...]
/// tip
[CODE: aGVsbG8=] is the base64 encoding of [CODE: hello].
///
And then Pydantic will decode the base64 string and give you the original bytes in the [CODE: data] field of the model.
You will receive a response like:
[CODE]BLOCK_2: { "description": "Some data", "content": "hello" }...]

metadata.breadcrumb: JSON with Bytes as Base64 > Pydantic `bytes`
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/json-base64-bytes.md

page_content:
[Context: JSON with Bytes as Base64 > Pydantic `bytes` for Output Data]

## Pydantic [CODE: bytes] for Output Data
You can also use [CODE: bytes] fields with [CODE: ser[ITALIC:json]bytes] in the model config for output data, and Pydantic will [ITALIC:serialize] the bytes as base64 when generating the JSON response.
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: model[ITALIC:config = {"ser]json_bytes": "base64"}...]

metadata.breadcrumb: JSON with Bytes as Base64 > Pydantic `bytes` for Output Data
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/json-base64-bytes.md

page_content:
[Context: JSON with Bytes as Base64 > Pydantic `bytes` for Input and Output Data]

## Pydantic [CODE: bytes] for Input and Output Data
And of course, you can use the same model configured to use base64 to handle both input ([ITALIC:validate]) with [CODE: val[ITALIC:json]bytes] and output ([ITALIC:serialize]) with [CODE: ser[ITALIC:json]bytes] when receiving and sending JSON data.
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: model[ITALIC:config = { "val]json[ITALIC:bytes": "base64", "ser]...]

hierarchy: Pydantic `bytes` for Input and Output Data
metadata.breadcrumb: JSON with Bytes as Base64 > Pydantic `bytes` for Input and Output Data
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/json-base64-bytes.md

page_content:
[Context: Advanced Middleware]

# Advanced Middleware
In the main tutorial you read how to add Custom Middleware to your application.
And then you also read how to handle [CORS with the [CODE: CORSMiddleware]](../tutorial/cors.md).
In this section we'll see how to use other middlewares.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/middleware.md

page_content:
[Context: Advanced Middleware > Adding ASGI middlewares]

## Adding ASGI middlewares
As [BOLD:FastAPI] is based on Starlette and implements the ASGI specification, you can use any ASGI middleware.
A middleware doesn't have to be made for FastAPI or Starlette to work, as long as it follows the ASGI spec.
In general, ASGI middlewares are classes that expect to receive an ASGI app as the first argument.
So, in the documentation for third-party ASGI middlewares they will probably tell you to do something like:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: from unicorn import UnicornMiddleware app = SomeAS...]
But FastAPI (actually Starlette) provides a simpler way to do it that makes sure that the internal middlewares handle server errors and custom exception handlers work properly.
For that, you use [CODE: app.add[ITALIC:middleware()] (as in the example for CORS).
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:1: from fastapi import FastAPI from unicorn import Un...]
[CODE: app.add]middleware()] receives a middleware class as the first argument and any additional arguments to be passed to the middleware.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/middleware.md

page_content:
[Context: Advanced Middleware > Adding ASGI middlewares]

## Adding ASGI middlewares
As [BOLD:FastAPI] is based on Starlette and implements the ASGI specification, you can use any ASGI middleware.
A middleware doesn't have to be made for FastAPI or Starlette to work, as long as it follows the ASGI spec.
In general, ASGI middlewares are classes that expect to receive an ASGI app as the first argument.
So, in the documentation for third-party ASGI middlewares they will probably tell you to do something like:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: from unicorn import UnicornMiddleware app = SomeAS...]
But FastAPI (actually Starlette) provides a simpler way to do it that makes sure that the internal middlewares handle server errors and custom exception handlers work properly.
For that, you use [CODE: app.add[ITALIC:middleware()] (as in the example for CORS).
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:1: from fastapi import FastAPI from unicorn import Un...]
[CODE: app.add]middleware()] receives a middleware class as the first argument and any additional arguments to be passed to the middleware.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/middleware.md

page_content:
[Context: Advanced Middleware > Integrated middlewares]

## Integrated middlewares
[BOLD:FastAPI] includes several middlewares for common use cases, we'll see next how to use them.
/// note | Technical Details
For the next examples, you could also use [CODE: from starlette.middleware.something import SomethingMiddleware].
[BOLD:FastAPI] provides several middlewares in [CODE: fastapi.middleware] just as a convenience for you, the developer. But most of the available middlewares come directly from Starlette.
///

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/middleware.md

page_content:
[Context: Advanced Middleware > `HTTPSRedirectMiddleware`]

## [CODE: HTTPSRedirectMiddleware]
Enforces that all incoming requests must either be [CODE: https] or [CODE: wss].
Any incoming request to [CODE: http] or [CODE: ws] will be redirected to the secure scheme instead.
```python
from fastapi.middleware.httpsredirect import HTTPSRedirectMiddleware

app.add_middleware(HTTPSRedirectMiddleware)

```

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/middleware.md

page_content:
[Context: Advanced Middleware > `TrustedHostMiddleware`]

## [CODE: TrustedHostMiddleware]
Enforces that all incoming requests have a correctly set [CODE: Host] header, in order to guard against HTTP Host Header attacks.
```python
from fastapi.middleware.trustedhost import TrustedHostMiddleware

app.add[ITALIC:middleware(
TrustedHostMiddleware, allowed]hosts=["example.com", "[ITALIC:.example.com"]
)
```
The following arguments are supported:
] [CODE: allowed[ITALIC:hosts] - A list of domain names that should be allowed as hostnames. Wildcard domains such as [CODE: [ITALIC:.example.com] are supported for matching subdomains. To allow any hostname either use [CODE: allowed]hosts=["]"]] or omit the middleware.
- [CODE: www_redirect] - If set to True, requests to non-www versions of the allowed hosts will be redirected to their www counterparts. Defaults to [CODE: True].
If an incoming request does not validate correctly then a [CODE: 400] response will be sent.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/middleware.md

page_content:
[Context: Advanced Middleware > `GZipMiddleware`]

## [CODE: GZipMiddleware]
Handles GZip responses for any request that includes [CODE: "gzip"] in the [CODE: Accept-Encoding] header.
The middleware will handle both standard and streaming responses.
```python
from fastapi.middleware.gzip import GZipMiddleware

app.add[ITALIC:middleware(GZipMiddleware, minimum]size=1000, compresslevel=5)

```
The following arguments are supported:
[ITALIC: [CODE: minimum_size] - Do not GZip responses that are smaller than this minimum size in bytes. Defaults to [CODE: 500].
] [CODE: compresslevel] - Used during GZip compression. It is an integer ranging from 1 to 9. Defaults to [CODE: 9]. Lower value results in faster compression but larger file sizes, while higher value results in slower compression but smaller file sizes.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/middleware.md

page_content:
[Context: Advanced Middleware > Other middlewares]

## Other middlewares
There are many other ASGI middlewares.
For example:
[ITALIC: [Uvicorn's [CODE: ProxyHeadersMiddleware]](https://github.com/encode/uvicorn/blob/master/uvicorn/middleware/proxy_headers.py)
] MessagePack
To see other available middlewares check Starlette's Middleware docs and the ASGI Awesome List.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/middleware.md

page_content:
[Context: OpenAPI Callbacks]

# OpenAPI Callbacks
You could create an API with a [ITALIC:path operation] that could trigger a request to an [ITALIC:external API] created by someone else (probably the same developer that would be [ITALIC:using] your API).
The process that happens when your API app calls the [ITALIC:external API] is named a "callback". Because the software that the external developer wrote sends a request to your API and then your API [ITALIC:calls back], sending a request to an [ITALIC:external API] (that was probably created by the same developer).
In this case, you could want to document how that external API [ITALIC:should] look like. What [ITALIC:path operation] it should have, what body it should expect, what response it should return, etc.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/openapi-callbacks.md

page_content:
[Context: OpenAPI Callbacks > An app with callbacks]

## An app with callbacks
Let's see all this with an example.
Imagine you develop an app that allows creating invoices.
These invoices will have an [CODE: id], [CODE: title] (optional), [CODE: customer], and [CODE: total].
The user of your API (an external developer) will create an invoice in your API with a POST request.
Then your API will (let's imagine):
[ITALIC: Send the invoice to some customer of the external developer.
] Collect the money.
[ITALIC: Send a notification back to the API user (the external developer).
] This will be done by sending a POST request (from [ITALIC:your API]) to some [ITALIC:external API] provided by that external developer (this is the "callback").

metadata.breadcrumb: OpenAPI Callbacks > An app with callbacks
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/openapi-callbacks.md

page_content:
[Context: OpenAPI Callbacks > The normal **FastAPI** app]

## The normal [BOLD:FastAPI] app
Let's first see how the normal API app would look like before adding the callback.
It will have a [ITALIC:path operation] that will receive an [CODE: Invoice] body, and a query parameter [CODE: callback[ITALIC:url] that will contain the URL for the callback.
This part is pretty normal, most of the code is probably already familiar to you:
```python
class Invoice(BaseModel):
id: str
title: str | None = None
customer: str
total: float
def create]invoice(invoice: Invoice, callback[ITALIC:url: HttpUrl | None = None):
"""
Create an invoice.

This will (let's imagine) let the API user (some external developer) create an
invoice.

And this path operation will:

[ITALIC: Send the invoice to the client.
] Collect the money from the client.
[ITALIC: Send a notification back to the API user (the external developer), as a callback.
] At this point is that the API will somehow send a POST request to the
external API with the notification of the invoice event
(e.g. "payment successful").
"""
# Send the invoice, collect the money, send the notification (the callback)
return {"msg": "Invoice received"}
```
/// tip
The [CODE: callback]url] query parameter uses a Pydantic Url type.
///
The only new thing is the [CODE: callbacks=invoices[ITALIC:callback]router.routes] as an argument to the [ITALIC:path operation decorator]. We'll see what that is next.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/openapi-callbacks.md

[Context: OpenAPI Callbacks > The normal **FastAPI** app]

This part is pretty normal, most of the code is probably already familiar to you:
```python

id: str

customer: str
total: float

invoice.

And this path operation will:

[ITALIC: Send the invoice to the client.

external API with the notification of the invoice event
(e.g. "payment successful").
```

/// tip
The [CODE: callback]url] query parameter uses a Pydantic Url type.

page_content:
[Context: OpenAPI Callbacks > Documenting the callback]

## Documenting the callback
The actual callback code will depend heavily on your own API app.
And it will probably vary a lot from one app to the next.
It could be just one or two lines of code, like:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: callback_url = "https://example.com/api/v1/invoice...]
But possibly the most important part of the callback is making sure that your API user (the external developer) implements the [ITALIC:external API] correctly, according to the data that [ITALIC:your API] is going to send in the request body of the callback, etc.
So, what we will do next is add the code to document how that [ITALIC:external API] should look like to receive the callback from [ITALIC:your API].
That documentation will show up in the Swagger UI at [CODE: /docs] in your API, and it will let external developers know how to build the [ITALIC:external API].
This example doesn't implement the callback itself (that could be just a line of code), only the documentation part.
/// tip
The actual callback is just an HTTP request.
When implementing the callback yourself, you could use something like HTTPX or Requests.
///

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/openapi-callbacks.md

page_content:
[Context: OpenAPI Callbacks > Documenting the callback]

## Documenting the callback
The actual callback code will depend heavily on your own API app.
And it will probably vary a lot from one app to the next.
It could be just one or two lines of code, like:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: callback_url = "https://example.com/api/v1/invoice...]
But possibly the most important part of the callback is making sure that your API user (the external developer) implements the [ITALIC:external API] correctly, according to the data that [ITALIC:your API] is going to send in the request body of the callback, etc.
So, what we will do next is add the code to document how that [ITALIC:external API] should look like to receive the callback from [ITALIC:your API].
That documentation will show up in the Swagger UI at [CODE: /docs] in your API, and it will let external developers know how to build the [ITALIC:external API].
This example doesn't implement the callback itself (that could be just a line of code), only the documentation part.
/// tip
The actual callback is just an HTTP request.
When implementing the callback yourself, you could use something like HTTPX or Requests.
///

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/openapi-callbacks.md

page_content:
[Context: OpenAPI Callbacks > Write the callback documentation code]

## Write the callback documentation code
This code won't be executed in your app, we only need it to [ITALIC:document] how that [ITALIC:external API] should look like.
But, you already know how to easily create automatic documentation for an API with [BOLD:FastAPI].
So we are going to use that same knowledge to document how the [ITALIC:external API] should look like... by creating the [ITALIC:path operation(s)] that the external API should implement (the ones your API will call).
/// tip
When writing the code to document a callback, it might be useful to imagine that you are that [ITALIC:external developer]. And that you are currently implementing the [ITALIC:external API], not [ITALIC:your API].
Temporarily adopting this point of view (of the [ITALIC:external developer]) can help you feel like it's more obvious where to put the parameters, the Pydantic model for the body, for the response, etc. for that [ITALIC:external API].
///

metadata.breadcrumb: OpenAPI Callbacks > Write the callback documentation code
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/openapi-callbacks.md

page_content:
[Context: OpenAPI Callbacks > Write the callback documentation code > Create a callback `APIRouter`]

### Create a callback [CODE: APIRouter]
First create a new [CODE: APIRouter] that will contain one or more callbacks.
```python
from fastapi import APIRouter, FastAPI
from pydantic import BaseModel, HttpUrl
invoices[ITALIC:callback]router = APIRouter()

```

metadata.breadcrumb: OpenAPI Callbacks > Write the callback documentation code > Create a callback `APIRouter`
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/openapi-callbacks.md

metadata.breadcrumb: OpenAPI Callbacks > Write the callback documentation code > Create the callback *path operation*
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/openapi-callbacks.md

metadata.breadcrumb: OpenAPI Callbacks > Write the callback documentation code > Create the callback *path operation*
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/openapi-callbacks.md

page_content:
[Context: OpenAPI Callbacks > Write the callback documentation code > The callback path expression]

### The callback path expression
The callback [ITALIC:path] can have an OpenAPI 3 expression that can contain parts of the original request sent to [ITALIC:your API].
In this case, it's the [CODE: str]:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: "{$callback[ITALIC:url}/invoices/{$request.body.id}"...]
So, if your API user (the external developer) sends a request to [ITALIC:your API] to:
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:1: https://yourapi.com/invoices/?callback]url=https:/...]
with a JSON body of:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]2: { "id": "2expen51ve", "customer": "Mr. Richie Rich...]
then [ITALIC:your API] will process the invoice, and at some point later, send a callback request to the [CODE: callback[ITALIC:url] (the [ITALIC:external API]):
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:3: https://www.external.org/events/invoices/2expen51v...]
with a JSON body containing something like:
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:4: { "description": "Payment celebration", "paid": tr...]
and it would expect a response from that [ITALIC:external API] with a JSON body like:
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:5: { "ok": true }...]
/// tip
Notice how the callback URL used contains the URL received as a query parameter in [CODE: callback]url] ([CODE: https://www.external.org/events]) and also the invoice [CODE: id] from inside of the JSON body ([CODE: 2expen51ve]).
///

metadata.breadcrumb: OpenAPI Callbacks > Write the callback documentation code > The callback path expression
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/openapi-callbacks.md

Now you can start your app and go to http://127.0.0.1:8000/docs.

page_content:
[Context: OpenAPI Webhooks]

# OpenAPI Webhooks
There are cases where you want to tell your API [BOLD:users] that your app could call [ITALIC:their] app (sending a request) with some data, normally to [BOLD:notify] of some type of [BOLD:event].
This means that instead of the normal process of your users sending requests to your API, it's [BOLD:your API] (or your app) that could [BOLD:send requests to their system] (to their API, their app).
This is normally called a [BOLD:webhook].

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/openapi-webhooks.md

page_content:
[Context: OpenAPI Webhooks > Webhooks steps]

## Webhooks steps
The process normally is that [BOLD:you define] in your code what is the message that you will send, the [BOLD:body of the request].
You also define in some way at which [BOLD:moments] your app will send those requests or events.
And [BOLD:your users] define in some way (for example in a web dashboard somewhere) the [BOLD:URL] where your app should send those requests.
All the [BOLD:logic] about how to register the URLs for webhooks and the code to actually send those requests is up to you. You write it however you want to in [BOLD:your own code].

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/openapi-webhooks.md

page_content:
[Context: OpenAPI Webhooks > Documenting webhooks with **FastAPI** and OpenAPI]

## Documenting webhooks with [BOLD:FastAPI] and OpenAPI
With [BOLD:FastAPI], using OpenAPI, you can define the names of these webhooks, the types of HTTP operations that your app can send (e.g. [CODE: POST], [CODE: PUT], etc.) and the request [BOLD:bodies] that your app would send.
This can make it a lot easier for your users to [BOLD:implement their APIs] to receive your [BOLD:webhook] requests, they might even be able to autogenerate some of their own API code.
/// info
Webhooks are available in OpenAPI 3.1.0 and above, supported by FastAPI [CODE: 0.99.0] and above.
///

metadata.breadcrumb: OpenAPI Webhooks > Documenting webhooks with **FastAPI** and OpenAPI
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/openapi-webhooks.md

page_content:
[Context: OpenAPI Webhooks > An app with webhooks]

## An app with webhooks
When you create a [BOLD:FastAPI] application, there is a [CODE: webhooks] attribute that you can use to define [ITALIC:webhooks], the same way you would define [ITALIC:path operations], for example with [CODE: @app.webhooks.post()].
```python
class Subscription(BaseModel):
username: str
monthly[ITALIC:fee: float
start]date: datetime
@app.webhooks.post("new-subscription")
def new_subscription(body: Subscription):
"""
When a new user subscribes to your service we'll send you a POST request with this
data to the URL that you register for the event [CODE: new-subscription] in the dashboard.
"""
```
The webhooks that you define will end up in the [BOLD:OpenAPI] schema and the automatic [BOLD:docs UI].
/// info
The [CODE: app.webhooks] object is actually just an [CODE: APIRouter], the same type you would use when structuring your app with multiple files.
///
Notice that with webhooks you are actually not declaring a [ITALIC:path] (like [CODE: /items/]), the text you pass there is just an [BOLD:identifier] of the webhook (the name of the event), for example in [CODE: @app.webhooks.post("new-subscription")], the webhook name is [CODE: new-subscription].
This is because it is expected that [BOLD:your users] would define the actual [BOLD:URL path] where they want to receive the webhook request in some other way (e.g. a web dashboard).

metadata.breadcrumb: OpenAPI Webhooks > An app with webhooks
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/openapi-webhooks.md

page_content:
[Context: OpenAPI Webhooks > An app with webhooks]

page_content:
[Context: Path Operation Advanced Configuration > OpenAPI operationId]

# Path Operation Advanced Configuration
## OpenAPI operationId
/// warning
If you are not an "expert" in OpenAPI, you probably don't need this.
///
You can set the OpenAPI [CODE: operationId] to be used in your [ITALIC:path operation] with the parameter [CODE: operation[ITALIC:id].
You would have to make sure that it is unique for each operation.
```python
@app.get("/items/", operation]id="some[ITALIC:specific]id[ITALIC:you]define")
async def read_items():
```

metadata.breadcrumb: Path Operation Advanced Configuration > OpenAPI operationId
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/path-operation-advanced-configuration.md

page_content:
[Context: Path Operation Advanced Configuration > OpenAPI operationId > Using the *path operation function* name as the operationId]

### Using the [ITALIC:path operation function] name as the operationId
If you want to use your APIs' function names as [CODE: operationId]s, you can iterate over all of them and override each [ITALIC:path operation's] [CODE: operation[ITALIC:id] using their [CODE: APIRoute.name].
You should do it after adding all your [ITALIC:path operations].
```python
from fastapi.routing import APIRoute

def use]route[ITALIC:names]as[ITALIC:operation]ids(app: FastAPI) -> None:
"""
Simplify operation IDs so that generated API clients have simpler function
names.

Should be called only after all routes have been added.
"""
for route in app.routes:
if isinstance(route, APIRoute):
route.operation[ITALIC:id = route.name # in this case, 'read]items'
use[ITALIC:route]names[ITALIC:as]operation_ids(app)
```
/// tip
If you manually call [CODE: app.openapi()], you should update the [CODE: operationId]s before that.
///
/// warning
If you do this, you have to make sure each one of your [ITALIC:path operation functions] has a unique name.
Even if they are in different modules (Python files).
///

hierarchy: Using the *path operation function* name as the operationId
metadata.breadcrumb: Path Operation Advanced Configuration > OpenAPI operationId > Using the *path operation function* name as the operationId
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/path-operation-advanced-configuration.md

page_content:
[Context: Path Operation Advanced Configuration > OpenAPI operationId > Using the *path operation function* name as the operationId]

### Using the [ITALIC:path operation function] name as the operationId
If you want to use your APIs' function names as [CODE: operationId]s, you can iterate over all of them and override each [ITALIC:path operation's] [CODE: operation[ITALIC:id] using their [CODE: APIRoute.name].
You should do it after adding all your [ITALIC:path operations].
```python
from fastapi.routing import APIRoute

def use]route[ITALIC:names]as[ITALIC:operation]ids(app: FastAPI) -> None:
"""
Simplify operation IDs so that generated API clients have simpler function
names.

Should be called only after all routes have been added.
"""
for route in app.routes:
if isinstance(route, APIRoute):
route.operation[ITALIC:id = route.name # in this case, 'read]items'
use[ITALIC:route]names[ITALIC:as]operation_ids(app)
```
/// tip
If you manually call [CODE: app.openapi()], you should update the [CODE: operationId]s before that.
///
/// warning
If you do this, you have to make sure each one of your [ITALIC:path operation functions] has a unique name.
Even if they are in different modules (Python files).
///

hierarchy: Using the *path operation function* name as the operationId
metadata.breadcrumb: Path Operation Advanced Configuration > OpenAPI operationId > Using the *path operation function* name as the operationId
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/path-operation-advanced-configuration.md

page_content:
[Context: Path Operation Advanced Configuration > Exclude from OpenAPI]

## Exclude from OpenAPI
To exclude a [ITALIC:path operation] from the generated OpenAPI schema (and thus, from the automatic documentation systems), use the parameter [CODE: include[ITALIC:in]schema] and set it to [CODE: False]:
```python
@app.get("/items/", include[ITALIC:in]schema=False)
async def read_items():
```

metadata.breadcrumb: Path Operation Advanced Configuration > Exclude from OpenAPI
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/path-operation-advanced-configuration.md

page_content:
[Context: Path Operation Advanced Configuration > Advanced description from docstring]

## Advanced description from docstring
You can limit the lines used from the docstring of a [ITALIC:path operation function] for OpenAPI.
Adding an [CODE: \f] (an escaped "form feed" character) causes [BOLD:FastAPI] to truncate the output used for OpenAPI at this point.
It won't show up in the documentation, but other tools (such as Sphinx) will be able to use the rest.
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: """ Create an item with all the information: - **n...]

metadata.breadcrumb: Path Operation Advanced Configuration > Advanced description from docstring
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/path-operation-advanced-configuration.md

page_content:
[Context: Path Operation Advanced Configuration > Additional Responses]

## Additional Responses
You probably have seen how to declare the [CODE: response[ITALIC:model] and [CODE: status]code] for a [ITALIC:path operation].
That defines the metadata about the main response of a [ITALIC:path operation].
You can also declare additional responses with their models, status codes, etc.
There's a whole chapter here in the documentation about it, you can read it at Additional Responses in OpenAPI.

metadata.breadcrumb: Path Operation Advanced Configuration > Additional Responses
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/path-operation-advanced-configuration.md

page_content:
[Context: Path Operation Advanced Configuration > OpenAPI Extra]

## OpenAPI Extra
When you declare a [ITALIC:path operation] in your application, [BOLD:FastAPI] automatically generates the relevant metadata about that [ITALIC:path operation] to be included in the OpenAPI schema.
/// note | Technical details
In the OpenAPI specification it is called the Operation Object.
///
It has all the information about the [ITALIC:path operation] and is used to generate the automatic documentation.
It includes the [CODE: tags], [CODE: parameters], [CODE: requestBody], [CODE: responses], etc.
This [ITALIC:path operation]-specific OpenAPI schema is normally generated automatically by [BOLD:FastAPI], but you can also extend it.
/// tip
This is a low level extension point.
If you only need to declare additional responses, a more convenient way to do it is with Additional Responses in OpenAPI.
///
You can extend the OpenAPI schema for a [ITALIC:path operation] using the parameter [CODE: openapi_extra].

metadata.breadcrumb: Path Operation Advanced Configuration > OpenAPI Extra
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/path-operation-advanced-configuration.md

metadata.breadcrumb: Path Operation Advanced Configuration > OpenAPI Extra > OpenAPI Extensions
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/path-operation-advanced-configuration.md

page_content:
[Context: Path Operation Advanced Configuration > OpenAPI Extra > Custom OpenAPI *path operation* schema]

### Custom OpenAPI [ITALIC:path operation] schema
The dictionary in [CODE: openapi[ITALIC:extra] will be deeply merged with the automatically generated OpenAPI schema for the [ITALIC:path operation].
So, you could add additional data to the automatically generated schema.
For example, you could decide to read and validate the request with your own code, without using the automatic features of FastAPI with Pydantic, but you could still want to define the request in the OpenAPI schema.
You could do that with [CODE: openapi]extra]:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: openapi[ITALIC:extra={ "requestBody": { "content": { "app...]
In this example, we didn't declare any Pydantic model. In fact, the request body is not even parsed as JSON, it is read directly as [CODE: bytes], and the function [CODE: magic]data_reader()] would be in charge of parsing it in some way.
Nevertheless, we can declare the expected schema for the request body.

metadata.breadcrumb: Path Operation Advanced Configuration > OpenAPI Extra > Custom OpenAPI *path operation* schema
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/path-operation-advanced-configuration.md

page_content:
[Context: Path Operation Advanced Configuration > OpenAPI Extra > Custom OpenAPI *path operation* schema]

### Custom OpenAPI [ITALIC:path operation] schema
The dictionary in [CODE: openapi[ITALIC:extra] will be deeply merged with the automatically generated OpenAPI schema for the [ITALIC:path operation].
So, you could add additional data to the automatically generated schema.
For example, you could decide to read and validate the request with your own code, without using the automatic features of FastAPI with Pydantic, but you could still want to define the request in the OpenAPI schema.
You could do that with [CODE: openapi]extra]:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: openapi[ITALIC:extra={ "requestBody": { "content": { "app...]
In this example, we didn't declare any Pydantic model. In fact, the request body is not even parsed as JSON, it is read directly as [CODE: bytes], and the function [CODE: magic]data_reader()] would be in charge of parsing it in some way.
Nevertheless, we can declare the expected schema for the request body.

metadata.breadcrumb: Path Operation Advanced Configuration > OpenAPI Extra > Custom OpenAPI *path operation* schema
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/path-operation-advanced-configuration.md

page_content:
[Context: Path Operation Advanced Configuration > OpenAPI Extra > Custom OpenAPI content type]

### Custom OpenAPI content type
Using this same trick, you could use a Pydantic model to define the JSON Schema that is then included in the custom OpenAPI schema section for the [ITALIC:path operation].
And you could do this even if the data type in the request is not JSON.
For example, in this application we don't use FastAPI's integrated functionality to extract the JSON Schema from Pydantic models nor the automatic validation for JSON. In fact, we are declaring the request content type as YAML, not JSON:
```python
openapi[ITALIC:extra={
"requestBody": {
"content": {"application/x-yaml": {"schema": Item.model]json[ITALIC:schema()}},
"required": True,
},
},
async def create]item(request: Request):
raw[ITALIC:body = await request.body()
```
Nevertheless, although we are not using the default integrated functionality, we are still using a Pydantic model to manually generate the JSON Schema for the data that we want to receive in YAML.
Then we use the request directly, and extract the body as [CODE: bytes]. This means that FastAPI won't even try to parse the request payload as JSON.
And then in our code, we parse that YAML content directly, and then we are again using the same Pydantic model to validate the YAML content:
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:0: try: data = yaml.safe]load(raw_body) except yaml.Y...]
/// tip
Here we reuse the same Pydantic model.
But the same way, we could have validated it in some other way.
///

metadata.breadcrumb: Path Operation Advanced Configuration > OpenAPI Extra > Custom OpenAPI content type
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/path-operation-advanced-configuration.md

page_content:
[Context: Path Operation Advanced Configuration > OpenAPI Extra > Custom OpenAPI content type]

### Custom OpenAPI content type
Using this same trick, you could use a Pydantic model to define the JSON Schema that is then included in the custom OpenAPI schema section for the [ITALIC:path operation].
And you could do this even if the data type in the request is not JSON.
For example, in this application we don't use FastAPI's integrated functionality to extract the JSON Schema from Pydantic models nor the automatic validation for JSON. In fact, we are declaring the request content type as YAML, not JSON:
```python
openapi[ITALIC:extra={
"requestBody": {
"content": {"application/x-yaml": {"schema": Item.model]json[ITALIC:schema()}},
"required": True,
},
},
async def create]item(request: Request):
raw[ITALIC:body = await request.body()
```
Nevertheless, although we are not using the default integrated functionality, we are still using a Pydantic model to manually generate the JSON Schema for the data that we want to receive in YAML.
Then we use the request directly, and extract the body as [CODE: bytes]. This means that FastAPI won't even try to parse the request payload as JSON.
And then in our code, we parse that YAML content directly, and then we are again using the same Pydantic model to validate the YAML content:
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:0: try: data = yaml.safe]load(raw_body) except yaml.Y...]
/// tip
Here we reuse the same Pydantic model.
But the same way, we could have validated it in some other way.
///

metadata.breadcrumb: Path Operation Advanced Configuration > OpenAPI Extra > Custom OpenAPI content type
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/path-operation-advanced-configuration.md

page_content:
[Context: Response - Change Status Code]

# Response - Change Status Code
You probably read before that you can set a default Response Status Code.
But in some cases you need to return a different status code than the default.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/response-change-status-code.md

page_content:
[Context: Response - Change Status Code > Use case]

## Use case
For example, imagine that you want to return an HTTP status code of "OK" [CODE: 200] by default.
But if the data didn't exist, you want to create it, and return an HTTP status code of "CREATED" [CODE: 201].
But you still want to be able to filter and convert the data you return with a [CODE: response_model].
For those cases, you can use a [CODE: Response] parameter.

metadata.breadcrumb: Response - Change Status Code > Use case
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/response-change-status-code.md

page_content:
[Context: Response - Change Status Code > Use a `Response` parameter]

## Use a [CODE: Response] parameter
You can declare a parameter of type [CODE: Response] in your [ITALIC:path operation function] (as you can do for cookies and headers).
And then you can set the [CODE: status[ITALIC:code] in that [ITALIC:temporal] response object.
```python
from fastapi import FastAPI, Response, status

def get]or[ITALIC:create]task(task[ITALIC:id: str, response: Response):
if task]id not in tasks:
response.status[ITALIC:code = status.HTTP]201[ITALIC:CREATED
return tasks[task]id]
```
And then you can return any object you need, as you normally would (a [CODE: dict], a database model, etc).
And if you declared a [CODE: response[ITALIC:model], it will still be used to filter and convert the object you returned.
[BOLD:FastAPI] will use that [ITALIC:temporal] response to extract the status code (also cookies and headers), and will put them in the final response that contains the value you returned, filtered by any [CODE: response]model].
You can also declare the [CODE: Response] parameter in dependencies, and set the status code in them. But keep in mind that the last one to be set will win.

metadata.breadcrumb: Response - Change Status Code > Use a `Response` parameter
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/response-change-status-code.md

page_content:
[Context: Response - Change Status Code > Use a `Response` parameter]
