```python
from fastapi import FastAPI, Response, status

def get]or[ITALIC:create]task(task[ITALIC:id: str, response: Response):
if task]id not in tasks:
response.status[ITALIC:code = status.HTTP]201[ITALIC:CREATED
return tasks[task]id]
```
And then you can return any object you need, as you normally would (a [CODE: dict], a database model, etc).
And if you declared a [CODE: response[ITALIC:model], it will still be used to filter and convert the object you returned.
[BOLD:FastAPI] will use that [ITALIC:temporal] response to extract the status code (also cookies and headers), and will put them in the final response that contains the value you returned, filtered by any [CODE: response]model].
You can also declare the [CODE: Response] parameter in dependencies, and set the status code in them. But keep in mind that the last one to be set will win.

metadata.breadcrumb: Response - Change Status Code > Use a `Response` parameter
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/response-change-status-code.md

page_content:
[Context: Response Cookies > Use a `Response` parameter]

# Response Cookies
## Use a [CODE: Response] parameter
You can declare a parameter of type [CODE: Response] in your [ITALIC:path operation function].
And then you can set cookies in that [ITALIC:temporal] response object.
```python
from fastapi import FastAPI, Response

response.set[ITALIC:cookie(key="fakesession", value="fake-cookie-session-value")
return {"message": "Come to the dark side, we have cookies"}
```
And then you can return any object you need, as you normally would (a [CODE: dict], a database model, etc).
And if you declared a [CODE: response]model], it will still be used to filter and convert the object you returned.
[BOLD:FastAPI] will use that [ITALIC:temporal] response to extract the cookies (also headers and status code), and will put them in the final response that contains the value you returned, filtered by any [CODE: response_model].
You can also declare the [CODE: Response] parameter in dependencies, and set cookies (and headers) in them.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/response-cookies.md

page_content:
[Context: Response Cookies > Use a `Response` parameter]

# Response Cookies
## Use a [CODE: Response] parameter
You can declare a parameter of type [CODE: Response] in your [ITALIC:path operation function].
And then you can set cookies in that [ITALIC:temporal] response object.
```python
from fastapi import FastAPI, Response

response.set[ITALIC:cookie(key="fakesession", value="fake-cookie-session-value")
return {"message": "Come to the dark side, we have cookies"}
```
And then you can return any object you need, as you normally would (a [CODE: dict], a database model, etc).
And if you declared a [CODE: response]model], it will still be used to filter and convert the object you returned.
[BOLD:FastAPI] will use that [ITALIC:temporal] response to extract the cookies (also headers and status code), and will put them in the final response that contains the value you returned, filtered by any [CODE: response_model].
You can also declare the [CODE: Response] parameter in dependencies, and set cookies (and headers) in them.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/response-cookies.md

page_content:
[Context: Response Cookies > Return a `Response` directly]

## Return a [CODE: Response] directly
You can also create cookies when returning a [CODE: Response] directly in your code.
To do that, you can create a response as described in Return a Response Directly.
Then set Cookies in it, and then return it:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: response = JSONResponse(content=content) response....]
/// tip
Keep in mind that if you return a response directly instead of using the [CODE: Response] parameter, FastAPI will return it directly.
So, you will have to make sure your data is of the correct type. E.g. it is compatible with JSON, if you are returning a [CODE: JSONResponse].
And also that you are not sending any data that should have been filtered by a [CODE: response_model].
///

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/response-cookies.md

page_content:
[Context: Response Cookies > Return a `Response` directly > More info]

### More info
/// note | Technical Details
You could also use [CODE: from starlette.responses import Response] or [CODE: from starlette.responses import JSONResponse].
[BOLD:FastAPI] provides the same [CODE: starlette.responses] as [CODE: fastapi.responses] just as a convenience for you, the developer. But most of the available responses come directly from Starlette.
And as the [CODE: Response] can be used frequently to set headers and cookies, [BOLD:FastAPI] also provides it at [CODE: fastapi.Response].
///
To see all the available parameters and options, check the documentation in Starlette.

metadata.breadcrumb: Response Cookies > Return a `Response` directly > More info
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/response-cookies.md

page_content:
[Context: Return a Response Directly]

# Return a Response Directly
When you create a [BOLD:FastAPI] [ITALIC:path operation] you can normally return any data from it: a [CODE: dict], a [CODE: list], a Pydantic model, a database model, etc.
If you declare a Response Model FastAPI will use it to serialize the data to JSON, using Pydantic.
If you don't declare a response model, FastAPI will use the [CODE: jsonable_encoder] explained in JSON Compatible Encoder and put it in a [CODE: JSONResponse].
You could also create a [CODE: JSONResponse] directly and return it.
/// tip
You will normally have much better performance using a Response Model than returning a [CODE: JSONResponse] directly, as that way it serializes the data using Pydantic, in Rust.
///

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/response-directly.md

page_content:
[Context: Return a Response Directly > Return a `Response`]

## Return a [CODE: Response]
You can return a [CODE: Response] or any sub-class of it.
/// info
[CODE: JSONResponse] itself is a sub-class of [CODE: Response].
///
And when you return a [CODE: Response], [BOLD:FastAPI] will pass it directly.
It won't do any data conversion with Pydantic models, it won't convert the contents to any type, etc.
This gives you a lot of [BOLD:flexibility]. You can return any data type, override any data declaration or validation, etc.
It also gives you a lot of [BOLD:responsibility]. You have to make sure that the data you return is correct, in the correct format, that it can be serialized, etc.

metadata.breadcrumb: Return a Response Directly > Return a `Response`
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/response-directly.md

page_content:
[Context: Return a Response Directly > Using the `jsonable_encoder` in a `Response`]

## Using the [CODE: jsonable[ITALIC:encoder] in a [CODE: Response]
Because [BOLD:FastAPI] doesn't make any changes to a [CODE: Response] you return, you have to make sure its contents are ready for it.
For example, you cannot put a Pydantic model in a [CODE: JSONResponse] without first converting it to a [CODE: dict] with all the data types (like [CODE: datetime], [CODE: UUID], etc) converted to JSON-compatible types.
For those cases, you can use the [CODE: jsonable]encoder] to convert your data before passing it to a response:
```python
from fastapi.responses import JSONResponse
from pydantic import BaseModel
json[ITALIC:compatible]item[ITALIC:data = jsonable]encoder(item)
return JSONResponse(content=json[ITALIC:compatible]item_data)
```
/// note | Technical Details
You could also use [CODE: from starlette.responses import JSONResponse].
[BOLD:FastAPI] provides the same [CODE: starlette.responses] as [CODE: fastapi.responses] just as a convenience for you, the developer. But most of the available responses come directly from Starlette.
///

metadata.breadcrumb: Return a Response Directly > Using the `jsonable_encoder` in a `Response`
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/response-directly.md

page_content:
[Context: Return a Response Directly > Using the `jsonable_encoder` in a `Response`]

## Using the [CODE: jsonable[ITALIC:encoder] in a [CODE: Response]
Because [BOLD:FastAPI] doesn't make any changes to a [CODE: Response] you return, you have to make sure its contents are ready for it.
For example, you cannot put a Pydantic model in a [CODE: JSONResponse] without first converting it to a [CODE: dict] with all the data types (like [CODE: datetime], [CODE: UUID], etc) converted to JSON-compatible types.
For those cases, you can use the [CODE: jsonable]encoder] to convert your data before passing it to a response:
```python
from fastapi.responses import JSONResponse
from pydantic import BaseModel
json[ITALIC:compatible]item[ITALIC:data = jsonable]encoder(item)
return JSONResponse(content=json[ITALIC:compatible]item_data)
```
/// note | Technical Details
You could also use [CODE: from starlette.responses import JSONResponse].
[BOLD:FastAPI] provides the same [CODE: starlette.responses] as [CODE: fastapi.responses] just as a convenience for you, the developer. But most of the available responses come directly from Starlette.
///

metadata.breadcrumb: Return a Response Directly > Using the `jsonable_encoder` in a `Response`
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/response-directly.md

page_content:
[Context: Return a Response Directly > Returning a custom `Response`]

## Returning a custom [CODE: Response]
The example above shows all the parts you need, but it's not very useful yet, as you could have just returned the [CODE: item] directly, and [BOLD:FastAPI] would put it in a [CODE: JSONResponse] for you, converting it to a [CODE: dict], etc. All that by default.
Now, let's see how you could use that to return a custom response.
Let's say that you want to return an XML response.
You could put your XML content in a string, put that in a [CODE: Response], and return it:
```python
from fastapi import FastAPI, Response

return Response(content=data, media_type="application/xml")
```

metadata.breadcrumb: Return a Response Directly > Returning a custom `Response`
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/response-directly.md

page_content:
[Context: Return a Response Directly > How a Response Model Works]

## How a Response Model Works
When you declare a Response Model - Return Type in a path operation, [BOLD:FastAPI] will use it to serialize the data to JSON, using Pydantic.
```python
async def create[ITALIC:item(item: Item) -> Item:
return item
async def read]items() -> list[Item]:
return [
```
As that will happen on the Rust side, the performance will be much better than if it was done with regular Python and the [CODE: JSONResponse] class.
When using a [CODE: response[ITALIC:model] or return type, FastAPI won't use the [CODE: jsonable]encoder] to convert the data (which would be slower) nor the [CODE: JSONResponse] class.
Instead it takes the JSON bytes generated with Pydantic using the response model (or return type) and returns a [CODE: Response] with the right media type for JSON directly ([CODE: application/json]).

metadata.breadcrumb: Return a Response Directly > How a Response Model Works
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/response-directly.md

page_content:
[Context: Return a Response Directly > Notes]

## Notes
When you return a [CODE: Response] directly its data is not validated, converted (serialized), or documented automatically.
But you can still document it as described in Additional Responses in OpenAPI.
You can see in later sections how to use/declare these custom [CODE: Response]s while still having automatic data conversion, documentation, etc.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/response-directly.md

page_content:
[Context: Response Headers > Use a `Response` parameter]

# Response Headers
## Use a [CODE: Response] parameter
You can declare a parameter of type [CODE: Response] in your [ITALIC:path operation function] (as you can do for cookies).
And then you can set headers in that [ITALIC:temporal] response object.
```python
from fastapi import FastAPI, Response

def get[ITALIC:headers(response: Response):
response.headers["X-Cat-Dog"] = "alone in the world"
```
And then you can return any object you need, as you normally would (a [CODE: dict], a database model, etc).
And if you declared a [CODE: response]model], it will still be used to filter and convert the object you returned.
[BOLD:FastAPI] will use that [ITALIC:temporal] response to extract the headers (also cookies and status code), and will put them in the final response that contains the value you returned, filtered by any [CODE: response_model].
You can also declare the [CODE: Response] parameter in dependencies, and set headers (and cookies) in them.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/response-headers.md

page_content:
[Context: Response Headers > Use a `Response` parameter]

# Response Headers
## Use a [CODE: Response] parameter

```python

def get[ITALIC:headers(response: Response):
response.headers["X-Cat-Dog"] = "alone in the world"
```
And then you can return any object you need, as you normally would (a [CODE: dict], a database model, etc).
And if you declared a [CODE: response]model], it will still be used to filter and convert the object you returned.
[BOLD:FastAPI] will use that [ITALIC:temporal] response to extract the headers (also cookies and status code), and will put them in the final response that contains the value you returned, filtered by any [CODE: response_model].
You can also declare the [CODE: Response] parameter in dependencies, and set headers (and cookies) in them.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/response-headers.md

page_content:
[Context: Response Headers > Return a `Response` directly]

## Return a [CODE: Response] directly
You can also add headers when you return a [CODE: Response] directly.
Create a response as described in Return a Response Directly and pass the headers as an additional parameter:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: headers = {"X-Cat-Dog": "alone in the world", "Con...]
/// note | Technical Details
You could also use [CODE: from starlette.responses import Response] or [CODE: from starlette.responses import JSONResponse].
[BOLD:FastAPI] provides the same [CODE: starlette.responses] as [CODE: fastapi.responses] just as a convenience for you, the developer. But most of the available responses come directly from Starlette.
And as the [CODE: Response] can be used frequently to set headers and cookies, [BOLD:FastAPI] also provides it at [CODE: fastapi.Response].
///

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/response-headers.md

page_content:
[Context: Response Headers > Custom Headers]

## Custom Headers
Keep in mind that custom proprietary headers can be added [using the [CODE: X-] prefix](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Headers).
But if you have custom headers that you want a client in a browser to be able to see, you need to add them to your CORS configurations (read more in CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing)), using the parameter [CODE: expose_headers] documented in Starlette's CORS docs.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/response-headers.md

page_content:
[Context: Settings and Environment Variables]

# Settings and Environment Variables
In many cases your application could need some external settings or configurations, for example secret keys, database credentials, credentials for email services, etc.
Most of these settings are variable (can change), like database URLs. And many could be sensitive, like secrets.
For this reason it's common to provide them in environment variables that are read by the application.
/// tip
To understand environment variables you can read Environment Variables.
///

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/settings.md

page_content:
[Context: Settings and Environment Variables > Types and validation]

## Types and validation
These environment variables can only handle text strings, as they are external to Python and have to be compatible with other programs and the rest of the system (and even with different operating systems, as Linux, Windows, macOS).
That means that any value read in Python from an environment variable will be a [CODE: str], and any conversion to a different type or any validation has to be done in code.

metadata.breadcrumb: Settings and Environment Variables > Types and validation
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/settings.md

page_content:
[Context: Settings and Environment Variables > Pydantic `Settings`]

## Pydantic [CODE: Settings]
Fortunately, Pydantic provides a great utility to handle these settings coming from environment variables with Pydantic: Settings management.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/settings.md

page_content:
[Context: Settings and Environment Variables > Pydantic `Settings` > Install `pydantic-settings`]

### Install [CODE: pydantic-settings]
First, make sure you create your virtual environment, activate it, and then install the [CODE: pydantic-settings] package:

[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: $ pip install pydantic-settings ---> 100%...]

It also comes included when you install the [CODE: all] extras with:

[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]1: $ pip install "fastapi[all]" ---> 100%...]

metadata.breadcrumb: Settings and Environment Variables > Pydantic `Settings` > Install `pydantic-settings`
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/settings.md

page_content:
[Context: Settings and Environment Variables > Pydantic `Settings` > Create the `Settings` object]

### Create the [CODE: Settings] object
Import [CODE: BaseSettings] from Pydantic and create a sub-class, very much like with a Pydantic model.
The same way as with Pydantic models, you declare class attributes with type annotations, and possibly default values.
You can use all the same validation features and tools you use for Pydantic models, like different data types and additional validations with [CODE: Field()].
```python
from pydantic[ITALIC:settings import BaseSettings

class Settings(BaseSettings):
app]name: str = "Awesome API"
admin[ITALIC:email: str
items]per[ITALIC:user: int = 50
settings = Settings()
app = FastAPI()
```
/// tip
If you want something quick to copy and paste, don't use this example, use the last one below.
///
Then, when you create an instance of that [CODE: Settings] class (in this case, in the [CODE: settings] object), Pydantic will read the environment variables in a case-insensitive way, so, an upper-case variable [CODE: APP]NAME] will still be read for the attribute [CODE: app[ITALIC:name].
Next it will convert and validate the data. So, when you use that [CODE: settings] object, you will have data of the types you declared (e.g. [CODE: items]per_user] will be an [CODE: int]).

metadata.breadcrumb: Settings and Environment Variables > Pydantic `Settings` > Create the `Settings` object
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/settings.md

page_content:
[Context: Settings and Environment Variables > Pydantic `Settings` > Create the `Settings` object]

### Create the [CODE: Settings] object
Import [CODE: BaseSettings] from Pydantic and create a sub-class, very much like with a Pydantic model.
The same way as with Pydantic models, you declare class attributes with type annotations, and possibly default values.
You can use all the same validation features and tools you use for Pydantic models, like different data types and additional validations with [CODE: Field()].
```python
from pydantic[ITALIC:settings import BaseSettings

class Settings(BaseSettings):
app]name: str = "Awesome API"
admin[ITALIC:email: str
items]per[ITALIC:user: int = 50
settings = Settings()
app = FastAPI()
```
/// tip
If you want something quick to copy and paste, don't use this example, use the last one below.
///
Then, when you create an instance of that [CODE: Settings] class (in this case, in the [CODE: settings] object), Pydantic will read the environment variables in a case-insensitive way, so, an upper-case variable [CODE: APP]NAME] will still be read for the attribute [CODE: app[ITALIC:name].
Next it will convert and validate the data. So, when you use that [CODE: settings] object, you will have data of the types you declared (e.g. [CODE: items]per_user] will be an [CODE: int]).

metadata.breadcrumb: Settings and Environment Variables > Pydantic `Settings` > Create the `Settings` object
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/settings.md

page_content:
[Context: Settings and Environment Variables > Pydantic `Settings` > Use the `settings`]

### Use the [CODE: settings]
Then you can use the new [CODE: settings] object in your application:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: "app[ITALIC:name": settings.app]name, "admin_email": sett...]

metadata.breadcrumb: Settings and Environment Variables > Pydantic `Settings` > Use the `settings`
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/settings.md

page_content:
[Context: Settings and Environment Variables > Pydantic `Settings` > Run the server]

### Run the server
Next, you would run the server passing the configurations as environment variables, for example you could set an [CODE: ADMIN[ITALIC:EMAIL] and [CODE: APP]NAME] with:

[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: $ ADMIN[ITALIC:EMAIL="deadpool@example.com" APP]NAME="Chi...]

/// tip
To set multiple env vars for a single command just separate them with a space, and put them all before the command.
///
And then the [CODE: admin[ITALIC:email] setting would be set to [CODE: "deadpool@example.com"].
The [CODE: app]name] would be [CODE: "ChimichangApp"].
And the [CODE: items[ITALIC:per]user] would keep its default value of [CODE: 50].

metadata.breadcrumb: Settings and Environment Variables > Pydantic `Settings` > Run the server
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/settings.md

page_content:
[Context: Settings and Environment Variables > Settings in another module]

## Settings in another module
You could put those settings in another module file as you saw in Bigger Applications - Multiple Files.
For example, you could have a file [CODE: config.py] with:
{[ITALIC: ../../docs[ITALIC:src/settings/app01]py310/config.py ]}
And then use it in a file [CODE: main.py]:
```python
from .config import settings

"app[ITALIC:name": settings.app]name,
"admin[ITALIC:email": settings.admin]email,
"items[ITALIC:per]user": settings.items[ITALIC:per]user,
```
/// tip
You would also need a file [CODE: [BOLD:init].py] as you saw in Bigger Applications - Multiple Files.
///

metadata.breadcrumb: Settings and Environment Variables > Settings in another module
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/settings.md

page_content:
[Context: Settings and Environment Variables > Settings in a dependency]

## Settings in a dependency
In some occasions it might be useful to provide the settings from a dependency, instead of having a global object with [CODE: settings] that is used everywhere.
This could be especially useful during testing, as it's very easy to override a dependency with your own custom settings.

metadata.breadcrumb: Settings and Environment Variables > Settings in a dependency
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/settings.md

page_content:
[Context: Settings and Environment Variables > Settings in a dependency > The config file]

### The config file
Coming from the previous example, your [CODE: config.py] file could look like:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: ...]
Notice that now we don't create a default instance [CODE: settings = Settings()].

metadata.breadcrumb: Settings and Environment Variables > Settings in a dependency > The config file
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/settings.md

page_content:
[Context: Settings and Environment Variables > Settings in a dependency > The main app file]

### The main app file
Now we create a dependency that returns a new [CODE: config.Settings()].
```python
from .config import Settings

def get[ITALIC:settings():
return Settings()
```
/// tip
We'll discuss the [CODE: @lru]cache] in a bit.
For now you can assume [CODE: get[ITALIC:settings()] is a normal function.
///
And then we can require it from the [ITALIC:path operation function] as a dependency and use it anywhere we need it.
```python
async def info(settings: Annotated[Settings, Depends(get]settings)]):
return {
"app[ITALIC:name": settings.app]name,
"admin[ITALIC:email": settings.admin]email,
"items[ITALIC:per]user": settings.items[ITALIC:per]user,
```

metadata.breadcrumb: Settings and Environment Variables > Settings in a dependency > The main app file
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/settings.md

page_content:
[Context: Settings and Environment Variables > Settings in a dependency > Settings and testing]

### Settings and testing
Then it would be very easy to provide a different settings object during testing by creating a dependency override for [CODE: get[ITALIC:settings]:
```python
def get]settings[ITALIC:override():
return Settings(admin]email="testing[ITALIC:admin@example.com")
app.dependency]overrides[get[ITALIC:settings] = get]settings[ITALIC:override

"admin]email": "testing[ITALIC:admin@example.com",
"items]per[ITALIC:user": 50,
```
In the dependency override we set a new value for the [CODE: admin]email] when creating the new [CODE: Settings] object, and then we return that new object.
Then we can test that it is used.

metadata.breadcrumb: Settings and Environment Variables > Settings in a dependency > Settings and testing
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/settings.md

metadata.breadcrumb: Settings and Environment Variables > Reading a `.env` file
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/settings.md

page_content:
[Context: Settings and Environment Variables > Reading a `.env` file > The `.env` file]

### The [CODE: .env] file
You could have a [CODE: .env] file with:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: ADMIN[ITALIC:EMAIL="deadpool@example.com" APP]NAME="Chimi...]

metadata.breadcrumb: Settings and Environment Variables > Reading a `.env` file > The `.env` file
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/settings.md

page_content:
[Context: Settings and Environment Variables > Reading a `.env` file > Read settings from `.env`]

### Read settings from [CODE: .env]
And then update your [CODE: config.py] with:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: model[ITALIC:config = SettingsConfigDict(env]file=".env")...]
/// tip
The [CODE: model[ITALIC:config] attribute is used just for Pydantic configuration. You can read more at Pydantic: Concepts: Configuration.
///
Here we define the config [CODE: env]file] inside of your Pydantic [CODE: Settings] class, and set the value to the filename with the dotenv file we want to use.

metadata.breadcrumb: Settings and Environment Variables > Reading a `.env` file > Read settings from `.env`
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/settings.md

page_content:
[Context: Settings and Environment Variables > Reading a `.env` file > Creating the `Settings` only once with `lru_cache`]

### Creating the [CODE: Settings] only once with [CODE: lru[ITALIC:cache]
Reading a file from disk is normally a costly (slow) operation, so you probably want to do it only once and then reuse the same settings object, instead of reading it for each request.
But every time we do:
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:0: Settings()...]
a new [CODE: Settings] object would be created, and at creation it would read the [CODE: .env] file again.
If the dependency function was just like:
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:1: def get]settings(): return Settings()...]
we would create that object for each request, and we would be reading the [CODE: .env] file for each request.
But as we are using the [CODE: @lru[ITALIC:cache] decorator on top, the [CODE: Settings] object will be created only once, the first time it's called.
```python
from functools import lru]cache
from typing import Annotated
@lru[ITALIC:cache
def get]settings():
```
Then for any subsequent call of [CODE: get[ITALIC:settings()] in the dependencies for the next requests, instead of executing the internal code of [CODE: get]settings()] and creating a new [CODE: Settings] object, it will return the same object that was returned on the first call, again and again.
#### [CODE: lru[ITALIC:cache] Technical Details
[CODE: @lru]cache] modifies the function it decorates to return the same value that was returned the first time, instead of computing it again, executing the code of the function every time.
So, the function below it will be executed once for each combination of arguments. And then the values returned by each of those combinations of arguments will be used again and again whenever the function is called with exactly the same combination of arguments.
For example, if you have a function:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]2: @lru[ITALIC:cache def say]hi(name: str, salutation: str =...]
your program could execute like this:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]3: sequenceDiagram participant code as Code participa...]
In the case of our dependency [CODE: get[ITALIC:settings()], the function doesn't even take any arguments, so it always returns the same value.
That way, it behaves almost as if it was just a global variable. But as it uses a dependency function, then we can override it easily for testing.
[CODE: @lru]cache] is part of [CODE: functools] which is part of Python's standard library, you can read more about it in the [Python docs for [CODE: @lru[ITALIC:cache]](https://docs.python.org/3/library/functools.html#functools.lru]cache).

metadata.breadcrumb: Settings and Environment Variables > Reading a `.env` file > Creating the `Settings` only once with `lru_cache`
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/settings.md

page_content:
[Context: Settings and Environment Variables > Recap]

## Recap
You can use Pydantic Settings to handle the settings or configurations for your application, with all the power of Pydantic models.
[ITALIC: By using a dependency you can simplify testing.
] You can use [CODE: .env] files with it.
- Using [CODE: @lru_cache] lets you avoid reading the dotenv file again and again for each request, while allowing you to override it during testing.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/settings.md

page_content:
[Context: Stream Data]

# Stream Data
If you want to stream data that can be structured as JSON, you should Stream JSON Lines.
But if you want to [BOLD:stream pure binary data] or strings, here's how you can do it.
/// info
Added in FastAPI 0.134.0.
///

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/stream-data.md

page_content:
[Context: Stream Data > Use Cases]

## Use Cases
You could use this if you want to stream pure strings, for example directly from the output of an [BOLD:AI LLM] service.
You could also use it to stream [BOLD:large binary files], where you stream each chunk of data as you read it, without having to read it all in memory at once.
You could also stream [BOLD:video] or [BOLD:audio] this way, it could even be generated as you process and send it.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/stream-data.md

page_content:
[Context: Stream Data > A `StreamingResponse` with `yield`]

## A [CODE: StreamingResponse] with [CODE: yield]
If you declare a [CODE: response[ITALIC:class=StreamingResponse] in your [ITALIC:path operation function], you can use [CODE: yield] to send each chunk of data in turn.
```python
@app.get("/story/stream", response]class=StreamingResponse)
async def stream_story() -> AsyncIterable[str]:
yield line

```
FastAPI will give each chunk of data to the [CODE: StreamingResponse] as is, it won't try to convert it to JSON or anything similar.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/stream-data.md

page_content:
[Context: Stream Data > A `StreamingResponse` with `yield` > Non-async *path operation functions*]

### Non-async [ITALIC:path operation functions]
You can also use regular [CODE: def] functions (without [CODE: async]), and use [CODE: yield] the same way.
```python
def stream[ITALIC:story]no_async() -> Iterable[str]:
for line in message.splitlines():
```

metadata.breadcrumb: Stream Data > A `StreamingResponse` with `yield` > Non-async *path operation functions*
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/stream-data.md

page_content:
[Context: Stream Data > A `StreamingResponse` with `yield` > No Annotation]

### No Annotation
You don't really need to declare the return type annotation for streaming binary data.
As FastAPI will not try to convert the data to JSON with Pydantic or serialize it in any way, in this case, the type annotation is only for your editor and tools to use, it won't be used by FastAPI.
```python
async def stream[ITALIC:story]no_annotation():
for line in message.splitlines():
```
This also means that with [CODE: StreamingResponse] you have the [BOLD:freedom] and [BOLD:responsibility] to produce and encode the data bytes exactly as you need them to be sent, independent of the type annotations. 🤓

metadata.breadcrumb: Stream Data > A `StreamingResponse` with `yield` > No Annotation
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/stream-data.md

page_content:
[Context: Stream Data > A `StreamingResponse` with `yield` > Stream Bytes]

### Stream Bytes
One of the main use cases would be to stream [CODE: bytes] instead of strings, you can of course do it.
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: yield line.encode("utf-8")...]

metadata.breadcrumb: Stream Data > A `StreamingResponse` with `yield` > Stream Bytes
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/stream-data.md

page_content:
[Context: Stream Data > A Custom `PNGStreamingResponse`]

## A Custom [CODE: PNGStreamingResponse]
In the examples above, the data bytes were streamed, but the response didn't have a [CODE: Content-Type] header, so the client didn't know what type of data it was receiving.
You can create a custom sub-class of [CODE: StreamingResponse] that sets the [CODE: Content-Type] header to the type of data you're streaming.
For example, you can create a [CODE: PNGStreamingResponse] that sets the [CODE: Content-Type] header to [CODE: image/png] using the [CODE: media[ITALIC:type] attribute:
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:0: media]type = "image/png"...]
Then you can use this new class in [CODE: response[ITALIC:class=PNGStreamingResponse] in your [ITALIC:path operation function]:
```python
@app.get("/image/stream", response]class=PNGStreamingResponse)
async def stream_image() -> AsyncIterable[bytes]:
```

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/stream-data.md

page_content:
[Context: Stream Data > A Custom `PNGStreamingResponse` > Simulate a File]

### Simulate a File
In this example, we are simulating a file with [CODE: io.BytesIO], which is a file-like object that lives only in memory, but lets us use the same interface.
For example, we can iterate over it to consume its contents, as we could with a file.
```python
from io import BytesIO

def read[ITALIC:image() -> BytesIO:
return BytesIO(binary]image)
with read[ITALIC:image() as image]file:
for chunk in image[ITALIC:file:
```
/// note | Technical Details
The other two variables, [CODE: image]base64] and [CODE: binary_image], are an image encoded in Base64, and then converted to bytes, to then pass it to [CODE: io.BytesIO].
Only so that it can live in the same file for this example and you can copy it and run it as is. 🥚
///
By using a [CODE: with] block, we make sure that the file-like object is closed after the generator function (the function with [CODE: yield]) is done. So, after it finishes sending the response.
It wouldn't be that important in this specific example because it's a fake in-memory file (with [CODE: io.BytesIO]), but with a real file, it would be important to make sure the file is closed after the work with it is done.

metadata.breadcrumb: Stream Data > A Custom `PNGStreamingResponse` > Simulate a File
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/stream-data.md

page_content:
[Context: Stream Data > A Custom `PNGStreamingResponse` > Simulate a File]

### Simulate a File
In this example, we are simulating a file with [CODE: io.BytesIO], which is a file-like object that lives only in memory, but lets us use the same interface.
For example, we can iterate over it to consume its contents, as we could with a file.
```python
from io import BytesIO

def read[ITALIC:image() -> BytesIO:
return BytesIO(binary]image)
with read[ITALIC:image() as image]file:
for chunk in image[ITALIC:file:
```
/// note | Technical Details
The other two variables, [CODE: image]base64] and [CODE: binary_image], are an image encoded in Base64, and then converted to bytes, to then pass it to [CODE: io.BytesIO].
Only so that it can live in the same file for this example and you can copy it and run it as is. 🥚
///
By using a [CODE: with] block, we make sure that the file-like object is closed after the generator function (the function with [CODE: yield]) is done. So, after it finishes sending the response.
It wouldn't be that important in this specific example because it's a fake in-memory file (with [CODE: io.BytesIO]), but with a real file, it would be important to make sure the file is closed after the work with it is done.

metadata.breadcrumb: Stream Data > A Custom `PNGStreamingResponse` > Simulate a File
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/stream-data.md

page_content:
[Context: Stream Data > A Custom `PNGStreamingResponse` > Files and Async]

### Files and Async
In most cases, file-like objects are not compatible with async and await by default.
For example, they don't have an [CODE: await file.read()], or [CODE: async for chunk in file].
And in many cases, reading them would be a blocking operation (that could block the event loop), because they are read from disk or from the network.
/// info
The example above is actually an exception, because the [CODE: io.BytesIO] object is already in memory, so reading it won't block anything.
But in many cases reading a file or a file-like object would block.
///
To avoid blocking the event loop, you can simply declare the [ITALIC:path operation function] with regular [CODE: def] instead of [CODE: async def], that way FastAPI will run it on a threadpool worker, to avoid blocking the main loop.
```python
def stream[ITALIC:image]no[ITALIC:async() -> Iterable[bytes]:
with read]image() as image_file:
```
/// tip
If you need to call blocking code from inside of an async function, or an async function from inside of a blocking function, you could use Asyncer, a sibling library to FastAPI.
///

metadata.breadcrumb: Stream Data > A Custom `PNGStreamingResponse` > Files and Async
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/stream-data.md

page_content:
[Context: Stream Data > A Custom `PNGStreamingResponse` > Files and Async]

### Files and Async
In most cases, file-like objects are not compatible with async and await by default.
For example, they don't have an [CODE: await file.read()], or [CODE: async for chunk in file].
And in many cases, reading them would be a blocking operation (that could block the event loop), because they are read from disk or from the network.
/// info
The example above is actually an exception, because the [CODE: io.BytesIO] object is already in memory, so reading it won't block anything.
But in many cases reading a file or a file-like object would block.
///
To avoid blocking the event loop, you can simply declare the [ITALIC:path operation function] with regular [CODE: def] instead of [CODE: async def], that way FastAPI will run it on a threadpool worker, to avoid blocking the main loop.
```python
def stream[ITALIC:image]no[ITALIC:async() -> Iterable[bytes]:
with read]image() as image_file:
```
/// tip
If you need to call blocking code from inside of an async function, or an async function from inside of a blocking function, you could use Asyncer, a sibling library to FastAPI.
///

metadata.breadcrumb: Stream Data > A Custom `PNGStreamingResponse` > Files and Async
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/stream-data.md

page_content:
[Context: Stream Data > A Custom `PNGStreamingResponse` > `yield from`]

### [CODE: yield from]
When you are iterating over something, like a file-like object, and then you are doing [CODE: yield] for each item, you could also use [CODE: yield from] to yield each item directly and skip the [CODE: for] loop.
This is not particular to FastAPI, it's just Python, but it's a nice trick to know.
```python
yield from image_file

```

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/stream-data.md

page_content:
[Context: Strict Content-Type Checking]

# Strict Content-Type Checking
By default, [BOLD:FastAPI] uses strict [CODE: Content-Type] header checking for JSON request bodies, this means that JSON requests [BOLD:must] include a valid [CODE: Content-Type] header (e.g. [CODE: application/json]) in order for the body to be parsed as JSON.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/strict-content-type.md

page_content:
[Context: Strict Content-Type Checking > CSRF Risk]

## CSRF Risk
This default behavior provides protection against a class of [BOLD:Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF)] attacks in a very specific scenario.
These attacks exploit the fact that browsers allow scripts to send requests without doing any CORS preflight check when they:
[ITALIC: don't have a [CODE: Content-Type] header (e.g. using [CODE: fetch()] with a [CODE: Blob] body)
] and don't send any authentication credentials.
This type of attack is mainly relevant when:
[ITALIC: the application is running locally (e.g. on [CODE: localhost]) or in an internal network
] and the application doesn't have any authentication, it expects that any request from the same network can be trusted.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/strict-content-type.md

page_content:
[Context: Strict Content-Type Checking > Example Attack]

## Example Attack
Imagine you build a way to run a local AI agent.
It provides an API at
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: http://localhost:8000/v1/agents/multivac...]
There's also a frontend at
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]1: http://localhost:8000...]
/// tip
Note that both have the same host.
///
Then using the frontend you can make the AI agent do things on your behalf.
As it's running [BOLD:locally], and not in the open internet, you decide to [BOLD:not have any authentication] set up, just trusting the access to the local network.
Then one of your users could install it and run it locally.
Then they could open a malicious website, e.g. something like
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]2: https://evilhackers.example.com...]
And that malicious website sends requests using [CODE: fetch()] with a [CODE: Blob] body to the local API at
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]3: http://localhost:8000/v1/agents/multivac...]
Even though the host of the malicious website and the local app is different, the browser won't trigger a CORS preflight request because:
[ITALIC: It's running without any authentication, it doesn't have to send any credentials.
] The browser thinks it's not sending JSON (because of the missing [CODE: Content-Type] header).
Then the malicious website could make the local AI agent send angry messages to the user's ex-boss... or worse.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/strict-content-type.md

page_content:
[Context: Strict Content-Type Checking > Example Attack]

## Example Attack
Imagine you build a way to run a local AI agent.
It provides an API at
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: http://localhost:8000/v1/agents/multivac...]
There's also a frontend at
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]1: http://localhost:8000...]
/// tip
Note that both have the same host.
///
Then using the frontend you can make the AI agent do things on your behalf.
As it's running [BOLD:locally], and not in the open internet, you decide to [BOLD:not have any authentication] set up, just trusting the access to the local network.
Then one of your users could install it and run it locally.
Then they could open a malicious website, e.g. something like
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]2: https://evilhackers.example.com...]
And that malicious website sends requests using [CODE: fetch()] with a [CODE: Blob] body to the local API at
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]3: http://localhost:8000/v1/agents/multivac...]
Even though the host of the malicious website and the local app is different, the browser won't trigger a CORS preflight request because:
[ITALIC: It's running without any authentication, it doesn't have to send any credentials.
] The browser thinks it's not sending JSON (because of the missing [CODE: Content-Type] header).
Then the malicious website could make the local AI agent send angry messages to the user's ex-boss... or worse.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/strict-content-type.md

page_content:
[Context: Strict Content-Type Checking > Open Internet]

## Open Internet
If your app is in the open internet, you wouldn't "trust the network" and let anyone send privileged requests without authentication.
Attackers could simply run a script to send requests to your API, no need for browser interaction, so you are probably already securing any privileged endpoints.
In that case [BOLD:this attack / risk doesn't apply to you].
This risk and attack is mainly relevant when the app runs on the [BOLD:local network] and that is the [BOLD:only assumed protection].

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/strict-content-type.md

page_content:
[Context: Strict Content-Type Checking > Allowing Requests Without Content-Type]

## Allowing Requests Without Content-Type
If you need to support clients that don't send a [CODE: Content-Type] header, you can disable strict checking by setting [CODE: strict[ITALIC:content]type=False]:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: app = FastAPI(strict[ITALIC:content]type=False)...]
With this setting, requests without a [CODE: Content-Type] header will have their body parsed as JSON, which is the same behavior as older versions of FastAPI.
/// info
This behavior and configuration was added in FastAPI 0.132.0.
///

metadata.breadcrumb: Strict Content-Type Checking > Allowing Requests Without Content-Type
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/strict-content-type.md

page_content:
[Context: Sub Applications - Mounts]

# Sub Applications - Mounts
If you need to have two independent FastAPI applications, with their own independent OpenAPI and their own docs UIs, you can have a main app and "mount" one (or more) sub-application(s).

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/sub-applications.md

page_content:
[Context: Sub Applications - Mounts > Mounting a **FastAPI** application]

## Mounting a [BOLD:FastAPI] application
"Mounting" means adding a completely "independent" application in a specific path, that then takes care of handling everything under that path, with the [ITALIC:path operations] declared in that sub-application.

metadata.breadcrumb: Sub Applications - Mounts > Mounting a **FastAPI** application
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/sub-applications.md

metadata.breadcrumb: Sub Applications - Mounts > Mounting a **FastAPI** application > Top-level application
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/sub-applications.md

metadata.breadcrumb: Sub Applications - Mounts > Mounting a **FastAPI** application > Sub-application
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/sub-applications.md

page_content:
[Context: Sub Applications - Mounts > Mounting a **FastAPI** application > Mount the sub-application]

### Mount the sub-application
In your top-level application, [CODE: app], mount the sub-application, [CODE: subapi].
In this case, it will be mounted at the path [CODE: /subapi]:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: subapi = FastAPI() app.mount("/subapi", subapi)...]

metadata.breadcrumb: Sub Applications - Mounts > Mounting a **FastAPI** application > Mount the sub-application
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/sub-applications.md

page_content:
[Context: Sub Applications - Mounts > Mounting a **FastAPI** application > Check the automatic API docs]

### Check the automatic API docs
Now, run the [CODE: fastapi] command:

[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: $ fastapi dev INFO
And open the docs at http://127.0.0.1:8000/docs.
You will see the automatic API docs for the main app, including only its own [ITALIC:path operations]:

And then, open the docs for the sub-application, at http://127.0.0.1:8000/subapi/docs.
You will see the automatic API docs for the sub-application, including only its own [ITALIC:path operations], all under the correct sub-path prefix [CODE: /subapi]:

If you try interacting with any of the two user interfaces, they will work correctly, because the browser will be able to talk to each specific app or sub-app.

metadata.breadcrumb: Sub Applications - Mounts > Mounting a **FastAPI** application > Check the automatic API docs
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/sub-applications.md

page_content:
[Context: Sub Applications - Mounts > Mounting a **FastAPI** application > Technical Details: `root_path`]

### Technical Details: [CODE: root[ITALIC:path]
When you mount a sub-application as described above, FastAPI will take care of communicating the mount path for the sub-application using a mechanism from the ASGI specification called a [CODE: root]path].
That way, the sub-application will know to use that path prefix for the docs UI.
And the sub-application could also have its own mounted sub-applications and everything would work correctly, because FastAPI handles all these [CODE: root[ITALIC:path]s automatically.
You will learn more about the [CODE: root]path] and how to use it explicitly in the section about Behind a Proxy.

metadata.breadcrumb: Sub Applications - Mounts > Mounting a **FastAPI** application > Technical Details: `root_path`
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/sub-applications.md

page_content:
[Context: Templates]

# Templates
You can use any template engine you want with [BOLD:FastAPI].
A common choice is Jinja2, the same one used by Flask and other tools.
There are utilities to configure it easily that you can use directly in your [BOLD:FastAPI] application (provided by Starlette).

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/templates.md

page_content:
[Context: Templates > Install dependencies]

## Install dependencies
Make sure you create a virtual environment, activate it, and install [CODE: jinja2]:

[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: $ pip install jinja2 ---> 100%...]

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/templates.md
Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/templates.md
Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/templates.md

page_content:
[Context: Templates > Writing templates]

## Writing templates
Then you can write a template at [CODE: templates/item.html] with, for example:
```jinja hl[ITALIC:lines="7"
{!../../docs]src/templates/templates/item.html!}
```

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/templates.md

page_content:
[Context: Templates > Writing templates > Template Context Values]

### Template Context Values
In the HTML that contains:
{% raw %}
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: Item ID: {{ id }}...]
{% endraw %}
...it will show the [CODE: id] taken from the "context" [CODE: dict] you passed:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]1: {"id": id}...]
For example, with an ID of [CODE: 42], this would render:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]2: Item ID: 42...]

metadata.breadcrumb: Templates > Writing templates > Template Context Values
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/templates.md

page_content:
[Context: Templates > Writing templates > Template `url_for` Arguments]

### Template [CODE: url[ITALIC:for] Arguments
You can also use [CODE: url]for()] inside of the template, it takes as arguments the same arguments that would be used by your [ITALIC:path operation function].
So, the section with:
{% raw %}
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: ...]
{% endraw %}
...will generate a link to the same URL that would be handled by the [ITALIC:path operation function] [CODE: read[ITALIC:item(id=id)].
For example, with an ID of [CODE: 42], this would render:
[CODE]BLOCK_1: ...]

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/templates.md

page_content:
[Context: Templates > Templates and static files]

## Templates and static files
You can also use [CODE: url[ITALIC:for()] inside of the template, and use it, for example, with the [CODE: StaticFiles] you mounted with the [CODE: name="static"].
```jinja hl]lines="4"
{!../../docs[ITALIC:src/templates/templates/item.html!}
```
In this example, it would link to a CSS file at [CODE: static/styles.css] with:
```CSS hl]lines="4"
{!../../docs_src/templates/static/styles.css!}
```
And because you are using [CODE: StaticFiles], that CSS file would be served automatically by your [BOLD:FastAPI] application at the URL [CODE: /static/styles.css].

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/templates.md

page_content:
[Context: Templates > More details]

## More details
For more details, including how to test templates, check Starlette's docs on templates.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/templates.md

page_content:
[Context: Testing Dependencies with Overrides > Overriding dependencies during testing]

# Testing Dependencies with Overrides
## Overriding dependencies during testing
There are some scenarios where you might want to override a dependency during testing.
You don't want the original dependency to run (nor any of the sub-dependencies it might have).
Instead, you want to provide a different dependency that will be used only during tests (possibly only some specific tests), and will provide a value that can be used where the value of the original dependency was used.

metadata.breadcrumb: Testing Dependencies with Overrides > Overriding dependencies during testing
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/testing-dependencies.md

page_content:
[Context: Testing Dependencies with Overrides > Overriding dependencies during testing > Use cases: external service]

### Use cases: external service
An example could be that you have an external authentication provider that you need to call.
You send it a token and it returns an authenticated user.
This provider might be charging you per request, and calling it might take some extra time than if you had a fixed mock user for tests.
You probably want to test the external provider once, but not necessarily call it for every test that runs.
In this case, you can override the dependency that calls that provider, and use a custom dependency that returns a mock user, only for your tests.

metadata.breadcrumb: Testing Dependencies with Overrides > Overriding dependencies during testing > Use cases: external service
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/testing-dependencies.md

page_content:
[Context: Testing Dependencies with Overrides > Overriding dependencies during testing > Use the `app.dependency_overrides` attribute]

### Use the [CODE: app.dependency[ITALIC:overrides] attribute
For these cases, your [BOLD:FastAPI] application has an attribute [CODE: app.dependency]overrides], it is a simple [CODE: dict].
To override a dependency for testing, you put as a key the original dependency (a function), and as the value, your dependency override (another function).
And then [BOLD:FastAPI] will call that override instead of the original dependency.
```python
async def override[ITALIC:dependency(q: str | None = None):
return {"q": q, "skip": 5, "limit": 10}
app.dependency]overrides[common[ITALIC:parameters] = override]dependency

```
/// tip
You can set a dependency override for a dependency used anywhere in your [BOLD:FastAPI] application.
The original dependency could be used in a [ITALIC:path operation function], a [ITALIC:path operation decorator] (when you don't use the return value), a [CODE: .include[ITALIC:router()] call, etc.
FastAPI will still be able to override it.
///
Then you can reset your overrides (remove them) by setting [CODE: app.dependency]overrides] to be an empty [CODE: dict]:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: app.dependency_overrides = {}...]
/// tip
If you want to override a dependency only during some tests, you can set the override at the beginning of the test (inside the test function) and reset it at the end (at the end of the test function).
///

metadata.breadcrumb: Testing Dependencies with Overrides > Overriding dependencies during testing > Use the `app.dependency_overrides` attribute
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/testing-dependencies.md

page_content:
[Context: Testing Dependencies with Overrides > Overriding dependencies during testing > Use the `app.dependency_overrides` attribute]

### Use the [CODE: app.dependency[ITALIC:overrides] attribute
For these cases, your [BOLD:FastAPI] application has an attribute [CODE: app.dependency]overrides], it is a simple [CODE: dict].
To override a dependency for testing, you put as a key the original dependency (a function), and as the value, your dependency override (another function).
And then [BOLD:FastAPI] will call that override instead of the original dependency.
```python
async def override[ITALIC:dependency(q: str | None = None):
return {"q": q, "skip": 5, "limit": 10}
app.dependency]overrides[common[ITALIC:parameters] = override]dependency

```
/// tip
You can set a dependency override for a dependency used anywhere in your [BOLD:FastAPI] application.
The original dependency could be used in a [ITALIC:path operation function], a [ITALIC:path operation decorator] (when you don't use the return value), a [CODE: .include[ITALIC:router()] call, etc.
FastAPI will still be able to override it.
///
Then you can reset your overrides (remove them) by setting [CODE: app.dependency]overrides] to be an empty [CODE: dict]:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: app.dependency_overrides = {}...]
/// tip
If you want to override a dependency only during some tests, you can set the override at the beginning of the test (inside the test function) and reset it at the end (at the end of the test function).
///

page_content:
[Context: Testing WebSockets]

# Testing WebSockets
You can use the same [CODE: TestClient] to test WebSockets.
For this, you use the [CODE: TestClient] in a [CODE: with] statement, connecting to the WebSocket:
```python
def test[ITALIC:websocket():
client = TestClient(app)
with client.websocket]connect("/ws") as websocket:
data = websocket.receive_json()
assert data == {"msg": "Hello WebSocket"}
```
/// note
For more details, check Starlette's documentation for testing WebSockets.
///

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/testing-websockets.md

page_content:
[Context: Using the Request Directly]

# Using the Request Directly
Up to now, you have been declaring the parts of the request that you need with their types.
Taking data from:
[ITALIC: The path as parameters.
] Headers.
[ITALIC: Cookies.
] etc.
And by doing so, [BOLD:FastAPI] is validating that data, converting it and generating documentation for your API automatically.
But there are situations where you might need to access the [CODE: Request] object directly.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/using-request-directly.md

page_content:
[Context: Using the Request Directly > Details about the `Request` object]

## Details about the [CODE: Request] object
As [BOLD:FastAPI] is actually [BOLD:Starlette] underneath, with a layer of several tools on top, you can use Starlette's [[CODE: Request]](https://www.starlette.dev/requests/) object directly when you need to.
It would also mean that if you get data from the [CODE: Request] object directly (for example, read the body) it won't be validated, converted or documented (with OpenAPI, for the automatic API user interface) by FastAPI.
Although any other parameter declared normally (for example, the body with a Pydantic model) would still be validated, converted, annotated, etc.
But there are specific cases where it's useful to get the [CODE: Request] object.

metadata.breadcrumb: Using the Request Directly > Details about the `Request` object
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/using-request-directly.md

page_content:
[Context: Using the Request Directly > Use the `Request` object directly]

## Use the [CODE: Request] object directly
Let's imagine you want to get the client's IP address/host inside of your [ITALIC:path operation function].
For that you need to access the request directly.
```python
from fastapi import FastAPI, Request

def read[ITALIC:root(item]id: str, request: Request):
client_host = request.client.host
```
By declaring a [ITALIC:path operation function] parameter with the type being the [CODE: Request] [BOLD:FastAPI] will know to pass the [CODE: Request] in that parameter.
/// tip
Note that in this case, we are declaring a path parameter beside the request parameter.
So, the path parameter will be extracted, validated, converted to the specified type and annotated with OpenAPI.
The same way, you can declare any other parameter as normally, and additionally, get the [CODE: Request] too.
///

metadata.breadcrumb: Using the Request Directly > Use the `Request` object directly
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/using-request-directly.md

page_content:
[Context: Using the Request Directly > `Request` documentation]

## [CODE: Request] documentation
You can read more details about the [[CODE: Request] object in the official Starlette documentation site](https://www.starlette.dev/requests/).
/// note | Technical Details
You could also use [CODE: from starlette.requests import Request].
[BOLD:FastAPI] provides it directly just as a convenience for you, the developer. But it comes directly from Starlette.
///

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/using-request-directly.md

[Context: WebSockets]

# WebSockets
You can use WebSockets with [BOLD:FastAPI].

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/websockets.md

page_content:
[Context: WebSockets > Install `websockets`]

## Install [CODE: websockets]
Make sure you create a virtual environment, activate it, and install [CODE: websockets] (a Python library that makes it easy to use the "WebSocket" protocol):

[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: $ pip install websockets ---> 100%...]

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/websockets.md
Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/websockets.md
Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/websockets.md

page_content:
[Context: WebSockets > Create a `websocket`]

## Create a [CODE: websocket]
In your [BOLD:FastAPI] application, create a [CODE: websocket]:
```python
from fastapi import FastAPI, WebSocket
from fastapi.responses import HTMLResponse
@app.websocket("/ws")
async def websocket_endpoint(websocket: WebSocket):
```
/// note | Technical Details
You could also use [CODE: from starlette.websockets import WebSocket].
[BOLD:FastAPI] provides the same [CODE: WebSocket] directly just as a convenience for you, the developer. But it comes directly from Starlette.
///

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/websockets.md

page_content:
[Context: WebSockets > Await for messages and send messages]

## Await for messages and send messages
In your WebSocket route you can [CODE: await] for messages and send messages.
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: await websocket.accept() while True: data = await ...]
You can receive and send binary, text, and JSON data.

hierarchy: Await for messages and send messages

page_content:

## Try it
Put your code in a file [CODE: main.py] and then run your application:

[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: $ fastapi dev INFO
Open your browser at http://127.0.0.1:8000.
You will see a simple page like:

You can type messages in the input box, and send them:

And your [BOLD:FastAPI] application with WebSockets will respond back:

You can send (and receive) many messages:

And all of them will use the same WebSocket connection.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/websockets.md
Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/websockets.md

### Try the WebSockets with dependencies
Run your application:

[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: $ fastapi dev INFO
Open your browser at http://127.0.0.1:8000.
There you can set:
[ITALIC: The "Item ID", used in the path.
] The "Token" used as a query parameter.
/// tip
Notice that the query [CODE: token] will be handled by a dependency.
///
With that you can connect the WebSocket and then send and receive messages:

page_content:
[Context: WebSockets > Handling disconnections and multiple clients]

## Handling disconnections and multiple clients
When a WebSocket connection is closed, the [CODE: await websocket.receive[ITALIC:text()] will raise a [CODE: WebSocketDisconnect] exception, which you can then catch and handle like in this example.
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:0: except WebSocketDisconnect: manager.disconnect(web...]
To try it out:
[ITALIC: Open the app with several browser tabs.
] Write messages from them.
- Then close one of the tabs.
That will raise the [CODE: WebSocketDisconnect] exception, and all the other clients will receive a message like:
[CODE]BLOCK_1: Client #1596980209979 left the chat...]
/// tip
The app above is a minimal and simple example to demonstrate how to handle and broadcast messages to several WebSocket connections.
But keep in mind that, as everything is handled in memory, in a single list, it will only work while the process is running, and will only work with a single process.
If you need something easy to integrate with FastAPI but that is more robust, supported by Redis, PostgreSQL or others, check encode/broadcaster.
///

metadata.breadcrumb: WebSockets > Handling disconnections and multiple clients
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/websockets.md

page_content:
[Context: WebSockets > Handling disconnections and multiple clients]

## Handling disconnections and multiple clients
When a WebSocket connection is closed, the [CODE: await websocket.receive[ITALIC:text()] will raise a [CODE: WebSocketDisconnect] exception, which you can then catch and handle like in this example.
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:0: except WebSocketDisconnect: manager.disconnect(web...]
To try it out:
[ITALIC: Open the app with several browser tabs.
] Write messages from them.
- Then close one of the tabs.
That will raise the [CODE: WebSocketDisconnect] exception, and all the other clients will receive a message like:
[CODE]BLOCK_1: Client #1596980209979 left the chat...]
/// tip
The app above is a minimal and simple example to demonstrate how to handle and broadcast messages to several WebSocket connections.
But keep in mind that, as everything is handled in memory, in a single list, it will only work while the process is running, and will only work with a single process.
If you need something easy to integrate with FastAPI but that is more robust, supported by Redis, PostgreSQL or others, check encode/broadcaster.
///

page_content:
[Context: Including WSGI - Flask, Django, others]

# Including WSGI - Flask, Django, others
You can mount WSGI applications as you saw with Sub Applications - Mounts, Behind a Proxy.
For that, you can use the [CODE: WSGIMiddleware] and use it to wrap your WSGI application, for example, Flask, Django, etc.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/wsgi.md

page_content:
[Context: Including WSGI - Flask, Django, others > Using `WSGIMiddleware`]

## Using [CODE: WSGIMiddleware]
/// info
This requires installing [CODE: a2wsgi] for example with [CODE: pip install a2wsgi].
///
You need to import [CODE: WSGIMiddleware] from [CODE: a2wsgi].
Then wrap the WSGI (e.g. Flask) app with the middleware.
And then mount that under a path.
```python
from a2wsgi import WSGIMiddleware
from fastapi import FastAPI
from flask import Flask, request
from markupsafe import escape
app.mount("/v1", WSGIMiddleware(flask_app))
```
/// note
Previously, it was recommended to use [CODE: WSGIMiddleware] from [CODE: fastapi.middleware.wsgi], but it is now deprecated.
It’s advised to use the [CODE: a2wsgi] package instead. The usage remains the same.
Just ensure that you have the [CODE: a2wsgi] package installed and import [CODE: WSGIMiddleware] correctly from [CODE: a2wsgi].
///

metadata.breadcrumb: Including WSGI - Flask, Django, others > Using `WSGIMiddleware`
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/wsgi.md

page_content:
[Context: Including WSGI - Flask, Django, others > Check it]

## Check it
Now, every request under the path [CODE: /v1/] will be handled by the Flask application.
And the rest will be handled by [BOLD:FastAPI].
If you run it and go to http://localhost:8000/v1/ you will see the response from Flask:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: Hello, World from Flask!...]
And if you go to http://localhost:8000/v2 you will see the response from FastAPI:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]1: { "message": "Hello World" }...]

metadata.breadcrumb: Including WSGI - Flask, Django, others > Check it
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > advanced/wsgi.md

[Context: Deploy FastAPI on Cloud Providers]

# Deploy FastAPI on Cloud Providers
You can use virtually [BOLD:any cloud provider] to deploy your FastAPI application.
In most of the cases, the main cloud providers have guides to deploy FastAPI with them.

[Context: Deploy FastAPI on Cloud Providers > FastAPI Cloud]

## FastAPI Cloud
[BOLD:FastAPI Cloud] is built by the same author and team behind [BOLD:FastAPI].
It streamlines the process of [BOLD:building], [BOLD:deploying], and [BOLD:accessing] an API with minimal effort.
It brings the same [BOLD:developer experience] of building apps with FastAPI to [BOLD:deploying] them to the cloud.
FastAPI Cloud is the primary sponsor and funding provider for the [ITALIC:FastAPI and friends] open source projects.

metadata.breadcrumb: Deploy FastAPI on Cloud Providers > FastAPI Cloud

page_content:
[Context: Deploy FastAPI on Cloud Providers > Cloud Providers - Sponsors]

## Cloud Providers - Sponsors
Some other cloud providers [BOLD:sponsor FastAPI] too.
You might also want to consider them to follow their guides and try their services:
[ITALIC: Render
] Railway
