page_content:
[Context: Deployments Concepts]

# Deployments Concepts
When deploying a [BOLD:FastAPI] application, or actually, any type of web API, there are several concepts that you probably care about, and using them you can find the [BOLD:most appropriate] way to [BOLD:deploy your application].
Some of the important concepts are:
[ITALIC: Security - HTTPS
] Running on startup
[ITALIC: Restarts
] Replication (the number of processes running)
[ITALIC: Memory
] Previous steps before starting
We'll see how they would affect [BOLD:deployments].
In the end, the ultimate objective is to be able to [BOLD:serve your API clients] in a way that is [BOLD:secure], to [BOLD:avoid disruptions], and to use the [BOLD:compute resources] (for example remote servers/virtual machines) as efficiently as possible.
I'll tell you a bit more about these [BOLD:concepts] here, and that would hopefully give you the [BOLD:intuition] you would need to decide how to deploy your API in very different environments, possibly even in [BOLD:future] ones that don't exist yet.
By considering these concepts, you will be able to [BOLD:evaluate and design] the best way to deploy [BOLD:your own APIs].
In the next chapters, I'll give you more [BOLD:concrete recipes] to deploy FastAPI applications.
But for now, let's check these important [BOLD:conceptual ideas]. These concepts also apply to any other type of web API.

[Context: Deployments Concepts]

# Deployments Concepts
When deploying a [BOLD:FastAPI] application, or actually, any type of web API, there are several concepts that you probably care about, and using them you can find the [BOLD:most appropriate] way to [BOLD:deploy your application].
Some of the important concepts are:
[ITALIC: Security - HTTPS
] Running on startup
[ITALIC: Restarts
] Replication (the number of processes running)
[ITALIC: Memory
] Previous steps before starting
We'll see how they would affect [BOLD:deployments].
In the end, the ultimate objective is to be able to [BOLD:serve your API clients] in a way that is [BOLD:secure], to [BOLD:avoid disruptions], and to use the [BOLD:compute resources] (for example remote servers/virtual machines) as efficiently as possible.
I'll tell you a bit more about these [BOLD:concepts] here, and that would hopefully give you the [BOLD:intuition] you would need to decide how to deploy your API in very different environments, possibly even in [BOLD:future] ones that don't exist yet.
By considering these concepts, you will be able to [BOLD:evaluate and design] the best way to deploy [BOLD:your own APIs].
In the next chapters, I'll give you more [BOLD:concrete recipes] to deploy FastAPI applications.
But for now, let's check these important [BOLD:conceptual ideas]. These concepts also apply to any other type of web API.

[Context: Deployments Concepts > Security - HTTPS]

## Security - HTTPS
In the previous chapter about HTTPS we learned about how HTTPS provides encryption for your API.
We also saw that HTTPS is normally provided by a component [BOLD:external] to your application server, a [BOLD:TLS Termination Proxy].
And there has to be something in charge of [BOLD:renewing the HTTPS certificates], it could be the same component or it could be something different.

[Context: Deployments Concepts > Security - HTTPS > Example Tools for HTTPS]

### Example Tools for HTTPS
Some of the tools you could use as a TLS Termination Proxy are:
[ITALIC: Traefik
] Automatically handles certificates renewals
[ITALIC: Caddy
] Automatically handles certificates renewals
[ITALIC: Nginx
] With an external component like Certbot for certificate renewals
[ITALIC: HAProxy
] With an external component like Certbot for certificate renewals
[ITALIC: Kubernetes with an Ingress Controller like Nginx
] With an external component like cert-manager for certificate renewals
- Handled internally by a cloud provider as part of their services (read below )
Another option is that you could use a [BOLD:cloud service] that does more of the work including setting up HTTPS. It could have some restrictions or charge you more, etc. But in that case, you wouldn't have to set up a TLS Termination Proxy yourself.
I'll show you some concrete examples in the next chapters.
---
Then the next concepts to consider are all about the program running your actual API (e.g. Uvicorn).

metadata.breadcrumb: Deployments Concepts > Security - HTTPS > Example Tools for HTTPS

page_content:
[Context: Deployments Concepts > Security - HTTPS > Example Tools for HTTPS]

### Example Tools for HTTPS
Some of the tools you could use as a TLS Termination Proxy are:
[ITALIC: Traefik
] Automatically handles certificates renewals
[ITALIC: Caddy
] Automatically handles certificates renewals
[ITALIC: Nginx
] With an external component like Certbot for certificate renewals
[ITALIC: HAProxy
] With an external component like Certbot for certificate renewals
[ITALIC: Kubernetes with an Ingress Controller like Nginx
] With an external component like cert-manager for certificate renewals
- Handled internally by a cloud provider as part of their services (read below )
Another option is that you could use a [BOLD:cloud service] that does more of the work including setting up HTTPS. It could have some restrictions or charge you more, etc. But in that case, you wouldn't have to set up a TLS Termination Proxy yourself.
I'll show you some concrete examples in the next chapters.
---
Then the next concepts to consider are all about the program running your actual API (e.g. Uvicorn).

metadata.breadcrumb: Deployments Concepts > Security - HTTPS > Example Tools for HTTPS

page_content:
[Context: Deployments Concepts > Program and Process]

## Program and Process
We will talk a lot about the running "[BOLD:process]", so it's useful to have clarity about what it means, and what's the difference with the word "[BOLD:program]".

[Context: Deployments Concepts > Program and Process > What is a Program]

### What is a Program
The word [BOLD:program] is commonly used to describe many things:
[ITALIC: The [BOLD:code] that you write, the [BOLD:Python files].
] The [BOLD:file] that can be [BOLD:executed] by the operating system, for example: [CODE: python], [CODE: python.exe] or [CODE: uvicorn].
- A particular program while it is [BOLD:running] on the operating system, using the CPU, and storing things in memory. This is also called a [BOLD:process].

metadata.breadcrumb: Deployments Concepts > Program and Process > What is a Program

page_content:
[Context: Deployments Concepts > Program and Process > What is a Process]

### What is a Process
The word [BOLD:process] is normally used in a more specific way, only referring to the thing that is running in the operating system (like in the last point above):
[ITALIC: A particular program while it is [BOLD:running] on the operating system.
] This doesn't refer to the file, nor to the code, it refers [BOLD:specifically] to the thing that is being [BOLD:executed] and managed by the operating system.
[ITALIC: Any program, any code, [BOLD:can only do things] when it is being [BOLD:executed]. So, when there's a [BOLD:process running].
] The process can be [BOLD:terminated] (or "killed") by you, or by the operating system. At that point, it stops running/being executed, and it can [BOLD:no longer do things].
[ITALIC: Each application that you have running on your computer has some process behind it, each running program, each window, etc. And there are normally many processes running [BOLD:at the same time] while a computer is on.
] There can be [BOLD:multiple processes] of the [BOLD:same program] running at the same time.
If you check out the "task manager" or "system monitor" (or similar tools) in your operating system, you will be able to see many of those processes running.
And, for example, you will probably see that there are multiple processes running the same browser program (Firefox, Chrome, Edge, etc). They normally run one process per tab, plus some other extra processes.

---
Now that we know the difference between the terms [BOLD:process] and [BOLD:program], let's continue talking about deployments.

metadata.breadcrumb: Deployments Concepts > Program and Process > What is a Process

[Context: Deployments Concepts > Program and Process > What is a Process]

[ITALIC: Each application that you have running on your computer has some process behind it, each running program, each window, etc. And there are normally many processes running [BOLD:at the same time] while a computer is on.

If you check out the "task manager" or "system monitor" (or similar tools) in your operating system, you will be able to see many of those processes running.
And, for example, you will probably see that there are multiple processes running the same browser program (Firefox, Chrome, Edge, etc). They normally run one process per tab, plus some other extra processes.

---
Now that we know the difference between the terms [BOLD:process] and [BOLD:program], let's continue talking about deployments.

page_content:
[Context: Deployments Concepts > Running on Startup]

## Running on Startup
In most cases, when you create a web API, you want it to be [BOLD:always running], uninterrupted, so that your clients can always access it. This is of course, unless you have a specific reason why you want it to run only in certain situations, but most of the time you want it constantly running and [BOLD:available].

[Context: Deployments Concepts > Running on Startup > In a Remote Server]

### In a Remote Server
When you set up a remote server (a cloud server, a virtual machine, etc.) the simplest thing you can do is use [CODE: fastapi run] (which uses Uvicorn) or something similar, manually, the same way you do when developing locally.
And it will work and will be useful [BOLD:during development].
But if your connection to the server is lost, the [BOLD:running process] will probably die.
And if the server is restarted (for example after updates, or migrations from the cloud provider) you probably [BOLD:won't notice it]. And because of that, you won't even know that you have to restart the process manually. So, your API will just stay dead.

metadata.breadcrumb: Deployments Concepts > Running on Startup > In a Remote Server

page_content:
[Context: Deployments Concepts > Running on Startup > Run Automatically on Startup]

### Run Automatically on Startup
In general, you will probably want the server program (e.g. Uvicorn) to be started automatically on server startup, and without needing any [BOLD:human intervention], to have a process always running with your API (e.g. Uvicorn running your FastAPI app).

metadata.breadcrumb: Deployments Concepts > Running on Startup > Run Automatically on Startup

page_content:
[Context: Deployments Concepts > Running on Startup > Separate Program]

### Separate Program
To achieve this, you will normally have a [BOLD:separate program] that would make sure your application is run on startup. And in many cases, it would also make sure other components or applications are also run, for example, a database.

metadata.breadcrumb: Deployments Concepts > Running on Startup > Separate Program

hierarchy: Example Tools to Run at Startup
metadata.breadcrumb: Deployments Concepts > Running on Startup > Example Tools to Run at Startup

page_content:
[Context: Deployments Concepts > Restarts]

## Restarts
Similar to making sure your application is run on startup, you probably also want to make sure it is [BOLD:restarted] after failures.

[Context: Deployments Concepts > Restarts > We Make Mistakes]

### We Make Mistakes
We, as humans, make [BOLD:mistakes], all the time. Software almost [ITALIC:always] has [BOLD:bugs] hidden in different places.
And we as developers keep improving the code as we find those bugs and as we implement new features (possibly adding new bugs too ).

metadata.breadcrumb: Deployments Concepts > Restarts > We Make Mistakes

page_content:
[Context: Deployments Concepts > Restarts > Small Errors Automatically Handled]

### Small Errors Automatically Handled
When building web APIs with FastAPI, if there's an error in our code, FastAPI will normally contain it to the single request that triggered the error.
The client will get a [BOLD:500 Internal Server Error] for that request, but the application will continue working for the next requests instead of just crashing completely.

metadata.breadcrumb: Deployments Concepts > Restarts > Small Errors Automatically Handled

page_content:
[Context: Deployments Concepts > Restarts > Bigger Errors - Crashes]

### Bigger Errors - Crashes
Nevertheless, there might be cases where we write some code that [BOLD:crashes the entire application] making Uvicorn and Python crash.
And still, you would probably not want the application to stay dead because there was an error in one place, you probably want it to [BOLD:continue running] at least for the [ITALIC:path operations] that are not broken.

metadata.breadcrumb: Deployments Concepts > Restarts > Bigger Errors - Crashes

page_content:
[Context: Deployments Concepts > Restarts > Restart After Crash]

### Restart After Crash
But in those cases with really bad errors that crash the running [BOLD:process], you would want an external component that is in charge of [BOLD:restarting] the process, at least a couple of times...
/// tip
...Although if the whole application is just [BOLD:crashing immediately] it probably doesn't make sense to keep restarting it forever. But in those cases, you will probably notice it during development, or at least right after deployment.
So let's focus on the main cases, where it could crash entirely in some particular cases [BOLD:in the future], and it still makes sense to restart it.
///
You would probably want to have the thing in charge of restarting your application as an [BOLD:external component], because by that point, the same application with Uvicorn and Python already crashed, so there's nothing in the same code of the same app that could do anything about it.

metadata.breadcrumb: Deployments Concepts > Restarts > Restart After Crash
Deployments Concepts > Restarts > Example Tools to Restart Automatically

page_content:
[Context: Deployments Concepts > Replication - Processes and Memory]

## Replication - Processes and Memory
With a FastAPI application, using a server program like the [CODE: fastapi] command that runs Uvicorn, running it once in [BOLD:one process] can serve multiple clients concurrently.
But in many cases, you will want to run several worker processes at the same time.

metadata.breadcrumb: Deployments Concepts > Replication - Processes and Memory

page_content:
[Context: Deployments Concepts > Replication - Processes and Memory > Multiple Processes - Workers]

### Multiple Processes - Workers
If you have more clients than what a single process can handle (for example if the virtual machine is not too big) and you have [BOLD:multiple cores] in the server's CPU, then you could have [BOLD:multiple processes] running with the same application at the same time, and distribute all the requests among them.
When you run [BOLD:multiple processes] of the same API program, they are commonly called [BOLD:workers].

metadata.breadcrumb: Deployments Concepts > Replication - Processes and Memory > Multiple Processes - Workers

page_content:
[Context: Deployments Concepts > Replication - Processes and Memory > Worker Processes and Ports]

### Worker Processes and Ports
Remember from the docs About HTTPS that only one process can be listening on one combination of port and IP address in a server?
This is still true.
So, to be able to have [BOLD:multiple processes] at the same time, there has to be a [BOLD:single process listening on a port] that then transmits the communication to each worker process in some way.

metadata.breadcrumb: Deployments Concepts > Replication - Processes and Memory > Worker Processes and Ports

page_content:
[Context: Deployments Concepts > Replication - Processes and Memory > Memory per Process]

### Memory per Process
Now, when the program loads things in memory, for example, a machine learning model in a variable, or the contents of a large file in a variable, all that [BOLD:consumes a bit of the memory (RAM)] of the server.
And multiple processes normally [BOLD:don't share any memory]. This means that each running process has its own things, variables, and memory. And if you are consuming a large amount of memory in your code, [BOLD:each process] will consume an equivalent amount of memory.

metadata.breadcrumb: Deployments Concepts > Replication - Processes and Memory > Memory per Process

page_content:
[Context: Deployments Concepts > Replication - Processes and Memory > Server Memory]

### Server Memory
For example, if your code loads a Machine Learning model with [BOLD:1 GB in size], when you run one process with your API, it will consume at least 1 GB of RAM. And if you start [BOLD:4 processes] (4 workers), each will consume 1 GB of RAM. So in total, your API will consume [BOLD:4 GB of RAM].
And if your remote server or virtual machine only has 3 GB of RAM, trying to load more than 4 GB of RAM will cause problems.

metadata.breadcrumb: Deployments Concepts > Replication - Processes and Memory > Server Memory

page_content:
[Context: Deployments Concepts > Replication - Processes and Memory > Multiple Processes - An Example]

### Multiple Processes - An Example
In this example, there's a [BOLD:Manager Process] that starts and controls two [BOLD:Worker Processes].
This Manager Process would probably be the one listening on the [BOLD:port] in the IP. And it would transmit all the communication to the worker processes.
Those worker processes would be the ones running your application, they would perform the main computations to receive a [BOLD:request] and return a [BOLD:response], and they would load anything you put in variables in RAM.

And of course, the same machine would probably have [BOLD:other processes] running as well, apart from your application.
An interesting detail is that the percentage of the [BOLD:CPU used] by each process can [BOLD:vary] a lot over time, but the [BOLD:memory (RAM)] normally stays more or less [BOLD:stable].
If you have an API that does a comparable amount of computations every time and you have a lot of clients, then the [BOLD:CPU utilization] will probably [ITALIC:also be stable] (instead of constantly going up and down quickly).

metadata.breadcrumb: Deployments Concepts > Replication - Processes and Memory > Multiple Processes - An Example

page_content:
[Context: Deployments Concepts > Replication - Processes and Memory > Multiple Processes - An Example]

### Multiple Processes - An Example
In this example, there's a [BOLD:Manager Process] that starts and controls two [BOLD:Worker Processes].
This Manager Process would probably be the one listening on the [BOLD:port] in the IP. And it would transmit all the communication to the worker processes.
Those worker processes would be the ones running your application, they would perform the main computations to receive a [BOLD:request] and return a [BOLD:response], and they would load anything you put in variables in RAM.

And of course, the same machine would probably have [BOLD:other processes] running as well, apart from your application.
An interesting detail is that the percentage of the [BOLD:CPU used] by each process can [BOLD:vary] a lot over time, but the [BOLD:memory (RAM)] normally stays more or less [BOLD:stable].
If you have an API that does a comparable amount of computations every time and you have a lot of clients, then the [BOLD:CPU utilization] will probably [ITALIC:also be stable] (instead of constantly going up and down quickly).

metadata.breadcrumb: Deployments Concepts > Replication - Processes and Memory > Multiple Processes - An Example

page_content:
[Context: Deployments Concepts > Previous Steps Before Starting]

## Previous Steps Before Starting
There are many cases where you want to perform some steps [BOLD:before starting] your application.
For example, you might want to run [BOLD:database migrations].
But in most cases, you will want to perform these steps only [BOLD:once].
So, you will want to have a [BOLD:single process] to perform those [BOLD:previous steps], before starting the application.
And you will have to make sure that it's a single process running those previous steps [ITALIC:even] if afterwards, you start [BOLD:multiple processes] (multiple workers) for the application itself. If those steps were run by [BOLD:multiple processes], they would [BOLD:duplicate] the work by running it in [BOLD:parallel], and if the steps were something delicate like a database migration, they could cause conflicts with each other.
Of course, there are some cases where there's no problem in running the previous steps multiple times, in that case, it's a lot easier to handle.
/// tip
Also, keep in mind that depending on your setup, in some cases you [BOLD:might not even need any previous steps] before starting your application.
In that case, you wouldn't have to worry about any of this. 🤷
///

metadata.breadcrumb: Deployments Concepts > Previous Steps Before Starting

page_content:
[Context: Deployments Concepts > Previous Steps Before Starting]

## Previous Steps Before Starting
There are many cases where you want to perform some steps [BOLD:before starting] your application.
For example, you might want to run [BOLD:database migrations].
But in most cases, you will want to perform these steps only [BOLD:once].
So, you will want to have a [BOLD:single process] to perform those [BOLD:previous steps], before starting the application.
And you will have to make sure that it's a single process running those previous steps [ITALIC:even] if afterwards, you start [BOLD:multiple processes] (multiple workers) for the application itself. If those steps were run by [BOLD:multiple processes], they would [BOLD:duplicate] the work by running it in [BOLD:parallel], and if the steps were something delicate like a database migration, they could cause conflicts with each other.
Of course, there are some cases where there's no problem in running the previous steps multiple times, in that case, it's a lot easier to handle.
/// tip
Also, keep in mind that depending on your setup, in some cases you [BOLD:might not even need any previous steps] before starting your application.
In that case, you wouldn't have to worry about any of this. 🤷
///

metadata.breadcrumb: Deployments Concepts > Previous Steps Before Starting
Deployments Concepts > Previous Steps Before Starting > Examples of Previous Steps Strategies

page_content:
[Context: Deployments Concepts > Resource Utilization]

## Resource Utilization
Your server(s) is (are) a [BOLD:resource], you can consume or [BOLD:utilize], with your programs, the computation time on the CPUs, and the RAM memory available.
How much of the system resources do you want to be consuming/utilizing? It might be easy to think "not much", but in reality, you will probably want to consume [BOLD:as much as possible without crashing].
If you are paying for 3 servers but you are using only a little bit of their RAM and CPU, you are probably [BOLD:wasting money] , and probably [BOLD:wasting server electric power] , etc.
In that case, it could be better to have only 2 servers and use a higher percentage of their resources (CPU, memory, disk, network bandwidth, etc).
On the other hand, if you have 2 servers and you are using [BOLD:100% of their CPU and RAM], at some point one process will ask for more memory, and the server will have to use the disk as "memory" (which can be thousands of times slower), or even [BOLD:crash]. Or one process might need to do some computation and would have to wait until the CPU is free again.
In this case, it would be better to get [BOLD:one extra server] and run some processes on it so that they all have [BOLD:enough RAM and CPU time].
There's also the chance that for some reason you have a [BOLD:spike] of usage of your API. Maybe it went viral, or maybe some other services or bots start using it. And you might want to have extra resources to be safe in those cases.
You could put an [BOLD:arbitrary number] to target, for example, something [BOLD:between 50% to 90%] of resource utilization. The point is that those are probably the main things you will want to measure and use to tweak your deployments.
You can use simple tools like [CODE: htop] to see the CPU and RAM used in your server or the amount used by each process. Or you can use more complex monitoring tools, which may be distributed across servers, etc.

[Context: Deployments Concepts > Resource Utilization]

[Context: Deployments Concepts > Recap]

## Recap
You have been reading here some of the main concepts that you would probably need to keep in mind when deciding how to deploy your application:
[ITALIC: Security - HTTPS
] Running on startup
[ITALIC: Restarts
] Replication (the number of processes running)
[ITALIC: Memory
] Previous steps before starting
Understanding these ideas and how to apply them should give you the intuition necessary to take any decisions when configuring and tweaking your deployments. 🤓
In the next sections, I'll give you more concrete examples of possible strategies you can follow.

[Context: FastAPI in Containers - Docker]

# FastAPI in Containers - Docker
When deploying FastAPI applications a common approach is to build a [BOLD:Linux container image]. It's normally done using [BOLD:Docker]. You can then deploy that container image in one of a few possible ways.
Using Linux containers has several advantages including [BOLD:security], [BOLD:replicability], [BOLD:simplicity], and others.
/// tip
In a hurry and already know this stuff? Jump to the [[CODE: Dockerfile] below ](#build-a-docker-image-for-fastapi).
///

Dockerfile Preview
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: FROM python:3.14 WORKDIR /code COPY ./requirements...]

[Context: FastAPI in Containers - Docker > What is a Container]

## What is a Container
Containers (mainly Linux containers) are a very [BOLD:lightweight] way to package applications including all their dependencies and necessary files while keeping them isolated from other containers (other applications or components) in the same system.
Linux containers run using the same Linux kernel of the host (machine, virtual machine, cloud server, etc). This just means that they are very lightweight (compared to full virtual machines emulating an entire operating system).
This way, containers consume [BOLD:little resources], an amount comparable to running the processes directly (a virtual machine would consume much more).
Containers also have their own [BOLD:isolated] running processes (commonly just one process), file system, and network, simplifying deployment, security, development, etc.

metadata.breadcrumb: FastAPI in Containers - Docker > What is a Container

page_content:
[Context: FastAPI in Containers - Docker > What is a Container Image]

## What is a Container Image
A [BOLD:container] is run from a [BOLD:container image].
A container image is a [BOLD:static] version of all the files, environment variables, and the default command/program that should be present in a container. [BOLD:Static] here means that the container [BOLD:image] is not running, it's not being executed, it's only the packaged files and metadata.
In contrast to a "[BOLD:container image]" that is the stored static contents, a "[BOLD:container]" normally refers to the running instance, the thing that is being [BOLD:executed].
When the [BOLD:container] is started and running (started from a [BOLD:container image]) it could create or change files, environment variables, etc. Those changes will exist only in that container, but would not persist in the underlying container image (would not be saved to disk).
A container image is comparable to the [BOLD:program] file and contents, e.g. [CODE: python] and some file [CODE: main.py].
And the [BOLD:container] itself (in contrast to the [BOLD:container image]) is the actual running instance of the image, comparable to a [BOLD:process]. In fact, a container is running only when it has a [BOLD:process running] (and normally it's only a single process). The container stops when there's no process running in it.

metadata.breadcrumb: FastAPI in Containers - Docker > What is a Container Image

page_content:
[Context: FastAPI in Containers - Docker > What is a Container Image]

## What is a Container Image
A [BOLD:container] is run from a [BOLD:container image].
A container image is a [BOLD:static] version of all the files, environment variables, and the default command/program that should be present in a container. [BOLD:Static] here means that the container [BOLD:image] is not running, it's not being executed, it's only the packaged files and metadata.
In contrast to a "[BOLD:container image]" that is the stored static contents, a "[BOLD:container]" normally refers to the running instance, the thing that is being [BOLD:executed].
When the [BOLD:container] is started and running (started from a [BOLD:container image]) it could create or change files, environment variables, etc. Those changes will exist only in that container, but would not persist in the underlying container image (would not be saved to disk).
A container image is comparable to the [BOLD:program] file and contents, e.g. [CODE: python] and some file [CODE: main.py].
And the [BOLD:container] itself (in contrast to the [BOLD:container image]) is the actual running instance of the image, comparable to a [BOLD:process]. In fact, a container is running only when it has a [BOLD:process running] (and normally it's only a single process). The container stops when there's no process running in it.

metadata.breadcrumb: FastAPI in Containers - Docker > What is a Container Image

page_content:
[Context: FastAPI in Containers - Docker > Container Images]

## Container Images
Docker has been one of the main tools to create and manage [BOLD:container images] and [BOLD:containers].
And there's a public Docker Hub with pre-made [BOLD:official container images] for many tools, environments, databases, and applications.
For example, there's an official Python Image.
And there are many other images for different things like databases, for example for:
[ITALIC: PostgreSQL
] MySQL
[ITALIC: MongoDB
] Redis, etc.
By using a pre-made container image it's very easy to [BOLD:combine] and use different tools. For example, to try out a new database. In most cases, you can use the [BOLD:official images], and just configure them with environment variables.
That way, in many cases you can learn about containers and Docker and reuse that knowledge with many different tools and components.
So, you would run [BOLD:multiple containers] with different things, like a database, a Python application, a web server with a React frontend application, and connect them together via their internal network.
All the container management systems (like Docker or Kubernetes) have these networking features integrated into them.

metadata.breadcrumb: FastAPI in Containers - Docker > Container Images

page_content:
[Context: FastAPI in Containers - Docker > Container Images]

## Container Images
Docker has been one of the main tools to create and manage [BOLD:container images] and [BOLD:containers].
And there's a public Docker Hub with pre-made [BOLD:official container images] for many tools, environments, databases, and applications.
For example, there's an official Python Image.
And there are many other images for different things like databases, for example for:
[ITALIC: PostgreSQL
] MySQL
[ITALIC: MongoDB
] Redis, etc.
By using a pre-made container image it's very easy to [BOLD:combine] and use different tools. For example, to try out a new database. In most cases, you can use the [BOLD:official images], and just configure them with environment variables.
That way, in many cases you can learn about containers and Docker and reuse that knowledge with many different tools and components.
So, you would run [BOLD:multiple containers] with different things, like a database, a Python application, a web server with a React frontend application, and connect them together via their internal network.
All the container management systems (like Docker or Kubernetes) have these networking features integrated into them.

metadata.breadcrumb: FastAPI in Containers - Docker > Container Images

page_content:
[Context: FastAPI in Containers - Docker > Containers and Processes]

## Containers and Processes
A [BOLD:container image] normally includes in its metadata the default program or command that should be run when the [BOLD:container] is started and the parameters to be passed to that program. Very similar to what would be if it was in the command line.
When a [BOLD:container] is started, it will run that command/program (although you can override it and make it run a different command/program).
A container is running as long as the [BOLD:main process] (command or program) is running.
A container normally has a [BOLD:single process], but it's also possible to start subprocesses from the main process, and that way you will have [BOLD:multiple processes] in the same container.
But it's not possible to have a running container without [BOLD:at least one running process]. If the main process stops, the container stops.

metadata.breadcrumb: FastAPI in Containers - Docker > Containers and Processes

page_content:
[Context: FastAPI in Containers - Docker > Build a Docker Image for FastAPI]

## Build a Docker Image for FastAPI
Okay, let's build something now!
I'll show you how to build a [BOLD:Docker image] for FastAPI [BOLD:from scratch], based on the [BOLD:official Python] image.
This is what you would want to do in [BOLD:most cases], for example:
[ITALIC: Using [BOLD:Kubernetes] or similar tools
] When running on a [BOLD:Raspberry Pi]
- Using a cloud service that would run a container image for you, etc.

hierarchy: Build a Docker Image for FastAPI
metadata.breadcrumb: FastAPI in Containers - Docker > Build a Docker Image for FastAPI

page_content:
[Context: FastAPI in Containers - Docker > Build a Docker Image for FastAPI > Package Requirements]

### Package Requirements
You would normally have the [BOLD:package requirements] for your application in some file.
It would depend mainly on the tool you use to [BOLD:install] those requirements.
The most common way to do it is to have a file [CODE: requirements.txt] with the package names and their versions, one per line.
You would of course use the same ideas you read in About FastAPI versions to set the ranges of versions.
For example, your [CODE: requirements.txt] could look like:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: fastapi[standard]>=0.113.0,=2.7....]
And you would normally install those package dependencies with [CODE: pip], for example:

[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]1: $ pip install -r requirements.txt ---> 100% Succes...]

/// info
There are other formats and tools to define and install package dependencies.
///

hierarchy: Build a Docker Image for FastAPI
metadata.breadcrumb: FastAPI in Containers - Docker > Build a Docker Image for FastAPI > Package Requirements

page_content:
[Context: FastAPI in Containers - Docker > Build a Docker Image for FastAPI > Create the **FastAPI** Code]

### Create the [BOLD:FastAPI] Code
[ITALIC: Create an [CODE: app] directory and enter it.
] Create an empty file [CODE: [BOLD:init].py].
- Create a [CODE: main.py] file with:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: from fastapi import FastAPI app = FastAPI() @app.g...]

hierarchy: Build a Docker Image for FastAPI
metadata.breadcrumb: FastAPI in Containers - Docker > Build a Docker Image for FastAPI > Create the **FastAPI** Code

page_content:
[Context: FastAPI in Containers - Docker > Build a Docker Image for FastAPI > Dockerfile]

### Dockerfile
Now in the same project directory create a file [CODE: Dockerfile] with:
```{ .dockerfile .annotate }
# (1)!
FROM python:3.14

# (2)!
WORKDIR /code

# (3)!
COPY ./requirements.txt /code/requirements.txt

# (4)!
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir --upgrade -r /code/requirements.txt

# (5)!
COPY ./app /code/app

# (6)!
CMD ["fastapi", "run", "app/main.py", "--port", "80"]
```
- Start from the official Python base image.
- Set the current working directory to [CODE: /code].
This is where we'll put the [CODE: requirements.txt] file and the [CODE: app] directory.
- Copy the file with the requirements to the [CODE: /code] directory.
Copy [BOLD:only] the file with the requirements first, not the rest of the code.
As this file [BOLD:doesn't change often], Docker will detect it and use the [BOLD:cache] for this step, enabling the cache for the next step too.
- Install the package dependencies in the requirements file.
The [CODE: --no-cache-dir] option tells [CODE: pip] to not save the downloaded packages locally, as that is only if [CODE: pip] was going to be run again to install the same packages, but that's not the case when working with containers.
/// note
The [CODE: --no-cache-dir] is only related to [CODE: pip], it has nothing to do with Docker or containers.
///
The [CODE: --upgrade] option tells [CODE: pip] to upgrade the packages if they are already installed.
Because the previous step copying the file could be detected by the [BOLD:Docker cache], this step will also [BOLD:use the Docker cache] when available.
Using the cache in this step will [BOLD:save] you a lot of [BOLD:time] when building the image again and again during development, instead of [BOLD:downloading and installing] all the dependencies [BOLD:every time].
- Copy the [CODE: ./app] directory inside the [CODE: /code] directory.
As this has all the code which is what [BOLD:changes most frequently] the Docker [BOLD:cache] won't be used for this or any [BOLD:following steps] easily.
So, it's important to put this [BOLD:near the end] of the [CODE: Dockerfile], to optimize the container image build times.
- Set the [BOLD:command] to use [CODE: fastapi run], which uses Uvicorn underneath.
[CODE: CMD] takes a list of strings, each of these strings is what you would type in the command line separated by spaces.
This command will be run from the [BOLD:current working directory], the same [CODE: /code] directory you set above with [CODE: WORKDIR /code].
/// tip
Review what each line does by clicking each number bubble in the code.
///
/// warning
Make sure to [BOLD:always] use the [BOLD:exec form] of the [CODE: CMD] instruction, as explained below.
///
#### Use [CODE: CMD] - Exec Form
The [[CODE: CMD]](https://docs.docker.com/reference/dockerfile/#cmd) Docker instruction can be written using two forms:
[BOLD:Exec] form:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: # Do this CMD ["fastapi", "run", "app/main.py", ...]
[BOLD:Shell] form:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]1: # Don't do this CMD fastapi run app/main.py --p...]
Make sure to always use the [BOLD:exec] form to ensure that FastAPI can shutdown gracefully and lifespan events are triggered.
You can read more about it in the Docker docs for shell and exec form.
This can be quite noticeable when using [CODE: docker compose]. See this Docker Compose FAQ section for more technical details: Why do my services take 10 seconds to recreate or stop?.
#### Directory Structure
You should now have a directory structure like:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]2: . app [BOLD:init].py main.py Dock...]
#### Behind a TLS Termination Proxy
If you are running your container behind a TLS Termination Proxy (load balancer) like Nginx or Traefik, add the option [CODE: --proxy-headers], this will tell Uvicorn (through the FastAPI CLI) to trust the headers sent by that proxy telling it that the application is running behind HTTPS, etc.
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]3: CMD ["fastapi", "run", "app/main.py", "--proxy-hea...]
#### Docker Cache
There's an important trick in this [CODE: Dockerfile], we first copy the [BOLD:file with the dependencies alone], not the rest of the code. Let me tell you why is that.
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]4: COPY ./requirements.txt /code/requirements.txt...]
Docker and other tools [BOLD:build] these container images [BOLD:incrementally], adding [BOLD:one layer on top of the other], starting from the top of the [CODE: Dockerfile] and adding any files created by each of the instructions of the [CODE: Dockerfile].
Docker and similar tools also use an [BOLD:internal cache] when building the image, if a file hasn't changed since the last time building the container image, then it will [BOLD:reuse the same layer] created the last time, instead of copying the file again and creating a new layer from scratch.
Just avoiding the copy of files doesn't necessarily improve things too much, but because it used the cache for that step, it can [BOLD:use the cache for the next step]. For example, it could use the cache for the instruction that installs dependencies with:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]5: RUN pip install --no-cache-dir --upgrade -r /code/...]
The file with the package requirements [BOLD:won't change frequently]. So, by copying only that file, Docker will be able to [BOLD:use the cache] for that step.
And then, Docker will be able to [BOLD:use the cache for the next step] that downloads and install those dependencies. And here's where we [BOLD:save a lot of time]. ...and avoid boredom waiting.
Downloading and installing the package dependencies [BOLD:could take minutes], but using the [BOLD:cache] would [BOLD:take seconds] at most.
And as you would be building the container image again and again during development to check that your code changes are working, there's a lot of accumulated time this would save.
Then, near the end of the [CODE: Dockerfile], we copy all the code. As this is what [BOLD:changes most frequently], we put it near the end, because almost always, anything after this step will not be able to use the cache.
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]6: COPY ./app /code/app...]

hierarchy: Build a Docker Image for FastAPI

Downloading and installing the package dependencies [BOLD:could take minutes], but using the [BOLD:cache] would [BOLD:take seconds] at most.
And as you would be building the container image again and again during development to check that your code changes are working, there's a lot of accumulated time this would save.
Then, near the end of the [CODE: Dockerfile], we copy all the code. As this is what [BOLD:changes most frequently], we put it near the end, because almost always, anything after this step will not be able to use the cache.
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]6: COPY ./app /code/app...]

page_content:
[Context: FastAPI in Containers - Docker > Build a Docker Image for FastAPI > Dockerfile]

### Dockerfile
Now in the same project directory create a file [CODE: Dockerfile] with:
```{ .dockerfile .annotate }
# (1)!
FROM python:3.14

# (2)!
WORKDIR /code

# (3)!
COPY ./requirements.txt /code/requirements.txt

# (4)!
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir --upgrade -r /code/requirements.txt

# (5)!
COPY ./app /code/app

# (6)!
CMD ["fastapi", "run", "app/main.py", "--port", "80"]
```
- Start from the official Python base image.
- Set the current working directory to [CODE: /code].
This is where we'll put the [CODE: requirements.txt] file and the [CODE: app] directory.
- Copy the file with the requirements to the [CODE: /code] directory.
Copy [BOLD:only] the file with the requirements first, not the rest of the code.
As this file [BOLD:doesn't change often], Docker will detect it and use the [BOLD:cache] for this step, enabling the cache for the next step too.
- Install the package dependencies in the requirements file.
The [CODE: --no-cache-dir] option tells [CODE: pip] to not save the downloaded packages locally, as that is only if [CODE: pip] was going to be run again to install the same packages, but that's not the case when working with containers.
/// note
The [CODE: --no-cache-dir] is only related to [CODE: pip], it has nothing to do with Docker or containers.
///
The [CODE: --upgrade] option tells [CODE: pip] to upgrade the packages if they are already installed.
Because the previous step copying the file could be detected by the [BOLD:Docker cache], this step will also [BOLD:use the Docker cache] when available.
Using the cache in this step will [BOLD:save] you a lot of [BOLD:time] when building the image again and again during development, instead of [BOLD:downloading and installing] all the dependencies [BOLD:every time].
- Copy the [CODE: ./app] directory inside the [CODE: /code] directory.
As this has all the code which is what [BOLD:changes most frequently] the Docker [BOLD:cache] won't be used for this or any [BOLD:following steps] easily.
So, it's important to put this [BOLD:near the end] of the [CODE: Dockerfile], to optimize the container image build times.
- Set the [BOLD:command] to use [CODE: fastapi run], which uses Uvicorn underneath.
[CODE: CMD] takes a list of strings, each of these strings is what you would type in the command line separated by spaces.
This command will be run from the [BOLD:current working directory], the same [CODE: /code] directory you set above with [CODE: WORKDIR /code].
/// tip
Review what each line does by clicking each number bubble in the code.
///
/// warning
Make sure to [BOLD:always] use the [BOLD:exec form] of the [CODE: CMD] instruction, as explained below.
///
#### Use [CODE: CMD] - Exec Form
The [[CODE: CMD]](https://docs.docker.com/reference/dockerfile/#cmd) Docker instruction can be written using two forms:
[BOLD:Exec] form:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: # Do this CMD ["fastapi", "run", "app/main.py", ...]
[BOLD:Shell] form:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]1: # Don't do this CMD fastapi run app/main.py --p...]
Make sure to always use the [BOLD:exec] form to ensure that FastAPI can shutdown gracefully and lifespan events are triggered.
You can read more about it in the Docker docs for shell and exec form.
This can be quite noticeable when using [CODE: docker compose]. See this Docker Compose FAQ section for more technical details: Why do my services take 10 seconds to recreate or stop?.
#### Directory Structure
You should now have a directory structure like:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]2: . app [BOLD:init].py main.py Dock...]
#### Behind a TLS Termination Proxy
If you are running your container behind a TLS Termination Proxy (load balancer) like Nginx or Traefik, add the option [CODE: --proxy-headers], this will tell Uvicorn (through the FastAPI CLI) to trust the headers sent by that proxy telling it that the application is running behind HTTPS, etc.
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]3: CMD ["fastapi", "run", "app/main.py", "--proxy-hea...]
#### Docker Cache
There's an important trick in this [CODE: Dockerfile], we first copy the [BOLD:file with the dependencies alone], not the rest of the code. Let me tell you why is that.
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]4: COPY ./requirements.txt /code/requirements.txt...]
Docker and other tools [BOLD:build] these container images [BOLD:incrementally], adding [BOLD:one layer on top of the other], starting from the top of the [CODE: Dockerfile] and adding any files created by each of the instructions of the [CODE: Dockerfile].
Docker and similar tools also use an [BOLD:internal cache] when building the image, if a file hasn't changed since the last time building the container image, then it will [BOLD:reuse the same layer] created the last time, instead of copying the file again and creating a new layer from scratch.
Just avoiding the copy of files doesn't necessarily improve things too much, but because it used the cache for that step, it can [BOLD:use the cache for the next step]. For example, it could use the cache for the instruction that installs dependencies with:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]5: RUN pip install --no-cache-dir --upgrade -r /code/...]
The file with the package requirements [BOLD:won't change frequently]. So, by copying only that file, Docker will be able to [BOLD:use the cache] for that step.
And then, Docker will be able to [BOLD:use the cache for the next step] that downloads and install those dependencies. And here's where we [BOLD:save a lot of time]. ...and avoid boredom waiting.
Downloading and installing the package dependencies [BOLD:could take minutes], but using the [BOLD:cache] would [BOLD:take seconds] at most.
And as you would be building the container image again and again during development to check that your code changes are working, there's a lot of accumulated time this would save.
Then, near the end of the [CODE: Dockerfile], we copy all the code. As this is what [BOLD:changes most frequently], we put it near the end, because almost always, anything after this step will not be able to use the cache.
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]6: COPY ./app /code/app...]

page_content:
[Context: FastAPI in Containers - Docker > Check it]

## Check it
You should be able to check it in your Docker container's URL, for example: http://192.168.99.100/items/5?q=somequery or http://127.0.0.1/items/5?q=somequery (or equivalent, using your Docker host).
You will see something like:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: {"item_id": 5, "q": "somequery"}...]

metadata.breadcrumb: FastAPI in Containers - Docker > Check it

page_content:
[Context: FastAPI in Containers - Docker > Interactive API docs]

## Interactive API docs
Now you can go to http://192.168.99.100/docs or http://127.0.0.1/docs (or equivalent, using your Docker host).
You will see the automatic interactive API documentation (provided by Swagger UI):
!Swagger UI

And you can also go to http://192.168.99.100/redoc or http://127.0.0.1/redoc (or equivalent, using your Docker host).
You will see the alternative automatic documentation (provided by ReDoc):
!ReDoc

metadata.breadcrumb: FastAPI in Containers - Docker > Alternative API docs

page_content:
[Context: FastAPI in Containers - Docker > Build a Docker Image with a Single-File FastAPI]

## Build a Docker Image with a Single-File FastAPI
If your FastAPI is a single file, for example, [CODE: main.py] without an [CODE: ./app] directory, your file structure could look like this:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: . Dockerfile main.py requirements.txt...]
Then you would just have to change the corresponding paths to copy the file inside the [CODE: Dockerfile]:
```{ .dockerfile .annotate hl_lines="10 13" }
FROM python:3.14

WORKDIR /code

COPY ./requirements.txt /code/requirements.txt

RUN pip install --no-cache-dir --upgrade -r /code/requirements.txt

# (1)!
COPY ./main.py /code/

# (2)!
CMD ["fastapi", "run", "main.py", "--port", "80"]
```
- Copy the [CODE: main.py] file to the [CODE: /code] directory directly (without any [CODE: ./app] directory).
- Use [CODE: fastapi run] to serve your application in the single file [CODE: main.py].
When you pass the file to [CODE: fastapi run] it will detect automatically that it is a single file and not part of a package and will know how to import it and serve your FastAPI app.

hierarchy: Build a Docker Image with a Single-File FastAPI

page_content:
[Context: FastAPI in Containers - Docker > Deployment Concepts]

## Deployment Concepts
Let's talk again about some of the same Deployment Concepts in terms of containers.
Containers are mainly a tool to simplify the process of [BOLD:building and deploying] an application, but they don't enforce a particular approach to handle these [BOLD:deployment concepts], and there are several possible strategies.
The [BOLD:good news] is that with each different strategy there's a way to cover all of the deployment concepts.
Let's review these [BOLD:deployment concepts] in terms of containers:
[ITALIC: HTTPS
] Running on startup
[ITALIC: Restarts
] Replication (the number of processes running)
[ITALIC: Memory
] Previous steps before starting

metadata.breadcrumb: FastAPI in Containers - Docker > Deployment Concepts

page_content:
[Context: FastAPI in Containers - Docker > HTTPS]

## HTTPS
If we focus just on the [BOLD:container image] for a FastAPI application (and later the running [BOLD:container]), HTTPS normally would be handled [BOLD:externally] by another tool.
It could be another container, for example with Traefik, handling [BOLD:HTTPS] and [BOLD:automatic] acquisition of [BOLD:certificates].
/// tip
Traefik has integrations with Docker, Kubernetes, and others, so it's very easy to set up and configure HTTPS for your containers with it.
///
Alternatively, HTTPS could be handled by a cloud provider as one of their services (while still running the application in a container).

[Context: FastAPI in Containers - Docker > Running on Startup and Restarts]

## Running on Startup and Restarts
There is normally another tool in charge of [BOLD:starting and running] your container.
It could be [BOLD:Docker] directly, [BOLD:Docker Compose], [BOLD:Kubernetes], a [BOLD:cloud service], etc.
In most (or all) cases, there's a simple option to enable running the container on startup and enabling restarts on failures. For example, in Docker, it's the command line option [CODE: --restart].
Without using containers, making applications run on startup and with restarts can be cumbersome and difficult. But when [BOLD:working with containers] in most cases that functionality is included by default.

metadata.breadcrumb: FastAPI in Containers - Docker > Running on Startup and Restarts

page_content:
[Context: FastAPI in Containers - Docker > Replication - Number of Processes]

## Replication - Number of Processes
If you have a cluster of machines with [BOLD:Kubernetes], Docker Swarm Mode, Nomad, or another similar complex system to manage distributed containers on multiple machines, then you will probably want to [BOLD:handle replication] at the [BOLD:cluster level] instead of using a [BOLD:process manager] (like Uvicorn with workers) in each container.
One of those distributed container management systems like Kubernetes normally has some integrated way of handling [BOLD:replication of containers] while still supporting [BOLD:load balancing] for the incoming requests. All at the [BOLD:cluster level].
In those cases, you would probably want to build a [BOLD:Docker image from scratch] as explained above, installing your dependencies, and running [BOLD:a single Uvicorn process] instead of using multiple Uvicorn workers.

metadata.breadcrumb: FastAPI in Containers - Docker > Replication - Number of Processes

page_content:
[Context: FastAPI in Containers - Docker > Replication - Number of Processes > Load Balancer]

### Load Balancer
When using containers, you would normally have some component [BOLD:listening on the main port]. It could possibly be another container that is also a [BOLD:TLS Termination Proxy] to handle [BOLD:HTTPS] or some similar tool.
As this component would take the [BOLD:load] of requests and distribute that among the workers in a (hopefully) [BOLD:balanced] way, it is also commonly called a [BOLD:Load Balancer].
/// tip
The same [BOLD:TLS Termination Proxy] component used for HTTPS would probably also be a [BOLD:Load Balancer].
///
And when working with containers, the same system you use to start and manage them would already have internal tools to transmit the [BOLD:network communication] (e.g. HTTP requests) from that [BOLD:load balancer] (that could also be a [BOLD:TLS Termination Proxy]) to the container(s) with your app.

metadata.breadcrumb: FastAPI in Containers - Docker > Replication - Number of Processes > Load Balancer

page_content:
[Context: FastAPI in Containers - Docker > Replication - Number of Processes > One Load Balancer - Multiple Worker Containers]

### One Load Balancer - Multiple Worker Containers
When working with [BOLD:Kubernetes] or similar distributed container management systems, using their internal networking mechanisms would allow the single [BOLD:load balancer] that is listening on the main [BOLD:port] to transmit communication (requests) to possibly [BOLD:multiple containers] running your app.
Each of these containers running your app would normally have [BOLD:just one process] (e.g. a Uvicorn process running your FastAPI application). They would all be [BOLD:identical containers], running the same thing, but each with its own process, memory, etc. That way you would take advantage of [BOLD:parallelization] in [BOLD:different cores] of the CPU, or even in [BOLD:different machines].
And the distributed container system with the [BOLD:load balancer] would [BOLD:distribute the requests] to each one of the containers with your app [BOLD:in turns]. So, each request could be handled by one of the multiple [BOLD:replicated containers] running your app.
And normally this [BOLD:load balancer] would be able to handle requests that go to [ITALIC:other] apps in your cluster (e.g. to a different domain, or under a different URL path prefix), and would transmit that communication to the right containers for [ITALIC:that other] application running in your cluster.

hierarchy: One Load Balancer - Multiple Worker Containers
metadata.breadcrumb: FastAPI in Containers - Docker > Replication - Number of Processes > One Load Balancer - Multiple Worker Containers

page_content:
[Context: FastAPI in Containers - Docker > Replication - Number of Processes > One Load Balancer - Multiple Worker Containers]

### One Load Balancer - Multiple Worker Containers
When working with [BOLD:Kubernetes] or similar distributed container management systems, using their internal networking mechanisms would allow the single [BOLD:load balancer] that is listening on the main [BOLD:port] to transmit communication (requests) to possibly [BOLD:multiple containers] running your app.
Each of these containers running your app would normally have [BOLD:just one process] (e.g. a Uvicorn process running your FastAPI application). They would all be [BOLD:identical containers], running the same thing, but each with its own process, memory, etc. That way you would take advantage of [BOLD:parallelization] in [BOLD:different cores] of the CPU, or even in [BOLD:different machines].
And the distributed container system with the [BOLD:load balancer] would [BOLD:distribute the requests] to each one of the containers with your app [BOLD:in turns]. So, each request could be handled by one of the multiple [BOLD:replicated containers] running your app.
And normally this [BOLD:load balancer] would be able to handle requests that go to [ITALIC:other] apps in your cluster (e.g. to a different domain, or under a different URL path prefix), and would transmit that communication to the right containers for [ITALIC:that other] application running in your cluster.

hierarchy: One Load Balancer - Multiple Worker Containers
metadata.breadcrumb: FastAPI in Containers - Docker > Replication - Number of Processes > One Load Balancer - Multiple Worker Containers

page_content:
[Context: FastAPI in Containers - Docker > Replication - Number of Processes > One Process per Container]

### One Process per Container
In this type of scenario, you probably would want to have [BOLD:a single (Uvicorn) process per container], as you would already be handling replication at the cluster level.
So, in this case, you [BOLD:would not] want to have a multiple workers in the container, for example with the [CODE: --workers] command line option. You would want to have just a [BOLD:single Uvicorn process] per container (but probably multiple containers).
Having another process manager inside the container (as would be with multiple workers) would only add [BOLD:unnecessary complexity] that you are most probably already taking care of with your cluster system.

metadata.breadcrumb: FastAPI in Containers - Docker > Replication - Number of Processes > One Process per Container

page_content:
[Context: FastAPI in Containers - Docker > Replication - Number of Processes > Containers with Multiple Processes and Special Cases]

### Containers with Multiple Processes and Special Cases
Of course, there are [BOLD:special cases] where you could want to have [BOLD:a container] with several [BOLD:Uvicorn worker processes] inside.
In those cases, you can use the [CODE: --workers] command line option to set the number of workers that you want to run:
```{ .dockerfile .annotate }
FROM python:3.14

WORKDIR /code

COPY ./requirements.txt /code/requirements.txt

RUN pip install --no-cache-dir --upgrade -r /code/requirements.txt

COPY ./app /code/app

# (1)!
CMD ["fastapi", "run", "app/main.py", "--port", "80", "--workers", "4"]
```
- Here we use the [CODE: --workers] command line option to set the number of workers to 4.
Here are some examples of when that could make sense:
#### A Simple App
You could want a process manager in the container if your application is [BOLD:simple enough] that can run it on a [BOLD:single server], not a cluster.
#### Docker Compose
You could be deploying to a [BOLD:single server] (not a cluster) with [BOLD:Docker Compose], so you wouldn't have an easy way to manage replication of containers (with Docker Compose) while preserving the shared network and [BOLD:load balancing].
Then you could want to have [BOLD:a single container] with a [BOLD:process manager] starting [BOLD:several worker processes] inside.
---
The main point is, [BOLD:none] of these are [BOLD:rules written in stone] that you have to blindly follow. You can use these ideas to [BOLD:evaluate your own use case] and decide what is the best approach for your system, checking out how to manage the concepts of:
[ITALIC: Security - HTTPS
] Running on startup
[ITALIC: Restarts
] Replication (the number of processes running)
[ITALIC: Memory
] Previous steps before starting

hierarchy: Containers with Multiple Processes and Special Cases
metadata.breadcrumb: FastAPI in Containers - Docker > Replication - Number of Processes > Containers with Multiple Processes and Special Cases

page_content:
[Context: FastAPI in Containers - Docker > Memory]

## Memory
If you run [BOLD:a single process per container] you will have a more or less well-defined, stable, and limited amount of memory consumed by each of those containers (more than one if they are replicated).
And then you can set those same memory limits and requirements in your configurations for your container management system (for example in [BOLD:Kubernetes]). That way it will be able to [BOLD:replicate the containers] in the [BOLD:available machines] taking into account the amount of memory needed by them, and the amount available in the machines in the cluster.
If your application is [BOLD:simple], this will probably [BOLD:not be a problem], and you might not need to specify hard memory limits. But if you are [BOLD:using a lot of memory] (for example with [BOLD:machine learning] models), you should check how much memory you are consuming and adjust the [BOLD:number of containers] that runs in [BOLD:each machine] (and maybe add more machines to your cluster).
If you run [BOLD:multiple processes per container] you will have to make sure that the number of processes started doesn't [BOLD:consume more memory] than what is available.

[Context: FastAPI in Containers - Docker > Memory]

## Memory
If you run [BOLD:a single process per container] you will have a more or less well-defined, stable, and limited amount of memory consumed by each of those containers (more than one if they are replicated).
And then you can set those same memory limits and requirements in your configurations for your container management system (for example in [BOLD:Kubernetes]). That way it will be able to [BOLD:replicate the containers] in the [BOLD:available machines] taking into account the amount of memory needed by them, and the amount available in the machines in the cluster.
If your application is [BOLD:simple], this will probably [BOLD:not be a problem], and you might not need to specify hard memory limits. But if you are [BOLD:using a lot of memory] (for example with [BOLD:machine learning] models), you should check how much memory you are consuming and adjust the [BOLD:number of containers] that runs in [BOLD:each machine] (and maybe add more machines to your cluster).
If you run [BOLD:multiple processes per container] you will have to make sure that the number of processes started doesn't [BOLD:consume more memory] than what is available.

[Context: FastAPI in Containers - Docker > Previous Steps Before Starting and Containers]

## Previous Steps Before Starting and Containers
If you are using containers (e.g. Docker, Kubernetes), then there are two main approaches you can use.

hierarchy: Previous Steps Before Starting and Containers
metadata.breadcrumb: FastAPI in Containers - Docker > Previous Steps Before Starting and Containers

page_content:
[Context: FastAPI in Containers - Docker > Previous Steps Before Starting and Containers > Multiple Containers]

### Multiple Containers
If you have [BOLD:multiple containers], probably each one running a [BOLD:single process] (for example, in a [BOLD:Kubernetes] cluster), then you would probably want to have a [BOLD:separate container] doing the work of the [BOLD:previous steps] in a single container, running a single process, [BOLD:before] running the replicated worker containers.
/// info
If you are using Kubernetes, this would probably be an Init Container.
///
If in your use case there's no problem in running those previous steps [BOLD:multiple times in parallel] (for example if you are not running database migrations, but just checking if the database is ready yet), then you could also just put them in each container right before starting the main process.

hierarchy: Previous Steps Before Starting and Containers
metadata.breadcrumb: FastAPI in Containers - Docker > Previous Steps Before Starting and Containers > Multiple Containers

page_content:
[Context: FastAPI in Containers - Docker > Previous Steps Before Starting and Containers > Single Container]

### Single Container
If you have a simple setup, with a [BOLD:single container] that then starts multiple [BOLD:worker processes] (or also just one process), then you could run those previous steps in the same container, right before starting the process with the app.

hierarchy: Previous Steps Before Starting and Containers
metadata.breadcrumb: FastAPI in Containers - Docker > Previous Steps Before Starting and Containers > Single Container

page_content:
[Context: FastAPI in Containers - Docker > Previous Steps Before Starting and Containers > Base Docker Image]

### Base Docker Image
There used to be an official FastAPI Docker image: tiangolo/uvicorn-gunicorn-fastapi. But it is now deprecated.
You should probably [BOLD:not] use this base Docker image (or any other similar one).
If you are using [BOLD:Kubernetes] (or others) and you are already setting [BOLD:replication] at the cluster level, with multiple [BOLD:containers]. In those cases, you are better off [BOLD:building an image from scratch] as described above: Build a Docker Image for FastAPI.
And if you need to have multiple workers, you can simply use the [CODE: --workers] command line option.
/// note | Technical Details
The Docker image was created when Uvicorn didn't support managing and restarting dead workers, so it was needed to use Gunicorn with Uvicorn, which added quite some complexity, just to have Gunicorn manage and restart the Uvicorn worker processes.
But now that Uvicorn (and the [CODE: fastapi] command) support using [CODE: --workers], there's no reason to use a base Docker image instead of building your own (it's pretty much the same amount of code ).
///

hierarchy: Previous Steps Before Starting and Containers
metadata.breadcrumb: FastAPI in Containers - Docker > Previous Steps Before Starting and Containers > Base Docker Image

page_content:
[Context: FastAPI in Containers - Docker > Previous Steps Before Starting and Containers > Base Docker Image]

### Base Docker Image
There used to be an official FastAPI Docker image: tiangolo/uvicorn-gunicorn-fastapi. But it is now deprecated.
You should probably [BOLD:not] use this base Docker image (or any other similar one).
If you are using [BOLD:Kubernetes] (or others) and you are already setting [BOLD:replication] at the cluster level, with multiple [BOLD:containers]. In those cases, you are better off [BOLD:building an image from scratch] as described above: Build a Docker Image for FastAPI.
And if you need to have multiple workers, you can simply use the [CODE: --workers] command line option.
/// note | Technical Details
The Docker image was created when Uvicorn didn't support managing and restarting dead workers, so it was needed to use Gunicorn with Uvicorn, which added quite some complexity, just to have Gunicorn manage and restart the Uvicorn worker processes.
But now that Uvicorn (and the [CODE: fastapi] command) support using [CODE: --workers], there's no reason to use a base Docker image instead of building your own (it's pretty much the same amount of code ).
///

After having a Container (Docker) Image there are several ways to deploy it.
For example:
[ITALIC: With [BOLD:Docker Compose] in a single server
] With a [BOLD:Kubernetes] cluster
[ITALIC: With a Docker Swarm Mode cluster
] With another tool like Nomad
- With a cloud service that takes your container image and deploys it

If you are using uv to install and manage your project, you can follow their uv Docker guide.
