page_content:
[Context: FastAPI in Containers - Docker > Recap]

## Recap
Using container systems (e.g. with [BOLD:Docker] and [BOLD:Kubernetes]) it becomes fairly straightforward to handle all the [BOLD:deployment concepts]:
[ITALIC: HTTPS
] Running on startup
[ITALIC: Restarts
] Replication (the number of processes running)
[ITALIC: Memory
] Previous steps before starting
In most cases, you probably won't want to use any base image, and instead [BOLD:build a container image from scratch] based on the official Python Docker image.
Taking care of the [BOLD:order] of instructions in the [CODE: Dockerfile] and the [BOLD:Docker cache] you can [BOLD:minimize build times], to maximize your productivity (and avoid boredom).

[Context: FastAPI Cloud]

# FastAPI Cloud
You can deploy your FastAPI app to FastAPI Cloud with [BOLD:one command], go and join the waiting list if you haven't.

[Context: FastAPI Cloud > Login]

## Login
Make sure you already have a [BOLD:FastAPI Cloud] account (we invited you from the waiting list ).
Then log in:

[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: $ fastapi login You are logged in to FastAPI Cloud...]

[Context: FastAPI Cloud > Deploy]

## Deploy
Now deploy your app, with [BOLD:one command]:

[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: $ fastapi deploy Deploying to FastAPI Cloud... D...]

That's it! Now you can access your app at that URL.

[Context: FastAPI Cloud > About FastAPI Cloud]

## About FastAPI Cloud
[BOLD:FastAPI Cloud] is built by the same author and team behind [BOLD:FastAPI].
It streamlines the process of [BOLD:building], [BOLD:deploying], and [BOLD:accessing] an API with minimal effort.
It brings the same [BOLD:developer experience] of building apps with FastAPI to [BOLD:deploying] them to the cloud.
It will also take care of most of the things you would need when deploying an app, like:
[ITALIC: HTTPS
] Replication, with autoscaling based on requests
[ITALIC: etc.
FastAPI Cloud is the primary sponsor and funding provider for the ]FastAPI and friends* open source projects.

[Context: FastAPI Cloud > Deploy to other cloud providers]

## Deploy to other cloud providers
FastAPI is open source and based on standards. You can deploy FastAPI apps to any cloud provider you choose.
Follow your cloud provider's guides to deploy FastAPI apps with them. 🤓

metadata.breadcrumb: FastAPI Cloud > Deploy to other cloud providers

page_content:
[Context: FastAPI Cloud > Deploy your own server]

## Deploy your own server
I will also teach you later in this [BOLD:Deployment] guide all the details, so you can understand what is going on, what needs to happen, or how to deploy FastAPI apps on your own, also with your own servers. 🤓

metadata.breadcrumb: FastAPI Cloud > Deploy your own server

page_content:
[Context: About HTTPS > Let's Encrypt]

## Let's Encrypt
Before Let's Encrypt, these [BOLD:HTTPS certificates] were sold by trusted third parties.
The process to acquire one of these certificates used to be cumbersome, require quite some paperwork and the certificates were quite expensive.
But then [BOLD:Let's Encrypt] was created.
It is a project from the Linux Foundation. It provides [BOLD:HTTPS certificates for free], in an automated way. These certificates use all the standard cryptographic security, and are short-lived (about 3 months), so the [BOLD:security is actually better] because of their reduced lifespan.
The domains are securely verified and the certificates are generated automatically. This also allows automating the renewal of these certificates.
The idea is to automate the acquisition and renewal of these certificates so that you can have [BOLD:secure HTTPS, for free, forever].

[Context: About HTTPS > HTTPS for Developers]

## HTTPS for Developers
Here's an example of how an HTTPS API could look like, step by step, paying attention mainly to the ideas important for developers.

[Context: About HTTPS > HTTPS for Developers > Domain Name]

### Domain Name
It would probably all start by you [BOLD:acquiring] some [BOLD:domain name]. Then, you would configure it in a DNS server (possibly your same cloud provider).
You would probably get a cloud server (a virtual machine) or something similar, and it would have a fixed [BOLD:public IP address].
In the DNS server(s) you would configure a record (an "[CODE: A record]") to point [BOLD:your domain] to the public [BOLD:IP address of your server].
You would probably do this just once, the first time, when setting everything up.
/// tip
This Domain Name part is way before HTTPS, but as everything depends on the domain and the IP address, it's worth mentioning it here.
///

metadata.breadcrumb: About HTTPS > HTTPS for Developers > Domain Name

page_content:
[Context: About HTTPS > HTTPS for Developers > DNS]

### DNS
Now let's focus on all the actual HTTPS parts.
First, the browser would check with the [BOLD:DNS servers] what is the [BOLD:IP for the domain], in this case, [CODE: someapp.example.com].
The DNS servers would tell the browser to use some specific [BOLD:IP address]. That would be the public IP address used by your server, that you configured in the DNS servers.

metadata.breadcrumb: About HTTPS > HTTPS for Developers > DNS

page_content:
[Context: About HTTPS > HTTPS for Developers > TLS Handshake Start]

### TLS Handshake Start
The browser would then communicate with that IP address on [BOLD:port 443] (the HTTPS port).
The first part of the communication is just to establish the connection between the client and the server and to decide the cryptographic keys they will use, etc.

This interaction between the client and the server to establish the TLS connection is called the [BOLD:TLS handshake].

metadata.breadcrumb: About HTTPS > HTTPS for Developers > TLS Handshake Start

page_content:
[Context: About HTTPS > HTTPS for Developers > TLS with SNI Extension]

### TLS with SNI Extension
[BOLD:Only one process] in the server can be listening on a specific [BOLD:port] in a specific [BOLD:IP address]. There could be other processes listening on other ports in the same IP address, but only one for each combination of IP address and port.
TLS (HTTPS) uses the specific port [CODE: 443] by default. So that's the port we would need.
As only one process can be listening on this port, the process that would do it would be the [BOLD:TLS Termination Proxy].
The TLS Termination Proxy would have access to one or more [BOLD:TLS certificates] (HTTPS certificates).
Using the [BOLD:SNI extension] discussed above, the TLS Termination Proxy would check which of the TLS (HTTPS) certificates available it should use for this connection, using the one that matches the domain expected by the client.
In this case, it would use the certificate for [CODE: someapp.example.com].

The client already [BOLD:trusts] the entity that generated that TLS certificate (in this case Let's Encrypt, but we'll see about that later), so it can [BOLD:verify] that the certificate is valid.
Then, using the certificate, the client and the TLS Termination Proxy [BOLD:decide how to encrypt] the rest of the [BOLD:TCP communication]. This completes the [BOLD:TLS Handshake] part.
After this, the client and the server have an [BOLD:encrypted TCP connection], this is what TLS provides. And then they can use that connection to start the actual [BOLD:HTTP communication].
And that's what [BOLD:HTTPS] is, it's just plain [BOLD:HTTP] inside a [BOLD:secure TLS connection] instead of a pure (unencrypted) TCP connection.
/// tip
Notice that the encryption of the communication happens at the [BOLD:TCP level], not at the HTTP level.
///

metadata.breadcrumb: About HTTPS > HTTPS for Developers > TLS with SNI Extension

page_content:
[Context: About HTTPS > HTTPS for Developers > TLS with SNI Extension]

### TLS with SNI Extension
[BOLD:Only one process] in the server can be listening on a specific [BOLD:port] in a specific [BOLD:IP address]. There could be other processes listening on other ports in the same IP address, but only one for each combination of IP address and port.
TLS (HTTPS) uses the specific port [CODE: 443] by default. So that's the port we would need.
As only one process can be listening on this port, the process that would do it would be the [BOLD:TLS Termination Proxy].
The TLS Termination Proxy would have access to one or more [BOLD:TLS certificates] (HTTPS certificates).
Using the [BOLD:SNI extension] discussed above, the TLS Termination Proxy would check which of the TLS (HTTPS) certificates available it should use for this connection, using the one that matches the domain expected by the client.
In this case, it would use the certificate for [CODE: someapp.example.com].

The client already [BOLD:trusts] the entity that generated that TLS certificate (in this case Let's Encrypt, but we'll see about that later), so it can [BOLD:verify] that the certificate is valid.
Then, using the certificate, the client and the TLS Termination Proxy [BOLD:decide how to encrypt] the rest of the [BOLD:TCP communication]. This completes the [BOLD:TLS Handshake] part.
After this, the client and the server have an [BOLD:encrypted TCP connection], this is what TLS provides. And then they can use that connection to start the actual [BOLD:HTTP communication].
And that's what [BOLD:HTTPS] is, it's just plain [BOLD:HTTP] inside a [BOLD:secure TLS connection] instead of a pure (unencrypted) TCP connection.
/// tip
Notice that the encryption of the communication happens at the [BOLD:TCP level], not at the HTTP level.
///

metadata.breadcrumb: About HTTPS > HTTPS for Developers > TLS with SNI Extension

page_content:
[Context: About HTTPS > HTTPS for Developers > HTTPS Request]

### HTTPS Request
Now that the client and server (specifically the browser and the TLS Termination Proxy) have an [BOLD:encrypted TCP connection], they can start the [BOLD:HTTP communication].
So, the client sends an [BOLD:HTTPS request]. This is just an HTTP request through an encrypted TLS connection.

metadata.breadcrumb: About HTTPS > HTTPS for Developers > HTTPS Request

page_content:
[Context: About HTTPS > HTTPS for Developers > Decrypt the Request]

### Decrypt the Request
The TLS Termination Proxy would use the encryption agreed to [BOLD:decrypt the request], and would transmit the [BOLD:plain (decrypted) HTTP request] to the process running the application (for example a process with Uvicorn running the FastAPI application).

metadata.breadcrumb: About HTTPS > HTTPS for Developers > Decrypt the Request

page_content:
[Context: About HTTPS > HTTPS for Developers > HTTP Response]

### HTTP Response
The application would process the request and send a [BOLD:plain (unencrypted) HTTP response] to the TLS Termination Proxy.

metadata.breadcrumb: About HTTPS > HTTPS for Developers > HTTP Response

page_content:
[Context: About HTTPS > HTTPS for Developers > HTTPS Response]

### HTTPS Response
The TLS Termination Proxy would then [BOLD:encrypt the response] using the cryptography agreed before (that started with the certificate for [CODE: someapp.example.com]), and send it back to the browser.
Next, the browser would verify that the response is valid and encrypted with the right cryptographic key, etc. It would then [BOLD:decrypt the response] and process it.

The client (browser) will know that the response comes from the correct server because it is using the cryptography they agreed using the [BOLD:HTTPS certificate] before.

metadata.breadcrumb: About HTTPS > HTTPS for Developers > HTTPS Response

page_content:
[Context: About HTTPS > HTTPS for Developers > Multiple Applications]

### Multiple Applications
In the same server (or servers), there could be [BOLD:multiple applications], for example, other API programs or a database.
Only one process can be handling the specific IP and port (the TLS Termination Proxy in our example) but the other applications/processes can be running on the server(s) too, as long as they don't try to use the same [BOLD:combination of public IP and port].

That way, the TLS Termination Proxy could handle HTTPS and certificates for [BOLD:multiple domains], for multiple applications, and then transmit the requests to the right application in each case.

metadata.breadcrumb: About HTTPS > HTTPS for Developers > Multiple Applications

page_content:
[Context: About HTTPS > HTTPS for Developers > Certificate Renewal]

### Certificate Renewal
At some point in the future, each certificate would [BOLD:expire] (about 3 months after acquiring it).
And then, there would be another program (in some cases it's another program, in some cases it could be the same TLS Termination Proxy) that would talk to Let's Encrypt, and renew the certificate(s).

The [BOLD:TLS certificates] are [BOLD:associated with a domain name], not with an IP address.
So, to renew the certificates, the renewal program needs to [BOLD:prove] to the authority (Let's Encrypt) that it indeed [BOLD:"owns" and controls that domain].
To do that, and to accommodate different application needs, there are several ways it can do it. Some popular ways are:
[ITALIC: [BOLD:Modify some DNS records].
] For this, the renewal program needs to support the APIs of the DNS provider, so, depending on the DNS provider you are using, this might or might not be an option.
[ITALIC: [BOLD:Run as a server] (at least during the certificate acquisition process) on the public IP address associated with the domain.
] As we said above, only one process can be listening on a specific IP and port.
[ITALIC: This is one of the reasons why it's very useful when the same TLS Termination Proxy also takes care of the certificate renewal process.
] Otherwise, you might have to stop the TLS Termination Proxy momentarily, start the renewal program to acquire the certificates, then configure them with the TLS Termination Proxy, and then restart the TLS Termination Proxy. This is not ideal, as your app(s) will not be available during the time that the TLS Termination Proxy is off.
All this renewal process, while still serving the app, is one of the main reasons why you would want to have a [BOLD:separate system to handle HTTPS] with a TLS Termination Proxy instead of just using the TLS certificates with the application server directly (e.g. Uvicorn).

metadata.breadcrumb: About HTTPS > HTTPS for Developers > Certificate Renewal

page_content:
[Context: About HTTPS > HTTPS for Developers > Certificate Renewal]

### Certificate Renewal
At some point in the future, each certificate would [BOLD:expire] (about 3 months after acquiring it).
And then, there would be another program (in some cases it's another program, in some cases it could be the same TLS Termination Proxy) that would talk to Let's Encrypt, and renew the certificate(s).

The [BOLD:TLS certificates] are [BOLD:associated with a domain name], not with an IP address.
So, to renew the certificates, the renewal program needs to [BOLD:prove] to the authority (Let's Encrypt) that it indeed [BOLD:"owns" and controls that domain].
To do that, and to accommodate different application needs, there are several ways it can do it. Some popular ways are:
[ITALIC: [BOLD:Modify some DNS records].
] For this, the renewal program needs to support the APIs of the DNS provider, so, depending on the DNS provider you are using, this might or might not be an option.
[ITALIC: [BOLD:Run as a server] (at least during the certificate acquisition process) on the public IP address associated with the domain.
] As we said above, only one process can be listening on a specific IP and port.
[ITALIC: This is one of the reasons why it's very useful when the same TLS Termination Proxy also takes care of the certificate renewal process.
] Otherwise, you might have to stop the TLS Termination Proxy momentarily, start the renewal program to acquire the certificates, then configure them with the TLS Termination Proxy, and then restart the TLS Termination Proxy. This is not ideal, as your app(s) will not be available during the time that the TLS Termination Proxy is off.
All this renewal process, while still serving the app, is one of the main reasons why you would want to have a [BOLD:separate system to handle HTTPS] with a TLS Termination Proxy instead of just using the TLS certificates with the application server directly (e.g. Uvicorn).

metadata.breadcrumb: About HTTPS > HTTPS for Developers > Certificate Renewal

page_content:
[Context: About HTTPS > Proxy Forwarded Headers]

## Proxy Forwarded Headers
When using a proxy to handle HTTPS, your [BOLD:application server] (for example Uvicorn via FastAPI CLI) doesn't known anything about the HTTPS process, it communicates with plain HTTP with the [BOLD:TLS Termination Proxy].
This [BOLD:proxy] would normally set some HTTP headers on the fly before transmitting the request to the [BOLD:application server], to let the application server know that the request is being [BOLD:forwarded] by the proxy.
/// note | Technical Details
The proxy headers are:
[ITALIC: X-Forwarded-For
] X-Forwarded-Proto
[ITALIC: X-Forwarded-Host
///
Nevertheless, as the [BOLD:application server] doesn't know it is behind a trusted [BOLD:proxy], by default, it wouldn't trust those headers.
But you can configure the [BOLD:application server] to trust the ]forwarded[ITALIC: headers sent by the [BOLD:proxy]. If you are using FastAPI CLI, you can use the ]CLI Option[ITALIC: [CODE: --forwarded-allow-ips] to tell it from which IPs it should trust those ]forwarded[ITALIC: headers.
For example, if the [BOLD:application server] is only receiving communication from the trusted [BOLD:proxy], you can set it to [CODE: --forwarded-allow-ips="]"] to make it trust all incoming IPs, as it will only receive requests from whatever is the IP used by the [BOLD:proxy].
This way the application would be able to know what is its own public URL, if it is using HTTPS, the domain, etc.
This would be useful for example to properly handle redirects.
/// tip
You can learn more about this in the documentation for Behind a Proxy - Enable Proxy Forwarded Headers
///

[Context: About HTTPS > Proxy Forwarded Headers]

## Proxy Forwarded Headers
When using a proxy to handle HTTPS, your [BOLD:application server] (for example Uvicorn via FastAPI CLI) doesn't known anything about the HTTPS process, it communicates with plain HTTP with the [BOLD:TLS Termination Proxy].
This [BOLD:proxy] would normally set some HTTP headers on the fly before transmitting the request to the [BOLD:application server], to let the application server know that the request is being [BOLD:forwarded] by the proxy.
/// note | Technical Details
The proxy headers are:
[ITALIC: X-Forwarded-For
] X-Forwarded-Proto
[ITALIC: X-Forwarded-Host
///
Nevertheless, as the [BOLD:application server] doesn't know it is behind a trusted [BOLD:proxy], by default, it wouldn't trust those headers.
But you can configure the [BOLD:application server] to trust the ]forwarded[ITALIC: headers sent by the [BOLD:proxy]. If you are using FastAPI CLI, you can use the ]CLI Option[ITALIC: [CODE: --forwarded-allow-ips] to tell it from which IPs it should trust those ]forwarded[ITALIC: headers.
For example, if the [BOLD:application server] is only receiving communication from the trusted [BOLD:proxy], you can set it to [CODE: --forwarded-allow-ips="]"] to make it trust all incoming IPs, as it will only receive requests from whatever is the IP used by the [BOLD:proxy].
This way the application would be able to know what is its own public URL, if it is using HTTPS, the domain, etc.
This would be useful for example to properly handle redirects.
/// tip
You can learn more about this in the documentation for Behind a Proxy - Enable Proxy Forwarded Headers
///

[Context: About HTTPS > Recap]

## Recap
Having [BOLD:HTTPS] is very important, and quite [BOLD:critical] in most cases. Most of the effort you as a developer have to put around HTTPS is just about [BOLD:understanding these concepts] and how they work.
But once you know the basic information of [BOLD:HTTPS for developers] you can easily combine and configure different tools to help you manage everything in a simple way.
In some of the next chapters, I'll show you several concrete examples of how to set up [BOLD:HTTPS] for [BOLD:FastAPI] applications.

[Context: Deployment]

# Deployment
Deploying a [BOLD:FastAPI] application is relatively easy.

[Context: Deployment > What Does Deployment Mean]

## What Does Deployment Mean
To [BOLD:deploy] an application means to perform the necessary steps to make it [BOLD:available to the users].
For a [BOLD:web API], it normally involves putting it in a [BOLD:remote machine], with a [BOLD:server program] that provides good performance, stability, etc, so that your [BOLD:users] can [BOLD:access] the application efficiently and without interruptions or problems.
This is in contrast to the [BOLD:development] stages, where you are constantly changing the code, breaking it and fixing it, stopping and restarting the development server, etc.

[Context: Deployment > Deployment Strategies]

## Deployment Strategies
There are several ways to do it depending on your specific use case and the tools that you use.
You could [BOLD:deploy a server] yourself using a combination of tools, you could use a [BOLD:cloud service] that does part of the work for you, or other possible options.
For example, we, the team behind FastAPI, built [BOLD:FastAPI Cloud], to make deploying FastAPI apps to the cloud as streamlined as possible, with the same developer experience of working with FastAPI.
I will show you some of the main concepts you should probably keep in mind when deploying a [BOLD:FastAPI] application (although most of it applies to any other type of web application).
You will see more details to keep in mind and some of the techniques to do it in the next sections.

[Context: Run a Server Manually > Use the `fastapi run` Command]

# Run a Server Manually
## Use the [CODE: fastapi run] Command
In short, use [CODE: fastapi run] to serve your FastAPI application:

[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: $ fastapi run
That would work for most of the cases.
You could use that command for example to start your [BOLD:FastAPI] app in a container, in a server, etc.

metadata.breadcrumb: Run a Server Manually > Use the `fastapi run` Command

page_content:
[Context: Run a Server Manually > ASGI Servers]

## ASGI Servers
Let's go a little deeper into the details.
FastAPI uses a standard for building Python web frameworks and servers called ASGI. FastAPI is an ASGI web framework.
The main thing you need to run a [BOLD:FastAPI] application (or any other ASGI application) in a remote server machine is an ASGI server program like [BOLD:Uvicorn], this is the one that comes by default in the [CODE: fastapi] command.
There are several alternatives, including:
[ITALIC: Uvicorn: a high performance ASGI server.
] Hypercorn: an ASGI server compatible with HTTP/2 and Trio among other features.
[ITALIC: Daphne: the ASGI server built for Django Channels.
] Granian: A Rust HTTP server for Python applications.
- NGINX Unit: NGINX Unit is a lightweight and versatile web application runtime.

metadata.breadcrumb: Run a Server Manually > ASGI Servers

page_content:
[Context: Run a Server Manually > Server Machine and Server Program]

## Server Machine and Server Program
There's a small detail about names to keep in mind.
The word "[BOLD:server]" is commonly used to refer to both the remote/cloud computer (the physical or virtual machine) and also the program that is running on that machine (e.g. Uvicorn).
Just keep in mind that when you read "server" in general, it could refer to one of those two things.
When referring to the remote machine, it's common to call it [BOLD:server], but also [BOLD:machine], [BOLD:VM] (virtual machine), [BOLD:node]. Those all refer to some type of remote machine, normally running Linux, where you run programs.

metadata.breadcrumb: Run a Server Manually > Server Machine and Server Program

page_content:
[Context: Run a Server Manually > Install the Server Program]

## Install the Server Program
When you install FastAPI, it comes with a production server, Uvicorn, and you can start it with the [CODE: fastapi run] command.
But you can also install an ASGI server manually.
Make sure you create a virtual environment, activate it, and then you can install the server application.
For example, to install Uvicorn:

[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: $ pip install "uvicorn[standard]" ---> 100%...]

A similar process would apply to any other ASGI server program.
/// tip
By adding the [CODE: standard], Uvicorn will install and use some recommended extra dependencies.
That including [CODE: uvloop], the high-performance drop-in replacement for [CODE: asyncio], that provides the big concurrency performance boost.
When you install FastAPI with something like [CODE: pip install "fastapi[standard]"] you already get [CODE: uvicorn[standard]] as well.
///

metadata.breadcrumb: Run a Server Manually > Install the Server Program

page_content:
[Context: Run a Server Manually > Run the Server Program]

## Run the Server Program
If you installed an ASGI server manually, you would normally need to pass an import string in a special format for it to import your FastAPI application:

[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: $ uvicorn main:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port 80
/// note
The command [CODE: uvicorn main:app] refers to:
[ITALIC: [CODE: main]: the file [CODE: main.py] (the Python "module").
] [CODE: app]: the object created inside of [CODE: main.py] with the line [CODE: app = FastAPI()].
It is equivalent to:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]1: from main import app...]
///
Each alternative ASGI server program would have a similar command, you can read more in their respective documentation.
/// warning
Uvicorn and other servers support a [CODE: --reload] option that is useful during development.
The [CODE: --reload] option consumes much more resources, is more unstable, etc.
It helps a lot during [BOLD:development], but you [BOLD:shouldn't] use it in [BOLD:production].
///

metadata.breadcrumb: Run a Server Manually > Run the Server Program

page_content:
[Context: Run a Server Manually > Run the Server Program]

## Run the Server Program
If you installed an ASGI server manually, you would normally need to pass an import string in a special format for it to import your FastAPI application:

[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: $ uvicorn main:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port 80
/// note
The command [CODE: uvicorn main:app] refers to:
[ITALIC: [CODE: main]: the file [CODE: main.py] (the Python "module").
] [CODE: app]: the object created inside of [CODE: main.py] with the line [CODE: app = FastAPI()].
It is equivalent to:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]1: from main import app...]
///
Each alternative ASGI server program would have a similar command, you can read more in their respective documentation.
/// warning
Uvicorn and other servers support a [CODE: --reload] option that is useful during development.
The [CODE: --reload] option consumes much more resources, is more unstable, etc.
It helps a lot during [BOLD:development], but you [BOLD:shouldn't] use it in [BOLD:production].
///

metadata.breadcrumb: Run a Server Manually > Run the Server Program

page_content:
[Context: Run a Server Manually > Deployment Concepts]

## Deployment Concepts
These examples run the server program (e.g Uvicorn), starting [BOLD:a single process], listening on all the IPs ([CODE: 0.0.0.0]) on a predefined port (e.g. [CODE: 80]).
This is the basic idea. But you will probably want to take care of some additional things, like:
[ITALIC: Security - HTTPS
] Running on startup
[ITALIC: Restarts
] Replication (the number of processes running)
[ITALIC: Memory
] Previous steps before starting
I'll tell you more about each of these concepts, how to think about them, and some concrete examples with strategies to handle them in the next chapters.

metadata.breadcrumb: Run a Server Manually > Deployment Concepts

page_content:
[Context: Server Workers - Uvicorn with Workers]

# Server Workers - Uvicorn with Workers
Let's check back those deployment concepts from before:
[ITALIC: Security - HTTPS
] Running on startup
[ITALIC: Restarts
] [BOLD:Replication (the number of processes running)]
[ITALIC: Memory
] Previous steps before starting
Up to this point, with all the tutorials in the docs, you have probably been running a [BOLD:server program], for example, using the [CODE: fastapi] command, that runs Uvicorn, running a [BOLD:single process].
When deploying applications you will probably want to have some [BOLD:replication of processes] to take advantage of [BOLD:multiple cores] and to be able to handle more requests.
As you saw in the previous chapter about Deployment Concepts, there are multiple strategies you can use.
Here I'll show you how to use [BOLD:Uvicorn] with [BOLD:worker processes] using the [CODE: fastapi] command or the [CODE: uvicorn] command directly.
/// info
If you are using containers, for example with Docker or Kubernetes, I'll tell you more about that in the next chapter: FastAPI in Containers - Docker.
In particular, when running on [BOLD:Kubernetes] you will probably [BOLD:not] want to use workers and instead run [BOLD:a single Uvicorn process per container], but I'll tell you about it later in that chapter.
///

[Context: Server Workers - Uvicorn with Workers]

# Server Workers - Uvicorn with Workers
Let's check back those deployment concepts from before:
[ITALIC: Security - HTTPS
] Running on startup
[ITALIC: Restarts
] [BOLD:Replication (the number of processes running)]
[ITALIC: Memory
] Previous steps before starting
Up to this point, with all the tutorials in the docs, you have probably been running a [BOLD:server program], for example, using the [CODE: fastapi] command, that runs Uvicorn, running a [BOLD:single process].
When deploying applications you will probably want to have some [BOLD:replication of processes] to take advantage of [BOLD:multiple cores] and to be able to handle more requests.
As you saw in the previous chapter about Deployment Concepts, there are multiple strategies you can use.
Here I'll show you how to use [BOLD:Uvicorn] with [BOLD:worker processes] using the [CODE: fastapi] command or the [CODE: uvicorn] command directly.
/// info
If you are using containers, for example with Docker or Kubernetes, I'll tell you more about that in the next chapter: FastAPI in Containers - Docker.
In particular, when running on [BOLD:Kubernetes] you will probably [BOLD:not] want to use workers and instead run [BOLD:a single Uvicorn process per container], but I'll tell you about it later in that chapter.
///

metadata.breadcrumb: Server Workers - Uvicorn with Workers > Multiple Workers
Server Workers - Uvicorn with Workers > Deployment Concepts

page_content:
[Context: Server Workers - Uvicorn with Workers > Containers and Docker]

## Containers and Docker
In the next chapter about FastAPI in Containers - Docker I'll explain some strategies you could use to handle the other [BOLD:deployment concepts].
I'll show you how to [BOLD:build your own image from scratch] to run a single Uvicorn process. It is a simple process and is probably what you would want to do when using a distributed container management system like [BOLD:Kubernetes].

metadata.breadcrumb: Server Workers - Uvicorn with Workers > Containers and Docker

page_content:
[Context: Server Workers - Uvicorn with Workers > Recap]

## Recap
You can use multiple worker processes with the [CODE: --workers] CLI option with the [CODE: fastapi] or [CODE: uvicorn] commands to take advantage of [BOLD:multi-core CPUs], to run [BOLD:multiple processes in parallel].
You could use these tools and ideas if you are setting up [BOLD:your own deployment system] while taking care of the other deployment concepts yourself.
Check out the next chapter to learn about [BOLD:FastAPI] with containers (e.g. Docker and Kubernetes). You will see that those tools have simple ways to solve the other [BOLD:deployment concepts] as well.

metadata.breadcrumb: Server Workers - Uvicorn with Workers > Recap

page_content:
[Context: About FastAPI versions]

# About FastAPI versions
[BOLD:FastAPI] is already being used in production in many applications and systems. And the test coverage is kept at 100%. But its development is still moving quickly.
New features are added frequently, bugs are fixed regularly, and the code is still continuously improving.
That's why the current versions are still [CODE: 0.x.x], this reflects that each version could potentially have breaking changes. This follows the Semantic Versioning conventions.
You can create production applications with [BOLD:FastAPI] right now (and you have probably been doing it for some time), you just have to make sure that you use a version that works correctly with the rest of your code.

[Context: About FastAPI versions > Pin your `fastapi` version]

## Pin your [CODE: fastapi] version
The first thing you should do is to "pin" the version of [BOLD:FastAPI] you are using to the specific latest version that you know works correctly for your application.
For example, let's say you are using version [CODE: 0.112.0] in your app.
If you use a [CODE: requirements.txt] file you could specify the version with:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: fastapi[standard]==0.112.0...]
that would mean that you would use exactly the version [CODE: 0.112.0].
Or you could also pin it with:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]1: fastapi[standard]>=0.112.0,<0.113.0...]
that would mean that you would use the versions [CODE: 0.112.0] or above, but less than [CODE: 0.113.0], for example, a version [CODE: 0.112.2] would still be accepted.
If you use any other tool to manage your installations, like [CODE: uv], Poetry, Pipenv, or others, they all have a way that you can use to define specific versions for your packages.

metadata.breadcrumb: About FastAPI versions > Pin your `fastapi` version

page_content:
[Context: About FastAPI versions > Available versions]

## Available versions
You can see the available versions (e.g. to check what is the current latest) in the Release Notes.

[Context: About FastAPI versions > Upgrading the FastAPI versions]

## Upgrading the FastAPI versions
You should add tests for your app.
With [BOLD:FastAPI] it's very easy (thanks to Starlette), check the docs: Testing
After you have tests, then you can upgrade the [BOLD:FastAPI] version to a more recent one, and make sure that all your code is working correctly by running your tests.
If everything is working, or after you make the necessary changes, and all your tests are passing, then you can pin your [CODE: fastapi] to that new recent version.

metadata.breadcrumb: About FastAPI versions > Upgrading the FastAPI versions

page_content:
[Context: About FastAPI versions > About Starlette]

## About Starlette
You shouldn't pin the version of [CODE: starlette].
Different versions of [BOLD:FastAPI] will use a specific newer version of Starlette.
So, you can just let [BOLD:FastAPI] use the correct Starlette version.

[Context: About FastAPI versions > About Pydantic]

## About Pydantic
Pydantic includes the tests for [BOLD:FastAPI] with its own tests, so new versions of Pydantic (above [CODE: 1.0.0]) are always compatible with FastAPI.
You can pin Pydantic to any version above [CODE: 1.0.0] that works for you.
For example:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: pydantic>=2.7.0,<3.0.0...]

page_content:
[Context: Conditional OpenAPI]

# Conditional OpenAPI
If you needed to, you could use settings and environment variables to configure OpenAPI conditionally depending on the environment, and even disable it entirely.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > How To - Recipes > how-to/conditional-openapi.md

page_content:
[Context: Conditional OpenAPI > About security, APIs, and docs]

## About security, APIs, and docs
Hiding your documentation user interfaces in production [ITALIC:shouldn't] be the way to protect your API.
That doesn't add any extra security to your API, the [ITALIC:path operations] will still be available where they are.
If there's a security flaw in your code, it will still exist.
Hiding the documentation just makes it more difficult to understand how to interact with your API, and could make it more difficult for you to debug it in production. It could be considered simply a form of Security through obscurity.
If you want to secure your API, there are several better things you can do, for example:
[ITALIC: Make sure you have well defined Pydantic models for your request bodies and responses.
] Configure any required permissions and roles using dependencies.
[ITALIC: Never store plaintext passwords, only password hashes.
] Implement and use well-known cryptographic tools, like pwdlib and JWT tokens, etc.
[ITALIC: Add more granular permission controls with OAuth2 scopes where needed.
] ...etc.
Nevertheless, you might have a very specific use case where you really need to disable the API docs for some environment (e.g. for production) or depending on configurations from environment variables.

metadata.breadcrumb: Conditional OpenAPI > About security, APIs, and docs
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > How To - Recipes > how-to/conditional-openapi.md

page_content:
[Context: Conditional OpenAPI > About security, APIs, and docs]

## About security, APIs, and docs
Hiding your documentation user interfaces in production [ITALIC:shouldn't] be the way to protect your API.
That doesn't add any extra security to your API, the [ITALIC:path operations] will still be available where they are.
If there's a security flaw in your code, it will still exist.
Hiding the documentation just makes it more difficult to understand how to interact with your API, and could make it more difficult for you to debug it in production. It could be considered simply a form of Security through obscurity.
If you want to secure your API, there are several better things you can do, for example:
[ITALIC: Make sure you have well defined Pydantic models for your request bodies and responses.
] Configure any required permissions and roles using dependencies.
[ITALIC: Never store plaintext passwords, only password hashes.
] Implement and use well-known cryptographic tools, like pwdlib and JWT tokens, etc.
[ITALIC: Add more granular permission controls with OAuth2 scopes where needed.
] ...etc.
Nevertheless, you might have a very specific use case where you really need to disable the API docs for some environment (e.g. for production) or depending on configurations from environment variables.

metadata.breadcrumb: Conditional OpenAPI > About security, APIs, and docs
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > How To - Recipes > how-to/conditional-openapi.md

page_content:
[Context: Conditional OpenAPI > Conditional OpenAPI from settings and env vars]

## Conditional OpenAPI from settings and env vars
You can easily use the same Pydantic settings to configure your generated OpenAPI and the docs UIs.
For example:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: openapi[ITALIC:url: str = "/openapi.json" app = FastAPI(o...]
Here we declare the setting [CODE: openapi]url] with the same default of [CODE: "/openapi.json"].
And then we use it when creating the [CODE: FastAPI] app.
Then you could disable OpenAPI (including the UI docs) by setting the environment variable [CODE: OPENAPI[ITALIC:URL] to the empty string, like:

[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:1: $ OPENAPI]URL= uvicorn main:app
Then if you go to the URLs at [CODE: /openapi.json], [CODE: /docs], or [CODE: /redoc] you will just get a [CODE: 404 Not Found] error like:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]2: { "detail": "Not Found" }...]

hierarchy: Conditional OpenAPI from settings and env vars

page_content:
[Context: Configure Swagger UI]

# Configure Swagger UI
You can configure some extra Swagger UI parameters.
To configure them, pass the [CODE: swagger[ITALIC:ui]parameters] argument when creating the [CODE: FastAPI()] app object or to the [CODE: get[ITALIC:swagger]ui[ITALIC:html()] function.
[CODE: swagger]ui_parameters] receives a dictionary with the configurations passed to Swagger UI directly.
FastAPI converts the configurations to [BOLD:JSON] to make them compatible with JavaScript, as that's what Swagger UI needs.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > How To - Recipes > how-to/configure-swagger-ui.md

page_content:
[Context: Configure Swagger UI > Disable Syntax Highlighting]

## Disable Syntax Highlighting
For example, you could disable syntax highlighting in Swagger UI.
Without changing the settings, syntax highlighting is enabled by default:

But you can disable it by setting [CODE: syntaxHighlight] to [CODE: False]:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: app = FastAPI(swagger[ITALIC:ui]parameters={"syntaxHighli...]
...and then Swagger UI won't show the syntax highlighting anymore:

metadata.breadcrumb: Configure Swagger UI > Disable Syntax Highlighting
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > How To - Recipes > how-to/configure-swagger-ui.md

page_content:
[Context: Configure Swagger UI > Change the Theme]

## Change the Theme
The same way you could set the syntax highlighting theme with the key [CODE: "syntaxHighlight.theme"] (notice that it has a dot in the middle):
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: app = FastAPI(swagger[ITALIC:ui]parameters={"syntaxHighli...]
That configuration would change the syntax highlighting color theme:

metadata.breadcrumb: Configure Swagger UI > Change the Theme
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > How To - Recipes > how-to/configure-swagger-ui.md

page_content:
[Context: Configure Swagger UI > Change Default Swagger UI Parameters]

## Change Default Swagger UI Parameters
FastAPI includes some default configuration parameters appropriate for most of the use cases.
It includes these default configurations:
{[ITALIC: ../../fastapi/openapi/docs.py ln[9:24] hl[18:24] ]}
You can override any of them by setting a different value in the argument [CODE: swagger[ITALIC:ui]parameters].
For example, to disable [CODE: deepLinking] you could pass these settings to [CODE: swagger[ITALIC:ui]parameters]:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: app = FastAPI(swagger[ITALIC:ui]parameters={"deepLinking"...]

metadata.breadcrumb: Configure Swagger UI > Change Default Swagger UI Parameters
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > How To - Recipes > how-to/configure-swagger-ui.md

page_content:
[Context: Configure Swagger UI > Other Swagger UI Parameters]

## Other Swagger UI Parameters
To see all the other possible configurations you can use, read the official docs for Swagger UI parameters.

metadata.breadcrumb: Configure Swagger UI > Other Swagger UI Parameters
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > How To - Recipes > how-to/configure-swagger-ui.md

page_content:
[Context: Configure Swagger UI > JavaScript-only settings]

## JavaScript-only settings
Swagger UI also allows other configurations to be [BOLD:JavaScript-only] objects (for example, JavaScript functions).
FastAPI also includes these JavaScript-only [CODE: presets] settings:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: presets: [ SwaggerUIBundle.presets.apis, SwaggerUI...]
These are [BOLD:JavaScript] objects, not strings, so you can't pass them from Python code directly.
If you need to use JavaScript-only configurations like those, you can use one of the methods above. Override all the Swagger UI [ITALIC:path operation] and manually write any JavaScript you need.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > How To - Recipes > how-to/configure-swagger-ui.md

page_content:
[Context: Custom Docs UI Static Assets (Self-Hosting)]

# Custom Docs UI Static Assets (Self-Hosting)
The API docs use [BOLD:Swagger UI] and [BOLD:ReDoc], and each of those need some JavaScript and CSS files.
By default, those files are served from a CDN.
But it's possible to customize it, you can set a specific CDN, or serve the files yourself.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > How To - Recipes > how-to/custom-docs-ui-assets.md

page_content:
[Context: Custom Docs UI Static Assets (Self-Hosting) > Custom CDN for JavaScript and CSS]

## Custom CDN for JavaScript and CSS
Let's say that you want to use a different CDN, for example you want to use [CODE: https://unpkg.com/].
This could be useful if for example you live in a country that restricts some URLs.

hierarchy: Custom CDN for JavaScript and CSS
metadata.breadcrumb: Custom Docs UI Static Assets (Self-Hosting) > Custom CDN for JavaScript and CSS
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > How To - Recipes > how-to/custom-docs-ui-assets.md

page_content:
[Context: Custom Docs UI Static Assets (Self-Hosting) > Custom CDN for JavaScript and CSS > Disable the automatic docs]

### Disable the automatic docs
The first step is to disable the automatic docs, as by default, those use the default CDN.
To disable them, set their URLs to [CODE: None] when creating your [CODE: FastAPI] app:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: app = FastAPI(docs[ITALIC:url=None, redoc]url=None)...]

hierarchy: Custom CDN for JavaScript and CSS

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > How To - Recipes > how-to/custom-docs-ui-assets.md

hierarchy: Custom CDN for JavaScript and CSS
metadata.breadcrumb: Custom Docs UI Static Assets (Self-Hosting) > Custom CDN for JavaScript and CSS > Include the custom docs
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > How To - Recipes > how-to/custom-docs-ui-assets.md

hierarchy: Custom CDN for JavaScript and CSS
metadata.breadcrumb: Custom Docs UI Static Assets (Self-Hosting) > Custom CDN for JavaScript and CSS > Include the custom docs
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > How To - Recipes > how-to/custom-docs-ui-assets.md

hierarchy: Custom CDN for JavaScript and CSS
metadata.breadcrumb: Custom Docs UI Static Assets (Self-Hosting) > Custom CDN for JavaScript and CSS > Include the custom docs
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > How To - Recipes > how-to/custom-docs-ui-assets.md

page_content:
[Context: Custom Docs UI Static Assets (Self-Hosting) > Custom CDN for JavaScript and CSS > Create a *path operation* to test it]

### Create a [ITALIC:path operation] to test it
Now, to be able to test that everything works, create a [ITALIC:path operation]:
```python
@app.get("/users/{username}")
async def read_user(username: str):
return {"message": f"Hello {username}"}
```

Create a *path operation* to test it

Now, you should be able to go to your docs at http://127.0.0.1:8000/docs, and reload the page, it will load those assets from the new CDN.

page_content:
[Context: Custom Docs UI Static Assets (Self-Hosting) > Self-hosting JavaScript and CSS for docs]

## Self-hosting JavaScript and CSS for docs
Self-hosting the JavaScript and CSS could be useful if, for example, you need your app to keep working even while offline, without open Internet access, or in a local network.
Here you'll see how to serve those files yourself, in the same FastAPI app, and configure the docs to use them.

hierarchy: Self-hosting JavaScript and CSS for docs
metadata.breadcrumb: Custom Docs UI Static Assets (Self-Hosting) > Self-hosting JavaScript and CSS for docs
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > How To - Recipes > how-to/custom-docs-ui-assets.md

page_content:
[Context: Custom Docs UI Static Assets (Self-Hosting) > Self-hosting JavaScript and CSS for docs > Project file structure]

### Project file structure
Let's say your project file structure looks like this:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: . app [BOLD:init].py main.py...]
Now create a directory to store those static files.
Your new file structure could look like this:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]1: . app [BOLD:init].py main.py stat...]

hierarchy: Self-hosting JavaScript and CSS for docs
metadata.breadcrumb: Custom Docs UI Static Assets (Self-Hosting) > Self-hosting JavaScript and CSS for docs > Project file structure
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > How To - Recipes > how-to/custom-docs-ui-assets.md

page_content:
[Context: Custom Docs UI Static Assets (Self-Hosting) > Self-hosting JavaScript and CSS for docs > Download the files]

### Download the files
Download the static files needed for the docs and put them on that [CODE: static/] directory.
You can probably right-click each link and select an option similar to "Save link as...".
[BOLD:Swagger UI] uses the files:
[ITALIC: [[CODE: swagger-ui-bundle.js]](https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/swagger-ui-dist@5/swagger-ui-bundle.js)
] [[CODE: swagger-ui.css]](https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/swagger-ui-dist@5/swagger-ui.css)
And [BOLD:ReDoc] uses the file:
- [[CODE: redoc.standalone.js]](https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/redoc@2/bundles/redoc.standalone.js)
After that, your file structure could look like:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: . app [BOLD:init].py main.py stat...]

hierarchy: Self-hosting JavaScript and CSS for docs
metadata.breadcrumb: Custom Docs UI Static Assets (Self-Hosting) > Self-hosting JavaScript and CSS for docs > Download the files
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > How To - Recipes > how-to/custom-docs-ui-assets.md

page_content:
[Context: Custom Docs UI Static Assets (Self-Hosting) > Self-hosting JavaScript and CSS for docs > Serve the static files]

### Serve the static files
[ITALIC: Import [CODE: StaticFiles].
] "Mount" a [CODE: StaticFiles()] instance in a specific path.
```python
from fastapi.staticfiles import StaticFiles

app.mount("/static", StaticFiles(directory="static"), name="static")

```

hierarchy: Self-hosting JavaScript and CSS for docs
metadata.breadcrumb: Custom Docs UI Static Assets (Self-Hosting) > Self-hosting JavaScript and CSS for docs > Serve the static files
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > How To - Recipes > how-to/custom-docs-ui-assets.md

page_content:
[Context: Custom Docs UI Static Assets (Self-Hosting) > Self-hosting JavaScript and CSS for docs > Test the static files]

### Test the static files
Start your application and go to http://127.0.0.1:8000/static/redoc.standalone.js.
You should see a very long JavaScript file for [BOLD:ReDoc].
It could start with something like:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: /*! For license information please see redoc.stand...]
That confirms that you are being able to serve static files from your app, and that you placed the static files for the docs in the correct place.
Now we can configure the app to use those static files for the docs.

[Context: Custom Request and APIRoute class]

# Custom Request and APIRoute class
In some cases, you may want to override the logic used by the [CODE: Request] and [CODE: APIRoute] classes.
In particular, this may be a good alternative to logic in a middleware.
For example, if you want to read or manipulate the request body before it is processed by your application.
/// danger
This is an "advanced" feature.
If you are just starting with [BOLD:FastAPI] you might want to skip this section.
///

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > How To - Recipes > how-to/custom-request-and-route.md

page_content:
[Context: Custom Request and APIRoute class > Use cases]

## Use cases
Some use cases include:
[ITALIC: Converting non-JSON request bodies to JSON (e.g. [[CODE: msgpack]](https://msgpack.org/index.html)).
] Decompressing gzip-compressed request bodies.
- Automatically logging all request bodies.

metadata.breadcrumb: Custom Request and APIRoute class > Use cases
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > How To - Recipes > how-to/custom-request-and-route.md

page_content:
[Context: Custom Request and APIRoute class > Handling custom request body encodings]

## Handling custom request body encodings
Let's see how to make use of a custom [CODE: Request] subclass to decompress gzip requests.
And an [CODE: APIRoute] subclass to use that custom request class.

metadata.breadcrumb: Custom Request and APIRoute class > Handling custom request body encodings
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > How To - Recipes > how-to/custom-request-and-route.md

page_content:
[Context: Custom Request and APIRoute class > Handling custom request body encodings > Create a custom `GzipRequest` class]

### Create a custom [CODE: GzipRequest] class
/// tip
This is a toy example to demonstrate how it works, if you need Gzip support, you can use the provided [[CODE: GzipMiddleware]](../advanced/middleware.md#gzipmiddleware).
///
First, we create a [CODE: GzipRequest] class, which will overwrite the [CODE: Request.body()] method to decompress the body in the presence of an appropriate header.
If there's no [CODE: gzip] in the header, it will not try to decompress the body.
That way, the same route class can handle gzip compressed or uncompressed requests.
```python
class GzipRequest(Request):
async def body(self) -> bytes:
if not hasattr(self, "[ITALIC:body"):
body = await super().body()
if "gzip" in self.headers.getlist("Content-Encoding"):
body = gzip.decompress(body)
self.]body = body
return self._body
```

metadata.breadcrumb: Custom Request and APIRoute class > Handling custom request body encodings > Create a custom `GzipRequest` class
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > How To - Recipes > how-to/custom-request-and-route.md

page_content:
[Context: Custom Request and APIRoute class > Handling custom request body encodings > Create a custom `GzipRoute` class]

### Create a custom [CODE: GzipRoute] class
Next, we create a custom subclass of [CODE: fastapi.routing.APIRoute] that will make use of the [CODE: GzipRequest].
This time, it will overwrite the method [CODE: APIRoute.get[ITALIC:route]handler()].
This method returns a function. And that function is what will receive a request and return a response.
Here we use it to create a [CODE: GzipRequest] from the original request.
```python
class GzipRoute(APIRoute):
def get[ITALIC:route]handler(self) -> Callable:
original[ITALIC:route]handler = super().get[ITALIC:route]handler()

async def custom[ITALIC:route]handler(request: Request) -> Response:
request = GzipRequest(request.scope, request.receive)
return await original[ITALIC:route]handler(request)

return custom[ITALIC:route]handler
```
/// note | Technical Details
A [CODE: Request] has a [CODE: request.scope] attribute, that's just a Python [CODE: dict] containing the metadata related to the request.
A [CODE: Request] also has a [CODE: request.receive], that's a function to "receive" the body of the request.
The [CODE: scope] [CODE: dict] and [CODE: receive] function are both part of the ASGI specification.
And those two things, [CODE: scope] and [CODE: receive], are what is needed to create a new [CODE: Request] instance.
To learn more about the [CODE: Request] check Starlette's docs about Requests.
///
The only thing the function returned by [CODE: GzipRequest.get[ITALIC:route]handler] does differently is convert the [CODE: Request] to a [CODE: GzipRequest].
Doing this, our [CODE: GzipRequest] will take care of decompressing the data (if necessary) before passing it to our [ITALIC:path operations].
After that, all of the processing logic is the same.
But because of our changes in [CODE: GzipRequest.body], the request body will be automatically decompressed when it is loaded by [BOLD:FastAPI] when needed.

metadata.breadcrumb: Custom Request and APIRoute class > Handling custom request body encodings > Create a custom `GzipRoute` class
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > How To - Recipes > how-to/custom-request-and-route.md

page_content:
[Context: Custom Request and APIRoute class > Handling custom request body encodings > Create a custom `GzipRoute` class]

Next, we create a custom subclass of [CODE: fastapi.routing.APIRoute] that will make use of the [CODE: GzipRequest].

page_content:
[Context: Custom Request and APIRoute class > Accessing the request body in an exception handler]

## Accessing the request body in an exception handler
/// tip
To solve this same problem, it's probably a lot easier to use the [CODE: body] in a custom handler for [CODE: RequestValidationError] (Handling Errors).
But this example is still valid and it shows how to interact with the internal components.
///
We can also use this same approach to access the request body in an exception handler.
All we need to do is handle the request inside a [CODE: try]/[CODE: except] block:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: try: return await original[ITALIC:route]handler(request) ...]
If an exception occurs, the[CODE: Request] instance will still be in scope, so we can read and make use of the request body when handling the error:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]1: body = await request.body() detail = {"errors": ex...]

hierarchy: Accessing the request body in an exception handler
metadata.breadcrumb: Custom Request and APIRoute class > Accessing the request body in an exception handler
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > How To - Recipes > how-to/custom-request-and-route.md

page_content:
[Context: Custom Request and APIRoute class > Custom `APIRoute` class in a router]

## Custom [CODE: APIRoute] class in a router
You can also set the [CODE: route[ITALIC:class] parameter of an [CODE: APIRouter]:
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:0: router = APIRouter(route]class=TimedRoute)...]
In this example, the [ITALIC:path operations] under the [CODE: router] will use the custom [CODE: TimedRoute] class, and will have an extra [CODE: X-Response-Time] header in the response with the time it took to generate the response:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]1: before = time.time() response: Response = await or...]

metadata.breadcrumb: Custom Request and APIRoute class > Custom `APIRoute` class in a router
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > How To - Recipes > how-to/custom-request-and-route.md

page_content:
[Context: Extending OpenAPI]

# Extending OpenAPI
There are some cases where you might need to modify the generated OpenAPI schema.
In this section you will see how.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > How To - Recipes > how-to/extending-openapi.md

page_content:
[Context: Extending OpenAPI > The normal process]

## The normal process
The normal (default) process, is as follows.
A [CODE: FastAPI] application (instance) has an [CODE: .openapi()] method that is expected to return the OpenAPI schema.
As part of the application object creation, a [ITALIC:path operation] for [CODE: /openapi.json] (or for whatever you set your [CODE: openapi[ITALIC:url]) is registered.
It just returns a JSON response with the result of the application's [CODE: .openapi()] method.
By default, what the method [CODE: .openapi()] does is check the property [CODE: .openapi]schema] to see if it has contents and return them.
If it doesn't, it generates them using the utility function at [CODE: fastapi.openapi.utils.get[ITALIC:openapi].
And that function [CODE: get]openapi()] receives as parameters:
[ITALIC: [CODE: title]: The OpenAPI title, shown in the docs.
] [CODE: version]: The version of your API, e.g. [CODE: 2.5.0].
[ITALIC: [CODE: openapi_version]: The version of the OpenAPI specification used. By default, the latest: [CODE: 3.1.0].
] [CODE: summary]: A short summary of the API.
[ITALIC: [CODE: description]: The description of your API, this can include markdown and will be shown in the docs.
] [CODE: routes]: A list of routes, these are each of the registered [ITALIC:path operations]. They are taken from [CODE: app.routes].
/// info
The parameter [CODE: summary] is available in OpenAPI 3.1.0 and above, supported by FastAPI 0.99.0 and above.
///

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > How To - Recipes > how-to/extending-openapi.md

page_content:
[Context: Extending OpenAPI > Overriding the defaults]

## Overriding the defaults
Using the information above, you can use the same utility function to generate the OpenAPI schema and override each part that you need.
For example, let's add ReDoc's OpenAPI extension to include a custom logo.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > How To - Recipes > how-to/extending-openapi.md

page_content:
[Context: Extending OpenAPI > Overriding the defaults > Normal **FastAPI**]

### Normal [BOLD:FastAPI]
First, write all your [BOLD:FastAPI] application as normally:
```python
from fastapi import FastAPI
from fastapi.openapi.utils import get[ITALIC:openapi
app = FastAPI()

@app.get("/items/")
async def read]items():
return [{"name": "Foo"}]
```

metadata.breadcrumb: Extending OpenAPI > Overriding the defaults > Normal **FastAPI**
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > How To - Recipes > how-to/extending-openapi.md

page_content:
[Context: Extending OpenAPI > Overriding the defaults > Generate the OpenAPI schema]

### Generate the OpenAPI schema
Then, use the same utility function to generate the OpenAPI schema, inside a [CODE: custom[ITALIC:openapi()] function:
```python
from fastapi.openapi.utils import get]openapi

openapi[ITALIC:schema = get]openapi(
title="Custom title",
version="2.5.0",
summary="This is a very custom OpenAPI schema",
description="Here's a longer description of the custom [BOLD:OpenAPI] schema",
routes=app.routes,
)
```

metadata.breadcrumb: Extending OpenAPI > Overriding the defaults > Generate the OpenAPI schema
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > How To - Recipes > how-to/extending-openapi.md

page_content:
[Context: Extending OpenAPI > Overriding the defaults > Modify the OpenAPI schema]

### Modify the OpenAPI schema
Now you can add the ReDoc extension, adding a custom [CODE: x-logo] to the [CODE: info] "object" in the OpenAPI schema:
CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: openapi_schema["info" = { "url": "https...]

metadata.breadcrumb: Extending OpenAPI > Overriding the defaults > Modify the OpenAPI schema
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > How To - Recipes > how-to/extending-openapi.md

page_content:
[Context: Extending OpenAPI > Overriding the defaults > Cache the OpenAPI schema]

### Cache the OpenAPI schema
You can use the property [CODE: .openapi[ITALIC:schema] as a "cache", to store your generated schema.
That way, your application won't have to generate the schema every time a user opens your API docs.
It will be generated only once, and then the same cached schema will be used for the next requests.
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:0: if app.openapi]schema: return app.openapi_schema a...]

metadata.breadcrumb: Extending OpenAPI > Overriding the defaults > Cache the OpenAPI schema
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > How To - Recipes > how-to/extending-openapi.md

page_content:
[Context: Extending OpenAPI > Overriding the defaults > Override the method]

### Override the method
Now you can replace the [CODE: .openapi()] method with your new function.
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: app.openapi = custom_openapi...]

metadata.breadcrumb: Extending OpenAPI > Overriding the defaults > Override the method
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > How To - Recipes > how-to/extending-openapi.md

page_content:
[Context: Extending OpenAPI > Overriding the defaults > Check it]

### Check it
Once you go to http://127.0.0.1:8000/redoc you will see that you are using your custom logo (in this example, [BOLD:FastAPI]'s logo):

page_content:
[Context: GraphQL]

# GraphQL
As [BOLD:FastAPI] is based on the [BOLD:ASGI] standard, it's very easy to integrate any [BOLD:GraphQL] library also compatible with ASGI.
You can combine normal FastAPI [ITALIC:path operations] with GraphQL on the same application.
/// tip
[BOLD:GraphQL] solves some very specific use cases.
It has [BOLD:advantages] and [BOLD:disadvantages] when compared to common [BOLD:web APIs].
Make sure you evaluate if the [BOLD:benefits] for your use case compensate the [BOLD:drawbacks]. 🤓
///

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > How To - Recipes > how-to/graphql.md
Root > Learn > Root > How To - Recipes > how-to/graphql.md

page_content:
[Context: GraphQL > GraphQL with Strawberry]

## GraphQL with Strawberry
If you need or want to work with [BOLD:GraphQL], [BOLD:Strawberry] is the [BOLD:recommended] library as it has the design closest to [BOLD:FastAPI's] design, it's all based on [BOLD:type annotations].
Depending on your use case, you might prefer to use a different library, but if you asked me, I would probably suggest you try [BOLD:Strawberry].
Here's a small preview of how you could integrate Strawberry with FastAPI:
```python
from strawberry.fastapi import GraphQLRouter

graphql[ITALIC:app = GraphQLRouter(schema)

app.include]router(graphql_app, prefix="/graphql")
```
You can learn more about Strawberry in the Strawberry documentation.
And also the docs about Strawberry with FastAPI.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > How To - Recipes > how-to/graphql.md

page_content:
[Context: GraphQL > Older `GraphQLApp` from Starlette]

## Older [CODE: GraphQLApp] from Starlette
Previous versions of Starlette included a [CODE: GraphQLApp] class to integrate with Graphene.
It was deprecated from Starlette, but if you have code that used it, you can easily [BOLD:migrate] to starlette-graphene3, that covers the same use case and has an [BOLD:almost identical interface].
/// tip
If you need GraphQL, I still would recommend you check out Strawberry, as it's based on type annotations instead of custom classes and types.
///

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > How To - Recipes > how-to/graphql.md

page_content:
[Context: GraphQL > Learn More]

You can learn more about [BOLD:GraphQL] in the official GraphQL documentation.

If something seems interesting and useful to your project, go ahead and check it, but otherwise, you might probably just skip them.

If you want to [BOLD:learn FastAPI] in a structured way (recommended), go and read the Tutorial - User Guide chapter by chapter instead.

page_content:
[Context: Migrate from Pydantic v1 to Pydantic v2]

# Migrate from Pydantic v1 to Pydantic v2
If you have an old FastAPI app, you might be using Pydantic version 1.
FastAPI version 0.100.0 had support for either Pydantic v1 or v2. It would use whichever you had installed.
FastAPI version 0.119.0 introduced partial support for Pydantic v1 from inside of Pydantic v2 (as [CODE: pydantic.v1]), to facilitate the migration to v2.
FastAPI 0.126.0 dropped support for Pydantic v1, while still supporting [CODE: pydantic.v1] for a little while.
/// warning
The Pydantic team stopped support for Pydantic v1 for the latest versions of Python, starting with [BOLD:Python 3.14].
This includes [CODE: pydantic.v1], which is no longer supported in Python 3.14 and above.
If you want to use the latest features of Python, you will need to make sure you use Pydantic v2.
///
If you have an old FastAPI app with Pydantic v1, here I'll show you how to migrate it to Pydantic v2, and the [BOLD:features in FastAPI 0.119.0] to help you with a gradual migration.

hierarchy: Migrate from Pydantic v1 to Pydantic v2
metadata.breadcrumb: Migrate from Pydantic v1 to Pydantic v2
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > How To - Recipes > how-to/migrate-from-pydantic-v1-to-pydantic-v2.md

[Context: Migrate from Pydantic v1 to Pydantic v2]

# Migrate from Pydantic v1 to Pydantic v2
If you have an old FastAPI app, you might be using Pydantic version 1.
FastAPI version 0.100.0 had support for either Pydantic v1 or v2. It would use whichever you had installed.
FastAPI version 0.119.0 introduced partial support for Pydantic v1 from inside of Pydantic v2 (as [CODE: pydantic.v1]), to facilitate the migration to v2.
FastAPI 0.126.0 dropped support for Pydantic v1, while still supporting [CODE: pydantic.v1] for a little while.
/// warning
The Pydantic team stopped support for Pydantic v1 for the latest versions of Python, starting with [BOLD:Python 3.14].
This includes [CODE: pydantic.v1], which is no longer supported in Python 3.14 and above.
If you want to use the latest features of Python, you will need to make sure you use Pydantic v2.
///
If you have an old FastAPI app with Pydantic v1, here I'll show you how to migrate it to Pydantic v2, and the [BOLD:features in FastAPI 0.119.0] to help you with a gradual migration.

page_content:
[Context: Migrate from Pydantic v1 to Pydantic v2 > Official Guide]

## Official Guide
Pydantic has an official Migration Guide from v1 to v2.
It also includes what has changed, how validations are now more correct and strict, possible caveats, etc.
You can read it to understand better what has changed.

hierarchy: Migrate from Pydantic v1 to Pydantic v2

page_content:
[Context: Migrate from Pydantic v1 to Pydantic v2 > Tests]

## Tests
Make sure you have tests for your app and you run them on continuous integration (CI).
This way, you can do the upgrade and make sure everything is still working as expected.

hierarchy: Migrate from Pydantic v1 to Pydantic v2
metadata.breadcrumb: Migrate from Pydantic v1 to Pydantic v2 > Tests
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > How To - Recipes > how-to/migrate-from-pydantic-v1-to-pydantic-v2.md

page_content:
[Context: Migrate from Pydantic v1 to Pydantic v2 > `bump-pydantic`]

## [CODE: bump-pydantic]
In many cases, when you use regular Pydantic models without customizations, you will be able to automate most of the process of migrating from Pydantic v1 to Pydantic v2.
You can use [[CODE: bump-pydantic]](https://github.com/pydantic/bump-pydantic) from the same Pydantic team.
This tool will help you to automatically change most of the code that needs to be changed.
After this, you can run the tests and check if everything works. If it does, you are done.

hierarchy: Migrate from Pydantic v1 to Pydantic v2
metadata.breadcrumb: Migrate from Pydantic v1 to Pydantic v2 > `bump-pydantic`
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > How To - Recipes > how-to/migrate-from-pydantic-v1-to-pydantic-v2.md

page_content:
[Context: Migrate from Pydantic v1 to Pydantic v2 > Pydantic v1 in v2]

## Pydantic v1 in v2
Pydantic v2 includes everything from Pydantic v1 as a submodule [CODE: pydantic.v1]. But this is no longer supported in versions above Python 3.13.
This means that you can install the latest version of Pydantic v2 and import and use the old Pydantic v1 components from this submodule, as if you had the old Pydantic v1 installed.
```python
from pydantic.v1 import BaseModel

class Item(BaseModel):
name: str
```

hierarchy: Migrate from Pydantic v1 to Pydantic v2
metadata.breadcrumb: Migrate from Pydantic v1 to Pydantic v2 > Pydantic v1 in v2
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > How To - Recipes > how-to/migrate-from-pydantic-v1-to-pydantic-v2.md

page_content:
[Context: Migrate from Pydantic v1 to Pydantic v2 > Pydantic v1 in v2 > FastAPI support for Pydantic v1 in v2]

### FastAPI support for Pydantic v1 in v2
Since FastAPI 0.119.0, there's also partial support for Pydantic v1 from inside of Pydantic v2, to facilitate the migration to v2.
So, you could upgrade Pydantic to the latest version 2, and change the imports to use the [CODE: pydantic.v1] submodule, and in many cases it would just work.
```python
from pydantic.v1 import BaseModel

class Item(BaseModel):
name: str
async def create_item(item: Item) -> Item:
return item
```
/// warning
Have in mind that as the Pydantic team no longer supports Pydantic v1 in recent versions of Python, starting from Python 3.14, using [CODE: pydantic.v1] is also not supported in Python 3.14 and above.
///

hierarchy: Migrate from Pydantic v1 to Pydantic v2
FastAPI support for Pydantic v1 in v2
metadata.breadcrumb: Migrate from Pydantic v1 to Pydantic v2 > Pydantic v1 in v2 > FastAPI support for Pydantic v1 in v2
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > How To - Recipes > how-to/migrate-from-pydantic-v1-to-pydantic-v2.md

page_content:
[Context: Migrate from Pydantic v1 to Pydantic v2 > Pydantic v1 in v2 > Pydantic v1 and v2 on the same app]

### Pydantic v1 and v2 on the same app
It's [BOLD:not supported] by Pydantic to have a model of Pydantic v2 with its own fields defined as Pydantic v1 models or vice versa.
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: graph TB subgraph " Not Supported" direction TB s...]
...but, you can have separated models using Pydantic v1 and v2 in the same app.
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]1: graph TB subgraph " Supported" direction TB subgr...]
In some cases, it's even possible to have both Pydantic v1 and v2 models in the same [BOLD:path operation] in your FastAPI app:
```python
from pydantic import BaseModel as BaseModelV2
from pydantic.v1 import BaseModel
class Item(BaseModel):
name: str
class ItemV2(BaseModelV2):
name: str
@app.post("/items/", response[ITALIC:model=ItemV2)
async def create]item(item: Item):
```
In this example above, the input model is a Pydantic v1 model, and the output model (defined in [CODE: response_model=ItemV2]) is a Pydantic v2 model.

hierarchy: Migrate from Pydantic v1 to Pydantic v2
Pydantic v1 and v2 on the same app
metadata.breadcrumb: Migrate from Pydantic v1 to Pydantic v2 > Pydantic v1 in v2 > Pydantic v1 and v2 on the same app
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > How To - Recipes > how-to/migrate-from-pydantic-v1-to-pydantic-v2.md

page_content:
[Context: Migrate from Pydantic v1 to Pydantic v2 > Pydantic v1 in v2 > Pydantic v1 and v2 on the same app]

### Pydantic v1 and v2 on the same app
It's [BOLD:not supported] by Pydantic to have a model of Pydantic v2 with its own fields defined as Pydantic v1 models or vice versa.
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: graph TB subgraph " Not Supported" direction TB s...]
...but, you can have separated models using Pydantic v1 and v2 in the same app.
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]1: graph TB subgraph " Supported" direction TB subgr...]
In some cases, it's even possible to have both Pydantic v1 and v2 models in the same [BOLD:path operation] in your FastAPI app:
```python
from pydantic import BaseModel as BaseModelV2
from pydantic.v1 import BaseModel
class Item(BaseModel):
name: str
class ItemV2(BaseModelV2):
name: str
@app.post("/items/", response[ITALIC:model=ItemV2)
async def create]item(item: Item):
```
In this example above, the input model is a Pydantic v1 model, and the output model (defined in [CODE: response_model=ItemV2]) is a Pydantic v2 model.

hierarchy: Migrate from Pydantic v1 to Pydantic v2
Pydantic v1 and v2 on the same app
metadata.breadcrumb: Migrate from Pydantic v1 to Pydantic v2 > Pydantic v1 in v2 > Pydantic v1 and v2 on the same app
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > How To - Recipes > how-to/migrate-from-pydantic-v1-to-pydantic-v2.md

page_content:
[Context: Migrate from Pydantic v1 to Pydantic v2 > Pydantic v1 in v2 > Pydantic v1 parameters]

### Pydantic v1 parameters
If you need to use some of the FastAPI-specific tools for parameters like [CODE: Body], [CODE: Query], [CODE: Form], etc. with Pydantic v1 models, you can import them from [CODE: fastapi.temp[ITALIC:pydantic]v1[ITALIC:params] while you finish the migration to Pydantic v2:
```python
from fastapi.temp]pydantic[ITALIC:v1]params import Body
from pydantic.v1 import BaseModel
async def create_item(item: Annotated[Item, Body(embed=True)]) -> Item:
return item
```

hierarchy: Migrate from Pydantic v1 to Pydantic v2
metadata.breadcrumb: Migrate from Pydantic v1 to Pydantic v2 > Pydantic v1 in v2 > Pydantic v1 parameters
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > How To - Recipes > how-to/migrate-from-pydantic-v1-to-pydantic-v2.md

page_content:
[Context: Migrate from Pydantic v1 to Pydantic v2 > Pydantic v1 in v2 > Migrate in steps]

### Migrate in steps
/// tip
First try with [CODE: bump-pydantic], if your tests pass and that works, then you're done in one command.
///
If [CODE: bump-pydantic] doesn't work for your use case, you can use the support for both Pydantic v1 and v2 models in the same app to do the migration to Pydantic v2 gradually.
You could fist upgrade Pydantic to use the latest version 2, and change the imports to use [CODE: pydantic.v1] for all your models.
Then, you can start migrating your models from Pydantic v1 to v2 in groups, in gradual steps.

hierarchy: Migrate from Pydantic v1 to Pydantic v2
metadata.breadcrumb: Migrate from Pydantic v1 to Pydantic v2 > Pydantic v1 in v2 > Migrate in steps
