page_content:
[Context: Separate OpenAPI Schemas for Input and Output or Not]

# Separate OpenAPI Schemas for Input and Output or Not
Since [BOLD:Pydantic v2] was released, the generated OpenAPI is a bit more exact and [BOLD:correct] than before.
In fact, in some cases, it will even have [BOLD:two JSON Schemas] in OpenAPI for the same Pydantic model, for input and output, depending on if they have [BOLD:default values].
Let's see how that works and how to change it if you need to do that.

hierarchy: Separate OpenAPI Schemas for Input and Output or Not
metadata.breadcrumb: Separate OpenAPI Schemas for Input and Output or Not
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > How To - Recipes > how-to/separate-openapi-schemas.md

page_content:
[Context: Separate OpenAPI Schemas for Input and Output or Not > Pydantic Models for Input and Output]

## Pydantic Models for Input and Output
Let's say you have a Pydantic model with default values, like this one:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: description: str | None = None...]

hierarchy: Separate OpenAPI Schemas for Input and Output or Not
Pydantic Models for Input and Output
metadata.breadcrumb: Separate OpenAPI Schemas for Input and Output or Not > Pydantic Models for Input and Output
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > How To - Recipes > how-to/separate-openapi-schemas.md

page_content:
[Context: Separate OpenAPI Schemas for Input and Output or Not > Pydantic Models for Input and Output > Model for Input]

### Model for Input
If you use this model as an input like here:
```python
def create_item(item: Item):
return item
```
...then the [CODE: description] field will [BOLD:not be required]. Because it has a default value of [CODE: None].

hierarchy: Separate OpenAPI Schemas for Input and Output or Not
Pydantic Models for Input and Output
metadata.breadcrumb: Separate OpenAPI Schemas for Input and Output or Not > Pydantic Models for Input and Output > Model for Input
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > How To - Recipes > how-to/separate-openapi-schemas.md

page_content:
[Context: Separate OpenAPI Schemas for Input and Output or Not > Pydantic Models for Input and Output > Input Model in Docs]

### Input Model in Docs
You can confirm that in the docs, the [CODE: description] field doesn't have a [BOLD:red asterisk], it's not marked as required:

hierarchy: Separate OpenAPI Schemas for Input and Output or Not
Pydantic Models for Input and Output
metadata.breadcrumb: Separate OpenAPI Schemas for Input and Output or Not > Pydantic Models for Input and Output > Input Model in Docs
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > How To - Recipes > how-to/separate-openapi-schemas.md

page_content:
[Context: Separate OpenAPI Schemas for Input and Output or Not > Pydantic Models for Input and Output > Model for Output]

### Model for Output
But if you use the same model as an output, like here:
```python
def read_items() -> list[Item]:
return [
```
...then because [CODE: description] has a default value, if you [BOLD:don't return anything] for that field, it will still have that [BOLD:default value].

hierarchy: Separate OpenAPI Schemas for Input and Output or Not
Pydantic Models for Input and Output
metadata.breadcrumb: Separate OpenAPI Schemas for Input and Output or Not > Pydantic Models for Input and Output > Model for Output
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > How To - Recipes > how-to/separate-openapi-schemas.md

page_content:
[Context: Separate OpenAPI Schemas for Input and Output or Not > Pydantic Models for Input and Output > Model for Output Response Data]

### Model for Output Response Data
If you interact with the docs and check the response, even though the code didn't add anything in one of the [CODE: description] fields, the JSON response contains the default value ([CODE: null]):


This means that it will [BOLD:always have a value], it's just that sometimes the value could be [CODE: None] (or [CODE: null] in JSON).
That means that, clients using your API don't have to check if the value exists or not, they can [BOLD:assume the field will always be there], but just that in some cases it will have the default value of [CODE: None].
The way to describe this in OpenAPI, is to mark that field as [BOLD:required], because it will always be there.
Because of that, the JSON Schema for a model can be different depending on if it's used for [BOLD:input or output]:
[ITALIC: for [BOLD:input] the [CODE: description] will [BOLD:not be required]
] for [BOLD:output] it will be [BOLD:required] (and possibly [CODE: None], or in JSON terms, [CODE: null])

Pydantic Models for Input and Output

### Model for Output Response Data
If you interact with the docs and check the response, even though the code didn't add anything in one of the [CODE: description] fields, the JSON response contains the default value ([CODE: null]):


This means that it will [BOLD:always have a value], it's just that sometimes the value could be [CODE: None] (or [CODE: null] in JSON).
That means that, clients using your API don't have to check if the value exists or not, they can [BOLD:assume the field will always be there], but just that in some cases it will have the default value of [CODE: None].
The way to describe this in OpenAPI, is to mark that field as [BOLD:required], because it will always be there.
Because of that, the JSON Schema for a model can be different depending on if it's used for [BOLD:input or output]:
[ITALIC: for [BOLD:input] the [CODE: description] will [BOLD:not be required]
] for [BOLD:output] it will be [BOLD:required] (and possibly [CODE: None], or in JSON terms, [CODE: null])

### Model for Output in Docs
You can check the output model in the docs too, [BOLD:both] [CODE: name] and [CODE: description] are marked as [BOLD:required] with a [BOLD:red asterisk]:

hierarchy: Separate OpenAPI Schemas for Input and Output or Not
Pydantic Models for Input and Output

page_content:
[Context: Separate OpenAPI Schemas for Input and Output or Not > Pydantic Models for Input and Output > Model for Input and Output in Docs]

### Model for Input and Output in Docs
And if you check all the available Schemas (JSON Schemas) in OpenAPI, you will see that there are two, one [CODE: Item-Input] and one [CODE: Item-Output].
For [CODE: Item-Input], [CODE: description] is [BOLD:not required], it doesn't have a red asterisk.
But for [CODE: Item-Output], [CODE: description] is [BOLD:required], it has a red asterisk.


With this feature from [BOLD:Pydantic v2], your API documentation is more [BOLD:precise], and if you have autogenerated clients and SDKs, they will be more precise too, with a better [BOLD:developer experience] and consistency.

page_content:
[Context: Separate OpenAPI Schemas for Input and Output or Not > Do not Separate Schemas]

## Do not Separate Schemas
Now, there are some cases where you might want to have the [BOLD:same schema for input and output].
Probably the main use case for this is if you already have some autogenerated client code/SDKs and you don't want to update all the autogenerated client code/SDKs yet, you probably will want to do it at some point, but maybe not right now.
In that case, you can disable this feature in [BOLD:FastAPI], with the parameter [CODE: separate[ITALIC:input]output[ITALIC:schemas=False].
/// info
Support for [CODE: separate]input[ITALIC:output]schemas] was added in FastAPI [CODE: 0.102.0]. 🤓
///
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: app = FastAPI(separate[ITALIC:input]output_schemas=False)...]

And now there will be one single schema for input and output for the model, only [CODE: Item], and it will have [CODE: description] as [BOLD:not required]:

[Context: Learn]

# Learn
Here are the introductory sections and the tutorials to learn [BOLD:FastAPI].
You could consider this a [BOLD:book], a [BOLD:course], the [BOLD:official] and recommended way to learn FastAPI.

[Context: `APIRouter` class]

# [CODE: APIRouter] class
Here's the reference information for the [CODE: APIRouter] class, with all its parameters, attributes and methods.
You can import the [CODE: APIRouter] class directly from [CODE: fastapi]:
```python
from fastapi import APIRouter
```
::: fastapi.APIRouter

[Context: Background Tasks - `BackgroundTasks`]

# Background Tasks - [CODE: BackgroundTasks]
You can declare a parameter in a [ITALIC:path operation function] or dependency function with the type [CODE: BackgroundTasks], and then you can use it to schedule the execution of background tasks after the response is sent.
You can import it directly from [CODE: fastapi]:
```python
from fastapi import BackgroundTasks
```
::: fastapi.BackgroundTasks

[Context: Dependencies - `Depends()` and `Security()` > `Depends()`]

# Dependencies - [CODE: Depends()] and [CODE: Security()]
## [CODE: Depends()]
Dependencies are handled mainly with the special function [CODE: Depends()] that takes a callable.
Here is the reference for it and its parameters.
You can import it directly from [CODE: fastapi]:
```python
from fastapi import Depends
```
::: fastapi.Depends

[Context: Dependencies - `Depends()` and `Security()` > `Security()`]

## [CODE: Security()]
For many scenarios, you can handle security (authorization, authentication, etc.) with dependencies, using [CODE: Depends()].
But when you want to also declare OAuth2 scopes, you can use [CODE: Security()] instead of [CODE: Depends()].
You can import [CODE: Security()] directly from [CODE: fastapi]:
```python
from fastapi import Security
```
::: fastapi.Security

[Context: Encoders - `jsonable_encoder`]

# Encoders - [CODE: jsonable[ITALIC:encoder]
::: fastapi.encoders.jsonable]encoder

[Context: Exceptions - `HTTPException` and `WebSocketException`]

# Exceptions - [CODE: HTTPException] and [CODE: WebSocketException]
These are the exceptions that you can raise to show errors to the client.
When you raise an exception, as would happen with normal Python, the rest of the execution is aborted. This way you can raise these exceptions from anywhere in the code to abort a request and show the error to the client.
You can use:
[ITALIC: [CODE: HTTPException]
] [CODE: WebSocketException]
These exceptions can be imported directly from [CODE: fastapi]:
```python
from fastapi import HTTPException, WebSocketException
```
::: fastapi.HTTPException
::: fastapi.WebSocketException

You can import the [CODE: FastAPI] class directly from [CODE: fastapi]:
```python
from fastapi import FastAPI
```

When you want to define dependencies that should be compatible with both HTTP and WebSockets, you can define a parameter that takes an [CODE: HTTPConnection] instead of a [CODE: Request] or a [CODE: WebSocket].

from fastapi.requests import HTTPConnection

[Context: Reference]

# Reference
Here's the reference or code API, the classes, functions, parameters, attributes, and
all the FastAPI parts you can use in your applications.
If you want to [BOLD:learn FastAPI] you are much better off reading the
FastAPI Tutorial.

[Context: Middleware]

# Middleware
There are several middlewares available provided by Starlette directly.
Read more about them in the FastAPI docs for Middleware.
::: fastapi.middleware.cors.CORSMiddleware
It can be imported from [CODE: fastapi]:
```python
from fastapi.middleware.cors import CORSMiddleware
```
::: fastapi.middleware.gzip.GZipMiddleware
It can be imported from [CODE: fastapi]:
```python
from fastapi.middleware.gzip import GZipMiddleware
```
::: fastapi.middleware.httpsredirect.HTTPSRedirectMiddleware
It can be imported from [CODE: fastapi]:
```python
from fastapi.middleware.httpsredirect import HTTPSRedirectMiddleware
```
::: fastapi.middleware.trustedhost.TrustedHostMiddleware
It can be imported from [CODE: fastapi]:
```python
from fastapi.middleware.trustedhost import TrustedHostMiddleware
```

[Context: Request Parameters]

# Request Parameters
Here's the reference information for the request parameters.
These are the special functions that you can put in [ITALIC:path operation function] parameters or dependency functions with [CODE: Annotated] to get data from the request.
It includes:
[ITALIC: [CODE: Query()]
] [CODE: Path()]
[ITALIC: [CODE: Body()]
] [CODE: Cookie()]
[ITALIC: [CODE: Header()]
] [CODE: Form()]
- [CODE: File()]
You can import them all directly from [CODE: fastapi]:
```python
from fastapi import Body, Cookie, File, Form, Header, Path, Query
```
::: fastapi.Query
::: fastapi.Path
::: fastapi.Body
::: fastapi.Cookie
::: fastapi.Header
::: fastapi.Form
::: fastapi.File

[Context: `Request` class]

# [CODE: Request] class
You can declare a parameter in a [ITALIC:path operation function] or dependency to be of type [CODE: Request] and then you can access the raw request object directly, without any validation, etc.
Read more about it in the FastAPI docs about using Request directly
You can import it directly from [CODE: fastapi]:
```python
from fastapi import Request
```
/// tip
When you want to define dependencies that should be compatible with both HTTP and WebSockets, you can define a parameter that takes an [CODE: HTTPConnection] instead of a [CODE: Request] or a [CODE: WebSocket].
///
::: fastapi.Request

[Context: `Response` class]

# [CODE: Response] class
You can declare a parameter in a [ITALIC:path operation function] or dependency to be of type [CODE: Response] and then you can set data for the response like headers or cookies.
You can also use it directly to create an instance of it and return it from your [ITALIC:path operations].
Read more about it in the FastAPI docs about returning a custom Response
You can import it directly from [CODE: fastapi]:
```python
from fastapi import Response
```
::: fastapi.Response

[Context: Custom Response Classes - File, HTML, Redirect, Streaming, etc.]

# Custom Response Classes - File, HTML, Redirect, Streaming, etc.
There are several custom response classes you can use to create an instance and return them directly from your [ITALIC:path operations].
Read more about it in the FastAPI docs for Custom Response - HTML, Stream, File, others.
You can import them directly from [CODE: fastapi.responses]:
```python
from fastapi.responses import (
FileResponse,
HTMLResponse,
JSONResponse,
ORJSONResponse,
PlainTextResponse,
RedirectResponse,
Response,
StreamingResponse,
UJSONResponse,
)
```

## FastAPI Responses
There were a couple of custom FastAPI response classes that were intended to optimize JSON performance.
However, they are now deprecated as you will now get better performance by using a Response Model - Return Type.
That way, Pydantic will serialize the data into JSON bytes on the Rust side, which will achieve better performance than these custom JSON responses.
Read more about it in [Custom Response - HTML, Stream, File, others - [CODE: orjson] or Response Model](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/advanced/custom-response/#orjson-or-response-model).

page_content:
[Context: Static Files - `StaticFiles`]

# Static Files - [CODE: StaticFiles]
You can use the [CODE: StaticFiles] class to serve static files, like JavaScript, CSS, images, etc.
Read more about it in the FastAPI docs for Static Files.
You can import it directly from [CODE: fastapi.staticfiles]:
```python
from fastapi.staticfiles import StaticFiles
```
::: fastapi.staticfiles.StaticFiles

[Context: Status Codes]

# Status Codes
You can import the [CODE: status] module from [CODE: fastapi]:
```python
from fastapi import status
```
[CODE: status] is provided directly by Starlette.
It contains a group of named constants (variables) with integer status codes.
For example:
[ITALIC: 200: [CODE: status.HTTP[ITALIC:200]OK]
] 403: [CODE: status.HTTP[ITALIC:403]FORBIDDEN]
- etc.
It can be convenient to quickly access HTTP (and WebSocket) status codes in your app, using autocompletion for the name without having to remember the integer status codes by memory.
Read more about it in the FastAPI docs about Response Status Code.

[Context: Status Codes > Example]

## Example
```python
from fastapi import FastAPI, status

app = FastAPI()

@app.get("/items/", status[ITALIC:code=status.HTTP]418[ITALIC:IM]A[ITALIC:TEAPOT)
def read]items():
return [{"name": "Plumbus"}, {"name": "Portal Gun"}]
```
::: fastapi.status

[Context: Templating - `Jinja2Templates`]

# Templating - [CODE: Jinja2Templates]
You can use the [CODE: Jinja2Templates] class to render Jinja templates.
Read more about it in the FastAPI docs for Templates.
You can import it directly from [CODE: fastapi.templating]:
```python
from fastapi.templating import Jinja2Templates
```
::: fastapi.templating.Jinja2Templates

[Context: Test Client - `TestClient`]

# Test Client - [CODE: TestClient]
You can use the [CODE: TestClient] class to test FastAPI applications without creating an actual HTTP and socket connection, just communicating directly with the FastAPI code.
Read more about it in the FastAPI docs for Testing.
You can import it directly from [CODE: fastapi.testclient]:
```python
from fastapi.testclient import TestClient
```
::: fastapi.testclient.TestClient

[Context: `UploadFile` class]

# [CODE: UploadFile] class
You can define [ITALIC:path operation function] parameters to be of the type [CODE: UploadFile] to receive files from the request.
You can import it directly from [CODE: fastapi]:
```python
from fastapi import UploadFile
```
::: fastapi.UploadFile

[Context: WebSockets]

# WebSockets
When defining WebSockets, you normally declare a parameter of type [CODE: WebSocket] and with it you can read data from the client and send data to it.
Read more about it in the FastAPI docs for WebSockets
It is provided directly by Starlette, but you can import it from [CODE: fastapi]:
```python
from fastapi import WebSocket
```
/// tip
When you want to define dependencies that should be compatible with both HTTP and WebSockets, you can define a parameter that takes an [CODE: HTTPConnection] instead of a [CODE: Request] or a [CODE: WebSocket].
///

[Context: WebSockets > WebSockets - additional classes]

## WebSockets - additional classes
Additional classes for handling WebSockets.
Provided directly by Starlette, but you can import it from [CODE: fastapi]:
```python
from fastapi.websockets import WebSocketDisconnect, WebSocketState
```
::: fastapi.websockets.WebSocketDisconnect
When a client disconnects, a [CODE: WebSocketDisconnect] exception is raised, you can catch it.
You can import it directly form [CODE: fastapi]:
```python
from fastapi import WebSocketDisconnect
```
Read more about it in the FastAPI docs for WebSockets
::: fastapi.websockets.WebSocketState
[CODE: WebSocketState] is an enumeration of the possible states of a WebSocket connection.

[Context: Resources]

# Resources
Additional resources, external links, and more.

[Context: Background Tasks]

# Background Tasks
You can define background tasks to be run [ITALIC:after] returning a response.
This is useful for operations that need to happen after a request, but that the client doesn't really have to be waiting for the operation to complete before receiving the response.
This includes, for example:
[ITALIC: Email notifications sent after performing an action:
] As connecting to an email server and sending an email tends to be "slow" (several seconds), you can return the response right away and send the email notification in the background.
[ITALIC: Processing data:
] For example, let's say you receive a file that must go through a slow process, you can return a response of "Accepted" (HTTP 202) and process the file in the background.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/background-tasks.md

page_content:
[Context: Background Tasks > Using `BackgroundTasks`]

## Using [CODE: BackgroundTasks]
First, import [CODE: BackgroundTasks] and define a parameter in your [ITALIC:path operation function] with a type declaration of [CODE: BackgroundTasks]:
```python
from fastapi import BackgroundTasks, FastAPI

async def send[ITALIC:notification(email: str, background]tasks: BackgroundTasks):
background[ITALIC:tasks.add]task(write_notification, email, message="some notification")
```
[BOLD:FastAPI] will create the object of type [CODE: BackgroundTasks] for you and pass it as that parameter.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/background-tasks.md

page_content:
[Context: Background Tasks > Create a task function]

## Create a task function
Create a function to be run as the background task.
It is just a standard function that can receive parameters.
It can be an [CODE: async def] or normal [CODE: def] function, [BOLD:FastAPI] will know how to handle it correctly.
In this case, the task function will write to a file (simulating sending an email).
And as the write operation doesn't use [CODE: async] and [CODE: await], we define the function with normal [CODE: def]:
```python
def write[ITALIC:notification(email: str, message=""):
with open("log.txt", mode="w") as email]file:
content = f"notification for {email}: {message}"
email_file.write(content)
```

metadata.breadcrumb: Background Tasks > Create a task function
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/background-tasks.md

page_content:
[Context: Background Tasks > Add the background task]

## Add the background task
Inside of your [ITALIC:path operation function], pass your task function to the [ITALIC:background tasks] object with the method [CODE: .add[ITALIC:task()]:
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:0: background]tasks.add[ITALIC:task(write]notification, emai...]
[CODE: .add[ITALIC:task()] receives as arguments:
[ITALIC: A task function to be run in the background ([CODE: write]notification]).
] Any sequence of arguments that should be passed to the task function in order ([CODE: email]).
- Any keyword arguments that should be passed to the task function ([CODE: message="some notification"]).

metadata.breadcrumb: Background Tasks > Add the background task
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/background-tasks.md

page_content:
[Context: Background Tasks > Dependency Injection]

## Dependency Injection
Using [CODE: BackgroundTasks] also works with the dependency injection system, you can declare a parameter of type [CODE: BackgroundTasks] at multiple levels: in a [ITALIC:path operation function], in a dependency (dependable), in a sub-dependency, etc.
[BOLD:FastAPI] knows what to do in each case and how to reuse the same object, so that all the background tasks are merged together and are run in the background afterwards:
```python
def get[ITALIC:query(background]tasks: BackgroundTasks, q: str | None = None):
if q:
message = f"found query: {q}\n"
background[ITALIC:tasks.add]task(write[ITALIC:log, message)
email: str, background]tasks: BackgroundTasks, q: Annotated[str, Depends(get[ITALIC:query)]
):
background]tasks.add[ITALIC:task(write]log, message)
return {"message": "Message sent"}
```
In this example, the messages will be written to the [CODE: log.txt] file [ITALIC:after] the response is sent.
If there was a query in the request, it will be written to the log in a background task.
And then another background task generated at the [ITALIC:path operation function] will write a message using the [CODE: email] path parameter.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/background-tasks.md

page_content:
[Context: Background Tasks > Dependency Injection]

## Dependency Injection
Using [CODE: BackgroundTasks] also works with the dependency injection system, you can declare a parameter of type [CODE: BackgroundTasks] at multiple levels: in a [ITALIC:path operation function], in a dependency (dependable), in a sub-dependency, etc.
[BOLD:FastAPI] knows what to do in each case and how to reuse the same object, so that all the background tasks are merged together and are run in the background afterwards:
```python
def get[ITALIC:query(background]tasks: BackgroundTasks, q: str | None = None):
if q:
message = f"found query: {q}\n"
background[ITALIC:tasks.add]task(write[ITALIC:log, message)
email: str, background]tasks: BackgroundTasks, q: Annotated[str, Depends(get[ITALIC:query)]
):
background]tasks.add[ITALIC:task(write]log, message)
return {"message": "Message sent"}
```
In this example, the messages will be written to the [CODE: log.txt] file [ITALIC:after] the response is sent.
If there was a query in the request, it will be written to the log in a background task.
And then another background task generated at the [ITALIC:path operation function] will write a message using the [CODE: email] path parameter.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/background-tasks.md

page_content:
[Context: Background Tasks > Technical Details]

## Technical Details
The class [CODE: BackgroundTasks] comes directly from [[CODE: starlette.background]](https://www.starlette.dev/background/).
It is imported/included directly into FastAPI so that you can import it from [CODE: fastapi] and avoid accidentally importing the alternative [CODE: BackgroundTask] (without the [CODE: s] at the end) from [CODE: starlette.background].
By only using [CODE: BackgroundTasks] (and not [CODE: BackgroundTask]), it's then possible to use it as a [ITALIC:path operation function] parameter and have [BOLD:FastAPI] handle the rest for you, just like when using the [CODE: Request] object directly.
It's still possible to use [CODE: BackgroundTask] alone in FastAPI, but you have to create the object in your code and return a Starlette [CODE: Response] including it.
You can see more details in Starlette's official docs for Background Tasks.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/background-tasks.md

page_content:
[Context: Background Tasks > Caveat]

## Caveat
If you need to perform heavy background computation and you don't necessarily need it to be run by the same process (for example, you don't need to share memory, variables, etc), you might benefit from using other bigger tools like Celery.
They tend to require more complex configurations, a message/job queue manager, like RabbitMQ or Redis, but they allow you to run background tasks in multiple processes, and especially, in multiple servers.
But if you need to access variables and objects from the same [BOLD:FastAPI] app, or you need to perform small background tasks (like sending an email notification), you can simply just use [CODE: BackgroundTasks].

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/background-tasks.md

page_content:
[Context: Background Tasks > Recap]

## Recap
Import and use [CODE: BackgroundTasks] with parameters in [ITALIC:path operation functions] and dependencies to add background tasks.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/background-tasks.md

page_content:
[Context: Bigger Applications - Multiple Files]

# Bigger Applications - Multiple Files
If you are building an application or a web API, it's rarely the case that you can put everything in a single file.
[BOLD:FastAPI] provides a convenience tool to structure your application while keeping all the flexibility.
/// info
If you come from Flask, this would be the equivalent of Flask's Blueprints.
///

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/bigger-applications.md

metadata.breadcrumb: Bigger Applications - Multiple Files > An example file structure
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/bigger-applications.md

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/bigger-applications.md

page_content:
[Context: Bigger Applications - Multiple Files > `APIRouter`]

## [CODE: APIRouter]
Let's say the file dedicated to handling just users is the submodule at [CODE: /app/routers/users.py].
You want to have the [ITALIC:path operations] related to your users separated from the rest of the code, to keep it organized.
But it's still part of the same [BOLD:FastAPI] application/web API (it's part of the same "Python Package").
You can create the [ITALIC:path operations] for that module using [CODE: APIRouter].

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/bigger-applications.md

page_content:
[Context: Bigger Applications - Multiple Files > `APIRouter` > Import `APIRouter`]

### Import [CODE: APIRouter]
You import it and create an "instance" the same way you would with the class [CODE: FastAPI]:
{[ITALIC: ../../docs[ITALIC:src/bigger]applications/app[ITALIC:an]py310/routers/users.py hl[1,3] title["app/routers/users.py"] ]}

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/bigger-applications.md

page_content:
[Context: Bigger Applications - Multiple Files > `APIRouter` > *Path operations* with `APIRouter`]

### [ITALIC:Path operations] with [CODE: APIRouter]
And then you use it to declare your [ITALIC:path operations].
Use it the same way you would use the [CODE: FastAPI] class:
{[ITALIC: ../../docs[ITALIC:src/bigger]applications/app[ITALIC:an]py310/routers/users.py hl[6,11,16] title["app/routers/users.py"] ]}
You can think of [CODE: APIRouter] as a "mini [CODE: FastAPI]" class.
All the same options are supported.
All the same [CODE: parameters], [CODE: responses], [CODE: dependencies], [CODE: tags], etc.
/// tip
In this example, the variable is called [CODE: router], but you can name it however you want.
///
We are going to include this [CODE: APIRouter] in the main [CODE: FastAPI] app, but first, let's check the dependencies and another [CODE: APIRouter].

page_content:
[Context: Bigger Applications - Multiple Files > Another module with `APIRouter` > Import the dependencies]

### Import the dependencies
This code lives in the module [CODE: app.routers.items], the file [CODE: app/routers/items.py].
And we need to get the dependency function from the module [CODE: app.dependencies], the file [CODE: app/dependencies.py].
So we use a relative import with [CODE: ..] for the dependencies:
{[ITALIC: ../../docs[ITALIC:src/bigger]applications/app[ITALIC:an]py310/routers/items.py hl[3] title["app/routers/items.py"] ]}
#### How relative imports work
/// tip
If you know perfectly how imports work, continue to the next section below.
///
A single dot [CODE: .], like in:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: from .dependencies import get[ITALIC:token]header...]
would mean:
[ITALIC: Starting in the same package that this module (the file [CODE: app/routers/items.py]) lives in (the directory [CODE: app/routers/])...
] find the module [CODE: dependencies] (an imaginary file at [CODE: app/routers/dependencies.py])...
[ITALIC: and from it, import the function [CODE: get[ITALIC:token]header].
But that file doesn't exist, our dependencies are in a file at [CODE: app/dependencies.py].
Remember how our app/file structure looks like:

---
The two dots [CODE: ..], like in:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]1: from ..dependencies import get[ITALIC:token]header...]
mean:
] Starting in the same package that this module (the file [CODE: app/routers/items.py]) lives in (the directory [CODE: app/routers/])...
[ITALIC: go to the parent package (the directory [CODE: app/])...
] and in there, find the module [CODE: dependencies] (the file at [CODE: app/dependencies.py])...
[ITALIC: and from it, import the function [CODE: get[ITALIC:token]header].
That works correctly!
---
The same way, if we had used three dots [CODE: ...], like in:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]2: from ...dependencies import get[ITALIC:token]header...]
that would mean:
] Starting in the same package that this module (the file [CODE: app/routers/items.py]) lives in (the directory [CODE: app/routers/])...
[ITALIC: go to the parent package (the directory [CODE: app/])...
] then go to the parent of that package (there's no parent package, [CODE: app] is the top level )...
[ITALIC: and in there, find the module [CODE: dependencies] (the file at [CODE: app/dependencies.py])...
] and from it, import the function [CODE: get[ITALIC:token]header].
That would refer to some package above [CODE: app/], with its own file [CODE: [BOLD:init].py], etc. But we don't have that. So, that would throw an error in our example.
But now you know how it works, so you can use relative imports in your own apps no matter how complex they are. 🤓

metadata.breadcrumb: Bigger Applications - Multiple Files > Another module with `APIRouter` > Import the dependencies

## The main [CODE: FastAPI]
Now, let's see the module at [CODE: app/main.py].
Here's where you import and use the class [CODE: FastAPI].
This will be the main file in your application that ties everything together.
And as most of your logic will now live in its own specific module, the main file will be quite simple.

You import and create a [CODE: FastAPI] class as normally.
And we can even declare global dependencies that will be combined with the dependencies for each [CODE: APIRouter]:
{[ITALIC: ../../docs[ITALIC:src/bigger]applications/app[ITALIC:an]py310/main.py hl[1,3,7] title["app/main.py"] ]}

### Import the [CODE: APIRouter]
Now we import the other submodules that have [CODE: APIRouter]s:
{[ITALIC: ../../docs[ITALIC:src/bigger]applications/app[ITALIC:an]py310/main.py hl[4:5] title["app/main.py"] ]}
As the files [CODE: app/routers/users.py] and [CODE: app/routers/items.py] are submodules that are part of the same Python package [CODE: app], we can use a single dot [CODE: .] to import them using "relative imports".

page_content:
[Context: Bigger Applications - Multiple Files > The main `FastAPI` > How the importing works]

### How the importing works
The section:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: from .routers import items, users...]
means:
[ITALIC: Starting in the same package that this module (the file [CODE: app/main.py]) lives in (the directory [CODE: app/])...
] look for the subpackage [CODE: routers] (the directory at [CODE: app/routers/])...
- and from it, import the submodule [CODE: items] (the file at [CODE: app/routers/items.py]) and [CODE: users] (the file at [CODE: app/routers/users.py])...
The module [CODE: items] will have a variable [CODE: router] ([CODE: items.router]). This is the same one we created in the file [CODE: app/routers/items.py], it's an [CODE: APIRouter] object.
And then we do the same for the module [CODE: users].
We could also import them like:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]1: from app.routers import items, users...]
/// info
The first version is a "relative import":
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]2: from .routers import items, users...]
The second version is an "absolute import":
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]3: from app.routers import items, users...]
To learn more about Python Packages and Modules, read the official Python documentation about Modules.
///

page_content:
[Context: Bigger Applications - Multiple Files > The main `FastAPI` > Avoid name collisions]

### Avoid name collisions
We are importing the submodule [CODE: items] directly, instead of importing just its variable [CODE: router].
This is because we also have another variable named [CODE: router] in the submodule [CODE: users].
If we had imported one after the other, like:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: from .routers.items import router from .routers.us...]
the [CODE: router] from [CODE: users] would overwrite the one from [CODE: items] and we wouldn't be able to use them at the same time.
So, to be able to use both of them in the same file, we import the submodules directly:
{[ITALIC: ../../docs[ITALIC:src/bigger]applications/app[ITALIC:an]py310/main.py hl[5] title["app/main.py"] ]}

page_content:
[Context: Bigger Applications - Multiple Files > The main `FastAPI` > Include an `APIRouter` with a custom `prefix`, `tags`, `responses`, and `dependencies`]

### Include an [CODE: APIRouter] with a custom [CODE: prefix], [CODE: tags], [CODE: responses], and [CODE: dependencies]
Now, let's imagine your organization gave you the [CODE: app/internal/admin.py] file.
It contains an [CODE: APIRouter] with some admin [ITALIC:path operations] that your organization shares between several projects.
For this example it will be super simple. But let's say that because it is shared with other projects in the organization, we cannot modify it and add a [CODE: prefix], [CODE: dependencies], [CODE: tags], etc. directly to the [CODE: APIRouter]:
{[ITALIC: ../../docs[ITALIC:src/bigger]applications/app[ITALIC:an]py310/internal/admin.py hl[3] title["app/internal/admin.py"] ]}
But we still want to set a custom [CODE: prefix] when including the [CODE: APIRouter] so that all its [ITALIC:path operations] start with [CODE: /admin], we want to secure it with the [CODE: dependencies] we already have for this project, and we want to include [CODE: tags] and [CODE: responses].
We can declare all that without having to modify the original [CODE: APIRouter] by passing those parameters to [CODE: app.include[ITALIC:router()]:
{[ITALIC: ../../docs]src/bigger[ITALIC:applications/app]an[ITALIC:py310/main.py hl[14:17] title["app/main.py"] ]}
That way, the original [CODE: APIRouter] will stay unmodified, so we can still share that same [CODE: app/internal/admin.py] file with other projects in the organization.
The result is that in our app, each of the [ITALIC:path operations] from the [CODE: admin] module will have:
[ITALIC: The prefix [CODE: /admin].
] The tag [CODE: admin].
[ITALIC: The dependency [CODE: get]token_header].
] The response [CODE: 418].
But that will only affect that [CODE: APIRouter] in our app, not in any other code that uses it.
So, for example, other projects could use the same [CODE: APIRouter] with a different authentication method.

hierarchy: Include an `APIRouter` with a custom `prefix`, `tags`, `responses`, and `dependencies`
metadata.breadcrumb: Bigger Applications - Multiple Files > The main `FastAPI` > Include an `APIRouter` with a custom `prefix`, `tags`, `responses`, and `dependencies`
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/bigger-applications.md

page_content:
[Context: Bigger Applications - Multiple Files > The main `FastAPI` > Include an `APIRouter` with a custom `prefix`, `tags`, `responses`, and `dependencies`]

### Include an [CODE: APIRouter] with a custom [CODE: prefix], [CODE: tags], [CODE: responses], and [CODE: dependencies]
Now, let's imagine your organization gave you the [CODE: app/internal/admin.py] file.
It contains an [CODE: APIRouter] with some admin [ITALIC:path operations] that your organization shares between several projects.
For this example it will be super simple. But let's say that because it is shared with other projects in the organization, we cannot modify it and add a [CODE: prefix], [CODE: dependencies], [CODE: tags], etc. directly to the [CODE: APIRouter]:
{[ITALIC: ../../docs[ITALIC:src/bigger]applications/app[ITALIC:an]py310/internal/admin.py hl[3] title["app/internal/admin.py"] ]}
But we still want to set a custom [CODE: prefix] when including the [CODE: APIRouter] so that all its [ITALIC:path operations] start with [CODE: /admin], we want to secure it with the [CODE: dependencies] we already have for this project, and we want to include [CODE: tags] and [CODE: responses].
We can declare all that without having to modify the original [CODE: APIRouter] by passing those parameters to [CODE: app.include[ITALIC:router()]:
{[ITALIC: ../../docs]src/bigger[ITALIC:applications/app]an[ITALIC:py310/main.py hl[14:17] title["app/main.py"] ]}
That way, the original [CODE: APIRouter] will stay unmodified, so we can still share that same [CODE: app/internal/admin.py] file with other projects in the organization.
The result is that in our app, each of the [ITALIC:path operations] from the [CODE: admin] module will have:
[ITALIC: The prefix [CODE: /admin].
] The tag [CODE: admin].
[ITALIC: The dependency [CODE: get]token_header].
] The response [CODE: 418].
But that will only affect that [CODE: APIRouter] in our app, not in any other code that uses it.
So, for example, other projects could use the same [CODE: APIRouter] with a different authentication method.

hierarchy: Include an `APIRouter` with a custom `prefix`, `tags`, `responses`, and `dependencies`

[Context: Bigger Applications - Multiple Files > The main `FastAPI` > Include an `APIRouter` with a custom `prefix`, `tags`, `responses`, and `dependencies`]

### Include an [CODE: APIRouter] with a custom [CODE: prefix], [CODE: tags], [CODE: responses], and [CODE: dependencies]
Now, let's imagine your organization gave you the [CODE: app/internal/admin.py] file.
It contains an [CODE: APIRouter] with some admin [ITALIC:path operations] that your organization shares between several projects.
For this example it will be super simple. But let's say that because it is shared with other projects in the organization, we cannot modify it and add a [CODE: prefix], [CODE: dependencies], [CODE: tags], etc. directly to the [CODE: APIRouter]:
{[ITALIC: ../../docs[ITALIC:src/bigger]applications/app[ITALIC:an]py310/internal/admin.py hl[3] title["app/internal/admin.py"] ]}
But we still want to set a custom [CODE: prefix] when including the [CODE: APIRouter] so that all its [ITALIC:path operations] start with [CODE: /admin], we want to secure it with the [CODE: dependencies] we already have for this project, and we want to include [CODE: tags] and [CODE: responses].
We can declare all that without having to modify the original [CODE: APIRouter] by passing those parameters to [CODE: app.include[ITALIC:router()]:
{[ITALIC: ../../docs]src/bigger[ITALIC:applications/app]an[ITALIC:py310/main.py hl[14:17] title["app/main.py"] ]}
That way, the original [CODE: APIRouter] will stay unmodified, so we can still share that same [CODE: app/internal/admin.py] file with other projects in the organization.
The result is that in our app, each of the [ITALIC:path operations] from the [CODE: admin] module will have:
[ITALIC: The prefix [CODE: /admin].
] The tag [CODE: admin].
[ITALIC: The dependency [CODE: get]token_header].
] The response [CODE: 418].
But that will only affect that [CODE: APIRouter] in our app, not in any other code that uses it.
So, for example, other projects could use the same [CODE: APIRouter] with a different authentication method.

hierarchy: Include an `APIRouter` with a custom `prefix`, `tags`, `responses`, and `dependencies`
metadata.breadcrumb: Bigger Applications - Multiple Files > The main `FastAPI` > Include an `APIRouter` with a custom `prefix`, `tags`, `responses`, and `dependencies`
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/bigger-applications.md

page_content:
[Context: Bigger Applications - Multiple Files > The main `FastAPI` > Include a *path operation*]

### Include a [ITALIC:path operation]
We can also add [ITALIC:path operations] directly to the [CODE: FastAPI] app.
Here we do it... just to show that we can 🤷:
{[ITALIC: ../../docs[ITALIC:src/bigger]applications/app[ITALIC:an]py310/main.py hl[21:23] title["app/main.py"] ]}
and it will work correctly, together with all the other [ITALIC:path operations] added with [CODE: app.include_router()].
/// info | Very Technical Details
[BOLD:Note]: this is a very technical detail that you probably can [BOLD:just skip].
---
The [CODE: APIRouter]s are not "mounted", they are not isolated from the rest of the application.
This is because we want to include their [ITALIC:path operations] in the OpenAPI schema and the user interfaces.
As we cannot just isolate them and "mount" them independently of the rest, the [ITALIC:path operations] are "cloned" (re-created), not included directly.
///

metadata.breadcrumb: Bigger Applications - Multiple Files > The main `FastAPI` > Include a *path operation*
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/bigger-applications.md

page_content:
[Context: Bigger Applications - Multiple Files > Configure the `entrypoint` in `pyproject.toml`]

## Configure the [CODE: entrypoint] in [CODE: pyproject.toml]
As your FastAPI [CODE: app] object lives in [CODE: app/main.py], you can configure the [CODE: entrypoint] in your [CODE: pyproject.toml] file like this:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: [tool.fastapi] entrypoint = "app.main:app"...]
that is equivalent to importing like:
```python
from app.main import app
```
That way the [CODE: fastapi] command will know where to find your app.
/// Note
You could also pass the path to the command, like:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]1: $ fastapi dev app/main.py...]
But you would have to remember to pass the correct path every time you call the [CODE: fastapi] command.
Additionally, other tools might not be able to find it, for example the VS Code Extension or FastAPI Cloud, so it is recommended to use the [CODE: entrypoint] in [CODE: pyproject.toml].
///

metadata.breadcrumb: Bigger Applications - Multiple Files > Configure the `entrypoint` in `pyproject.toml`
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/bigger-applications.md

page_content:
[Context: Bigger Applications - Multiple Files > Check the automatic API docs]

## Check the automatic API docs
Now, run your app:

[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: $ fastapi dev INFO
And open the docs at http://127.0.0.1:8000/docs.
You will see the automatic API docs, including the paths from all the submodules, using the correct paths (and prefixes) and the correct tags:

metadata.breadcrumb: Bigger Applications - Multiple Files > Check the automatic API docs
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/bigger-applications.md

page_content:
[Context: Bigger Applications - Multiple Files > Include the same router multiple times with different `prefix`]

## Include the same router multiple times with different [CODE: prefix]
You can also use [CODE: .include_router()] multiple times with the [ITALIC:same] router using different prefixes.
This could be useful, for example, to expose the same API under different prefixes, e.g. [CODE: /api/v1] and [CODE: /api/latest].
This is an advanced usage that you might not really need, but it's there in case you do.

hierarchy: Include the same router multiple times with different `prefix`
metadata.breadcrumb: Bigger Applications - Multiple Files > Include the same router multiple times with different `prefix`
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/bigger-applications.md

page_content:
[Context: Bigger Applications - Multiple Files > Include an `APIRouter` in another]

## Include an [CODE: APIRouter] in another
The same way you can include an [CODE: APIRouter] in a [CODE: FastAPI] application, you can include an [CODE: APIRouter] in another [CODE: APIRouter] using:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: router.include[ITALIC:router(other]router)...]
Make sure you do it before including [CODE: router] in the [CODE: FastAPI] app, so that the [ITALIC:path operations] from [CODE: other_router] are also included.

metadata.breadcrumb: Bigger Applications - Multiple Files > Include an `APIRouter` in another
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/bigger-applications.md

page_content:
[Context: Body - Fields]

# Body - Fields
The same way you can declare additional validation and metadata in [ITALIC:path operation function] parameters with [CODE: Query], [CODE: Path] and [CODE: Body], you can declare validation and metadata inside of Pydantic models using Pydantic's [CODE: Field].

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/body-fields.md

page_content:
[Context: Body - Fields > Import `Field`]

## Import [CODE: Field]
First, you have to import it:
```python
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field

```
/// warning
Notice that [CODE: Field] is imported directly from [CODE: pydantic], not from [CODE: fastapi] as are all the rest ([CODE: Query], [CODE: Path], [CODE: Body], etc).
///

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/body-fields.md

page_content:
[Context: Body - Fields > Declare model attributes]

## Declare model attributes
You can then use [CODE: Field] with model attributes:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: description: str | None = Field( default=None, tit...]
[CODE: Field] works the same way as [CODE: Query], [CODE: Path] and [CODE: Body], it has all the same parameters, etc.
/// note | Technical Details
Actually, [CODE: Query], [CODE: Path] and others you'll see next create objects of subclasses of a common [CODE: Param] class, which is itself a subclass of Pydantic's [CODE: FieldInfo] class.
And Pydantic's [CODE: Field] returns an instance of [CODE: FieldInfo] as well.
[CODE: Body] also returns objects of a subclass of [CODE: FieldInfo] directly. And there are others you will see later that are subclasses of the [CODE: Body] class.
Remember that when you import [CODE: Query], [CODE: Path], and others from [CODE: fastapi], those are actually functions that return special classes.
///
/// tip
Notice how each model's attribute with a type, default value and [CODE: Field] has the same structure as a [ITALIC:path operation function's] parameter, with [CODE: Field] instead of [CODE: Path], [CODE: Query] and [CODE: Body].
///

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/body-fields.md

page_content:
[Context: Body - Fields > Declare model attributes]

## Declare model attributes
You can then use [CODE: Field] with model attributes:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: description: str | None = Field( default=None, tit...]
[CODE: Field] works the same way as [CODE: Query], [CODE: Path] and [CODE: Body], it has all the same parameters, etc.
/// note | Technical Details
Actually, [CODE: Query], [CODE: Path] and others you'll see next create objects of subclasses of a common [CODE: Param] class, which is itself a subclass of Pydantic's [CODE: FieldInfo] class.
And Pydantic's [CODE: Field] returns an instance of [CODE: FieldInfo] as well.
[CODE: Body] also returns objects of a subclass of [CODE: FieldInfo] directly. And there are others you will see later that are subclasses of the [CODE: Body] class.
Remember that when you import [CODE: Query], [CODE: Path], and others from [CODE: fastapi], those are actually functions that return special classes.
///
/// tip
Notice how each model's attribute with a type, default value and [CODE: Field] has the same structure as a [ITALIC:path operation function's] parameter, with [CODE: Field] instead of [CODE: Path], [CODE: Query] and [CODE: Body].
///

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/body-fields.md

page_content:
[Context: Body - Fields > Add extra information]

## Add extra information
You can declare extra information in [CODE: Field], [CODE: Query], [CODE: Body], etc. And it will be included in the generated JSON Schema.
You will learn more about adding extra information later in the docs, when learning to declare examples.
/// warning
Extra keys passed to [CODE: Field] will also be present in the resulting OpenAPI schema for your application.
As these keys may not necessarily be part of the OpenAPI specification, some OpenAPI tools, for example the OpenAPI validator, may not work with your generated schema.
///

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/body-fields.md

page_content:
[Context: Body - Fields > Recap]

## Recap
You can use Pydantic's [CODE: Field] to declare extra validations and metadata for model attributes.
You can also use the extra keyword arguments to pass additional JSON Schema metadata.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/body-fields.md

page_content:
[Context: Body - Multiple Parameters]

# Body - Multiple Parameters
Now that we have seen how to use [CODE: Path] and [CODE: Query], let's see more advanced uses of request body declarations.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/body-multiple-params.md

page_content:
[Context: Body - Multiple Parameters > Mix `Path`, `Query` and body parameters]

## Mix [CODE: Path], [CODE: Query] and body parameters
First, of course, you can mix [CODE: Path], [CODE: Query] and request body parameter declarations freely and [BOLD:FastAPI] will know what to do.
And you can also declare body parameters as optional, by setting the default to [CODE: None]:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: item_id: Annotated[int, Path(title="The ID of the ...]
/// note
Notice that, in this case, the [CODE: item] that would be taken from the body is optional. As it has a [CODE: None] default value.
///

metadata.breadcrumb: Body - Multiple Parameters > Mix `Path`, `Query` and body parameters
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/body-multiple-params.md

page_content:
[Context: Body - Multiple Parameters > Multiple body parameters]

## Multiple body parameters
In the previous example, the [ITALIC:path operations] would expect a JSON body with the attributes of an [CODE: Item], like:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: { "name": "Foo", "description": "The pretender", "...]
But you can also declare multiple body parameters, e.g. [CODE: item] and [CODE: user]:
```python
async def update[ITALIC:item(item]id: int, item: Item, user: User):
results = {"item[ITALIC:id": item]id, "item": item, "user": user}
```
In this case, [BOLD:FastAPI] will notice that there is more than one body parameter in the function (there are two parameters that are Pydantic models).
So, it will then use the parameter names as keys (field names) in the body, and expect a body like:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]1: { "item": { "name": "Foo", "description": "The pre...]
/// note
Notice that even though the [CODE: item] was declared the same way as before, it is now expected to be inside of the body with a key [CODE: item].
///
[BOLD:FastAPI] will do the automatic conversion from the request, so that the parameter [CODE: item] receives its specific content and the same for [CODE: user].
It will perform the validation of the compound data, and will document it like that for the OpenAPI schema and automatic docs.

metadata.breadcrumb: Body - Multiple Parameters > Multiple body parameters
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/body-multiple-params.md

page_content:
[Context: Body - Multiple Parameters > Multiple body parameters]

## Multiple body parameters
In the previous example, the [ITALIC:path operations] would expect a JSON body with the attributes of an [CODE: Item], like:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: { "name": "Foo", "description": "The pretender", "...]
But you can also declare multiple body parameters, e.g. [CODE: item] and [CODE: user]:
```python
async def update[ITALIC:item(item]id: int, item: Item, user: User):
results = {"item[ITALIC:id": item]id, "item": item, "user": user}
```
In this case, [BOLD:FastAPI] will notice that there is more than one body parameter in the function (there are two parameters that are Pydantic models).
So, it will then use the parameter names as keys (field names) in the body, and expect a body like:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]1: { "item": { "name": "Foo", "description": "The pre...]
/// note
Notice that even though the [CODE: item] was declared the same way as before, it is now expected to be inside of the body with a key [CODE: item].
///
[BOLD:FastAPI] will do the automatic conversion from the request, so that the parameter [CODE: item] receives its specific content and the same for [CODE: user].
It will perform the validation of the compound data, and will document it like that for the OpenAPI schema and automatic docs.

metadata.breadcrumb: Body - Multiple Parameters > Multiple body parameters
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/body-multiple-params.md

page_content:
[Context: Body - Multiple Parameters > Singular values in body]

## Singular values in body
The same way there is a [CODE: Query] and [CODE: Path] to define extra data for query and path parameters, [BOLD:FastAPI] provides an equivalent [CODE: Body].
For example, extending the previous model, you could decide that you want to have another key [CODE: importance] in the same body, besides the [CODE: item] and [CODE: user].
If you declare it as is, because it is a singular value, [BOLD:FastAPI] will assume that it is a query parameter.
But you can instruct [BOLD:FastAPI] to treat it as another body key using [CODE: Body]:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: item[ITALIC:id: int, item: Item, user: User, importance: ...]
In this case, [BOLD:FastAPI] will expect a body like:
[CODE]BLOCK_1: { "item": { "name": "Foo", "description": "The pre...]
Again, it will convert the data types, validate, document, etc.

metadata.breadcrumb: Body - Multiple Parameters > Singular values in body
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/body-multiple-params.md

page_content:
[Context: Body - Multiple Parameters > Multiple body params and query]

## Multiple body params and query
Of course, you can also declare additional query parameters whenever you need, additional to any body parameters.
As, by default, singular values are interpreted as query parameters, you don't have to explicitly add a [CODE: Query], you can just do:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: q: str | None = None...]
For example:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]1: q: str | None = None, ):...]
/// info
[CODE: Body] also has all the same extra validation and metadata parameters as [CODE: Query], [CODE: Path] and others you will see later.
///

metadata.breadcrumb: Body - Multiple Parameters > Multiple body params and query
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/body-multiple-params.md

page_content:
[Context: Body - Multiple Parameters > Embed a single body parameter]

## Embed a single body parameter
Let's say you only have a single [CODE: item] body parameter from a Pydantic model [CODE: Item].
By default, [BOLD:FastAPI] will then expect its body directly.
But if you want it to expect a JSON with a key [CODE: item] and inside of it the model contents, as it does when you declare extra body parameters, you can use the special [CODE: Body] parameter [CODE: embed]:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: item: Item = Body(embed=True)...]
as in:
```python
async def update[ITALIC:item(item]id: int, item: Annotated[Item, Body(embed=True)]):
results = {"item[ITALIC:id": item]id, "item": item}
```
In this case [BOLD:FastAPI] will expect a body like:
```JSON hl[ITALIC:lines="2"
{
"item": {
"name": "Foo",
"description": "The pretender",
"price": 42.0,
"tax": 3.2
}
}
```
instead of:
[CODE]BLOCK_1: { "name": "Foo", "description": "The pretender", "...]

metadata.breadcrumb: Body - Multiple Parameters > Embed a single body parameter
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/body-multiple-params.md

page_content:
[Context: Body - Multiple Parameters > Recap]

## Recap
You can add multiple body parameters to your [ITALIC:path operation function], even though a request can only have a single body.
But [BOLD:FastAPI] will handle it, give you the correct data in your function, and validate and document the correct schema in the [ITALIC:path operation].
You can also declare singular values to be received as part of the body.
And you can instruct [BOLD:FastAPI] to embed the body in a key even when there is only a single parameter declared.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/body-multiple-params.md

page_content:
[Context: Body - Nested Models]

# Body - Nested Models
With [BOLD:FastAPI], you can define, validate, document, and use arbitrarily deeply nested models (thanks to Pydantic).

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/body-nested-models.md

page_content:
[Context: Body - Nested Models > List fields]

## List fields
You can define an attribute to be a subtype. For example, a Python [CODE: list]:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: tags: list = []...]
This will make [CODE: tags] be a list, although it doesn't declare the type of the elements of the list.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/body-nested-models.md

page_content:
[Context: Body - Nested Models > List fields with type parameter]

## List fields with type parameter
But Python has a specific way to declare lists with internal types, or "type parameters":

metadata.breadcrumb: Body - Nested Models > List fields with type parameter
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/body-nested-models.md

page_content:
[Context: Body - Nested Models > List fields with type parameter > Declare a `list` with a type parameter]

### Declare a [CODE: list] with a type parameter
To declare types that have type parameters (internal types), like [CODE: list], [CODE: dict], [CODE: tuple],
pass the internal type(s) as "type parameters" using square brackets: [CODE: [] and [CODE: ]]
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: my[ITALIC:list: list[str]...]
That's all standard Python syntax for type declarations.
Use that same standard syntax for model attributes with internal types.
So, in our example, we can make [CODE: tags] be specifically a "list of strings":
[CODE]BLOCK_1: tags: list[str] = []...]

hierarchy: Declare a `list` with a type parameter
metadata.breadcrumb: Body - Nested Models > List fields with type parameter > Declare a `list` with a type parameter
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/body-nested-models.md

page_content:
[Context: Body - Nested Models > Set types]

## Set types
But then we think about it, and realize that tags shouldn't repeat, they would probably be unique strings.
And Python has a special data type for sets of unique items, the [CODE: set].
Then we can declare [CODE: tags] as a set of strings:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: tags: set[str] = set()...]
With this, even if you receive a request with duplicate data, it will be converted to a set of unique items.
And whenever you output that data, even if the source had duplicates, it will be output as a set of unique items.
And it will be annotated / documented accordingly too.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/body-nested-models.md

page_content:
[Context: Body - Nested Models > Nested Models]

## Nested Models
Each attribute of a Pydantic model has a type.
But that type can itself be another Pydantic model.
So, you can declare deeply nested JSON "objects" with specific attribute names, types and validations.
All that, arbitrarily nested.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/body-nested-models.md

page_content:
[Context: Body - Nested Models > Nested Models > Define a submodel]

### Define a submodel
For example, we can define an [CODE: Image] model:
```python
class Image(BaseModel):
url: str
name: str
```

metadata.breadcrumb: Body - Nested Models > Nested Models > Define a submodel
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/body-nested-models.md

page_content:
[Context: Body - Nested Models > Nested Models > Use the submodel as a type]

### Use the submodel as a type
And then we can use it as the type of an attribute:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: image: Image | None = None...]
This would mean that [BOLD:FastAPI] would expect a body similar to:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]1: { "name": "Foo", "description": "The pretender", "...]
Again, doing just that declaration, with [BOLD:FastAPI] you get:
[ITALIC: Editor support (completion, etc.), even for nested models
] Data conversion
[ITALIC: Data validation
] Automatic documentation

hierarchy: Use the submodel as a type
metadata.breadcrumb: Body - Nested Models > Nested Models > Use the submodel as a type
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/body-nested-models.md

page_content:
[Context: Body - Nested Models > Special types and validation]

## Special types and validation
Apart from normal singular types like [CODE: str], [CODE: int], [CODE: float], etc. you can use more complex singular types that inherit from [CODE: str].
To see all the options you have, checkout Pydantic's Type Overview. You will see some examples in the next chapter.
For example, as in the [CODE: Image] model we have a [CODE: url] field, we can declare it to be an instance of Pydantic's [CODE: HttpUrl] instead of a [CODE: str]:
```python
from pydantic import BaseModel, HttpUrl

url: HttpUrl
name: str
```
The string will be checked to be a valid URL, and documented in JSON Schema / OpenAPI as such.

metadata.breadcrumb: Body - Nested Models > Special types and validation
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/body-nested-models.md

metadata.breadcrumb: Body - Nested Models > Attributes with lists of submodels
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/body-nested-models.md

metadata.breadcrumb: Body - Nested Models > Deeply nested models
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/body-nested-models.md

page_content:
[Context: Body - Nested Models > Bodies of pure lists]

## Bodies of pure lists
If the top level value of the JSON body you expect is a JSON [CODE: array] (a Python [CODE: list]), you can declare the type in the parameter of the function, the same as in Pydantic models:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: images: list[Image]...]
as in:
```python
async def create[ITALIC:multiple]images(images: list[Image]):
return images
```

metadata.breadcrumb: Body - Nested Models > Bodies of pure lists
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/body-nested-models.md

page_content:
[Context: Body - Nested Models > Editor support everywhere]

## Editor support everywhere
And you get editor support everywhere.
Even for items inside of lists:

You couldn't get this kind of editor support if you were working directly with [CODE: dict] instead of Pydantic models.
But you don't have to worry about them either, incoming dicts are converted automatically and your output is converted automatically to JSON too.

metadata.breadcrumb: Body - Nested Models > Editor support everywhere
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/body-nested-models.md

page_content:
[Context: Body - Nested Models > Bodies of arbitrary `dict`s]

## Bodies of arbitrary [CODE: dict]s
You can also declare a body as a [CODE: dict] with keys of some type and values of some other type.
This way, you don't have to know beforehand what the valid field/attribute names are (as would be the case with Pydantic models).
This would be useful if you want to receive keys that you don't already know.
---
Another useful case is when you want to have keys of another type (e.g., [CODE: int]).
That's what we are going to see here.
In this case, you would accept any [CODE: dict] as long as it has [CODE: int] keys with [CODE: float] values:
```python
async def create[ITALIC:index]weights(weights: dict[int, float]):
return weights
```
/// tip
Keep in mind that JSON only supports [CODE: str] as keys.
But Pydantic has automatic data conversion.
This means that, even though your API clients can only send strings as keys, as long as those strings contain pure integers, Pydantic will convert them and validate them.
And the [CODE: dict] you receive as [CODE: weights] will actually have [CODE: int] keys and [CODE: float] values.
///

metadata.breadcrumb: Body - Nested Models > Bodies of arbitrary `dict`s
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/body-nested-models.md

[Context: Body - Nested Models > Recap]

## Recap
With [BOLD:FastAPI] you have the maximum flexibility provided by Pydantic models, while keeping your code simple, short and elegant.
But with all the benefits:
[ITALIC: Editor support (completion everywhere!)
] Data conversion (a.k.a. parsing / serialization)
[ITALIC: Data validation
] Schema documentation
- Automatic docs

page_content:
[Context: Body - Updates > Update replacing with `PUT`]

# Body - Updates
## Update replacing with [CODE: PUT]
To update an item you can use the [HTTP [CODE: PUT]](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Methods/PUT) operation.
You can use the [CODE: jsonable[ITALIC:encoder] to convert the input data to data that can be stored as JSON (e.g. with a NoSQL database). For example, converting [CODE: datetime] to [CODE: str].
```python
@app.put("/items/{item]id}", response[ITALIC:model=Item)
async def update]item(item[ITALIC:id: str, item: Item):
update]item[ITALIC:encoded = jsonable]encoder(item)
items[item[ITALIC:id] = update]item[ITALIC:encoded
return update]item_encoded
```
[CODE: PUT] is used to receive data that should replace the existing data.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/body-updates.md

page_content:
[Context: Body - Updates > Update replacing with `PUT` > Warning about replacing]

### Warning about replacing
That means that if you want to update the item [CODE: bar] using [CODE: PUT] with a body containing:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: { "name": "Barz", "price": 3, "description": None,...]
because it doesn't include the already stored attribute [CODE: "tax": 20.2], the input model would take the default value of [CODE: "tax": 10.5].
And the data would be saved with that "new" [CODE: tax] of [CODE: 10.5].

metadata.breadcrumb: Body - Updates > Update replacing with `PUT` > Warning about replacing
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/body-updates.md

page_content:
[Context: Body - Updates > Partial updates with `PATCH`]

## Partial updates with [CODE: PATCH]
You can also use the [HTTP [CODE: PATCH]](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Methods/PATCH) operation to [ITALIC:partially] update data.
This means that you can send only the data that you want to update, leaving the rest intact.
/// note
[CODE: PATCH] is less commonly used and known than [CODE: PUT].
And many teams use only [CODE: PUT], even for partial updates.
You are [BOLD:free] to use them however you want, [BOLD:FastAPI] doesn't impose any restrictions.
But this guide shows you, more or less, how they are intended to be used.
///

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/body-updates.md

page_content:
[Context: Body - Updates > Partial updates with `PATCH` > Using Pydantic's `exclude_unset` parameter]

### Using Pydantic's [CODE: exclude[ITALIC:unset] parameter
If you want to receive partial updates, it's very useful to use the parameter [CODE: exclude]unset] in Pydantic's model's [CODE: .model[ITALIC:dump()].
Like [CODE: item.model]dump(exclude[ITALIC:unset=True)].
That would generate a [CODE: dict] with only the data that was set when creating the [CODE: item] model, excluding default values.
Then you can use this to generate a [CODE: dict] with only the data that was set (sent in the request), omitting default values:
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:0: update]data = item.model[ITALIC:dump(exclude]unset=True) ...]

metadata.breadcrumb: Body - Updates > Partial updates with `PATCH` > Using Pydantic's `exclude_unset` parameter
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/body-updates.md

page_content:
[Context: Body - Updates > Partial updates with `PATCH` > Using Pydantic's `update` parameter]

### Using Pydantic's [CODE: update] parameter
Now, you can create a copy of the existing model using [CODE: .model[ITALIC:copy()], and pass the [CODE: update] parameter with a [CODE: dict] containing the data to update.
Like [CODE: stored]item[ITALIC:model.model]copy(update=update[ITALIC:data)]:
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:0: updated]item = stored[ITALIC:item]model.model_copy(update...]

metadata.breadcrumb: Body - Updates > Partial updates with `PATCH` > Using Pydantic's `update` parameter

page_content:
[Context: Request Body]

# Request Body
When you need to send data from a client (let's say, a browser) to your API, you send it as a [BOLD:request body].
A [BOLD:request] body is data sent by the client to your API. A [BOLD:response] body is the data your API sends to the client.
Your API almost always has to send a [BOLD:response] body. But clients don't necessarily need to send [BOLD:request bodies] all the time, sometimes they only request a path, maybe with some query parameters, but don't send a body.
To declare a [BOLD:request] body, you use Pydantic models with all their power and benefits.
/// info
To send data, you should use one of: [CODE: POST] (the more common), [CODE: PUT], [CODE: DELETE] or [CODE: PATCH].
Sending a body with a [CODE: GET] request has an undefined behavior in the specifications, nevertheless, it is supported by FastAPI, only for very complex/extreme use cases.
As it is discouraged, the interactive docs with Swagger UI won't show the documentation for the body when using [CODE: GET], and proxies in the middle might not support it.
///

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/body.md

# Request Body
When you need to send data from a client (let's say, a browser) to your API, you send it as a [BOLD:request body].
A [BOLD:request] body is data sent by the client to your API. A [BOLD:response] body is the data your API sends to the client.
Your API almost always has to send a [BOLD:response] body. But clients don't necessarily need to send [BOLD:request bodies] all the time, sometimes they only request a path, maybe with some query parameters, but don't send a body.
To declare a [BOLD:request] body, you use Pydantic models with all their power and benefits.
/// info
To send data, you should use one of: [CODE: POST] (the more common), [CODE: PUT], [CODE: DELETE] or [CODE: PATCH].
Sending a body with a [CODE: GET] request has an undefined behavior in the specifications, nevertheless, it is supported by FastAPI, only for very complex/extreme use cases.
As it is discouraged, the interactive docs with Swagger UI won't show the documentation for the body when using [CODE: GET], and proxies in the middle might not support it.
///

page_content:
[Context: Request Body > Import Pydantic's `BaseModel`]

## Import Pydantic's [CODE: BaseModel]
First, you need to import [CODE: BaseModel] from [CODE: pydantic]:
```python
from pydantic import BaseModel

```

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/body.md

page_content:
[Context: Request Body > Create your data model]

## Create your data model
Then you declare your data model as a class that inherits from [CODE: BaseModel].
Use standard Python types for all the attributes:
```python
class Item(BaseModel):
name: str
description: str | None = None
price: float
tax: float | None = None
```
The same as when declaring query parameters, when a model attribute has a default value, it is not required. Otherwise, it is required. Use [CODE: None] to make it just optional.
For example, this model above declares a JSON "[CODE: object]" (or Python [CODE: dict]) like:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: { "name": "Foo", "description": "An optional descr...]
...as [CODE: description] and [CODE: tax] are optional (with a default value of [CODE: None]), this JSON "[CODE: object]" would also be valid:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]1: { "name": "Foo", "price": 45.2 }...]

metadata.breadcrumb: Request Body > Create your data model
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/body.md

page_content:
[Context: Request Body > Declare it as a parameter]

## Declare it as a parameter
To add it to your [ITALIC:path operation], declare it the same way you declared path and query parameters:
```python
async def create_item(item: Item):
return item
```
...and declare its type as the model you created, [CODE: Item].

metadata.breadcrumb: Request Body > Declare it as a parameter

Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/body.md

page_content:
[Context: Request Body > Automatic docs]

## Automatic docs
The JSON Schemas of your models will be part of your OpenAPI generated schema, and will be shown in the interactive API docs:

And will also be used in the API docs inside each [ITALIC:path operation] that needs them:

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/body.md

page_content:
[Context: Request Body > Editor support]

## Editor support
In your editor, inside your function you will get type hints and completion everywhere (this wouldn't happen if you received a [CODE: dict] instead of a Pydantic model):

You also get error checks for incorrect type operations:

This is not by chance, the whole framework was built around that design.
And it was thoroughly tested at the design phase, before any implementation, to ensure it would work with all the editors.
There were even some changes to Pydantic itself to support this.
The previous screenshots were taken with Visual Studio Code.
But you would get the same editor support with PyCharm and most of the other Python editors:

/// tip
If you use PyCharm as your editor, you can use the Pydantic PyCharm Plugin.
It improves editor support for Pydantic models, with:
[ITALIC: auto-completion
] type checks
[ITALIC: refactoring
] searching
- inspections
///

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/body.md

page_content:
[Context: Request Body > Use the model]

## Use the model
Inside of the function, you can access all the attributes of the model object directly:
{[ITALIC: ../../docs[ITALIC:src/body/tutorial002]py310.py ]}

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/body.md

page_content:
[Context: Request Body > Request body + path parameters]

## Request body + path parameters
You can declare path parameters and request body at the same time.
[BOLD:FastAPI] will recognize that the function parameters that match path parameters should be [BOLD:taken from the path], and that function parameters that are declared to be Pydantic models should be [BOLD:taken from the request body].
```python
@app.put("/items/{item[ITALIC:id}")
async def update]item(item_id: int, item: Item):
```

metadata.breadcrumb: Request Body > Request body + path parameters
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/body.md

page_content:
[Context: Request Body > Request body + path + query parameters]

## Request body + path + query parameters
You can also declare [BOLD:body], [BOLD:path] and [BOLD:query] parameters, all at the same time.
[BOLD:FastAPI] will recognize each of them and take the data from the correct place.
```python
async def update[ITALIC:item(item]id: int, item: Item, q: str | None = None):
result = {"item[ITALIC:id": item]id, *[ITALIC:item.model_dump()}
```
The function parameters will be recognized as follows:
] If the parameter is also declared in the [BOLD:path], it will be used as a path parameter.
[ITALIC: If the parameter is of a [BOLD:singular type] (like [CODE: int], [CODE: float], [CODE: str], [CODE: bool], etc) it will be interpreted as a [BOLD:query] parameter.
] If the parameter is declared to be of the type of a [BOLD:Pydantic model], it will be interpreted as a request [BOLD:body].
/// note
FastAPI will know that the value of [CODE: q] is not required because of the default value [CODE: = None].
The [CODE: str | None] is not used by FastAPI to determine that the value is not required, it will know it's not required because it has a default value of [CODE: = None].
But adding the type annotations will allow your editor to give you better support and detect errors.
///

metadata.breadcrumb: Request Body > Request body + path + query parameters
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/body.md

[Context: Request Body > Request body + path + query parameters]
