] If the parameter is also declared in the [BOLD:path], it will be used as a path parameter.
[ITALIC: If the parameter is of a [BOLD:singular type] (like [CODE: int], [CODE: float], [CODE: str], [CODE: bool], etc) it will be interpreted as a [BOLD:query] parameter.
] If the parameter is declared to be of the type of a [BOLD:Pydantic model], it will be interpreted as a request [BOLD:body].
/// note
FastAPI will know that the value of [CODE: q] is not required because of the default value [CODE: = None].
The [CODE: str | None] is not used by FastAPI to determine that the value is not required, it will know it's not required because it has a default value of [CODE: = None].
But adding the type annotations will allow your editor to give you better support and detect errors.

page_content:
[Context: Cookie Parameter Models > Cookies with a Pydantic Model]

## Cookies with a Pydantic Model
Declare the [BOLD:cookie] parameters that you need in a [BOLD:Pydantic model], and then declare the parameter as [CODE: Cookie]:
```python
class Cookies(BaseModel):
session[ITALIC:id: str
fatebook]tracker: str | None = None
googall[ITALIC:tracker: str | None = None
async def read]items(cookies: Annotated[Cookies, Cookie()]):
return cookies
```
[BOLD:FastAPI] will [BOLD:extract] the data for [BOLD:each field] from the [BOLD:cookies] received in the request and give you the Pydantic model you defined.

metadata.breadcrumb: Cookie Parameter Models > Cookies with a Pydantic Model
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/cookie-param-models.md

page_content:
[Context: Cookie Parameter Models > Check the Docs]

## Check the Docs
You can see the defined cookies in the docs UI at [CODE: /docs]:


/// info
Have in mind that, as [BOLD:browsers handle cookies] in special ways and behind the scenes, they [BOLD:don't] easily allow [BOLD:JavaScript] to touch them.
If you go to the [BOLD:API docs UI] at [CODE: /docs] you will be able to see the [BOLD:documentation] for cookies for your [ITALIC:path operations].
But even if you [BOLD:fill the data] and click "Execute", because the docs UI works with [BOLD:JavaScript], the cookies won't be sent, and you will see an [BOLD:error] message as if you didn't write any values.
///

metadata.breadcrumb: Cookie Parameter Models > Check the Docs
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/cookie-param-models.md

page_content:
[Context: Cookie Parameter Models > Forbid Extra Cookies]

## Forbid Extra Cookies
In some special use cases (probably not very common), you might want to [BOLD:restrict] the cookies that you want to receive.
Your API now has the power to control its own cookie consent. 🤪
You can use Pydantic's model configuration to [CODE: forbid] any [CODE: extra] fields:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: model[ITALIC:config = {"extra": "forbid"}...]
If a client tries to send some [BOLD:extra cookies], they will receive an [BOLD:error] response.
Poor cookie banners with all their effort to get your consent for the API to reject it.
For example, if the client tries to send a [CODE: santa]tracker] cookie with a value of [CODE: good-list-please], the client will receive an [BOLD:error] response telling them that the [CODE: santa[ITALIC:tracker] cookie is not allowed:
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:1: { "detail": [ { "type": "extra]forbidden", "loc": ...]

metadata.breadcrumb: Cookie Parameter Models > Forbid Extra Cookies
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/cookie-param-models.md

page_content:
[Context: CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing)]

# CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing)
CORS or "Cross-Origin Resource Sharing" refers to the situations when a frontend running in a browser has JavaScript code that communicates with a backend, and the backend is in a different "origin" than the frontend.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/cors.md

page_content:
[Context: CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) > Origin]

## Origin
An origin is the combination of protocol ([CODE: http], [CODE: https]), domain ([CODE: myapp.com], [CODE: localhost], [CODE: localhost.tiangolo.com]), and port ([CODE: 80], [CODE: 443], [CODE: 8080]).
So, all these are different origins:
[ITALIC: [CODE: http://localhost]
] [CODE: https://localhost]
- [CODE: http://localhost:8080]
Even if they are all in [CODE: localhost], they use different protocols or ports, so, they are different "origins".

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/cors.md

page_content:
[Context: CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) > Steps]

## Steps
So, let's say you have a frontend running in your browser at [CODE: http://localhost:8080], and its JavaScript is trying to communicate with a backend running at [CODE: http://localhost] (because we don't specify a port, the browser will assume the default port [CODE: 80]).
Then, the browser will send an HTTP [CODE: OPTIONS] request to the [CODE: :80]-backend, and if the backend sends the appropriate headers authorizing the communication from this different origin ([CODE: http://localhost:8080]) then the [CODE: :8080]-browser will let the JavaScript in the frontend send its request to the [CODE: :80]-backend.
To achieve this, the [CODE: :80]-backend must have a list of "allowed origins".
In this case, the list would have to include [CODE: http://localhost:8080] for the [CODE: :8080]-frontend to work correctly.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/cors.md

page_content:
[Context: CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) > Wildcards]

## Wildcards
It's also possible to declare the list as [CODE: "*"] (a "wildcard") to say that all are allowed.
But that will only allow certain types of communication, excluding everything that involves credentials: Cookies, Authorization headers like those used with Bearer Tokens, etc.
So, for everything to work correctly, it's better to specify explicitly the allowed origins.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/cors.md

page_content:
[Context: CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) > Use `CORSMiddleware`]

## Use [CODE: CORSMiddleware]
You can configure it in your [BOLD:FastAPI] application using the [CODE: CORSMiddleware].
[ITALIC: Import [CODE: CORSMiddleware].
] Create a list of allowed origins (as strings).
[ITALIC: Add it as a "middleware" to your [BOLD:FastAPI] application.
You can also specify whether your backend allows:
] Credentials (Authorization headers, Cookies, etc).
[ITALIC: Specific HTTP methods ([CODE: POST], [CODE: PUT]) or all of them with the wildcard [CODE: "]"].
[ITALIC: Specific HTTP headers or all of them with the wildcard [CODE: "]"].
```python
from fastapi.middleware.cors import CORSMiddleware

origins = [
"http://localhost.tiangolo.com",
"https://localhost.tiangolo.com",
"http://localhost",
"http://localhost:8080",
]
app.add[ITALIC:middleware(
CORSMiddleware,
allow]origins=origins,
allow[ITALIC:credentials=True,
allow]methods=["[ITALIC:"],
allow[ITALIC:headers=["]"],
)
```
The default parameters used by the [CODE: CORSMiddleware] implementation are restrictive by default, so you'll need to explicitly enable particular origins, methods, or headers, in order for browsers to be permitted to use them in a Cross-Domain context.
The following arguments are supported:
[ITALIC: [CODE: allow]origins] - A list of origins that should be permitted to make cross-origin requests. E.g. [CODE: ['https://example.org', 'https://www.example.org']]. You can use [CODE: [']']] to allow any origin.
[ITALIC: [CODE: allow[ITALIC:origin]regex] - A regex string to match against origins that should be permitted to make cross-origin requests. e.g. [CODE: 'https://.]\.example\.org'].
[ITALIC: [CODE: allow[ITALIC:methods] - A list of HTTP methods that should be allowed for cross-origin requests. Defaults to [CODE: ['GET']]. You can use [CODE: [']']] to allow all standard methods.
[ITALIC: [CODE: allow]headers] - A list of HTTP request headers that should be supported for cross-origin requests. Defaults to [CODE: []]. You can use [CODE: [']']] to allow all headers. The [CODE: Accept], [CODE: Accept-Language], [CODE: Content-Language] and [CODE: Content-Type] headers are always allowed for simple CORS requests.
[ITALIC: [CODE: allow[ITALIC:credentials] - Indicate that cookies should be supported for cross-origin requests. Defaults to [CODE: False].
None of [CODE: allow]origins], [CODE: allow[ITALIC:methods] and [CODE: allow]headers] can be set to [CODE: [']']] if [CODE: allow[ITALIC:credentials] is set to [CODE: True]. All of them must be explicitly specified.
[ITALIC: [CODE: expose]headers] - Indicate any response headers that should be made accessible to the browser. Defaults to [CODE: []].
] [CODE: max_age] - Sets a maximum time in seconds for browsers to cache CORS responses. Defaults to [CODE: 600].
The middleware responds to two particular types of HTTP request...

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/cors.md

page_content:
[Context: CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) > Use `CORSMiddleware`]

## Use [CODE: CORSMiddleware]
You can configure it in your [BOLD:FastAPI] application using the [CODE: CORSMiddleware].
[ITALIC: Import [CODE: CORSMiddleware].
] Create a list of allowed origins (as strings).
[ITALIC: Add it as a "middleware" to your [BOLD:FastAPI] application.
You can also specify whether your backend allows:
] Credentials (Authorization headers, Cookies, etc).
[ITALIC: Specific HTTP methods ([CODE: POST], [CODE: PUT]) or all of them with the wildcard [CODE: "]"].
[ITALIC: Specific HTTP headers or all of them with the wildcard [CODE: "]"].
```python
from fastapi.middleware.cors import CORSMiddleware

origins = [
"http://localhost.tiangolo.com",
"https://localhost.tiangolo.com",
"http://localhost",
"http://localhost:8080",
]
app.add[ITALIC:middleware(
CORSMiddleware,
allow]origins=origins,
allow[ITALIC:credentials=True,
allow]methods=["[ITALIC:"],
allow[ITALIC:headers=["]"],
)
```
The default parameters used by the [CODE: CORSMiddleware] implementation are restrictive by default, so you'll need to explicitly enable particular origins, methods, or headers, in order for browsers to be permitted to use them in a Cross-Domain context.
The following arguments are supported:
[ITALIC: [CODE: allow]origins] - A list of origins that should be permitted to make cross-origin requests. E.g. [CODE: ['https://example.org', 'https://www.example.org']]. You can use [CODE: [']']] to allow any origin.
[ITALIC: [CODE: allow[ITALIC:origin]regex] - A regex string to match against origins that should be permitted to make cross-origin requests. e.g. [CODE: 'https://.]\.example\.org'].
[ITALIC: [CODE: allow[ITALIC:methods] - A list of HTTP methods that should be allowed for cross-origin requests. Defaults to [CODE: ['GET']]. You can use [CODE: [']']] to allow all standard methods.
[ITALIC: [CODE: allow]headers] - A list of HTTP request headers that should be supported for cross-origin requests. Defaults to [CODE: []]. You can use [CODE: [']']] to allow all headers. The [CODE: Accept], [CODE: Accept-Language], [CODE: Content-Language] and [CODE: Content-Type] headers are always allowed for simple CORS requests.
[ITALIC: [CODE: allow[ITALIC:credentials] - Indicate that cookies should be supported for cross-origin requests. Defaults to [CODE: False].
None of [CODE: allow]origins], [CODE: allow[ITALIC:methods] and [CODE: allow]headers] can be set to [CODE: [']']] if [CODE: allow[ITALIC:credentials] is set to [CODE: True]. All of them must be explicitly specified.
[ITALIC: [CODE: expose]headers] - Indicate any response headers that should be made accessible to the browser. Defaults to [CODE: []].
] [CODE: max_age] - Sets a maximum time in seconds for browsers to cache CORS responses. Defaults to [CODE: 600].
The middleware responds to two particular types of HTTP request...

page_content:
[Context: CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) > Use `CORSMiddleware` > CORS preflight requests]

### CORS preflight requests
These are any [CODE: OPTIONS] request with [CODE: Origin] and [CODE: Access-Control-Request-Method] headers.
In this case the middleware will intercept the incoming request and respond with appropriate CORS headers, and either a [CODE: 200] or [CODE: 400] response for informational purposes.

metadata.breadcrumb: CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) > Use `CORSMiddleware` > CORS preflight requests
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/cors.md

page_content:
[Context: CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) > Use `CORSMiddleware` > Simple requests]

### Simple requests
Any request with an [CODE: Origin] header. In this case the middleware will pass the request through as normal, but will include appropriate CORS headers on the response.

metadata.breadcrumb: CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) > Use `CORSMiddleware` > Simple requests
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/cors.md

page_content:
[Context: CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) > More info]

## More info
For more info about CORS, check the Mozilla CORS documentation.
/// note | Technical Details
You could also use [CODE: from starlette.middleware.cors import CORSMiddleware].
[BOLD:FastAPI] provides several middlewares in [CODE: fastapi.middleware] just as a convenience for you, the developer. But most of the available middlewares come directly from Starlette.
///

page_content:
[Context: Debugging]

# Debugging
You can connect the debugger in your editor, for example with Visual Studio Code or PyCharm.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/debugging.md

page_content:
[Context: Debugging > Call `uvicorn`]

## Call [CODE: uvicorn]
In your FastAPI application, import and run [CODE: uvicorn] directly:
```python
import uvicorn
from fastapi import FastAPI
uvicorn.run(app, host="0.0.0.0", port=8000)
```

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/debugging.md

page_content:
[Context: Debugging > Call `uvicorn` > About `__name__ == "__main__"`]

### About [CODE: [BOLD:name] == "[BOLD:main]"]
The main purpose of the [CODE: [BOLD:name] == "[BOLD:main]"] is to have some code that is executed when your file is called with:

[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: $ python myapp.py...]

but is not called when another file imports it, like in:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]1: from myapp import app...]
#### More details
Let's say your file is named [CODE: myapp.py].
If you run it with:

[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]2: $ python myapp.py...]

then the internal variable [CODE: [BOLD:name]] in your file, created automatically by Python, will have as value the string [CODE: "[BOLD:main]"].
So, the section:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]3: uvicorn.run(app, host="0.0.0.0", port=8000)...]
will run.
---
This won't happen if you import that module (file).
So, if you have another file [CODE: importer.py] with:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]4: from myapp import app # Some more code...]
in that case, the automatically created variable [CODE: [BOLD:name]] inside of [CODE: myapp.py] will not have the value [CODE: "[BOLD:main]"].
So, the line:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]5: uvicorn.run(app, host="0.0.0.0", port=8000)...]
will not be executed.
/// info
For more information, check the official Python docs.
///

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/debugging.md

page_content:
[Context: Debugging > Call `uvicorn` > About `__name__ == "__main__"`]

So, the line:

/// info
For more information, check the official Python docs.
///

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/debugging.md

page_content:
[Context: Debugging > Run your code with your debugger]

## Run your code with your debugger
Because you are running the Uvicorn server directly from your code, you can call your Python program (your FastAPI application) directly from the debugger.
---
For example, in Visual Studio Code, you can:
[ITALIC: Go to the "Debug" panel.
] "Add configuration...".
[ITALIC: Select "Python"
] Run the debugger with the option "[CODE: Python: Current File (Integrated Terminal)]".
It will then start the server with your [BOLD:FastAPI] code, stop at your breakpoints, etc.
Here's how it might look:

---
If you use Pycharm, you can:
[ITALIC: Open the "Run" menu.
] Select the option "Debug...".
[ITALIC: Then a context menu shows up.
] Select the file to debug (in this case, [CODE: main.py]).
It will then start the server with your [BOLD:FastAPI] code, stop at your breakpoints, etc.
Here's how it might look:

hierarchy: Run your code with your debugger

page_content:
[Context: JSON Compatible Encoder]

# JSON Compatible Encoder
There are some cases where you might need to convert a data type (like a Pydantic model) to something compatible with JSON (like a [CODE: dict], [CODE: list], etc).
For example, if you need to store it in a database.
For that, [BOLD:FastAPI] provides a [CODE: jsonable_encoder()] function.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/encoder.md

page_content:
[Context: JSON Compatible Encoder > Using the `jsonable_encoder`]

## Using the [CODE: jsonable[ITALIC:encoder]
Let's imagine that you have a database [CODE: fake]db] that only receives JSON compatible data.
For example, it doesn't receive [CODE: datetime] objects, as those are not compatible with JSON.
So, a [CODE: datetime] object would have to be converted to a [CODE: str] containing the data in ISO format.
The same way, this database wouldn't receive a Pydantic model (an object with attributes), only a [CODE: dict].
You can use [CODE: jsonable[ITALIC:encoder] for that.
It receives an object, like a Pydantic model, and returns a JSON compatible version:
```python
from fastapi.encoders import jsonable]encoder
from pydantic import BaseModel
json[ITALIC:compatible]item[ITALIC:data = jsonable]encoder(item)
fake[ITALIC:db[id] = json]compatible[ITALIC:item]data
```
In this example, it would convert the Pydantic model to a [CODE: dict], and the [CODE: datetime] to a [CODE: str].
The result of calling it is something that can be encoded with the Python standard [[CODE: json.dumps()]](https://docs.python.org/3/library/json.html#json.dumps).
It doesn't return a large [CODE: str] containing the data in JSON format (as a string). It returns a Python standard data structure (e.g. a [CODE: dict]) with values and sub-values that are all compatible with JSON.
/// note
[CODE: jsonable_encoder] is actually used by [BOLD:FastAPI] internally to convert data. But it is useful in many other scenarios.
///

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/encoder.md

page_content:
[Context: JSON Compatible Encoder > Using the `jsonable_encoder`]

page_content:
[Context: Extra Data Types]

# Extra Data Types
Up to now, you have been using common data types, like:
[ITALIC: [CODE: int]
] [CODE: float]
[ITALIC: [CODE: str]
] [CODE: bool]
But you can also use more complex data types.
And you will still have the same features as seen up to now:
[ITALIC: Great editor support.
] Data conversion from incoming requests.
[ITALIC: Data conversion for response data.
] Data validation.
- Automatic annotation and documentation.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/extra-data-types.md

metadata.breadcrumb: Extra Data Types > Other data types
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/extra-data-types.md

metadata.breadcrumb: Extra Data Types > Other data types
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/extra-data-types.md

page_content:
[Context: Extra Data Types > Example]

## Example
Here's an example [ITALIC:path operation] with parameters using some of the above types.
```python
from datetime import datetime, time, timedelta
from typing import Annotated
from uuid import UUID

item[ITALIC:id: UUID,
start]datetime: Annotated[datetime, Body()],
end[ITALIC:datetime: Annotated[datetime, Body()],
process]after: Annotated[timedelta, Body()],
repeat[ITALIC:at: Annotated[time | None, Body()] = None,
```
Note that the parameters inside the function have their natural data type, and you can, for example, perform normal date manipulations, like:
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:0: start]process = start[ITALIC:datetime + process]after dur...]

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/extra-data-types.md

page_content:
[Context: Extra Models]

# Extra Models
Continuing with the previous example, it will be common to have more than one related model.
This is especially the case for user models, because:
[ITALIC: The [BOLD:input model] needs to be able to have a password.
] The [BOLD:output model] should not have a password.
- The [BOLD:database model] would probably need to have a hashed password.
/// danger
Never store user's plaintext passwords. Always store a "secure hash" that you can then verify.
If you don't know, you will learn what a "password hash" is in the security chapters.
///

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/extra-models.md

page_content:
[Context: Extra Models > Multiple models]

## Multiple models
Here's a general idea of how the models could look like with their password fields and the places where they are used:
```python
class UserIn(BaseModel):
username: str
password: str
email: EmailStr
class UserOut(BaseModel):
username: str
class UserInDB(BaseModel):
username: str
hashed[ITALIC:password: str
email: EmailStr
def fake]password[ITALIC:hasher(raw]password: str):
return "supersecret" + raw[ITALIC:password
def fake]save[ITALIC:user(user]in: UserIn):
hashed[ITALIC:password = fake]password[ITALIC:hasher(user]in.password)
user[ITALIC:in]db = UserInDB(**user[ITALIC:in.model]dump(), hashed[ITALIC:password=hashed]password)
@app.post("/user/", response[ITALIC:model=UserOut)
async def create]user(user_in: UserIn):
```

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/extra-models.md

page_content:
[Context: Extra Models > Multiple models > About `**user_in.model_dump()`]

### About [CODE: [BOLD:user[ITALIC:in.model]dump()]
#### Pydantic's [CODE: .model[ITALIC:dump()]
[CODE: user]in] is a Pydantic model of class [CODE: UserIn].
Pydantic models have a [CODE: .model[ITALIC:dump()] method that returns a [CODE: dict] with the model's data.
So, if we create a Pydantic object [CODE: user]in] like:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: user[ITALIC:in = UserIn(username="john", password="secret...]
and then we call:
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:1: user]dict = user[ITALIC:in.model]dump()...]
we now have a [CODE: dict] with the data in the variable [CODE: user[ITALIC:dict] (it's a [CODE: dict] instead of a Pydantic model object).
And if we call:
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:2: print(user]dict)...]
we would get a Python [CODE: dict] with:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]3: { 'username': 'john', 'password': 'secret', 'email...]
#### Unpacking a [CODE: dict]
If we take a [CODE: dict] like [CODE: user[ITALIC:dict] and pass it to a function (or class) with [CODE: ]user]dict], Python will "unpack" it. It will pass the keys and values of the [CODE: user[ITALIC:dict] directly as key-value arguments.
So, continuing with the [CODE: user]dict] from above, writing:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]4: UserInDB([BOLD:user[ITALIC:dict)...]
would result in something equivalent to:
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:5: UserInDB( username="john", password="secret", emai...]
Or more exactly, using [CODE: user]dict] directly, with whatever contents it might have in the future:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]6: UserInDB( username = user[ITALIC:dict["username"], passwo...]
#### A Pydantic model from the contents of another
As in the example above we got [CODE: user]dict] from [CODE: user[ITALIC:in.model]dump()], this code:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]7: user[ITALIC:dict = user]in.model[ITALIC:dump() UserInDB(]user]d...]
would be equivalent to:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]8: UserInDB([BOLD:user[ITALIC:in.model]dump())...]
...because [CODE: user[ITALIC:in.model]dump()] is a [CODE: dict], and then we make Python "unpack" it by passing it to [CODE: UserInDB] prefixed with [CODE: ]].
So, we get a Pydantic model from the data in another Pydantic model.
#### Unpacking a [CODE: dict] and extra keywords
And then adding the extra keyword argument [CODE: hashed[ITALIC:password=hashed]password], like in:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]9: UserInDB(**user[ITALIC:in.model]dump(), hashed[ITALIC:password=h...]
...ends up being like:
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:10: UserInDB( username = user]dict["username"], passwo...]
/// warning
The supporting additional functions [CODE: fake[ITALIC:password]hasher] and [CODE: fake[ITALIC:save]user] are just to demo a possible flow of the data, but they of course are not providing any real security.
///

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/extra-models.md

page_content:
[Context: Extra Models > Multiple models > About `**user_in.model_dump()`]

### About [CODE: [BOLD:user[ITALIC:in.model]dump()]

[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]6: UserInDB( username = user[ITALIC:dict["username"], passwo...]
#### A Pydantic model from the contents of another
As in the example above we got [CODE: user]dict] from [CODE: user[ITALIC:in.model]dump()], this code:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]7: user[ITALIC:dict = user]in.model[ITALIC:dump() UserInDB(]user]d...]
would be equivalent to:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]8: UserInDB([BOLD:user[ITALIC:in.model]dump())...]
...because [CODE: user[ITALIC:in.model]dump()] is a [CODE: dict], and then we make Python "unpack" it by passing it to [CODE: UserInDB] prefixed with [CODE: ]].
So, we get a Pydantic model from the data in another Pydantic model.
#### Unpacking a [CODE: dict] and extra keywords
And then adding the extra keyword argument [CODE: hashed[ITALIC:password=hashed]password], like in:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]9: UserInDB(**user[ITALIC:in.model]dump(), hashed[ITALIC:password=h...]
...ends up being like:
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:10: UserInDB( username = user]dict["username"], passwo...]
/// warning
The supporting additional functions [CODE: fake[ITALIC:password]hasher] and [CODE: fake[ITALIC:save]user] are just to demo a possible flow of the data, but they of course are not providing any real security.
///

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/extra-models.md

page_content:
[Context: Extra Models > Multiple models > About `**user_in.model_dump()`]

### About [CODE: [BOLD:user[ITALIC:in.model]dump()]
#### Pydantic's [CODE: .model[ITALIC:dump()]
[CODE: user]in] is a Pydantic model of class [CODE: UserIn].
Pydantic models have a [CODE: .model[ITALIC:dump()] method that returns a [CODE: dict] with the model's data.
So, if we create a Pydantic object [CODE: user]in] like:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: user[ITALIC:in = UserIn(username="john", password="secret...]
and then we call:
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:1: user]dict = user[ITALIC:in.model]dump()...]
we now have a [CODE: dict] with the data in the variable [CODE: user[ITALIC:dict] (it's a [CODE: dict] instead of a Pydantic model object).
And if we call:
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:2: print(user]dict)...]
we would get a Python [CODE: dict] with:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]3: { 'username': 'john', 'password': 'secret', 'email...]
#### Unpacking a [CODE: dict]
If we take a [CODE: dict] like [CODE: user[ITALIC:dict] and pass it to a function (or class) with [CODE: ]user]dict], Python will "unpack" it. It will pass the keys and values of the [CODE: user[ITALIC:dict] directly as key-value arguments.
So, continuing with the [CODE: user]dict] from above, writing:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]4: UserInDB([BOLD:user[ITALIC:dict)...]
would result in something equivalent to:
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:5: UserInDB( username="john", password="secret", emai...]
Or more exactly, using [CODE: user]dict] directly, with whatever contents it might have in the future:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]6: UserInDB( username = user[ITALIC:dict["username"], passwo...]
#### A Pydantic model from the contents of another
As in the example above we got [CODE: user]dict] from [CODE: user[ITALIC:in.model]dump()], this code:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]7: user[ITALIC:dict = user]in.model[ITALIC:dump() UserInDB(]user]d...]
would be equivalent to:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]8: UserInDB([BOLD:user[ITALIC:in.model]dump())...]
...because [CODE: user[ITALIC:in.model]dump()] is a [CODE: dict], and then we make Python "unpack" it by passing it to [CODE: UserInDB] prefixed with [CODE: ]].
So, we get a Pydantic model from the data in another Pydantic model.
#### Unpacking a [CODE: dict] and extra keywords
And then adding the extra keyword argument [CODE: hashed[ITALIC:password=hashed]password], like in:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]9: UserInDB(**user[ITALIC:in.model]dump(), hashed[ITALIC:password=h...]
...ends up being like:
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:10: UserInDB( username = user]dict["username"], passwo...]
/// warning
The supporting additional functions [CODE: fake[ITALIC:password]hasher] and [CODE: fake[ITALIC:save]user] are just to demo a possible flow of the data, but they of course are not providing any real security.
///

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/extra-models.md

page_content:
[Context: Extra Models > Reduce duplication]

## Reduce duplication
Reducing code duplication is one of the core ideas in [BOLD:FastAPI].
As code duplication increments the chances of bugs, security issues, code desynchronization issues (when you update in one place but not in the others), etc.
And these models are all sharing a lot of the data and duplicating attribute names and types.
We could do better.
We can declare a [CODE: UserBase] model that serves as a base for our other models. And then we can make subclasses of that model that inherit its attributes (type declarations, validation, etc).
All the data conversion, validation, documentation, etc. will still work as normally.
That way, we can declare just the differences between the models (with plaintext [CODE: password], with [CODE: hashed[ITALIC:password] and without password):
```python
class UserBase(BaseModel):
username: str
class UserIn(UserBase):
password: str
class UserOut(UserBase):
pass
class UserInDB(UserBase):
hashed]password: str
```

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/extra-models.md

page_content:
[Context: Extra Models > Reduce duplication]

## Reduce duplication
Reducing code duplication is one of the core ideas in [BOLD:FastAPI].
As code duplication increments the chances of bugs, security issues, code desynchronization issues (when you update in one place but not in the others), etc.

All the data conversion, validation, documentation, etc. will still work as normally.
That way, we can declare just the differences between the models (with plaintext [CODE: password], with [CODE: hashed[ITALIC:password] and without password):
```python
class UserBase(BaseModel):
username: str
class UserIn(UserBase):
password: str
class UserOut(UserBase):
pass
class UserInDB(UserBase):
hashed]password: str
```

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/extra-models.md

page_content:
[Context: Extra Models > `Union` or `anyOf`]

## [CODE: Union] or [CODE: anyOf]
You can declare a response to be the [CODE: Union] of two or more types, that means, that the response would be any of them.
It will be defined in OpenAPI with [CODE: anyOf].
To do that, use the standard Python type hint [[CODE: typing.Union]](https://docs.python.org/3/library/typing.html#typing.Union):
/// note
When defining a [[CODE: Union]](https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/concepts/types/#unions), include the most specific type first, followed by the less specific type. In the example below, the more specific [CODE: PlaneItem] comes before [CODE: CarItem] in [CODE: Union[PlaneItem, CarItem]].
///
```python
from fastapi import FastAPI
from pydantic import BaseModel

size: int

return items[item_id]
```

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/extra-models.md

page_content:
[Context: Extra Models > `Union` or `anyOf` > `Union` in Python 3.10]

### [CODE: Union] in Python 3.10
In this example we pass [CODE: Union[PlaneItem, CarItem]] as the value of the argument [CODE: response[ITALIC:model].
Because we are passing it as a [BOLD:value to an argument] instead of putting it in a [BOLD:type annotation], we have to use [CODE: Union] even in Python 3.10.
If it was in a type annotation we could have used the vertical bar, as:
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:0: some]variable: PlaneItem | CarItem...]
But if we put that in the assignment [CODE: response_model=PlaneItem | CarItem] we would get an error, because Python would try to perform an [BOLD:invalid operation] between [CODE: PlaneItem] and [CODE: CarItem] instead of interpreting that as a type annotation.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/extra-models.md

page_content:
[Context: Extra Models > List of models]

## List of models
The same way, you can declare responses of lists of objects.
For that, use the standard Python [CODE: list]:
```python
@app.get("/items/", response[ITALIC:model=list[Item])
async def read]items():
```

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/extra-models.md

page_content:
[Context: Extra Models > Response with arbitrary `dict`]

## Response with arbitrary [CODE: dict]
You can also declare a response using a plain arbitrary [CODE: dict], declaring just the type of the keys and values, without using a Pydantic model.
This is useful if you don't know the valid field/attribute names (that would be needed for a Pydantic model) beforehand.
In this case, you can use [CODE: dict]:
```python
@app.get("/keyword-weights/", response[ITALIC:model=dict[str, float])
async def read]keyword_weights():
```

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/extra-models.md

page_content:
[Context: Extra Models > Recap]

## Recap
Use multiple Pydantic models and inherit freely for each case.
You don't need to have a single data model per entity if that entity must be able to have different "states". As the case with the user "entity" with a state including [CODE: password], [CODE: password_hash] and no password.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/extra-models.md

# First Steps
The simplest FastAPI file could look like this:
{[ITALIC: ../../docs[ITALIC:src/first]steps/tutorial001[ITALIC:py310.py ]}
Copy that to a file [CODE: main.py].
Run the live server:

[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:0: $ fastapi dev
In the output, there's a line with something like:
```hl]lines="4"
INFO: Uvicorn running on http://127.0.0.1:8000 (Press CTRL+C to quit)
```
That line shows the URL where your app is being served on your local machine.

page_content:
[Context: First Steps > OpenAPI]

### OpenAPI
[BOLD:FastAPI] generates a "schema" with all your API using the [BOLD:OpenAPI] standard for defining APIs.
#### "Schema"
A "schema" is a definition or description of something. Not the code that implements it, but just an abstract description.
#### API "schema"
In this case, OpenAPI is a specification that dictates how to define a schema of your API.
This schema definition includes your API paths, the possible parameters they take, etc.
#### Data "schema"
The term "schema" might also refer to the shape of some data, like a JSON content.
In that case, it would mean the JSON attributes, and data types they have, etc.
#### OpenAPI and JSON Schema
OpenAPI defines an API schema for your API. And that schema includes definitions (or "schemas") of the data sent and received by your API using [BOLD:JSON Schema], the standard for JSON data schemas.
#### Check the [CODE: openapi.json]
If you are curious about how the raw OpenAPI schema looks like, FastAPI automatically generates a JSON (schema) with the descriptions of all your API.
You can see it directly at: http://127.0.0.1:8000/openapi.json.
It will show a JSON starting with something like:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: { "openapi": "3.1.0", "info": { "title": "FastAPI"...]
#### What is OpenAPI for
The OpenAPI schema is what powers the two interactive documentation systems included.
And there are dozens of alternatives, all based on OpenAPI. You could easily add any of those alternatives to your application built with [BOLD:FastAPI].
You could also use it to generate code automatically, for clients that communicate with your API. For example, frontend, mobile or IoT applications.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/first-steps.md

page_content:
[Context: First Steps > Configure the app `entrypoint` in `pyproject.toml`]

### Configure the app [CODE: entrypoint] in [CODE: pyproject.toml]
You can configure where your app is located in a [CODE: pyproject.toml] file like:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: [tool.fastapi] entrypoint = "main:app"...]
That [CODE: entrypoint] will tell the [CODE: fastapi] command that it should import the app like:
```python
from main import app
```
If your code was structured like:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]1: . backend main.py [BOLD:init].py...]
Then you would set the [CODE: entrypoint] as:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]2: [tool.fastapi] entrypoint = "backend.main:app"...]
which would be equivalent to:
```python
from backend.main import app
```

metadata.breadcrumb: First Steps > Configure the app `entrypoint` in `pyproject.toml`
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/first-steps.md

page_content:
[Context: First Steps > `fastapi dev` with path]

### [CODE: fastapi dev] with path
You can also pass the file path to the [CODE: fastapi dev] command, and it will guess the FastAPI app object to use:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: $ fastapi dev main.py...]
But you would have to remember to pass the correct path every time you call the [CODE: fastapi] command.
Additionally, other tools might not be able to find it, for example the VS Code Extension or FastAPI Cloud, so it is recommended to use the [CODE: entrypoint] in [CODE: pyproject.toml].

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/first-steps.md

page_content:
[Context: First Steps > Deploy your app (optional)]

### Deploy your app (optional)
You can optionally deploy your FastAPI app to FastAPI Cloud, go and join the waiting list if you haven't.
If you already have a [BOLD:FastAPI Cloud] account (we invited you from the waiting list ), you can deploy your application with one command.
Before deploying, make sure you are logged in:

[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: $ fastapi login You are logged in to FastAPI Cloud...]

Then deploy your app:

[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]1: $ fastapi deploy Deploying to FastAPI Cloud... D...]

That's it! Now you can access your app at that URL.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/first-steps.md

page_content:
[Context: First Steps > Recap, step by step > Step 1: import `FastAPI`]

## Recap, step by step
### Step 1: import [CODE: FastAPI]
```python
from fastapi import FastAPI

```
[CODE: FastAPI] is a Python class that provides all the functionality for your API.
/// note | Technical Details
[CODE: FastAPI] is a class that inherits directly from [CODE: Starlette].
You can use all the Starlette functionality with [CODE: FastAPI] too.
///

metadata.breadcrumb: First Steps > Recap, step by step > Step 1: import `FastAPI`
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/first-steps.md

page_content:
[Context: First Steps > Recap, step by step > Step 2: create a `FastAPI` "instance"]

### Step 2: create a [CODE: FastAPI] "instance"
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: app = FastAPI()...]
Here the [CODE: app] variable will be an "instance" of the class [CODE: FastAPI].
This will be the main point of interaction to create all your API.

metadata.breadcrumb: First Steps > Recap, step by step > Step 2: create a `FastAPI` "instance"
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/first-steps.md

page_content:
[Context: First Steps > Recap, step by step > Step 3: create a *path operation*]

### Step 3: create a [ITALIC:path operation]
#### Path
"Path" here refers to the last part of the URL starting from the first [CODE: /].
So, in a URL like:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: https://example.com/items/foo...]
...the path would be:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]1: /items/foo...]
/// info
A "path" is also commonly called an "endpoint" or a "route".
///
While building an API, the "path" is the main way to separate "concerns" and "resources".
#### Operation
"Operation" here refers to one of the HTTP "methods".
One of:
[ITALIC: [CODE: POST]
] [CODE: GET]
[ITALIC: [CODE: PUT]
] [CODE: DELETE]
...and the more exotic ones:
[ITALIC: [CODE: OPTIONS]
] [CODE: HEAD]
[ITALIC: [CODE: PATCH]
] [CODE: TRACE]
In the HTTP protocol, you can communicate to each path using one (or more) of these "methods".
---
When building APIs, you normally use these specific HTTP methods to perform a specific action.
Normally you use:
[ITALIC: [CODE: POST]: to create data.
] [CODE: GET]: to read data.
[ITALIC: [CODE: PUT]: to update data.
] [CODE: DELETE]: to delete data.
So, in OpenAPI, each of the HTTP methods is called an "operation".
We are going to call them "[BOLD:operations]" too.
#### Define a [ITALIC:path operation decorator]
```python
@app.get("/")
async def root():
```
The [CODE: @app.get("/")] tells [BOLD:FastAPI] that the function right below is in charge of handling requests that go to:
[ITALIC: the path [CODE: /]
] using a get operation
/// info | [CODE: @decorator] Info
That [CODE: @something] syntax in Python is called a "decorator".
You put it on top of a function. Like a pretty decorative hat (I guess that's where the term came from).
A "decorator" takes the function below and does something with it.
In our case, this decorator tells [BOLD:FastAPI] that the function below corresponds to the [BOLD:path] [CODE: /] with an [BOLD:operation] [CODE: get].
It is the "[BOLD:path operation decorator]".
///
You can also use the other operations:
[ITALIC: [CODE: @app.post()]
] [CODE: @app.put()]
[ITALIC: [CODE: @app.delete()]
And the more exotic ones:
] [CODE: @app.options()]
[ITALIC: [CODE: @app.head()]
] [CODE: @app.patch()]
- [CODE: @app.trace()]
/// tip
You are free to use each operation (HTTP method) as you wish.
[BOLD:FastAPI] doesn't enforce any specific meaning.
The information here is presented as a guideline, not a requirement.
For example, when using GraphQL you normally perform all the actions using only [CODE: POST] operations.
///

metadata.breadcrumb: First Steps > Recap, step by step > Step 3: create a *path operation*
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/first-steps.md

For example, when using GraphQL you normally perform all the actions using only [CODE: POST] operations.
///

metadata.breadcrumb: First Steps > Recap, step by step > Step 3: create a *path operation*
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/first-steps.md

page_content:
[Context: First Steps > Recap, step by step > Step 3: create a *path operation*]

### Step 3: create a [ITALIC:path operation]
#### Path
"Path" here refers to the last part of the URL starting from the first [CODE: /].
So, in a URL like:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: https://example.com/items/foo...]
...the path would be:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]1: /items/foo...]
/// info
A "path" is also commonly called an "endpoint" or a "route".
///
While building an API, the "path" is the main way to separate "concerns" and "resources".
#### Operation
"Operation" here refers to one of the HTTP "methods".
One of:
[ITALIC: [CODE: POST]
] [CODE: GET]
[ITALIC: [CODE: PUT]
] [CODE: DELETE]
...and the more exotic ones:
[ITALIC: [CODE: OPTIONS]
] [CODE: HEAD]
[ITALIC: [CODE: PATCH]
] [CODE: TRACE]
In the HTTP protocol, you can communicate to each path using one (or more) of these "methods".
---
When building APIs, you normally use these specific HTTP methods to perform a specific action.
Normally you use:
[ITALIC: [CODE: POST]: to create data.
] [CODE: GET]: to read data.
[ITALIC: [CODE: PUT]: to update data.
] [CODE: DELETE]: to delete data.
So, in OpenAPI, each of the HTTP methods is called an "operation".
We are going to call them "[BOLD:operations]" too.
#### Define a [ITALIC:path operation decorator]
```python
@app.get("/")
async def root():
```
The [CODE: @app.get("/")] tells [BOLD:FastAPI] that the function right below is in charge of handling requests that go to:
[ITALIC: the path [CODE: /]
] using a get operation
/// info | [CODE: @decorator] Info
That [CODE: @something] syntax in Python is called a "decorator".
You put it on top of a function. Like a pretty decorative hat (I guess that's where the term came from).
A "decorator" takes the function below and does something with it.
In our case, this decorator tells [BOLD:FastAPI] that the function below corresponds to the [BOLD:path] [CODE: /] with an [BOLD:operation] [CODE: get].
It is the "[BOLD:path operation decorator]".
///
You can also use the other operations:
[ITALIC: [CODE: @app.post()]
] [CODE: @app.put()]
[ITALIC: [CODE: @app.delete()]
And the more exotic ones:
] [CODE: @app.options()]
[ITALIC: [CODE: @app.head()]
] [CODE: @app.patch()]
- [CODE: @app.trace()]
/// tip
You are free to use each operation (HTTP method) as you wish.
[BOLD:FastAPI] doesn't enforce any specific meaning.
The information here is presented as a guideline, not a requirement.
For example, when using GraphQL you normally perform all the actions using only [CODE: POST] operations.
///

metadata.breadcrumb: First Steps > Recap, step by step > Step 3: create a *path operation*
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/first-steps.md

page_content:
[Context: First Steps > Recap, step by step > Step 4: define the **path operation function**]

### Step 4: define the [BOLD:path operation function]
This is our "[BOLD:path operation function]":
[ITALIC: [BOLD:path]: is [CODE: /].
] [BOLD:operation]: is [CODE: get].
[ITALIC: [BOLD:function]: is the function below the "decorator" (below [CODE: @app.get("/")]).
```python
async def root():
return {"message": "Hello World"}
```
This is a Python function.
It will be called by [BOLD:FastAPI] whenever it receives a request to the URL "[CODE: /]" using a [CODE: GET] operation.
In this case, it is an [CODE: async] function.
---
You could also define it as a normal function instead of [CODE: async def]:
```python
def root():
return {"message": "Hello World"}
```
/// note
If you don't know the difference, check the Async: ]"In a hurry?"*.
///

metadata.breadcrumb: First Steps > Recap, step by step > Step 4: define the **path operation function**
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/first-steps.md

page_content:
[Context: First Steps > Recap, step by step > Step 5: return the content]

### Step 5: return the content
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: return {"message": "Hello World"}...]
You can return a [CODE: dict], [CODE: list], singular values as [CODE: str], [CODE: int], etc.
You can also return Pydantic models (you'll see more about that later).
There are many other objects and models that will be automatically converted to JSON (including ORMs, etc). Try using your favorite ones, it's highly probable that they are already supported.

metadata.breadcrumb: First Steps > Recap, step by step > Step 5: return the content
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/first-steps.md

page_content:
[Context: First Steps > Recap, step by step > Step 6: Deploy it]

### Step 6: Deploy it
Deploy your app to [BOLD:FastAPI Cloud] with one command: [CODE: fastapi deploy].
#### About FastAPI Cloud
[BOLD:FastAPI Cloud] is built by the same author and team behind [BOLD:FastAPI].
It streamlines the process of [BOLD:building], [BOLD:deploying], and [BOLD:accessing] an API with minimal effort.
It brings the same [BOLD:developer experience] of building apps with FastAPI to [BOLD:deploying] them to the cloud.
FastAPI Cloud is the primary sponsor and funding provider for the [ITALIC:FastAPI and friends] open source projects.
#### Deploy to other cloud providers
FastAPI is open source and based on standards. You can deploy FastAPI apps to any cloud provider you choose.
Follow your cloud provider's guides to deploy FastAPI apps with them. 🤓

metadata.breadcrumb: First Steps > Recap, step by step > Step 6: Deploy it
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/first-steps.md
Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/first-steps.md

page_content:
[Context: Handling Errors]

# Handling Errors
There are many situations in which you need to notify an error to a client that is using your API.
This client could be a browser with a frontend, a code from someone else, an IoT device, etc.
You could need to tell the client that:
[ITALIC: The client doesn't have enough privileges for that operation.
] The client doesn't have access to that resource.
[ITALIC: The item the client was trying to access doesn't exist.
] etc.
In these cases, you would normally return an [BOLD:HTTP status code] in the range of [BOLD:400] (from 400 to 499).
This is similar to the 200 HTTP status codes (from 200 to 299). Those "200" status codes mean that somehow there was a "success" in the request.
The status codes in the 400 range mean that there was an error from the client.
Remember all those [BOLD:"404 Not Found"] errors (and jokes)?

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/handling-errors.md

page_content:
[Context: Handling Errors > Use `HTTPException`]

## Use [CODE: HTTPException]
To return HTTP responses with errors to the client you use [CODE: HTTPException].

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/handling-errors.md

page_content:
[Context: Handling Errors > Use `HTTPException` > Import `HTTPException`]

### Import [CODE: HTTPException]
```python
from fastapi import FastAPI, HTTPException

```

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/handling-errors.md

page_content:
[Context: Handling Errors > Use `HTTPException` > Raise an `HTTPException` in your code]

### Raise an [CODE: HTTPException] in your code
[CODE: HTTPException] is a normal Python exception with additional data relevant for APIs.
Because it's a Python exception, you don't [CODE: return] it, you [CODE: raise] it.
This also means that if you are inside a utility function that you are calling inside of your [ITALIC:path operation function], and you raise the [CODE: HTTPException] from inside of that utility function, it won't run the rest of the code in the [ITALIC:path operation function], it will terminate that request right away and send the HTTP error from the [CODE: HTTPException] to the client.
The benefit of raising an exception over returning a value will be more evident in the section about Dependencies and Security.
In this example, when the client requests an item by an ID that doesn't exist, raise an exception with a status code of [CODE: 404]:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: raise HTTPException(status_code=404, detail="Item ...]

metadata.breadcrumb: Handling Errors > Use `HTTPException` > Raise an `HTTPException` in your code
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/handling-errors.md

page_content:
[Context: Handling Errors > Use `HTTPException` > Raise an `HTTPException` in your code]

### Raise an [CODE: HTTPException] in your code
[CODE: HTTPException] is a normal Python exception with additional data relevant for APIs.
Because it's a Python exception, you don't [CODE: return] it, you [CODE: raise] it.
This also means that if you are inside a utility function that you are calling inside of your [ITALIC:path operation function], and you raise the [CODE: HTTPException] from inside of that utility function, it won't run the rest of the code in the [ITALIC:path operation function], it will terminate that request right away and send the HTTP error from the [CODE: HTTPException] to the client.
The benefit of raising an exception over returning a value will be more evident in the section about Dependencies and Security.
In this example, when the client requests an item by an ID that doesn't exist, raise an exception with a status code of [CODE: 404]:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: raise HTTPException(status_code=404, detail="Item ...]

metadata.breadcrumb: Handling Errors > Use `HTTPException` > Raise an `HTTPException` in your code
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/handling-errors.md

page_content:
[Context: Handling Errors > Use `HTTPException` > The resulting response]

### The resulting response
If the client requests [CODE: http://example.com/items/foo] (an [CODE: item[ITALIC:id] [CODE: "foo"]), that client will receive an HTTP status code of 200, and a JSON response of:
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:0: { "item": "The Foo Wrestlers" }...]
But if the client requests [CODE: http://example.com/items/bar] (a non-existent [CODE: item]id] [CODE: "bar"]), that client will receive an HTTP status code of 404 (the "not found" error), and a JSON response of:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]1: { "detail": "Item not found" }...]
/// tip
When raising an [CODE: HTTPException], you can pass any value that can be converted to JSON as the parameter [CODE: detail], not only [CODE: str].
You could pass a [CODE: dict], a [CODE: list], etc.
They are handled automatically by [BOLD:FastAPI] and converted to JSON.
///

metadata.breadcrumb: Handling Errors > Use `HTTPException` > The resulting response
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/handling-errors.md

page_content:
[Context: Handling Errors > Add custom headers]

## Add custom headers
There are some situations in where it's useful to be able to add custom headers to the HTTP error. For example, for some types of security.
You probably won't need to use it directly in your code.
But in case you needed it for an advanced scenario, you can add custom headers:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: headers={"X-Error": "There goes my error"}, )...]

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/handling-errors.md

page_content:
[Context: Handling Errors > Install custom exception handlers]

## Install custom exception handlers
You can add custom exception handlers with the same exception utilities from Starlette.
Let's say you have a custom exception [CODE: UnicornException] that you (or a library you use) might [CODE: raise].
And you want to handle this exception globally with FastAPI.
You could add a custom exception handler with [CODE: @app.exception[ITALIC:handler()]:
```python
class UnicornException(Exception):
def [BOLD:init](self, name: str):
self.name = name
@app.exception]handler(UnicornException)
async def unicorn[ITALIC:exception]handler(request: Request, exc: UnicornException):
return JSONResponse(
status[ITALIC:code=418,
content={"message": f"Oops! {exc.name} did something. There goes a rainbow..."},
)
raise UnicornException(name=name)
return {"unicorn]name": name}
```
Here, if you request [CODE: /unicorns/yolo], the [ITALIC:path operation] will [CODE: raise] a [CODE: UnicornException].
But it will be handled by the [CODE: unicorn[ITALIC:exception]handler].
So, you will receive a clean error, with an HTTP status code of [CODE: 418] and a JSON content of:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: {"message": "Oops! yolo did something. There goes ...]
/// note | Technical Details
You could also use [CODE: from starlette.requests import Request] and [CODE: from starlette.responses import JSONResponse].
[BOLD:FastAPI] provides the same [CODE: starlette.responses] as [CODE: fastapi.responses] just as a convenience for you, the developer. But most of the available responses come directly from Starlette. The same with [CODE: Request].
///

metadata.breadcrumb: Handling Errors > Install custom exception handlers
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/handling-errors.md

page_content:
[Context: Handling Errors > Install custom exception handlers]

## Install custom exception handlers
You can add custom exception handlers with the same exception utilities from Starlette.
Let's say you have a custom exception [CODE: UnicornException] that you (or a library you use) might [CODE: raise].
And you want to handle this exception globally with FastAPI.
You could add a custom exception handler with [CODE: @app.exception[ITALIC:handler()]:
```python
class UnicornException(Exception):
def [BOLD:init](self, name: str):
self.name = name
@app.exception]handler(UnicornException)
async def unicorn[ITALIC:exception]handler(request: Request, exc: UnicornException):
return JSONResponse(
status[ITALIC:code=418,
content={"message": f"Oops! {exc.name} did something. There goes a rainbow..."},
)
raise UnicornException(name=name)
return {"unicorn]name": name}
```
Here, if you request [CODE: /unicorns/yolo], the [ITALIC:path operation] will [CODE: raise] a [CODE: UnicornException].
But it will be handled by the [CODE: unicorn[ITALIC:exception]handler].
So, you will receive a clean error, with an HTTP status code of [CODE: 418] and a JSON content of:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: {"message": "Oops! yolo did something. There goes ...]
/// note | Technical Details
You could also use [CODE: from starlette.requests import Request] and [CODE: from starlette.responses import JSONResponse].
[BOLD:FastAPI] provides the same [CODE: starlette.responses] as [CODE: fastapi.responses] just as a convenience for you, the developer. But most of the available responses come directly from Starlette. The same with [CODE: Request].
///

metadata.breadcrumb: Handling Errors > Install custom exception handlers
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/handling-errors.md

page_content:
[Context: Handling Errors > Override the default exception handlers]

## Override the default exception handlers
[BOLD:FastAPI] has some default exception handlers.
These handlers are in charge of returning the default JSON responses when you [CODE: raise] an [CODE: HTTPException] and when the request has invalid data.
You can override these exception handlers with your own.

metadata.breadcrumb: Handling Errors > Override the default exception handlers
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/handling-errors.md

page_content:
[Context: Handling Errors > Override the default exception handlers > Override request validation exceptions]

### Override request validation exceptions
When a request contains invalid data, [BOLD:FastAPI] internally raises a [CODE: RequestValidationError].
And it also includes a default exception handler for it.
To override it, import the [CODE: RequestValidationError] and use it with [CODE: @app.exception[ITALIC:handler(RequestValidationError)] to decorate the exception handler.
The exception handler will receive a [CODE: Request] and the exception.
```python
from fastapi.exceptions import RequestValidationError
from fastapi.responses import PlainTextResponse
@app.exception]handler(RequestValidationError)
async def validation[ITALIC:exception]handler(request, exc: RequestValidationError):
message = "Validation errors:"
for error in exc.errors():
message += f"\nField: {error['loc']}, Error: {error['msg']}"
return PlainTextResponse(message, status[ITALIC:code=400)
```
Now, if you go to [CODE: /items/foo], instead of getting the default JSON error with:
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:0: { "detail": [ { "loc": [ "path", "item]id" ], "msg...]
you will get a text version, with:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]1: Validation errors: Field: ('path', 'item_id'), Err...]

metadata.breadcrumb: Handling Errors > Override the default exception handlers > Override request validation exceptions
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/handling-errors.md

page_content:
[Context: Handling Errors > Override the default exception handlers > Override request validation exceptions]

### Override request validation exceptions
When a request contains invalid data, [BOLD:FastAPI] internally raises a [CODE: RequestValidationError].
And it also includes a default exception handler for it.
To override it, import the [CODE: RequestValidationError] and use it with [CODE: @app.exception[ITALIC:handler(RequestValidationError)] to decorate the exception handler.
The exception handler will receive a [CODE: Request] and the exception.
```python
from fastapi.exceptions import RequestValidationError
from fastapi.responses import PlainTextResponse
@app.exception]handler(RequestValidationError)
async def validation[ITALIC:exception]handler(request, exc: RequestValidationError):
message = "Validation errors:"
for error in exc.errors():
message += f"\nField: {error['loc']}, Error: {error['msg']}"
return PlainTextResponse(message, status[ITALIC:code=400)
```
Now, if you go to [CODE: /items/foo], instead of getting the default JSON error with:
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:0: { "detail": [ { "loc": [ "path", "item]id" ], "msg...]
you will get a text version, with:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]1: Validation errors: Field: ('path', 'item_id'), Err...]

metadata.breadcrumb: Handling Errors > Override the default exception handlers > Override request validation exceptions
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/handling-errors.md

metadata.breadcrumb: Handling Errors > Override the default exception handlers > Override the `HTTPException` error handler
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/handling-errors.md

metadata.breadcrumb: Handling Errors > Override the default exception handlers > Override the `HTTPException` error handler
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/handling-errors.md

page_content:
[Context: Handling Errors > Override the default exception handlers > Use the `RequestValidationError` body]

### Use the [CODE: RequestValidationError] body
The [CODE: RequestValidationError] contains the [CODE: body] it received with invalid data.
You could use it while developing your app to log the body and debug it, return it to the user, etc.
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: content=jsonable[ITALIC:encoder({"detail": exc.errors(), ...]
Now try sending an invalid item like:
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:1: { "title": "towel", "size": "XL" }...]
You will receive a response telling you that the data is invalid containing the received body:
```JSON hl]lines="12-15"
{
"detail": [
{
"loc": [
"body",
"size"
],
"msg": "value is not a valid integer",
"type": "type[ITALIC:error.integer"
}
],
"body": {
"title": "towel",
"size": "XL"
}
}
```
#### FastAPI's [CODE: HTTPException] vs Starlette's [CODE: HTTPException]
[BOLD:FastAPI] has its own [CODE: HTTPException].
And [BOLD:FastAPI]'s [CODE: HTTPException] error class inherits from Starlette's [CODE: HTTPException] error class.
The only difference is that [BOLD:FastAPI]'s [CODE: HTTPException] accepts any JSON-able data for the [CODE: detail] field, while Starlette's [CODE: HTTPException] only accepts strings for it.
So, you can keep raising [BOLD:FastAPI]'s [CODE: HTTPException] as normally in your code.
But when you register an exception handler, you should register it for Starlette's [CODE: HTTPException].
This way, if any part of Starlette's internal code, or a Starlette extension or plug-in, raises a Starlette [CODE: HTTPException], your handler will be able to catch and handle it.
In this example, to be able to have both [CODE: HTTPException]s in the same code, Starlette's exceptions is renamed to [CODE: StarletteHTTPException]:
[CODE]BLOCK_2: from starlette.exceptions import HTTPException as ...]

metadata.breadcrumb: Handling Errors > Override the default exception handlers > Use the `RequestValidationError` body
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/handling-errors.md

[Context: Handling Errors > Override the default exception handlers > Use the `RequestValidationError` body]

"detail": [
{

],
"msg": "value is not a valid integer",

}

In this example, to be able to have both [CODE: HTTPException]s in the same code, Starlette's exceptions is renamed to [CODE: StarletteHTTPException]:

page_content:
[Context: Header Parameter Models]

# Header Parameter Models
If you have a group of related [BOLD:header parameters], you can create a [BOLD:Pydantic model] to declare them.
This would allow you to [BOLD:re-use the model] in [BOLD:multiple places] and also to declare validations and metadata for all the parameters at once.
/// note
This is supported since FastAPI version [CODE: 0.115.0]. 🤓
///

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/header-param-models.md

page_content:
[Context: Header Parameter Models > Header Parameters with a Pydantic Model]

## Header Parameters with a Pydantic Model
Declare the [BOLD:header parameters] that you need in a [BOLD:Pydantic model], and then declare the parameter as [CODE: Header]:
```python
class CommonHeaders(BaseModel):
host: str
save[ITALIC:data: bool
if]modified[ITALIC:since: str | None = None
traceparent: str | None = None
x]tag: list[str] = []
async def read_items(headers: Annotated[CommonHeaders, Header()]):
return headers
```
[BOLD:FastAPI] will [BOLD:extract] the data for [BOLD:each field] from the [BOLD:headers] in the request and give you the Pydantic model you defined.

hierarchy: Header Parameters with a Pydantic Model
metadata.breadcrumb: Header Parameter Models > Header Parameters with a Pydantic Model
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/header-param-models.md

page_content:
[Context: Header Parameter Models > Check the Docs]

## Check the Docs
You can see the required headers in the docs UI at [CODE: /docs]:

metadata.breadcrumb: Header Parameter Models > Check the Docs
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/header-param-models.md

page_content:
[Context: Header Parameter Models > Forbid Extra Headers]

## Forbid Extra Headers
In some special use cases (probably not very common), you might want to [BOLD:restrict] the headers that you want to receive.
You can use Pydantic's model configuration to [CODE: forbid] any [CODE: extra] fields:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: model[ITALIC:config = {"extra": "forbid"}...]
If a client tries to send some [BOLD:extra headers], they will receive an [BOLD:error] response.
For example, if the client tries to send a [CODE: tool] header with a value of [CODE: plumbus], they will receive an [BOLD:error] response telling them that the header parameter [CODE: tool] is not allowed:
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:1: { "detail": [ { "type": "extra]forbidden", "loc": ...]

metadata.breadcrumb: Header Parameter Models > Forbid Extra Headers
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/header-param-models.md

page_content:
[Context: Header Parameter Models > Disable Convert Underscores]

## Disable Convert Underscores
The same way as with regular header parameters, when you have underscore characters in the parameter names, they are [BOLD:automatically converted to hyphens].
For example, if you have a header parameter [CODE: save[ITALIC:data] in the code, the expected HTTP header will be [CODE: save-data], and it will show up like that in the docs.
If for some reason you need to disable this automatic conversion, you can do it as well for Pydantic models for header parameters.
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:0: headers: Annotated[CommonHeaders, Header(convert]u...]
/// warning
Before setting [CODE: convert_underscores] to [CODE: False], bear in mind that some HTTP proxies and servers disallow the usage of headers with underscores.
///

metadata.breadcrumb: Header Parameter Models > Disable Convert Underscores
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/header-param-models.md

page_content:
[Context: Header Parameter Models > Summary]

## Summary
You can use [BOLD:Pydantic models] to declare [BOLD:headers] in [BOLD:FastAPI].

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/header-param-models.md

page_content:
[Context: Header Parameters]

# Header Parameters
You can define Header parameters the same way you define [CODE: Query], [CODE: Path] and [CODE: Cookie] parameters.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/header-params.md

page_content:
[Context: Header Parameters > Import `Header`]

## Import [CODE: Header]
First import [CODE: Header]:
```python
from fastapi import FastAPI, Header

```

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/header-params.md
Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/header-params.md

page_content:
[Context: Header Parameters > Automatic conversion]

## Automatic conversion
[CODE: Header] has a little extra functionality on top of what [CODE: Path], [CODE: Query] and [CODE: Cookie] provide.
Most of the standard headers are separated by a "hyphen" character, also known as the "minus symbol" ([CODE: -]).
But a variable like [CODE: user-agent] is invalid in Python.
So, by default, [CODE: Header] will convert the parameter names characters from underscore ([CODE: [ITALIC:]) to hyphen ([CODE: -]) to extract and document the headers.
Also, HTTP headers are case-insensitive, so, you can declare them with standard Python style (also known as "snake]case").
So, you can use [CODE: user[ITALIC:agent] as you normally would in Python code, instead of needing to capitalize the first letters as [CODE: User]Agent] or something similar.
If for some reason you need to disable automatic conversion of underscores to hyphens, set the parameter [CODE: convert[ITALIC:underscores] of [CODE: Header] to [CODE: False]:
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:0: strange]header: Annotated[str | None, Header(conve...]
/// warning
Before setting [CODE: convert_underscores] to [CODE: False], bear in mind that some HTTP proxies and servers disallow the usage of headers with underscores.
///

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/header-params.md

page_content:
[Context: Header Parameters > Automatic conversion]

## Automatic conversion

If for some reason you need to disable automatic conversion of underscores to hyphens, set the parameter [CODE: convert[ITALIC:underscores] of [CODE: Header] to [CODE: False]:
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:0: strange]header: Annotated[str | None, Header(conve...]
/// warning
Before setting [CODE: convert_underscores] to [CODE: False], bear in mind that some HTTP proxies and servers disallow the usage of headers with underscores.
///

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/header-params.md

page_content:
[Context: Header Parameters > Duplicate headers]

## Duplicate headers
It is possible to receive duplicate headers. That means, the same header with multiple values.
You can define those cases using a list in the type declaration.
You will receive all the values from the duplicate header as a Python [CODE: list].
For example, to declare a header of [CODE: X-Token] that can appear more than once, you can write:
```python
async def read[ITALIC:items(x]token: Annotated[list[str] | None, Header()] = None):
return {"X-Token values": x[ITALIC:token}
```
If you communicate with that [ITALIC:path operation] sending two HTTP headers like:
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:0: X-Token: foo X-Token: bar...]
The response would be like:
[CODE]BLOCK_1: { "X-Token values": [ "bar", "foo" ] }...]

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/header-params.md

page_content:
[Context: Header Parameters > Recap]

## Recap
Declare headers with [CODE: Header], using the same common pattern as [CODE: Query], [CODE: Path] and [CODE: Cookie].
And don't worry about underscores in your variables, [BOLD:FastAPI] will take care of converting them.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/header-params.md

page_content:
[Context: Tutorial - User Guide]

# Tutorial - User Guide
This tutorial shows you how to use [BOLD:FastAPI] with most of its features, step by step.
Each section gradually builds on the previous ones, but it's structured to separate topics, so that you can go directly to any specific one to solve your specific API needs.
It is also built to work as a future reference so you can come back and see exactly what you need.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/index.md

page_content:
[Context: Tutorial - User Guide > Run the code]

## Run the code
All the code blocks can be copied and used directly (they are actually tested Python files).
To run any of the examples, copy the code to a file [CODE: main.py], and start [CODE: fastapi dev]:

[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: $ fastapi dev
It is [BOLD:HIGHLY encouraged] that you write or copy the code, edit it and run it locally.
Using it in your editor is what really shows you the benefits of FastAPI, seeing how little code you have to write, all the type checks, autocompletion, etc.
---

metadata.breadcrumb: Tutorial - User Guide > Run the code
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/index.md
Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/index.md
Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/index.md

page_content:
[Context: Tutorial - User Guide > Advanced User Guide]

## Advanced User Guide
There is also an [BOLD:Advanced User Guide] that you can read later after this [BOLD:Tutorial - User guide].
The [BOLD:Advanced User Guide] builds on this one, uses the same concepts, and teaches you some extra features.
But you should first read the [BOLD:Tutorial - User Guide] (what you are reading right now).
It's designed so that you can build a complete application with just the [BOLD:Tutorial - User Guide], and then extend it in different ways, depending on your needs, using some of the additional ideas from the [BOLD:Advanced User Guide].

metadata.breadcrumb: Tutorial - User Guide > Advanced User Guide
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/index.md

page_content:
[Context: Metadata and Docs URLs]

# Metadata and Docs URLs
You can customize several metadata configurations in your [BOLD:FastAPI] application.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/metadata.md

page_content:
[Context: Metadata and Docs URLs > Metadata for API]

## Metadata for API
You can set the following fields that are used in the OpenAPI specification and the automatic API docs UIs:
| Parameter | Type | Description |
|------------|------|-------------|
| [CODE: title] | [CODE: str] | The title of the API. |
| [CODE: summary] | [CODE: str] | A short summary of the API. Available since OpenAPI 3.1.0, FastAPI 0.99.0. |
| [CODE: description] | [CODE: str] | A short description of the API. It can use Markdown. |
| [CODE: version] | [CODE: string] | The version of the API. This is the version of your own application, not of OpenAPI. For example [CODE: 2.5.0]. |
| [CODE: terms[ITALIC:of]service] | [CODE: str] | A URL to the Terms of Service for the API. If provided, this has to be a URL. |
| [CODE: contact] | [CODE: dict] | The contact information for the exposed API. It can contain several fields. contact fieldsParameterTypeDescriptionnamestrThe identifying name of the contact person/organization.urlstrThe URL pointing to the contact information. MUST be in the format of a URL.emailstrThe email address of the contact person/organization. MUST be in the format of an email address. |
| [CODE: license[ITALIC:info] | [CODE: dict] | The license information for the exposed API. It can contain several fields. license]info fieldsParameterTypeDescriptionnamestrREQUIRED (if a license[ITALIC:info is set). The license name used for the API.identifierstrAn SPDX license expression for the API. The identifier field is mutually exclusive of the url field. Available since OpenAPI 3.1.0, FastAPI 0.99.0.urlstrA URL to the license used for the API. MUST be in the format of a URL. |
You can set them as follows:
```python
description = """
ChimichangApp API helps you do awesome stuff.

## Items

You can [BOLD:read items].

## Users

You will be able to:

[ITALIC: [BOLD:Create users] (]not implemented[ITALIC:).
] [BOLD:Read users] (]not implemented[ITALIC:).
"""
title="ChimichangApp",
description=description,
summary="Deadpool's favorite app. Nuff said.",
version="0.0.1",
terms]of[ITALIC:service="http://example.com/terms/",
contact={
"name": "Deadpoolio the Amazing",
"url": "http://x-force.example.com/contact/",
"email": "dp@x-force.example.com",
},
license]info={
"name": "Apache 2.0",
"url": "https://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0.html",
},
```
/// tip
You can write Markdown in the [CODE: description] field and it will be rendered in the output.
///
With this configuration, the automatic API docs would look like:

metadata.breadcrumb: Metadata and Docs URLs > Metadata for API
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/metadata.md
