[Context: Metadata and Docs URLs > License identifier]

## License identifier
Since OpenAPI 3.1.0 and FastAPI 0.99.0, you can also set the [CODE: license[ITALIC:info] with an [CODE: identifier] instead of a [CODE: url].
For example:
[CODE]BLOCK_0: "identifier": "Apache-2.0", },...]

metadata.breadcrumb: Metadata and Docs URLs > License identifier
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/metadata.md

page_content:
[Context: Metadata and Docs URLs > Metadata for tags]

## Metadata for tags
You can also add additional metadata for the different tags used to group your path operations with the parameter [CODE: openapi_tags].
It takes a list containing one dictionary for each tag.
Each dictionary can contain:
[ITALIC: [CODE: name] ([BOLD:required]): a [CODE: str] with the same tag name you use in the [CODE: tags] parameter in your ]path operations[ITALIC: and [CODE: APIRouter]s.
] [CODE: description]: a [CODE: str] with a short description for the tag. It can have Markdown and will be shown in the docs UI.
[ITALIC: [CODE: externalDocs]: a [CODE: dict] describing external documentation with:
] [CODE: description]: a [CODE: str] with a short description for the external docs.
- [CODE: url] ([BOLD:required]): a [CODE: str] with the URL for the external documentation.

metadata.breadcrumb: Metadata and Docs URLs > Metadata for tags
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/metadata.md

page_content:
[Context: Metadata and Docs URLs > Metadata for tags > Create metadata for tags]

### Create metadata for tags
Let's try that in an example with tags for [CODE: users] and [CODE: items].
Create metadata for your tags and pass it to the [CODE: openapi[ITALIC:tags] parameter:
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:0: tags]metadata = [ { "name": "users", "description"...]
Notice that you can use Markdown inside of the descriptions, for example "login" will be shown in bold ([BOLD:login]) and "fancy" will be shown in italics ([ITALIC:fancy]).
/// tip
You don't have to add metadata for all the tags that you use.
///

metadata.breadcrumb: Metadata and Docs URLs > Metadata for tags > Create metadata for tags
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/metadata.md

metadata.breadcrumb: Metadata and Docs URLs > Metadata for tags > Use your tags
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/metadata.md

page_content:
[Context: Metadata and Docs URLs > Metadata for tags > Check the docs]

### Check the docs
Now, if you check the docs, they will show all the additional metadata:

metadata.breadcrumb: Metadata and Docs URLs > Metadata for tags > Check the docs
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/metadata.md

page_content:
[Context: Metadata and Docs URLs > Metadata for tags > Order of tags]

### Order of tags
The order of each tag metadata dictionary also defines the order shown in the docs UI.
For example, even though [CODE: users] would go after [CODE: items] in alphabetical order, it is shown before them, because we added their metadata as the first dictionary in the list.

metadata.breadcrumb: Metadata and Docs URLs > Metadata for tags > Order of tags
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/metadata.md

page_content:
[Context: Metadata and Docs URLs > OpenAPI URL]

## OpenAPI URL
By default, the OpenAPI schema is served at [CODE: /openapi.json].
But you can configure it with the parameter [CODE: openapi[ITALIC:url].
For example, to set it to be served at [CODE: /api/v1/openapi.json]:
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:0: app = FastAPI(openapi]url="/api/v1/openapi.json")...]
If you want to disable the OpenAPI schema completely you can set [CODE: openapi_url=None], that will also disable the documentation user interfaces that use it.

metadata.breadcrumb: Metadata and Docs URLs > OpenAPI URL
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/metadata.md

page_content:
[Context: Metadata and Docs URLs > Docs URLs]

## Docs URLs
You can configure the two documentation user interfaces included:
[ITALIC: [BOLD:Swagger UI]: served at [CODE: /docs].
] You can set its URL with the parameter [CODE: docs[ITALIC:url].
[ITALIC: You can disable it by setting [CODE: docs]url=None].
] [BOLD:ReDoc]: served at [CODE: /redoc].
[ITALIC: You can set its URL with the parameter [CODE: redoc[ITALIC:url].
] You can disable it by setting [CODE: redoc]url=None].
For example, to set Swagger UI to be served at [CODE: /documentation] and disable ReDoc:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: app = FastAPI(docs[ITALIC:url="/documentation", redoc]url...]

metadata.breadcrumb: Metadata and Docs URLs > Docs URLs

Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/middleware.md
Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/middleware.md

page_content:
[Context: Middleware > Create a middleware]

## Create a middleware
To create a middleware you use the decorator [CODE: @app.middleware("http")] on top of a function.
The middleware function receives:
[ITALIC: The [CODE: request].
] A function [CODE: call[ITALIC:next] that will receive the [CODE: request] as a parameter.
[ITALIC: This function will pass the [CODE: request] to the corresponding ]path operation[ITALIC:.
] Then it returns the [CODE: response] generated by the corresponding [ITALIC:path operation].
- You can then further modify the [CODE: response] before returning it.
```python
@app.middleware("http")
async def add]process[ITALIC:time]header(request: Request, call[ITALIC:next):
response = await call]next(request)
process[ITALIC:time = time.perf]counter() - start[ITALIC:time
return response
```
/// tip
Keep in mind that custom proprietary headers can be added [using the [CODE: X-] prefix](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Headers).
But if you have custom headers that you want a client in a browser to be able to see, you need to add them to your CORS configurations (CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing)) using the parameter [CODE: expose]headers] documented in Starlette's CORS docs.
///
/// note | Technical Details
You could also use [CODE: from starlette.requests import Request].
[BOLD:FastAPI] provides it as a convenience for you, the developer. But it comes directly from Starlette.
///

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/middleware.md

page_content:
[Context: Middleware > Create a middleware]

## Create a middleware
To create a middleware you use the decorator [CODE: @app.middleware("http")] on top of a function.
The middleware function receives:
[ITALIC: The [CODE: request].
] A function [CODE: call[ITALIC:next] that will receive the [CODE: request] as a parameter.
[ITALIC: This function will pass the [CODE: request] to the corresponding ]path operation[ITALIC:.
] Then it returns the [CODE: response] generated by the corresponding [ITALIC:path operation].
- You can then further modify the [CODE: response] before returning it.
```python
@app.middleware("http")
async def add]process[ITALIC:time]header(request: Request, call[ITALIC:next):
response = await call]next(request)
process[ITALIC:time = time.perf]counter() - start[ITALIC:time
return response
```
/// tip
Keep in mind that custom proprietary headers can be added [using the [CODE: X-] prefix](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Headers).
But if you have custom headers that you want a client in a browser to be able to see, you need to add them to your CORS configurations (CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing)) using the parameter [CODE: expose]headers] documented in Starlette's CORS docs.
///
/// note | Technical Details
You could also use [CODE: from starlette.requests import Request].
[BOLD:FastAPI] provides it as a convenience for you, the developer. But it comes directly from Starlette.
///

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/middleware.md

page_content:
[Context: Middleware > Create a middleware > Before and after the `response`]

### Before and after the [CODE: response]
You can add code to be run with the [CODE: request], before any [ITALIC:path operation] receives it.
And also after the [CODE: response] is generated, before returning it.
For example, you could add a custom header [CODE: X-Process-Time] containing the time in seconds that it took to process the request and generate a response:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: start[ITALIC:time = time.perf]counter() response = await ...]
/// tip
Here we use [[CODE: time.perf[ITALIC:counter()]](https://docs.python.org/3/library/time.html#time.perf]counter) instead of [CODE: time.time()] because it can be more precise for these use cases. 🤓
///

metadata.breadcrumb: Middleware > Create a middleware > Before and after the `response`
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/middleware.md

page_content:
[Context: Middleware > Multiple middleware execution order]

## Multiple middleware execution order
When you add multiple middlewares using either [CODE: @app.middleware()] decorator or [CODE: app.add[ITALIC:middleware()] method, each new middleware wraps the application, forming a stack. The last middleware added is the [ITALIC:outermost], and the first is the [ITALIC:innermost].
On the request path, the [ITALIC:outermost] middleware runs first.
On the response path, it runs last.
For example:
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:0: app.add]middleware(MiddlewareA) app.add_middleware...]
This results in the following execution order:
[ITALIC: [BOLD:Request]: MiddlewareB → MiddlewareA → route
] [BOLD:Response]: route → MiddlewareA → MiddlewareB
This stacking behavior ensures that middlewares are executed in a predictable and controllable order.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/middleware.md

page_content:
[Context: Middleware > Other middlewares]

## Other middlewares
You can later read more about other middlewares in the Advanced User Guide: Advanced Middleware.
You will read about how to handle CORS with a middleware in the next section.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/middleware.md
Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/path-operation-configuration.md

page_content:
[Context: Path Operation Configuration > Response Status Code]

## Response Status Code
You can define the (HTTP) [CODE: status[ITALIC:code] to be used in the response of your [ITALIC:path operation].
You can pass directly the [CODE: int] code, like [CODE: 404].
But if you don't remember what each number code is for, you can use the shortcut constants in [CODE: status]:
```python
from fastapi import FastAPI, status
from pydantic import BaseModel
@app.post("/items/", status]code=status.HTTP[ITALIC:201]CREATED)
async def create_item(item: Item) -> Item:
```
That status code will be used in the response and will be added to the OpenAPI schema.
/// note | Technical Details
You could also use [CODE: from starlette import status].
[BOLD:FastAPI] provides the same [CODE: starlette.status] as [CODE: fastapi.status] just as a convenience for you, the developer. But it comes directly from Starlette.
///

metadata.breadcrumb: Path Operation Configuration > Response Status Code
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/path-operation-configuration.md

page_content:
[Context: Path Operation Configuration > Tags]

## Tags
You can add tags to your [ITALIC:path operation], pass the parameter [CODE: tags] with a [CODE: list] of [CODE: str] (commonly just one [CODE: str]):
```python
@app.post("/items/", tags=["items"])
async def create[ITALIC:item(item: Item) -> Item:
@app.get("/items/", tags=["items"])
async def read]items():
@app.get("/users/", tags=["users"])
async def read_users():
```
They will be added to the OpenAPI schema and used by the automatic documentation interfaces:

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/path-operation-configuration.md

page_content:
[Context: Path Operation Configuration > Tags > Tags with Enums]

### Tags with Enums
If you have a big application, you might end up accumulating [BOLD:several tags], and you would want to make sure you always use the [BOLD:same tag] for related [ITALIC:path operations].
In these cases, it could make sense to store the tags in an [CODE: Enum].
[BOLD:FastAPI] supports that the same way as with plain strings:
```python
from enum import Enum

class Tags(Enum):
items = "items"
users = "users"
@app.get("/items/", tags=[Tags.items])
async def get[ITALIC:items():
@app.get("/users/", tags=[Tags.users])
async def read]users():
```

metadata.breadcrumb: Path Operation Configuration > Tags > Tags with Enums
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/path-operation-configuration.md

page_content:
[Context: Path Operation Configuration > Summary and description]

## Summary and description
You can add a [CODE: summary] and [CODE: description]:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: summary="Create an item", description="Create an i...]

metadata.breadcrumb: Path Operation Configuration > Summary and description

page_content:

## Description from docstring
As descriptions tend to be long and cover multiple lines, you can declare the [ITALIC:path operation] description in the function docstring and [BOLD:FastAPI] will read it from there.
You can write Markdown in the docstring, it will be interpreted and displayed correctly (taking into account docstring indentation).
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: """ Create an item with all the information: - **n...]
It will be used in the interactive docs:

## Deprecate a [ITALIC:path operation]
If you need to mark a [ITALIC:path operation] as deprecated, but without removing it, pass the parameter [CODE: deprecated]:
```python
@app.get("/elements/", tags=["items"], deprecated=True)
async def read_elements():
```
It will be clearly marked as deprecated in the interactive docs:

Check how deprecated and non-deprecated [ITALIC:path operations] look like:

[Context: Path Parameters and Numeric Validations]

# Path Parameters and Numeric Validations
In the same way that you can declare more validations and metadata for query parameters with [CODE: Query], you can declare the same type of validations and metadata for path parameters with [CODE: Path].

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/path-params-numeric-validations.md

page_content:
[Context: Path Parameters and Numeric Validations > Import `Path`]

## Import [CODE: Path]
First, import [CODE: Path] from [CODE: fastapi], and import [CODE: Annotated]:
```python
from typing import Annotated

from fastapi import FastAPI, Path, Query

```
/// info
FastAPI added support for [CODE: Annotated] (and started recommending it) in version 0.95.0.
If you have an older version, you would get errors when trying to use [CODE: Annotated].
Make sure you Upgrade the FastAPI version to at least 0.95.1 before using [CODE: Annotated].
///

metadata.breadcrumb: Path Parameters and Numeric Validations > Import `Path`
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/path-params-numeric-validations.md

page_content:
[Context: Path Parameters and Numeric Validations > Declare metadata]

## Declare metadata
You can declare all the same parameters as for [CODE: Query].
For example, to declare a [CODE: title] metadata value for the path parameter [CODE: item[ITALIC:id] you can type:
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:0: item]id: Annotated[int, Path(title="The ID of the ...]
/// note
A path parameter is always required as it has to be part of the path. Even if you declared it with [CODE: None] or set a default value, it would not affect anything, it would still be always required.
///

metadata.breadcrumb: Path Parameters and Numeric Validations > Declare metadata

page_content:
[Context: Path Parameters and Numeric Validations > Order the parameters as you need]

## Order the parameters as you need
/// tip
This is probably not as important or necessary if you use [CODE: Annotated].
///
Let's say that you want to declare the query parameter [CODE: q] as a required [CODE: str].
And you don't need to declare anything else for that parameter, so you don't really need to use [CODE: Query].
But you still need to use [CODE: Path] for the [CODE: item[ITALIC:id] path parameter. And you don't want to use [CODE: Annotated] for some reason.
Python will complain if you put a value with a "default" before a value that doesn't have a "default".
But you can re-order them, and have the value without a default (the query parameter [CODE: q]) first.
It doesn't matter for [BOLD:FastAPI]. It will detect the parameters by their names, types and default declarations ([CODE: Query], [CODE: Path], etc), it doesn't care about the order.
So, you can declare your function as:
```python
async def read]items(q: str, item[ITALIC:id: int = Path(title="The ID of the item to get")):
results = {"item]id": item[ITALIC:id}
```
But keep in mind that if you use [CODE: Annotated], you won't have this problem, it won't matter as you're not using the function parameter default values for [CODE: Query()] or [CODE: Path()].
{[ITALIC: ../../docs]src/path[ITALIC:params]numeric[ITALIC:validations/tutorial002]an_py310.py ]}

hierarchy: Order the parameters as you need

Python will complain if you put a value with a "default" before a value that doesn't have a "default".

So, you can declare your function as:

page_content:
[Context: Path Parameters and Numeric Validations > Order the parameters as you need, tricks]

## Order the parameters as you need, tricks
/// tip
This is probably not as important or necessary if you use [CODE: Annotated].
///
Here's a [BOLD:small trick] that can be handy, but you won't need it often.
If you want to:
[ITALIC: declare the [CODE: q] query parameter without a [CODE: Query] nor any default value
] declare the path parameter [CODE: item[ITALIC:id] using [CODE: Path]
[ITALIC: have them in a different order
] not use [CODE: Annotated]
...Python has a little special syntax for that.
Pass [CODE: [ITALIC:], as the first parameter of the function.
Python won't do anything with that [CODE: ]], but it will know that all the following parameters should be called as keyword arguments (key-value pairs), also known as kwargs. Even if they don't have a default value.
```python
async def read]items(*, item[ITALIC:id: int = Path(title="The ID of the item to get"), q: str):
results = {"item]id": item_id}
```

## Number validations: greater than or equal
With [CODE: Query] and [CODE: Path] (and others you'll see later) you can declare number constraints.
Here, with [CODE: ge=1], [CODE: item[ITALIC:id] will need to be an integer number "[CODE: g]reater than or [CODE: e]qual" to [CODE: 1].
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:0: item]id: Annotated[int, Path(title="The ID of the ...]

The same applies for:
[ITALIC: [CODE: gt]: [CODE: g]reater [CODE: t]han
] [CODE: le]: [CODE: l]ess than or [CODE: e]qual
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: item_id: Annotated[int, Path(title="The ID of the ...]

page_content:
[Context: Path Parameters and Numeric Validations > Number validations: floats, greater than and less than]

## Number validations: floats, greater than and less than
Number validations also work for [CODE: float] values.
Here's where it becomes important to be able to declare gt and not just ge. As with it you can require, for example, that a value must be greater than [CODE: 0], even if it is less than [CODE: 1].
So, [CODE: 0.5] would be a valid value. But [CODE: 0.0] or [CODE: 0] would not.
And the same for lt.
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: size: Annotated[float, Query(gt=0, lt=10.5)], ):...]

page_content:
[Context: Path Parameters]

# Path Parameters
You can declare path "parameters" or "variables" with the same syntax used by Python format strings:
```python
@app.get("/items/{item[ITALIC:id}")
async def read]item(item[ITALIC:id):
```
The value of the path parameter [CODE: item]id] will be passed to your function as the argument [CODE: item[ITALIC:id].
So, if you run this example and go to http://127.0.0.1:8000/items/foo, you will see a response of:
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:0: {"item]id":"foo"}...]

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/path-params.md

page_content:
[Context: Path Parameters > Path parameters with types]

## Path parameters with types
You can declare the type of a path parameter in the function, using standard Python type annotations:
```python
async def read[ITALIC:item(item]id: int):
return {"item[ITALIC:id": item]id}
```
In this case, [CODE: item_id] is declared to be an [CODE: int].
/// check
This will give you editor support inside of your function, with error checks, completion, etc.
///

metadata.breadcrumb: Path Parameters > Path parameters with types
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/path-params.md

page_content:
[Context: Path Parameters > Data <dfn title="also known as: serialization, parsing, marshalling">conversion</dfn>]

## Data conversion
If you run this example and open your browser at http://127.0.0.1:8000/items/3, you will see a response of:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: {"item_id":3}...]
/// check
Notice that the value your function received (and returned) is [CODE: 3], as a Python [CODE: int], not a string [CODE: "3"].
So, with that type declaration, [BOLD:FastAPI] gives you automatic request "parsing".
///

hierarchy: Data <dfn title="also known as: serialization, parsing, marshalling">conversion</dfn>
metadata.breadcrumb: Path Parameters > Data <dfn title="also known as: serialization, parsing, marshalling">conversion</dfn>
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/path-params.md

page_content:
[Context: Path Parameters > Data validation]

## Data validation
But if you go to the browser at http://127.0.0.1:8000/items/foo, you will see a nice HTTP error of:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: { "detail": [ { "type": "int[ITALIC:parsing", "loc": [ "p...]
because the path parameter [CODE: item]id] had a value of [CODE: "foo"], which is not an [CODE: int].
The same error would appear if you provided a [CODE: float] instead of an [CODE: int], as in: http://127.0.0.1:8000/items/4.2
/// check
So, with the same Python type declaration, [BOLD:FastAPI] gives you data validation.
Notice that the error also clearly states exactly the point where the validation didn't pass.
This is incredibly helpful while developing and debugging code that interacts with your API.
///

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/path-params.md

page_content:
[Context: Path Parameters > Documentation]

## Documentation
And when you open your browser at http://127.0.0.1:8000/docs, you will see an automatic, interactive, API documentation like:

/// check
Again, just with that same Python type declaration, [BOLD:FastAPI] gives you automatic, interactive documentation (integrating Swagger UI).
Notice that the path parameter is declared to be an integer.
///

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/path-params.md

page_content:
[Context: Path Parameters > Standards-based benefits, alternative documentation]

## Standards-based benefits, alternative documentation
And because the generated schema is from the OpenAPI standard, there are many compatible tools.
Because of this, [BOLD:FastAPI] itself provides an alternative API documentation (using ReDoc), which you can access at http://127.0.0.1:8000/redoc:

The same way, there are many compatible tools. Including code generation tools for many languages.

metadata.breadcrumb: Path Parameters > Standards-based benefits, alternative documentation
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/path-params.md

page_content:
[Context: Path Parameters > Pydantic]

## Pydantic
All the data validation is performed under the hood by Pydantic, so you get all the benefits from it. And you know you are in good hands.
You can use the same type declarations with [CODE: str], [CODE: float], [CODE: bool] and many other complex data types.
Several of these are explored in the next chapters of the tutorial.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/path-params.md

page_content:
[Context: Path Parameters > Order matters]

## Order matters
When creating [ITALIC:path operations], you can find situations where you have a fixed path.
Like [CODE: /users/me], let's say that it's to get data about the current user.
And then you can also have a path [CODE: /users/{user[ITALIC:id}] to get data about a specific user by some user ID.
Because [ITALIC:path operations] are evaluated in order, you need to make sure that the path for [CODE: /users/me] is declared before the one for [CODE: /users/{user]id}]:
```python
@app.get("/users/me")
async def read[ITALIC:user]me():
@app.get("/users/{user[ITALIC:id}")
async def read]user(user[ITALIC:id: str):
```
Otherwise, the path for [CODE: /users/{user]id}] would match also for [CODE: /users/me], "thinking" that it's receiving a parameter [CODE: user[ITALIC:id] with a value of [CODE: "me"].
Similarly, you cannot redefine a path operation:
```python
@app.get("/users")
async def read]users():
@app.get("/users")
async def read_users2():
```
The first one will always be used since the path matches first.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/path-params.md

page_content:
[Context: Path Parameters > Order matters]

## Order matters
When creating [ITALIC:path operations], you can find situations where you have a fixed path.
Like [CODE: /users/me], let's say that it's to get data about the current user.
And then you can also have a path [CODE: /users/{user[ITALIC:id}] to get data about a specific user by some user ID.
Because [ITALIC:path operations] are evaluated in order, you need to make sure that the path for [CODE: /users/me] is declared before the one for [CODE: /users/{user]id}]:
```python
@app.get("/users/me")
async def read[ITALIC:user]me():
@app.get("/users/{user[ITALIC:id}")
async def read]user(user[ITALIC:id: str):
```
Otherwise, the path for [CODE: /users/{user]id}] would match also for [CODE: /users/me], "thinking" that it's receiving a parameter [CODE: user[ITALIC:id] with a value of [CODE: "me"].
Similarly, you cannot redefine a path operation:
```python
@app.get("/users")
async def read]users():
@app.get("/users")
async def read_users2():
```
The first one will always be used since the path matches first.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/path-params.md

page_content:
[Context: Path Parameters > Predefined values]

## Predefined values
If you have a [ITALIC:path operation] that receives a [ITALIC:path parameter], but you want the possible valid [ITALIC:path parameter] values to be predefined, you can use a standard Python [CODE: Enum].

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/path-params.md

page_content:
[Context: Path Parameters > Predefined values > Create an `Enum` class]

### Create an [CODE: Enum] class
Import [CODE: Enum] and create a sub-class that inherits from [CODE: str] and from [CODE: Enum].
By inheriting from [CODE: str] the API docs will be able to know that the values must be of type [CODE: string] and will be able to render correctly.
Then create class attributes with fixed values, which will be the available valid values:
```python
from enum import Enum

class ModelName(str, Enum):
alexnet = "alexnet"
resnet = "resnet"
lenet = "lenet"
```
/// tip
If you are wondering, "AlexNet", "ResNet", and "LeNet" are just names of Machine Learning models.
///

metadata.breadcrumb: Path Parameters > Predefined values > Create an `Enum` class
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/path-params.md

page_content:
[Context: Path Parameters > Predefined values > Declare a *path parameter*]

### Declare a [ITALIC:path parameter]
Then create a [ITALIC:path parameter] with a type annotation using the enum class you created ([CODE: ModelName]):
```python
async def get[ITALIC:model(model]name: ModelName):
if model_name is ModelName.alexnet:
```

metadata.breadcrumb: Path Parameters > Predefined values > Declare a *path parameter*
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/path-params.md

page_content:
[Context: Path Parameters > Predefined values > Check the docs]

### Check the docs
Because the available values for the [ITALIC:path parameter] are predefined, the interactive docs can show them nicely:

metadata.breadcrumb: Path Parameters > Predefined values > Check the docs
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/path-params.md

page_content:
[Context: Path Parameters > Predefined values > Working with Python *enumerations*]

### Working with Python [ITALIC:enumerations]
The value of the [ITALIC:path parameter] will be an [ITALIC:enumeration member].
#### Compare [ITALIC:enumeration members]
You can compare it with the [ITALIC:enumeration member] in your created enum [CODE: ModelName]:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: if model[ITALIC:name is ModelName.alexnet: return {"model...]
#### Get the [ITALIC:enumeration value]
You can get the actual value (a [CODE: str] in this case) using [CODE: model]name.value], or in general, [CODE: your[ITALIC:enum]member.value]:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]1: if model[ITALIC:name.value == "lenet": return {"model]nam...]
/// tip
You could also access the value [CODE: "lenet"] with [CODE: ModelName.lenet.value].
///
#### Return [ITALIC:enumeration members]
You can return [ITALIC:enum members] from your [ITALIC:path operation], even nested in a JSON body (e.g. a [CODE: dict]).
They will be converted to their corresponding values (strings in this case) before returning them to the client:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]2: return {"model[ITALIC:name": model]name, "message": "Deep...]
In your client you will get a JSON response like:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]3: { "model_name": "alexnet", "message": "Deep Learni...]

metadata.breadcrumb: Path Parameters > Predefined values > Working with Python *enumerations*
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/path-params.md

They will be converted to their corresponding values (strings in this case) before returning them to the client:

page_content:
[Context: Path Parameters > Path parameters containing paths]

## Path parameters containing paths
Let's say you have a [ITALIC:path operation] with a path [CODE: /files/{file[ITALIC:path}].
But you need [CODE: file]path] itself to contain a [ITALIC:path], like [CODE: home/johndoe/myfile.txt].
So, the URL for that file would be something like: [CODE: /files/home/johndoe/myfile.txt].

metadata.breadcrumb: Path Parameters > Path parameters containing paths
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/path-params.md

page_content:
[Context: Path Parameters > Path parameters containing paths > OpenAPI support]

### OpenAPI support
OpenAPI doesn't support a way to declare a [ITALIC:path parameter] to contain a [ITALIC:path] inside, as that could lead to scenarios that are difficult to test and define.
Nevertheless, you can still do it in [BOLD:FastAPI], using one of the internal tools from Starlette.
And the docs would still work, although not adding any documentation telling that the parameter should contain a path.

metadata.breadcrumb: Path Parameters > Path parameters containing paths > OpenAPI support
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/path-params.md

page_content:
[Context: Path Parameters > Path parameters containing paths > Path convertor]

### Path convertor
Using an option directly from Starlette you can declare a [ITALIC:path parameter] containing a [ITALIC:path] using a URL like:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: /files/{file[ITALIC:path:path}...]
In this case, the name of the parameter is [CODE: file]path], and the last part, [CODE: :path], tells it that the parameter should match any [ITALIC:path].
So, you can use it with:
```python
@app.get("/files/{file[ITALIC:path:path}")
async def read]file(file_path: str):
```
/// tip
You might need the parameter to contain [CODE: /home/johndoe/myfile.txt], with a leading slash ([CODE: /]).
In that case, the URL would be: [CODE: /files//home/johndoe/myfile.txt], with a double slash ([CODE: //]) between [CODE: files] and [CODE: home].
///

metadata.breadcrumb: Path Parameters > Path parameters containing paths > Path convertor
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/path-params.md

page_content:
[Context: Path Parameters > Recap]

## Recap
With [BOLD:FastAPI], by using short, intuitive and standard Python type declarations, you get:
[ITALIC: Editor support: error checks, autocompletion, etc.
] Data "parsing"
[ITALIC: Data validation
] API annotation and automatic documentation
And you only have to declare them once.
That's probably the main visible advantage of [BOLD:FastAPI] compared to alternative frameworks (apart from the raw performance).

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/path-params.md

page_content:
[Context: Query Parameter Models]

# Query Parameter Models
If you have a group of [BOLD:query parameters] that are related, you can create a [BOLD:Pydantic model] to declare them.
This would allow you to [BOLD:re-use the model] in [BOLD:multiple places] and also to declare validations and metadata for all the parameters at once.
/// note
This is supported since FastAPI version [CODE: 0.115.0]. 🤓
///

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/query-param-models.md

page_content:
[Context: Query Parameter Models > Query Parameters with a Pydantic Model]

## Query Parameters with a Pydantic Model
Declare the [BOLD:query parameters] that you need in a [BOLD:Pydantic model], and then declare the parameter as [CODE: Query]:
```python
class FilterParams(BaseModel):
limit: int = Field(100, gt=0, le=100)
offset: int = Field(0, ge=0)
order[ITALIC:by: Literal["created]at", "updated[ITALIC:at"] = "created]at"
tags: list[str] = []
async def read[ITALIC:items(filter]query: Annotated[FilterParams, Query()]):
return filter_query
```
[BOLD:FastAPI] will [BOLD:extract] the data for [BOLD:each field] from the [BOLD:query parameters] in the request and give you the Pydantic model you defined.

hierarchy: Query Parameters with a Pydantic Model

You can see the query parameters in the docs UI at [CODE: /docs]:

page_content:
[Context: Query Parameter Models > Forbid Extra Query Parameters]

## Forbid Extra Query Parameters
In some special use cases (probably not very common), you might want to [BOLD:restrict] the query parameters that you want to receive.
You can use Pydantic's model configuration to [CODE: forbid] any [CODE: extra] fields:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: model[ITALIC:config = {"extra": "forbid"}...]
If a client tries to send some [BOLD:extra] data in the [BOLD:query parameters], they will receive an [BOLD:error] response.
For example, if the client tries to send a [CODE: tool] query parameter with a value of [CODE: plumbus], like:
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:1: https://example.com/items/?limit=10&tool=plumbus...]
They will receive an [BOLD:error] response telling them that the query parameter [CODE: tool] is not allowed:
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:2: { "detail": [ { "type": "extra]forbidden", "loc": ...]

metadata.breadcrumb: Query Parameter Models > Forbid Extra Query Parameters

page_content:
[Context: Query Parameters and String Validations]

# Query Parameters and String Validations
[BOLD:FastAPI] allows you to declare additional information and validation for your parameters.
Let's take this application as example:
```python
async def read[ITALIC:items(q: str | None = None):
results = {"items": [{"item]id": "Foo"}, {"item_id": "Bar"}]}
```
The query parameter [CODE: q] is of type [CODE: str | None], that means that it's of type [CODE: str] but could also be [CODE: None], and indeed, the default value is [CODE: None], so FastAPI will know it's not required.
/// note
FastAPI will know that the value of [CODE: q] is not required because of the default value [CODE: = None].
Having [CODE: str | None] will allow your editor to give you better support and detect errors.
///

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/query-params-str-validations.md

page_content:
[Context: Query Parameters and String Validations > Additional validation]

## Additional validation
We are going to enforce that even though [CODE: q] is optional, whenever it is provided, [BOLD:its length doesn't exceed 50 characters].

metadata.breadcrumb: Query Parameters and String Validations > Additional validation
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/query-params-str-validations.md

page_content:
[Context: Query Parameters and String Validations > Additional validation > Import `Query` and `Annotated`]

### Import [CODE: Query] and [CODE: Annotated]
To achieve that, first import:
[ITALIC: [CODE: Query] from [CODE: fastapi]
] [CODE: Annotated] from [CODE: typing]
```python
from typing import Annotated

from fastapi import FastAPI, Query

```
/// info
FastAPI added support for [CODE: Annotated] (and started recommending it) in version 0.95.0.
If you have an older version, you would get errors when trying to use [CODE: Annotated].
Make sure you Upgrade the FastAPI version to at least 0.95.1 before using [CODE: Annotated].
///

metadata.breadcrumb: Query Parameters and String Validations > Additional validation > Import `Query` and `Annotated`
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/query-params-str-validations.md

page_content:
[Context: Query Parameters and String Validations > Use `Annotated` in the type for the `q` parameter]

## Use [CODE: Annotated] in the type for the [CODE: q] parameter
Remember I told you before that [CODE: Annotated] can be used to add metadata to your parameters in the Python Types Intro?
Now it's the time to use it with FastAPI.
We had this type annotation:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: q: str | None = None...]
What we will do is wrap that with [CODE: Annotated], so it becomes:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]1: q: Annotated[str | None] = None...]
Both of those versions mean the same thing, [CODE: q] is a parameter that can be a [CODE: str] or [CODE: None], and by default, it is [CODE: None].
Now let's jump to the fun stuff.

hierarchy: Use `Annotated` in the type for the `q` parameter
metadata.breadcrumb: Query Parameters and String Validations > Use `Annotated` in the type for the `q` parameter
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/query-params-str-validations.md

page_content:
[Context: Query Parameters and String Validations > Add `Query` to `Annotated` in the `q` parameter]

## Add [CODE: Query] to [CODE: Annotated] in the [CODE: q] parameter
Now that we have this [CODE: Annotated] where we can put more information (in this case some additional validation), add [CODE: Query] inside of [CODE: Annotated], and set the parameter [CODE: max[ITALIC:length] to [CODE: 50]:
```python
async def read]items(q: Annotated[str | None, Query(max[ITALIC:length=50)] = None):
results = {"items": [{"item]id": "Foo"}, {"item[ITALIC:id": "Bar"}]}
```
Notice that the default value is still [CODE: None], so the parameter is still optional.
But now, having [CODE: Query(max]length=50)] inside of [CODE: Annotated], we are telling FastAPI that we want it to have [BOLD:additional validation] for this value, we want it to have maximum 50 characters.
/// tip
Here we are using [CODE: Query()] because this is a [BOLD:query parameter]. Later we will see others like [CODE: Path()], [CODE: Body()], [CODE: Header()], and [CODE: Cookie()], that also accept the same arguments as [CODE: Query()].
///
FastAPI will now:
[ITALIC: [BOLD:Validate] the data making sure that the max length is 50 characters
] Show a [BOLD:clear error] for the client when the data is not valid
[ITALIC: [BOLD:Document] the parameter in the OpenAPI schema ]path operation* (so it will show up in the [BOLD:automatic docs UI])

metadata.breadcrumb: Query Parameters and String Validations > Add `Query` to `Annotated` in the `q` parameter
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/query-params-str-validations.md


/// tip
Here we are using [CODE: Query()] because this is a [BOLD:query parameter]. Later we will see others like [CODE: Path()], [CODE: Body()], [CODE: Header()], and [CODE: Cookie()], that also accept the same arguments as [CODE: Query()].

FastAPI will now:
[ITALIC: [BOLD:Validate] the data making sure that the max length is 50 characters

page_content:
[Context: Query Parameters and String Validations > Alternative (old): `Query` as the default value]

## Alternative (old): [CODE: Query] as the default value
Previous versions of FastAPI (before 0.95.0) required you to use [CODE: Query] as the default value of your parameter, instead of putting it in [CODE: Annotated], there's a high chance that you will see code using it around, so I'll explain it to you.
/// tip
For new code and whenever possible, use [CODE: Annotated] as explained above. There are multiple advantages (explained below) and no disadvantages.
///
This is how you would use [CODE: Query()] as the default value of your function parameter, setting the parameter [CODE: max[ITALIC:length] to 50:
```python
async def read]items(q: str | None = Query(default=None, max[ITALIC:length=50)):
results = {"items": [{"item]id": "Foo"}, {"item[ITALIC:id": "Bar"}]}
```
As in this case (without using [CODE: Annotated]) we have to replace the default value [CODE: None] in the function with [CODE: Query()], we now need to set the default value with the parameter [CODE: Query(default=None)], it serves the same purpose of defining that default value (at least for FastAPI).
So:
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:0: q: str | None = Query(default=None)...]
...makes the parameter optional, with a default value of [CODE: None], the same as:
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:1: q: str | None = None...]
But the [CODE: Query] version declares it explicitly as being a query parameter.
Then, we can pass more parameters to [CODE: Query]. In this case, the [CODE: max]length] parameter that applies to strings:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]2: q: str | None = Query(default=None, max_length=50)...]
This will validate the data, show a clear error when the data is not valid, and document the parameter in the OpenAPI schema [ITALIC:path operation].

metadata.breadcrumb: Query Parameters and String Validations > Alternative (old): `Query` as the default value
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/query-params-str-validations.md

Previous versions of FastAPI (before 0.95.0) required you to use [CODE: Query] as the default value of your parameter, instead of putting it in [CODE: Annotated], there's a high chance that you will see code using it around, so I'll explain it to you.
/// tip
For new code and whenever possible, use [CODE: Annotated] as explained above. There are multiple advantages (explained below) and no disadvantages.
///
This is how you would use [CODE: Query()] as the default value of your function parameter, setting the parameter [CODE: max[ITALIC:length] to 50:
```python

```

[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:0: q: str | None = Query(default=None)...]

page_content:
[Context: Query Parameters and String Validations > Alternative (old): `Query` as the default value > `Query` as the default value or in `Annotated`]

### [CODE: Query] as the default value or in [CODE: Annotated]
Keep in mind that when using [CODE: Query] inside of [CODE: Annotated] you cannot use the [CODE: default] parameter for [CODE: Query].
Instead, use the actual default value of the function parameter. Otherwise, it would be inconsistent.
For example, this is not allowed:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: q: Annotated[str, Query(default="rick")] = "morty"...]
...because it's not clear if the default value should be [CODE: "rick"] or [CODE: "morty"].
So, you would use (preferably):
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]1: q: Annotated[str, Query()] = "rick"...]
...or in older code bases you will find:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]2: q: str = Query(default="rick")...]

hierarchy: `Query` as the default value or in `Annotated`
metadata.breadcrumb: Query Parameters and String Validations > Alternative (old): `Query` as the default value > `Query` as the default value or in `Annotated`
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/query-params-str-validations.md

page_content:
[Context: Query Parameters and String Validations > Alternative (old): `Query` as the default value > Advantages of `Annotated`]

### Advantages of [CODE: Annotated]
[BOLD:Using [CODE: Annotated] is recommended] instead of the default value in function parameters, it is [BOLD:better] for multiple reasons. 🤓
The [BOLD:default] value of the [BOLD:function parameter] is the [BOLD:actual default] value, that's more intuitive with Python in general.
You could [BOLD:call] that same function in [BOLD:other places] without FastAPI, and it would [BOLD:work as expected]. If there's a [BOLD:required] parameter (without a default value), your [BOLD:editor] will let you know with an error, [BOLD:Python] will also complain if you run it without passing the required parameter.
When you don't use [CODE: Annotated] and instead use the [BOLD:(old) default value style], if you call that function without FastAPI in [BOLD:other places], you have to [BOLD:remember] to pass the arguments to the function for it to work correctly, otherwise the values will be different from what you expect (e.g. [CODE: QueryInfo] or something similar instead of [CODE: str]). And your editor won't complain, and Python won't complain running that function, only when the operations inside error out.
Because [CODE: Annotated] can have more than one metadata annotation, you could now even use the same function with other tools, like Typer.

metadata.breadcrumb: Query Parameters and String Validations > Alternative (old): `Query` as the default value > Advantages of `Annotated`
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/query-params-str-validations.md

When you don't use [CODE: Annotated] and instead use the [BOLD:(old) default value style], if you call that function without FastAPI in [BOLD:other places], you have to [BOLD:remember] to pass the arguments to the function for it to work correctly, otherwise the values will be different from what you expect (e.g. [CODE: QueryInfo] or something similar instead of [CODE: str]). page_content:
[Context: Query Parameters and String Validations > Add more validations]

## Add more validations
You can also add a parameter [CODE: min[ITALIC:length]:
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:0: q: Annotated[str | None, Query(min]length=3, max_l...]

metadata.breadcrumb: Query Parameters and String Validations > Add more validations
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/query-params-str-validations.md

page_content:
[Context: Query Parameters and String Validations > Add regular expressions]

## Add regular expressions
You can define a regular expression [CODE: pattern] that the parameter should match:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: str | None, Query(min[ITALIC:length=3, max]length=50, pat...]
This specific regular expression pattern checks that the received parameter value:
[ITALIC: [CODE: ^]: starts with the following characters, doesn't have characters before.
] [CODE: fixedquery]: has the exact value [CODE: fixedquery].
- [CODE: $]: ends there, doesn't have any more characters after [CODE: fixedquery].
If you feel lost with all these [BOLD:"regular expression"] ideas, don't worry. They are a hard topic for many people. You can still do a lot of stuff without needing regular expressions yet.
Now you know that whenever you need them you can use them in [BOLD:FastAPI].

metadata.breadcrumb: Query Parameters and String Validations > Add regular expressions
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/query-params-str-validations.md

page_content:
[Context: Query Parameters and String Validations > Default values]

## Default values
You can, of course, use default values other than [CODE: None].
Let's say that you want to declare the [CODE: q] query parameter to have a [CODE: min[ITALIC:length] of [CODE: 3], and to have a default value of [CODE: "fixedquery"]:
```python
async def read]items(q: Annotated[str, Query(min[ITALIC:length=3)] = "fixedquery"):
results = {"items": [{"item]id": "Foo"}, {"item_id": "Bar"}]}
```
/// note
Having a default value of any type, including [CODE: None], makes the parameter optional (not required).
///

metadata.breadcrumb: Query Parameters and String Validations > Default values
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/query-params-str-validations.md

page_content:
[Context: Query Parameters and String Validations > Required parameters]

## Required parameters
When we don't need to declare more validations or metadata, we can make the [CODE: q] query parameter required just by not declaring a default value, like:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: q: str...]
instead of:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]1: q: str | None = None...]
But we are now declaring it with [CODE: Query], for example like:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]2: q: Annotated[str | None, Query(min[ITALIC:length=3)] = No...]
So, when you need to declare a value as required while using [CODE: Query], you can simply not declare a default value:
```python
async def read]items(q: Annotated[str, Query(min[ITALIC:length=3)]):
results = {"items": [{"item]id": "Foo"}, {"item_id": "Bar"}]}
```

metadata.breadcrumb: Query Parameters and String Validations > Required parameters
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/query-params-str-validations.md

page_content:
[Context: Query Parameters and String Validations > Required parameters > Required, can be `None`]

### Required, can be [CODE: None]
You can declare that a parameter can accept [CODE: None], but that it's still required. This would force clients to send a value, even if the value is [CODE: None].
To do that, you can declare that [CODE: None] is a valid type but simply do not declare a default value:
```python
async def read[ITALIC:items(q: Annotated[str | None, Query(min]length=3)]):
results = {"items": [{"item[ITALIC:id": "Foo"}, {"item]id": "Bar"}]}
```

metadata.breadcrumb: Query Parameters and String Validations > Required parameters > Required, can be `None`
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/query-params-str-validations.md

page_content:
[Context: Query Parameters and String Validations > Query parameter list / multiple values]

## Query parameter list / multiple values
When you define a query parameter explicitly with [CODE: Query] you can also declare it to receive a list of values, or said in another way, to receive multiple values.
For example, to declare a query parameter [CODE: q] that can appear multiple times in the URL, you can write:
```python
async def read[ITALIC:items(q: Annotated[list[str] | None, Query()] = None):
query]items = {"q": q}
```
Then, with a URL like:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: http://localhost:8000/items/?q=foo&q=bar...]
you would receive the multiple [CODE: q] [ITALIC:query parameters'] values ([CODE: foo] and [CODE: bar]) in a Python [CODE: list] inside your [ITALIC:path operation function], in the [ITALIC:function parameter] [CODE: q].
So, the response to that URL would be:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]1: { "q": [ "foo", "bar" ] }...]
/// tip
To declare a query parameter with a type of [CODE: list], like in the example above, you need to explicitly use [CODE: Query], otherwise it would be interpreted as a request body.
///
The interactive API docs will update accordingly, to allow multiple values:

metadata.breadcrumb: Query Parameters and String Validations > Query parameter list / multiple values
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/query-params-str-validations.md

[Context: Query Parameters and String Validations > Query parameter list / multiple values]

```python
query]items = {"q": q}
```

[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: http://localhost:8000/items/?q=foo&q=bar...]
you would receive the multiple [CODE: q] [ITALIC:query parameters'] values ([CODE: foo] and [CODE: bar]) in a Python [CODE: list] inside your [ITALIC:path operation function], in the [ITALIC:function parameter] [CODE: q].
So, the response to that URL would be:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]1: { "q": [ "foo", "bar" ] }...]
/// tip
To declare a query parameter with a type of [CODE: list], like in the example above, you need to explicitly use [CODE: Query], otherwise it would be interpreted as a request body.

The interactive API docs will update accordingly, to allow multiple values:

page_content:
[Context: Query Parameters and String Validations > Query parameter list / multiple values > Query parameter list / multiple values with defaults]

### Query parameter list / multiple values with defaults
You can also define a default [CODE: list] of values if none are provided:
```python
async def read[ITALIC:items(q: Annotated[list[str], Query()] = ["foo", "bar"]):
query]items = {"q": q}
```
If you go to:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: http://localhost:8000/items/...]
the default of [CODE: q] will be: [CODE: ["foo", "bar"]] and your response will be:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]1: { "q": [ "foo", "bar" ] }...]
#### Using just [CODE: list]
You can also use [CODE: list] directly instead of [CODE: list[str]]:
```python
async def read[ITALIC:items(q: Annotated[list, Query()] = []):
query]items = {"q": q}
```
/// note
Keep in mind that in this case, FastAPI won't check the contents of the list.
For example, [CODE: list[int]] would check (and document) that the contents of the list are integers. But [CODE: list] alone wouldn't.
///

hierarchy: Query parameter list / multiple values with defaults
metadata.breadcrumb: Query Parameters and String Validations > Query parameter list / multiple values > Query parameter list / multiple values with defaults
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/query-params-str-validations.md

page_content:
[Context: Query Parameters and String Validations > Declare more metadata]

## Declare more metadata
You can add more information about the parameter.
That information will be included in the generated OpenAPI and used by the documentation user interfaces and external tools.
/// note
Keep in mind that different tools might have different levels of OpenAPI support.
Some of them might not show all the extra information declared yet, although in most of the cases, the missing feature is already planned for development.
///
You can add a [CODE: title]:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: q: Annotated[str | None, Query(title="Query string...]
And a [CODE: description]:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]1: description="Query string for the items to search ...]

metadata.breadcrumb: Query Parameters and String Validations > Declare more metadata
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/query-params-str-validations.md

page_content:
[Context: Query Parameters and String Validations > Alias parameters]

## Alias parameters
Imagine that you want the parameter to be [CODE: item-query].
Like in:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: http://127.0.0.1:8000/items/?item-query=foobaritem...]
But [CODE: item-query] is not a valid Python variable name.
The closest would be [CODE: item[ITALIC:query].
But you still need it to be exactly [CODE: item-query]...
Then you can declare an [CODE: alias], and that alias is what will be used to find the parameter value:
```python
async def read]items(q: Annotated[str | None, Query(alias="item-query")] = None):
results = {"items": [{"item[ITALIC:id": "Foo"}, {"item]id": "Bar"}]}
```

metadata.breadcrumb: Query Parameters and String Validations > Alias parameters
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/query-params-str-validations.md

page_content:
[Context: Query Parameters and String Validations > Deprecating parameters]

## Deprecating parameters
Now let's say you don't like this parameter anymore.
You have to leave it there a while because there are clients using it, but you want the docs to clearly show it as deprecated.
Then pass the parameter [CODE: deprecated=True] to [CODE: Query]:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: deprecated=True, ),...]
The docs will show it like this:

metadata.breadcrumb: Query Parameters and String Validations > Deprecating parameters
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/query-params-str-validations.md

page_content:
[Context: Query Parameters and String Validations > Exclude parameters from OpenAPI]

## Exclude parameters from OpenAPI
To exclude a query parameter from the generated OpenAPI schema (and thus, from the automatic documentation systems), set the parameter [CODE: include[ITALIC:in]schema] of [CODE: Query] to [CODE: False]:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: hidden[ITALIC:query: Annotated[str | None, Query(include]...]

metadata.breadcrumb: Query Parameters and String Validations > Exclude parameters from OpenAPI
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/query-params-str-validations.md

metadata.breadcrumb: Query Parameters and String Validations > Custom Validation
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/query-params-str-validations.md

metadata.breadcrumb: Query Parameters and String Validations > Custom Validation
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/query-params-str-validations.md

page_content:
[Context: Query Parameters and String Validations > Custom Validation > Understand that Code]

### Understand that Code
The important point is just using [BOLD:[CODE: AfterValidator] with a function inside [CODE: Annotated]]. Feel free to skip this part. 🤸
---
But if you're curious about this specific code example and you're still entertained, here are some extra details.
#### String with [CODE: value.startswith()]
Did you notice? a string using [CODE: value.startswith()] can take a tuple, and it will check each value in the tuple:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: if not id.startswith(("isbn-", "imdb-")): raise Va...]
#### A Random Item
With [CODE: data.items()] we get an iterable object with tuples containing the key and value for each dictionary item.
We convert this iterable object into a proper [CODE: list] with [CODE: list(data.items())].
Then with [CODE: random.choice()] we can get a [BOLD:random value] from the list, so, we get a tuple with [CODE: (id, name)]. It will be something like [CODE: ("imdb-tt0371724", "The Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy")].
Then we [BOLD:assign those two values] of the tuple to the variables [CODE: id] and [CODE: name].
So, if the user didn't provide an item ID, they will still receive a random suggestion.
...we do all this in a [BOLD:single simple line]. 🤯 Don't you love Python?
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]1: id, item = random.choice(list(data.items())) retur...]

metadata.breadcrumb: Query Parameters and String Validations > Custom Validation > Understand that Code
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/query-params-str-validations.md

page_content:
[Context: Query Parameters and String Validations > Recap]

## Recap
You can declare additional validations and metadata for your parameters.
Generic validations and metadata:
[ITALIC: [CODE: alias]
] [CODE: title]
[ITALIC: [CODE: description]
] [CODE: deprecated]
Validations specific for strings:
[ITALIC: [CODE: min[ITALIC:length]
] [CODE: max]length]
- [CODE: pattern]
Custom validations using [CODE: AfterValidator].
In these examples you saw how to declare validations for [CODE: str] values.
See the next chapters to learn how to declare validations for other types, like numbers.

page_content:
[Context: Query Parameters]

# Query Parameters
When you declare other function parameters that are not part of the path parameters, they are automatically interpreted as "query" parameters.
```python
async def read[ITALIC:item(skip: int = 0, limit: int = 10):
return fake]items[ITALIC:db[skip : skip + limit]
```
The query is the set of key-value pairs that go after the [CODE: ?] in a URL, separated by [CODE: &] characters.
For example, in the URL:
[CODE]BLOCK_0: http://127.0.0.1:8000/items/?skip=0&limit=10...]
...the query parameters are:
[ITALIC: [CODE: skip]: with a value of [CODE: 0]
] [CODE: limit]: with a value of [CODE: 10]
As they are part of the URL, they are "naturally" strings.
But when you declare them with Python types (in the example above, as [CODE: int]), they are converted to that type and validated against it.
All the same process that applied for path parameters also applies for query parameters:
[ITALIC: Editor support (obviously)
] Data "parsing"
[ITALIC: Data validation
] Automatic documentation

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/query-params.md

page_content:
[Context: Query Parameters]

# Query Parameters
When you declare other function parameters that are not part of the path parameters, they are automatically interpreted as "query" parameters.
```python
async def read[ITALIC:item(skip: int = 0, limit: int = 10):
return fake]items[ITALIC:db[skip : skip + limit]
```
The query is the set of key-value pairs that go after the [CODE: ?] in a URL, separated by [CODE: &] characters.
For example, in the URL:
[CODE]BLOCK_0: http://127.0.0.1:8000/items/?skip=0&limit=10...]
...the query parameters are:
[ITALIC: [CODE: skip]: with a value of [CODE: 0]
] [CODE: limit]: with a value of [CODE: 10]
As they are part of the URL, they are "naturally" strings.
But when you declare them with Python types (in the example above, as [CODE: int]), they are converted to that type and validated against it.
All the same process that applied for path parameters also applies for query parameters:
[ITALIC: Editor support (obviously)
] Data "parsing"
[ITALIC: Data validation
] Automatic documentation

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/query-params.md

page_content:
[Context: Query Parameters > Defaults]

## Defaults
As query parameters are not a fixed part of a path, they can be optional and can have default values.
In the example above they have default values of [CODE: skip=0] and [CODE: limit=10].
So, going to the URL:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: http://127.0.0.1:8000/items/...]
would be the same as going to:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]1: http://127.0.0.1:8000/items/?skip=0&limit=10...]
But if you go to, for example:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]2: http://127.0.0.1:8000/items/?skip=20...]
The parameter values in your function will be:
[ITALIC: [CODE: skip=20]: because you set it in the URL
] [CODE: limit=10]: because that was the default value

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/query-params.md

page_content:
[Context: Query Parameters > Optional parameters]

## Optional parameters
The same way, you can declare optional query parameters, by setting their default to [CODE: None]:
```python
async def read[ITALIC:item(item]id: str, q: str | None = None):
if q:
```
In this case, the function parameter [CODE: q] will be optional, and will be [CODE: None] by default.
/// check
Also notice that [BOLD:FastAPI] is smart enough to notice that the path parameter [CODE: item_id] is a path parameter and [CODE: q] is not, so, it's a query parameter.
///

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/query-params.md

page_content:
[Context: Query Parameters > Query parameter type conversion]

## Query parameter type conversion
You can also declare [CODE: bool] types, and they will be converted:
```python
async def read[ITALIC:item(item]id: str, q: str | None = None, short: bool = False):
item = {"item[ITALIC:id": item]id}
```
In this case, if you go to:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: http://127.0.0.1:8000/items/foo?short=1...]
or
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]1: http://127.0.0.1:8000/items/foo?short=True...]
or
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]2: http://127.0.0.1:8000/items/foo?short=true...]
or
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]3: http://127.0.0.1:8000/items/foo?short=on...]
or
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]4: http://127.0.0.1:8000/items/foo?short=yes...]
or any other case variation (uppercase, first letter in uppercase, etc), your function will see the parameter [CODE: short] with a [CODE: bool] value of [CODE: True]. Otherwise as [CODE: False].

metadata.breadcrumb: Query Parameters > Query parameter type conversion
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/query-params.md

page_content:
[Context: Query Parameters > Multiple path and query parameters]

## Multiple path and query parameters
You can declare multiple path parameters and query parameters at the same time, [BOLD:FastAPI] knows which is which.
And you don't have to declare them in any specific order.
They will be detected by name:
```python
@app.get("/users/{user[ITALIC:id}/items/{item]id}")
async def read[ITALIC:user]item(
user[ITALIC:id: int, item]id: str, q: str | None = None, short: bool = False
):
```

metadata.breadcrumb: Query Parameters > Multiple path and query parameters
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/query-params.md

page_content:
[Context: Query Parameters > Required query parameters]

## Required query parameters
When you declare a default value for non-path parameters (for now, we have only seen query parameters), then it is not required.
If you don't want to add a specific value but just make it optional, set the default as [CODE: None].
But when you want to make a query parameter required, you can just not declare any default value:
```python
@app.get("/items/{item[ITALIC:id}")
async def read]user[ITALIC:item(item]id: str, needy: str):
```
Here the query parameter [CODE: needy] is a required query parameter of type [CODE: str].
If you open in your browser a URL like:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: http://127.0.0.1:8000/items/foo-item...]
...without adding the required parameter [CODE: needy], you will see an error like:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]1: { "detail": [ { "type": "missing", "loc": [ "query...]
As [CODE: needy] is a required parameter, you would need to set it in the URL:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]2: http://127.0.0.1:8000/items/foo-item?needy=soooone...]
...this would work:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]3: { "item[ITALIC:id": "foo-item", "needy": "sooooneedy" }...]
And of course, you can define some parameters as required, some as having a default value, and some entirely optional:
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:4: item]id: str, needy: str, skip: int = 0, limit: in...]
In this case, there are 3 query parameters:
[ITALIC: [CODE: needy], a required [CODE: str].
] [CODE: skip], an [CODE: int] with a default value of [CODE: 0].
- [CODE: limit], an optional [CODE: int].
/// tip
You could also use [CODE: Enum]s the same way as with Path Parameters.
///

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/query-params.md

page_content:
[Context: Query Parameters > Required query parameters]

## Required query parameters
When you declare a default value for non-path parameters (for now, we have only seen query parameters), then it is not required.

But when you want to make a query parameter required, you can just not declare any default value:
```python

async def read]user[ITALIC:item(item]id: str, needy: str):
```

If you open in your browser a URL like:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: http://127.0.0.1:8000/items/foo-item...]
...without adding the required parameter [CODE: needy], you will see an error like:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]1: { "detail": [ { "type": "missing", "loc": [ "query...]
As [CODE: needy] is a required parameter, you would need to set it in the URL:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]2: http://127.0.0.1:8000/items/foo-item?needy=soooone...]
...this would work:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]3: { "item[ITALIC:id": "foo-item", "needy": "sooooneedy" }...]
And of course, you can define some parameters as required, some as having a default value, and some entirely optional:
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:4: item]id: str, needy: str, skip: int = 0, limit: in...]
In this case, there are 3 query parameters:
[ITALIC: [CODE: needy], a required [CODE: str].
] [CODE: skip], an [CODE: int] with a default value of [CODE: 0].
- [CODE: limit], an optional [CODE: int].
/// tip
You could also use [CODE: Enum]s the same way as with Path Parameters.
///

page_content:
[Context: Request Files]

# Request Files
You can define files to be uploaded by the client using [CODE: File].
/// info
To receive uploaded files, first install [[CODE: python-multipart]](https://github.com/Kludex/python-multipart).
Make sure you create a virtual environment, activate it, and then install it, for example:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: $ pip install python-multipart...]
This is because uploaded files are sent as "form data".
///

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/request-files.md

page_content:
[Context: Request Files > Import `File`]

## Import [CODE: File]
Import [CODE: File] and [CODE: UploadFile] from [CODE: fastapi]:
```python
from fastapi import FastAPI, File, UploadFile

```

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/request-files.md

page_content:
[Context: Request Files > Define `File` Parameters]

## Define [CODE: File] Parameters
Create file parameters the same way you would for [CODE: Body] or [CODE: Form]:
```python
async def create[ITALIC:file(file: Annotated[bytes, File()]):
return {"file]size": len(file)}
```
/// info
[CODE: File] is a class that inherits directly from [CODE: Form].
But remember that when you import [CODE: Query], [CODE: Path], [CODE: File] and others from [CODE: fastapi], those are actually functions that return special classes.
///
/// tip
To declare File bodies, you need to use [CODE: File], because otherwise the parameters would be interpreted as query parameters or body (JSON) parameters.
///
The files will be uploaded as "form data".
If you declare the type of your [ITALIC:path operation function] parameter as [CODE: bytes], [BOLD:FastAPI] will read the file for you and you will receive the contents as [CODE: bytes].
Keep in mind that this means that the whole contents will be stored in memory. This will work well for small files.
But there are several cases in which you might benefit from using [CODE: UploadFile].

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/request-files.md

page_content:
[Context: Request Files > Define `File` Parameters]

## Define [CODE: File] Parameters
Create file parameters the same way you would for [CODE: Body] or [CODE: Form]:
```python
async def create[ITALIC:file(file: Annotated[bytes, File()]):
return {"file]size": len(file)}
```
/// info
[CODE: File] is a class that inherits directly from [CODE: Form].
But remember that when you import [CODE: Query], [CODE: Path], [CODE: File] and others from [CODE: fastapi], those are actually functions that return special classes.
///
/// tip
To declare File bodies, you need to use [CODE: File], because otherwise the parameters would be interpreted as query parameters or body (JSON) parameters.
///
The files will be uploaded as "form data".
If you declare the type of your [ITALIC:path operation function] parameter as [CODE: bytes], [BOLD:FastAPI] will read the file for you and you will receive the contents as [CODE: bytes].
Keep in mind that this means that the whole contents will be stored in memory. This will work well for small files.
But there are several cases in which you might benefit from using [CODE: UploadFile].

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/request-files.md

page_content:
[Context: Request Files > File Parameters with `UploadFile`]

## File Parameters with [CODE: UploadFile]
Define a file parameter with a type of [CODE: UploadFile]:
```python
async def create[ITALIC:upload]file(file: UploadFile):
return {"filename": file.filename}
```
Using [CODE: UploadFile] has several advantages over [CODE: bytes]:
[ITALIC: You don't have to use [CODE: File()] in the default value of the parameter.
] It uses a "spooled" file:
[ITALIC: A file stored in memory up to a maximum size limit, and after passing this limit it will be stored in disk.
] This means that it will work well for large files like images, videos, large binaries, etc. without consuming all the memory.
[ITALIC: You can get metadata from the uploaded file.
] It has a file-like [CODE: async] interface.
- It exposes an actual Python [[CODE: SpooledTemporaryFile]](https://docs.python.org/3/library/tempfile.html#tempfile.SpooledTemporaryFile) object that you can pass directly to other libraries that expect a file-like object.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/request-files.md

page_content:
[Context: Request Files > File Parameters with `UploadFile`]

## File Parameters with [CODE: UploadFile]
Define a file parameter with a type of [CODE: UploadFile]:
```python
async def create[ITALIC:upload]file(file: UploadFile):
return {"filename": file.filename}
```
Using [CODE: UploadFile] has several advantages over [CODE: bytes]:
[ITALIC: You don't have to use [CODE: File()] in the default value of the parameter.
] It uses a "spooled" file:
[ITALIC: A file stored in memory up to a maximum size limit, and after passing this limit it will be stored in disk.
] This means that it will work well for large files like images, videos, large binaries, etc. without consuming all the memory.
[ITALIC: You can get metadata from the uploaded file.
] It has a file-like [CODE: async] interface.
- It exposes an actual Python [[CODE: SpooledTemporaryFile]](https://docs.python.org/3/library/tempfile.html#tempfile.SpooledTemporaryFile) object that you can pass directly to other libraries that expect a file-like object.

page_content:
[Context: Request Files > Optional File Upload]

## Optional File Upload
You can make a file optional by using standard type annotations and setting a default value of [CODE: None]:
```python
async def create[ITALIC:file(file: Annotated[bytes | None, File()] = None):
if not file:
async def create]upload_file(file: UploadFile | None = None):
if not file:
```

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/request-files.md

page_content:
[Context: Request Files > `UploadFile` with Additional Metadata]

## [CODE: UploadFile] with Additional Metadata
You can also use [CODE: File()] with [CODE: UploadFile], for example, to set additional metadata:
```python
async def create[ITALIC:file(file: Annotated[bytes, File(description="A file read as bytes")]):
return {"file]size": len(file)}
file: Annotated[UploadFile, File(description="A file read as UploadFile")],
):
```

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/request-files.md

page_content:
[Context: Request Files > Multiple File Uploads]

## Multiple File Uploads
It's possible to upload several files at the same time.
They would be associated to the same "form field" sent using "form data".
To use that, declare a list of [CODE: bytes] or [CODE: UploadFile]:
```python
async def create[ITALIC:files(files: Annotated[list[bytes], File()]):
return {"file]sizes": [len(file) for file in files]}
async def create[ITALIC:upload]files(files: list[UploadFile]):
return {"filenames": [file.filename for file in files]}
```
You will receive, as declared, a [CODE: list] of [CODE: bytes] or [CODE: UploadFile]s.
/// note | Technical Details
You could also use [CODE: from starlette.responses import HTMLResponse].
[BOLD:FastAPI] provides the same [CODE: starlette.responses] as [CODE: fastapi.responses] just as a convenience for you, the developer. But most of the available responses come directly from Starlette.
///

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/request-files.md

page_content:
[Context: Request Files > Multiple File Uploads > Multiple File Uploads with Additional Metadata]

### Multiple File Uploads with Additional Metadata
And the same way as before, you can use [CODE: File()] to set additional parameters, even for [CODE: UploadFile]:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: files: Annotated[list[bytes], File(description="Mu...]

hierarchy: Multiple File Uploads with Additional Metadata
metadata.breadcrumb: Request Files > Multiple File Uploads > Multiple File Uploads with Additional Metadata
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/request-files.md

page_content:
[Context: Request Files > Recap]

## Recap
Use [CODE: File], [CODE: bytes], and [CODE: UploadFile] to declare files to be uploaded in the request, sent as form data.

page_content:
[Context: Form Models > Pydantic Models for Forms]

## Pydantic Models for Forms
You just need to declare a [BOLD:Pydantic model] with the fields you want to receive as [BOLD:form fields], and then declare the parameter as [CODE: Form]:
```python
class FormData(BaseModel):
username: str
password: str
async def login(data: Annotated[FormData, Form()]):
return data
```
[BOLD:FastAPI] will [BOLD:extract] the data for [BOLD:each field] from the [BOLD:form data] in the request and give you the Pydantic model you defined.

metadata.breadcrumb: Form Models > Pydantic Models for Forms
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/request-form-models.md

page_content:
[Context: Form Models > Check the Docs]

## Check the Docs
You can verify it in the docs UI at [CODE: /docs]:

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/request-form-models.md

page_content:
[Context: Form Models > Forbid Extra Form Fields]

## Forbid Extra Form Fields
In some special use cases (probably not very common), you might want to [BOLD:restrict] the form fields to only those declared in the Pydantic model. And [BOLD:forbid] any [BOLD:extra] fields.
/// note
This is supported since FastAPI version [CODE: 0.114.0]. 🤓
///
You can use Pydantic's model configuration to [CODE: forbid] any [CODE: extra] fields:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: model[ITALIC:config = {"extra": "forbid"}...]
If a client tries to send some extra data, they will receive an [BOLD:error] response.
For example, if the client tries to send the form fields:
[ITALIC: [CODE: username]: [CODE: Rick]
] [CODE: password]: [CODE: Portal Gun]
- [CODE: extra]: [CODE: Mr. Poopybutthole]
They will receive an error response telling them that the field [CODE: extra] is not allowed:
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:1: { "detail": [ { "type": "extra]forbidden", "loc": ...]

metadata.breadcrumb: Form Models > Forbid Extra Form Fields

[Context: Form Models > Summary]

## Summary
You can use Pydantic models to declare form fields in FastAPI.
