[Context: Request Forms and Files]

# Request Forms and Files
You can define files and form fields at the same time using [CODE: File] and [CODE: Form].
/// info
To receive uploaded files and/or form data, first install [[CODE: python-multipart]](https://github.com/Kludex/python-multipart).
Make sure you create a virtual environment, activate it, and then install it, for example:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: $ pip install python-multipart...]
///

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/request-forms-and-files.md

page_content:
[Context: Request Forms and Files > Import `File` and `Form`]

## Import [CODE: File] and [CODE: Form]
```python
from fastapi import FastAPI, File, Form, UploadFile

```

metadata.breadcrumb: Request Forms and Files > Import `File` and `Form`
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/request-forms-and-files.md

page_content:
[Context: Request Forms and Files > Define `File` and `Form` parameters]

## Define [CODE: File] and [CODE: Form] parameters
Create file and form parameters the same way you would for [CODE: Body] or [CODE: Query]:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: file: Annotated[bytes, File()], fileb: Annotated[U...]
The files and form fields will be uploaded as form data and you will receive the files and form fields.
And you can declare some of the files as [CODE: bytes] and some as [CODE: UploadFile].
/// warning
You can declare multiple [CODE: File] and [CODE: Form] parameters in a [ITALIC:path operation], but you can't also declare [CODE: Body] fields that you expect to receive as JSON, as the request will have the body encoded using [CODE: multipart/form-data] instead of [CODE: application/json].
This is not a limitation of [BOLD:FastAPI], it's part of the HTTP protocol.
///

metadata.breadcrumb: Request Forms and Files > Define `File` and `Form` parameters

page_content:
[Context: Response Model - Return Type]

# Response Model - Return Type
You can declare the type used for the response by annotating the [ITALIC:path operation function] [BOLD:return type].
You can use [BOLD:type annotations] the same way you would for input data in function [BOLD:parameters], you can use Pydantic models, lists, dictionaries, scalar values like integers, booleans, etc.
```python
async def create[ITALIC:item(item: Item) -> Item:
return item
async def read]items() -> list[Item]:
return [
```
FastAPI will use this return type to:
[ITALIC: [BOLD:Validate] the returned data.
] If the data is invalid (e.g. you are missing a field), it means that [ITALIC:your] app code is broken, not returning what it should, and it will return a server error instead of returning incorrect data. This way you and your clients can be certain that they will receive the data and the data shape expected.
[ITALIC: Add a [BOLD:JSON Schema] for the response, in the OpenAPI ]path operation[ITALIC:.
] This will be used by the [BOLD:automatic docs].
[ITALIC: It will also be used by automatic client code generation tools.
] [BOLD:Serialize] the returned data to JSON using Pydantic, which is written in [BOLD:Rust], so it will be [BOLD:much faster].
But most importantly:
[ITALIC: It will [BOLD:limit and filter] the output data to what is defined in the return type.
] This is particularly important for [BOLD:security], we'll see more of that below.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/response-model.md

page_content:
[Context: Response Model - Return Type]

# Response Model - Return Type
You can declare the type used for the response by annotating the [ITALIC:path operation function] [BOLD:return type].
You can use [BOLD:type annotations] the same way you would for input data in function [BOLD:parameters], you can use Pydantic models, lists, dictionaries, scalar values like integers, booleans, etc.
```python
async def create[ITALIC:item(item: Item) -> Item:
return item
async def read]items() -> list[Item]:
return [
```
FastAPI will use this return type to:
[ITALIC: [BOLD:Validate] the returned data.
] If the data is invalid (e.g. you are missing a field), it means that [ITALIC:your] app code is broken, not returning what it should, and it will return a server error instead of returning incorrect data. This way you and your clients can be certain that they will receive the data and the data shape expected.
[ITALIC: Add a [BOLD:JSON Schema] for the response, in the OpenAPI ]path operation[ITALIC:.
] This will be used by the [BOLD:automatic docs].
[ITALIC: It will also be used by automatic client code generation tools.
] [BOLD:Serialize] the returned data to JSON using Pydantic, which is written in [BOLD:Rust], so it will be [BOLD:much faster].
But most importantly:
[ITALIC: It will [BOLD:limit and filter] the output data to what is defined in the return type.
] This is particularly important for [BOLD:security], we'll see more of that below.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/response-model.md

page_content:
[Context: Response Model - Return Type > `response_model` Parameter]

## [CODE: response[ITALIC:model] Parameter
There are some cases where you need or want to return some data that is not exactly what the type declares.
For example, you could want to [BOLD:return a dictionary] or a database object, but [BOLD:declare it as a Pydantic model]. This way the Pydantic model would do all the data documentation, validation, etc. for the object that you returned (e.g. a dictionary or database object).
If you added the return type annotation, tools and editors would complain with a (correct) error telling you that your function is returning a type (e.g. a dict) that is different from what you declared (e.g. a Pydantic model).
In those cases, you can use the [ITALIC:path operation decorator] parameter [CODE: response]model] instead of the return type.
You can use the [CODE: response[ITALIC:model] parameter in any of the [ITALIC:path operations]:
[ITALIC: [CODE: @app.get()]
] [CODE: @app.post()]
[ITALIC: [CODE: @app.put()]
] [CODE: @app.delete()]
[ITALIC: etc.
```python
@app.post("/items/", response]model=Item)
async def create[ITALIC:item(item: Item) -> Any:
@app.get("/items/", response]model=list[Item])
async def read[ITALIC:items() -> Any:
return [
{"name": "Portal Gun", "price": 42.0},
{"name": "Plumbus", "price": 32.0},
]
```
/// note
Notice that [CODE: response]model] is a parameter of the "decorator" method ([CODE: get], [CODE: post], etc). Not of your ]path operation function*, like all the parameters and body.
///
[CODE: response[ITALIC:model] receives the same type you would declare for a Pydantic model field, so, it can be a Pydantic model, but it can also be, e.g. a [CODE: list] of Pydantic models, like [CODE: List[Item]].
FastAPI will use this [CODE: response]model] to do all the data documentation, validation, etc. and also to [BOLD:convert and filter the output data] to its type declaration.
/// tip
If you have strict type checks in your editor, mypy, etc, you can declare the function return type as [CODE: Any].
That way you tell the editor that you are intentionally returning anything. But FastAPI will still do the data documentation, validation, filtering, etc. with the [CODE: response_model].
///

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/response-model.md

[Context: Response Model - Return Type > `response_model` Parameter]

## [CODE: response[ITALIC:model] Parameter
There are some cases where you need or want to return some data that is not exactly what the type declares.

You can use the [CODE: response[ITALIC:model] parameter in any of the [ITALIC:path operations]:
[ITALIC: [CODE: @app.get()]
] [CODE: @app.post()]
[ITALIC: [CODE: @app.put()]
] [CODE: @app.delete()]
[ITALIC: etc.
```python
@app.post("/items/", response]model=Item)
async def create[ITALIC:item(item: Item) -> Any:
@app.get("/items/", response]model=list[Item])
async def read[ITALIC:items() -> Any:
return [
{"name": "Portal Gun", "price": 42.0},

```
/// note
Notice that [CODE: response]model] is a parameter of the "decorator" method ([CODE: get], [CODE: post], etc). Not of your ]path operation function*, like all the parameters and body.
///
[CODE: response[ITALIC:model] receives the same type you would declare for a Pydantic model field, so, it can be a Pydantic model, but it can also be, e.g. a [CODE: list] of Pydantic models, like [CODE: List[Item]].

/// tip
If you have strict type checks in your editor, mypy, etc, you can declare the function return type as [CODE: Any].

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/response-model.md

page_content:
[Context: Response Model - Return Type > `response_model` Parameter > `response_model` Priority]

### [CODE: response[ITALIC:model] Priority
If you declare both a return type and a [CODE: response]model], the [CODE: response[ITALIC:model] will take priority and be used by FastAPI.
This way you can add correct type annotations to your functions even when you are returning a type different than the response model, to be used by the editor and tools like mypy. And still you can have FastAPI do the data validation, documentation, etc. using the [CODE: response]model].
You can also use [CODE: response_model=None] to disable creating a response model for that [ITALIC:path operation], you might need to do it if you are adding type annotations for things that are not valid Pydantic fields, you will see an example of that in one of the sections below.

metadata.breadcrumb: Response Model - Return Type > `response_model` Parameter > `response_model` Priority
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/response-model.md

page_content:
[Context: Response Model - Return Type > Return the same input data]

## Return the same input data
Here we are declaring a [CODE: UserIn] model, it will contain a plaintext password:
```python
class UserIn(BaseModel):
username: str
password: str
email: EmailStr
```
/// info
To use [CODE: EmailStr], first install [[CODE: email-validator]](https://github.com/JoshData/python-email-validator).
Make sure you create a virtual environment, activate it, and then install it, for example:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: $ pip install email-validator...]
or with:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]1: $ pip install "pydantic[email]"...]
///
And we are using this model to declare our input and the same model to declare our output:
```python
async def create_user(user: UserIn) -> UserIn:
return user
```
Now, whenever a browser is creating a user with a password, the API will return the same password in the response.
In this case, it might not be a problem, because it's the same user sending the password.
But if we use the same model for another [ITALIC:path operation], we could be sending our user's passwords to every client.
/// danger
Never store the plain password of a user or send it in a response like this, unless you know all the caveats and you know what you are doing.
///

metadata.breadcrumb: Response Model - Return Type > Return the same input data
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/response-model.md

page_content:
[Context: Response Model - Return Type > Return the same input data]

## Return the same input data
Here we are declaring a [CODE: UserIn] model, it will contain a plaintext password:
```python
class UserIn(BaseModel):
username: str
password: str
email: EmailStr
```
/// info
To use [CODE: EmailStr], first install [[CODE: email-validator]](https://github.com/JoshData/python-email-validator).
Make sure you create a virtual environment, activate it, and then install it, for example:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: $ pip install email-validator...]
or with:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]1: $ pip install "pydantic[email]"...]
///
And we are using this model to declare our input and the same model to declare our output:
```python
async def create_user(user: UserIn) -> UserIn:
return user
```
Now, whenever a browser is creating a user with a password, the API will return the same password in the response.
In this case, it might not be a problem, because it's the same user sending the password.
But if we use the same model for another [ITALIC:path operation], we could be sending our user's passwords to every client.
/// danger
Never store the plain password of a user or send it in a response like this, unless you know all the caveats and you know what you are doing.
///

metadata.breadcrumb: Response Model - Return Type > Return the same input data
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/response-model.md

page_content:
[Context: Response Model - Return Type > Add an output model]

## Add an output model
We can instead create an input model with the plaintext password and an output model without it:
```python
class UserIn(BaseModel):
username: str
password: str
email: EmailStr
class UserOut(BaseModel):
username: str
```
Here, even though our [ITALIC:path operation function] is returning the same input user that contains the password:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: return user...]
...we declared the [CODE: response[ITALIC:model] to be our model [CODE: UserOut], that doesn't include the password:
```python
@app.post("/user/", response]model=UserOut)
async def create_user(user: UserIn) -> Any:
```
So, [BOLD:FastAPI] will take care of filtering out all the data that is not declared in the output model (using Pydantic).

metadata.breadcrumb: Response Model - Return Type > Add an output model
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/response-model.md

page_content:
[Context: Response Model - Return Type > Add an output model > `response_model` or Return Type]

### [CODE: response[ITALIC:model] or Return Type
In this case, because the two models are different, if we annotated the function return type as [CODE: UserOut], the editor and tools would complain that we are returning an invalid type, as those are different classes.
That's why in this example we have to declare it in the [CODE: response]model] parameter.
...but continue reading below to see how to overcome that.

metadata.breadcrumb: Response Model - Return Type > Add an output model > `response_model` or Return Type
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/response-model.md

page_content:
[Context: Response Model - Return Type > Return Type and Data Filtering]

## Return Type and Data Filtering
Let's continue from the previous example. We wanted to [BOLD:annotate the function with one type], but we wanted to be able to return from the function something that actually includes [BOLD:more data].
We want FastAPI to keep [BOLD:filtering] the data using the response model. So that even though the function returns more data, the response will only include the fields declared in the response model.
In the previous example, because the classes were different, we had to use the [CODE: response[ITALIC:model] parameter. But that also means that we don't get the support from the editor and tools checking the function return type.
But in most of the cases where we need to do something like this, we want the model just to [BOLD:filter/remove] some of the data as in this example.
And in those cases, we can use classes and inheritance to take advantage of function [BOLD:type annotations] to get better support in the editor and tools, and still get the FastAPI [BOLD:data filtering].
```python
class BaseUser(BaseModel):
username: str
email: EmailStr
full]name: str | None = None
class UserIn(BaseUser):
password: str
async def create_user(user: UserIn) -> BaseUser:
return user
```
With this, we get tooling support, from editors and mypy as this code is correct in terms of types, but we also get the data filtering from FastAPI.
How does this work? Let's check that out. 🤓

metadata.breadcrumb: Response Model - Return Type > Return Type and Data Filtering
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/response-model.md

page_content:
[Context: Response Model - Return Type > Return Type and Data Filtering]

## Return Type and Data Filtering
Let's continue from the previous example. We wanted to [BOLD:annotate the function with one type], but we wanted to be able to return from the function something that actually includes [BOLD:more data].
We want FastAPI to keep [BOLD:filtering] the data using the response model. So that even though the function returns more data, the response will only include the fields declared in the response model.
In the previous example, because the classes were different, we had to use the [CODE: response[ITALIC:model] parameter. But that also means that we don't get the support from the editor and tools checking the function return type.
But in most of the cases where we need to do something like this, we want the model just to [BOLD:filter/remove] some of the data as in this example.
And in those cases, we can use classes and inheritance to take advantage of function [BOLD:type annotations] to get better support in the editor and tools, and still get the FastAPI [BOLD:data filtering].
```python
class BaseUser(BaseModel):
username: str
email: EmailStr
full]name: str | None = None
class UserIn(BaseUser):
password: str
async def create_user(user: UserIn) -> BaseUser:
return user
```
With this, we get tooling support, from editors and mypy as this code is correct in terms of types, but we also get the data filtering from FastAPI.
How does this work? Let's check that out. 🤓

metadata.breadcrumb: Response Model - Return Type > Return Type and Data Filtering
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/response-model.md

page_content:
[Context: Response Model - Return Type > Return Type and Data Filtering > Type Annotations and Tooling]

### Type Annotations and Tooling
First let's see how editors, mypy and other tools would see this.
[CODE: BaseUser] has the base fields. Then [CODE: UserIn] inherits from [CODE: BaseUser] and adds the [CODE: password] field, so, it will include all the fields from both models.
We annotate the function return type as [CODE: BaseUser], but we are actually returning a [CODE: UserIn] instance.
The editor, mypy, and other tools won't complain about this because, in typing terms, [CODE: UserIn] is a subclass of [CODE: BaseUser], which means it's a [ITALIC:valid] type when what is expected is anything that is a [CODE: BaseUser].

metadata.breadcrumb: Response Model - Return Type > Return Type and Data Filtering > Type Annotations and Tooling
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/response-model.md

page_content:
[Context: Response Model - Return Type > Return Type and Data Filtering > FastAPI Data Filtering]

### FastAPI Data Filtering
Now, for FastAPI, it will see the return type and make sure that what you return includes [BOLD:only] the fields that are declared in the type.
FastAPI does several things internally with Pydantic to make sure that those same rules of class inheritance are not used for the returned data filtering, otherwise you could end up returning much more data than what you expected.
This way, you can get the best of both worlds: type annotations with [BOLD:tooling support] and [BOLD:data filtering].

metadata.breadcrumb: Response Model - Return Type > Return Type and Data Filtering > FastAPI Data Filtering
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/response-model.md

page_content:
[Context: Response Model - Return Type > See it in the docs]

## See it in the docs
When you see the automatic docs, you can check that the input model and output model will both have their own JSON Schema:

And both models will be used for the interactive API documentation:

metadata.breadcrumb: Response Model - Return Type > See it in the docs
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/response-model.md

page_content:
[Context: Response Model - Return Type > Other Return Type Annotations]

## Other Return Type Annotations
There might be cases where you return something that is not a valid Pydantic field and you annotate it in the function, only to get the support provided by tooling (the editor, mypy, etc).

metadata.breadcrumb: Response Model - Return Type > Other Return Type Annotations
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/response-model.md

page_content:
[Context: Response Model - Return Type > Other Return Type Annotations > Return a Response Directly]

### Return a Response Directly
The most common case would be returning a Response directly as explained later in the advanced docs.
```python
async def get_portal(teleport: bool = False) -> Response:
if teleport:
return RedirectResponse(url="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dQw4w9WgXcQ")
return JSONResponse(content={"message": "Here's your interdimensional portal."})
```
This simple case is handled automatically by FastAPI because the return type annotation is the class (or a subclass of) [CODE: Response].
And tools will also be happy because both [CODE: RedirectResponse] and [CODE: JSONResponse] are subclasses of [CODE: Response], so the type annotation is correct.

metadata.breadcrumb: Response Model - Return Type > Other Return Type Annotations > Return a Response Directly
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/response-model.md

page_content:
[Context: Response Model - Return Type > Other Return Type Annotations > Annotate a Response Subclass]

### Annotate a Response Subclass
You can also use a subclass of [CODE: Response] in the type annotation:
```python
async def get_teleport() -> RedirectResponse:
return RedirectResponse(url="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dQw4w9WgXcQ")
```
This will also work because [CODE: RedirectResponse] is a subclass of [CODE: Response], and FastAPI will automatically handle this simple case.

metadata.breadcrumb: Response Model - Return Type > Other Return Type Annotations > Annotate a Response Subclass
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/response-model.md

page_content:
[Context: Response Model - Return Type > Other Return Type Annotations > Invalid Return Type Annotations]

### Invalid Return Type Annotations
But when you return some other arbitrary object that is not a valid Pydantic type (e.g. a database object) and you annotate it like that in the function, FastAPI will try to create a Pydantic response model from that type annotation, and will fail.
The same would happen if you had something like a union between different types where one or more of them are not valid Pydantic types, for example this would fail :
```python
async def get_portal(teleport: bool = False) -> Response | dict:
if teleport:
```
...this fails because the type annotation is not a Pydantic type and is not just a single [CODE: Response] class or subclass, it's a union (any of the two) between a [CODE: Response] and a [CODE: dict].

metadata.breadcrumb: Response Model - Return Type > Other Return Type Annotations > Invalid Return Type Annotations
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/response-model.md

page_content:
[Context: Response Model - Return Type > Other Return Type Annotations > Disable Response Model]

### Disable Response Model
Continuing from the example above, you might not want to have the default data validation, documentation, filtering, etc. that is performed by FastAPI.
But you might want to still keep the return type annotation in the function to get the support from tools like editors and type checkers (e.g. mypy).
In this case, you can disable the response model generation by setting [CODE: response[ITALIC:model=None]:
```python
@app.get("/portal", response]model=None)
async def get_portal(teleport: bool = False) -> Response | dict:
```
This will make FastAPI skip the response model generation and that way you can have any return type annotations you need without it affecting your FastAPI application. 🤓

metadata.breadcrumb: Response Model - Return Type > Other Return Type Annotations > Disable Response Model
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/response-model.md

page_content:
[Context: Response Model - Return Type > Response Model encoding parameters]

## Response Model encoding parameters
Your response model could have default values, like:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: description: str | None = None price: float tax: f...]
[ITALIC: [CODE: description: Union[str, None] = None] (or [CODE: str | None = None] in Python 3.10) has a default of [CODE: None].
] [CODE: tax: float = 10.5] has a default of [CODE: 10.5].
- [CODE: tags: List[str] = []] has a default of an empty list: [CODE: []].
but you might want to omit them from the result if they were not actually stored.
For example, if you have models with many optional attributes in a NoSQL database, but you don't want to send very long JSON responses full of default values.

metadata.breadcrumb: Response Model - Return Type > Response Model encoding parameters
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/response-model.md

page_content:
[Context: Response Model - Return Type > Response Model encoding parameters > Use the `response_model_exclude_unset` parameter]

### Use the [CODE: response[ITALIC:model]exclude[ITALIC:unset] parameter
You can set the [ITALIC:path operation decorator] parameter [CODE: response]model[ITALIC:exclude]unset=True]:
```python
@app.get("/items/{item[ITALIC:id}", response]model=Item, response[ITALIC:model]exclude[ITALIC:unset=True)
async def read]item(item[ITALIC:id: str):
```
and those default values won't be included in the response, only the values actually set.
So, if you send a request to that [ITALIC:path operation] for the item with ID [CODE: foo], the response (not including default values) will be:
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:0: { "name": "Foo", "price": 50.2 }...]
/// info
You can also use:
[ITALIC: [CODE: response]model[ITALIC:exclude]defaults=True]
] [CODE: response[ITALIC:model]exclude[ITALIC:none=True]
as described in the Pydantic docs for [CODE: exclude]defaults] and [CODE: exclude[ITALIC:none].
///
#### Data with values for fields with defaults
But if your data has values for the model's fields with default values, like the item with ID [CODE: bar]:
```Python hl]lines="3 5"
{
"name": "Bar",
"description": "The bartenders",
"price": 62,
"tax": 20.2
}
```
they will be included in the response.
#### Data with the same values as the defaults
If the data has the same values as the default ones, like the item with ID [CODE: baz]:
```Python hl_lines="3 5-6"
{
"name": "Baz",
"description": None,
"price": 50.2,
"tax": 10.5,
"tags": []
}
```
FastAPI is smart enough (actually, Pydantic is smart enough) to realize that, even though [CODE: description], [CODE: tax], and [CODE: tags] have the same values as the defaults, they were set explicitly (instead of taken from the defaults).
So, they will be included in the JSON response.
/// tip
Notice that the default values can be anything, not only [CODE: None].
They can be a list ([CODE: []]), a [CODE: float] of [CODE: 10.5], etc.
///

metadata.breadcrumb: Response Model - Return Type > Response Model encoding parameters > Use the `response_model_exclude_unset` parameter
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/response-model.md

page_content:
[Context: Response Model - Return Type > Response Model encoding parameters > `response_model_include` and `response_model_exclude`]

### [CODE: response[ITALIC:model]include] and [CODE: response[ITALIC:model]exclude]
You can also use the [ITALIC:path operation decorator] parameters [CODE: response[ITALIC:model]include] and [CODE: response[ITALIC:model]exclude].
They take a [CODE: set] of [CODE: str] with the name of the attributes to include (omitting the rest) or to exclude (including the rest).
This can be used as a quick shortcut if you have only one Pydantic model and want to remove some data from the output.
/// tip
But it is still recommended to use the ideas above, using multiple classes, instead of these parameters.
This is because the JSON Schema generated in your app's OpenAPI (and the docs) will still be the one for the complete model, even if you use [CODE: response[ITALIC:model]include] or [CODE: response[ITALIC:model]exclude] to omit some attributes.
This also applies to [CODE: response[ITALIC:model]by[ITALIC:alias] that works similarly.
///
```python
response]model[ITALIC:include={"name", "description"},
)
@app.get("/items/{item]id}/public", response[ITALIC:model=Item, response]model[ITALIC:exclude={"tax"})
async def read]item[ITALIC:public]data(item[ITALIC:id: str):
```
/// tip
The syntax [CODE: {"name", "description"}] creates a [CODE: set] with those two values.
It is equivalent to [CODE: set(["name", "description"])].
///
#### Using [CODE: list]s instead of [CODE: set]s
If you forget to use a [CODE: set] and use a [CODE: list] or [CODE: tuple] instead, FastAPI will still convert it to a [CODE: set] and it will work correctly:
```python
response]model[ITALIC:include=["name", "description"],
)
@app.get("/items/{item]id}/public", response[ITALIC:model=Item, response]model[ITALIC:exclude=["tax"])
async def read]item[ITALIC:public]data(item_id: str):
```

metadata.breadcrumb: Response Model - Return Type > Response Model encoding parameters > `response_model_include` and `response_model_exclude`

page_content:
[Context: Response Model - Return Type > Recap]

## Recap
Use the [ITALIC:path operation decorator's] parameter [CODE: response[ITALIC:model] to define response models and especially to ensure private data is filtered out.
Use [CODE: response]model[ITALIC:exclude]unset] to return only the values explicitly set.

page_content:
[Context: Response Status Code > Shortcut to remember the names]

## Shortcut to remember the names
Let's see the previous example again:
```python
@app.post("/items/", status[ITALIC:code=201)
async def create]item(name: str):
```
[CODE: 201] is the status code for "Created".
But you don't have to memorize what each of these codes mean.
You can use the convenience variables from [CODE: fastapi.status].
```python
from fastapi import FastAPI, status

@app.post("/items/", status[ITALIC:code=status.HTTP]201[ITALIC:CREATED)
async def create]item(name: str):
```
They are just a convenience, they hold the same number, but that way you can use the editor's autocomplete to find them:

/// note | Technical Details
You could also use [CODE: from starlette import status].
[BOLD:FastAPI] provides the same [CODE: starlette.status] as [CODE: fastapi.status] just as a convenience for you, the developer. But it comes directly from Starlette.
///

metadata.breadcrumb: Response Status Code > Shortcut to remember the names
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/response-status-code.md

page_content:
[Context: Declare Request Example Data]

# Declare Request Example Data
You can declare examples of the data your app can receive.
Here are several ways to do it.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/schema-extra-example.md

page_content:
[Context: Declare Request Example Data > Extra JSON Schema data in Pydantic models]

## Extra JSON Schema data in Pydantic models
You can declare [CODE: examples] for a Pydantic model that will be added to the generated JSON Schema.
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: model[ITALIC:config = { "json]schema[ITALIC:extra": { "examples"...]
That extra info will be added as-is to the output [BOLD:JSON Schema] for that model, and it will be used in the API docs.
You can use the attribute [CODE: model]config] that takes a [CODE: dict] as described in Pydantic's docs: Configuration.
You can set [CODE: "json[ITALIC:schema]extra"] with a [CODE: dict] containing any additional data you would like to show up in the generated JSON Schema, including [CODE: examples].
/// tip
You could use the same technique to extend the JSON Schema and add your own custom extra info.
For example you could use it to add metadata for a frontend user interface, etc.
///
/// info
OpenAPI 3.1.0 (used since FastAPI 0.99.0) added support for [CODE: examples], which is part of the [BOLD:JSON Schema] standard.
Before that, it only supported the keyword [CODE: example] with a single example. That is still supported by OpenAPI 3.1.0, but is deprecated and is not part of the JSON Schema standard. So you are encouraged to migrate [CODE: example] to [CODE: examples]. 🤓
You can read more at the end of this page.
///

hierarchy: Extra JSON Schema data in Pydantic models
metadata.breadcrumb: Declare Request Example Data > Extra JSON Schema data in Pydantic models
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/schema-extra-example.md

You can set [CODE: "json[ITALIC:schema]extra"] with a [CODE: dict] containing any additional data you would like to show up in the generated JSON Schema, including [CODE: examples].

Before that, it only supported the keyword [CODE: example] with a single example. That is still supported by OpenAPI 3.1.0, but is deprecated and is not part of the JSON Schema standard. So you are encouraged to migrate [CODE: example] to [CODE: examples]. 🤓

page_content:
[Context: Declare Request Example Data > `Field` additional arguments]

## [CODE: Field] additional arguments
When using [CODE: Field()] with Pydantic models, you can also declare additional [CODE: examples]:
```python
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field

name: str = Field(examples=["Foo"])
description: str | None = Field(default=None, examples=["A very nice Item"])
price: float = Field(examples=[35.4])
tax: float | None = Field(default=None, examples=[3.2])
```

metadata.breadcrumb: Declare Request Example Data > `Field` additional arguments

page_content:
[Context: Declare Request Example Data > `examples` in JSON Schema - OpenAPI]

## [CODE: examples] in JSON Schema - OpenAPI
When using any of:
[ITALIC: [CODE: Path()]
] [CODE: Query()]
[ITALIC: [CODE: Header()]
] [CODE: Cookie()]
[ITALIC: [CODE: Body()]
] [CODE: Form()]
- [CODE: File()]
you can also declare a group of [CODE: examples] with additional information that will be added to their [BOLD:JSON Schemas] inside of [BOLD:OpenAPI].

[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: examples=[ { "name": "Foo", "description": "A very...]

page_content:
[Context: Declare Request Example Data > `examples` in JSON Schema - OpenAPI > `Body` with multiple `examples`]

### [CODE: Body] with multiple [CODE: examples]
You can of course also pass multiple [CODE: examples]:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: examples=[ { "name": "Foo", "description": "A very...]
When you do this, the examples will be part of the internal [BOLD:JSON Schema] for that body data.
Nevertheless, at the time of writing this, Swagger UI, the tool in charge of showing the docs UI, doesn't support showing multiple examples for the data in [BOLD:JSON Schema]. But read below for a workaround.

metadata.breadcrumb: Declare Request Example Data > `examples` in JSON Schema - OpenAPI > `Body` with multiple `examples`
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/schema-extra-example.md

page_content:
[Context: Declare Request Example Data > `examples` in JSON Schema - OpenAPI > OpenAPI-specific `examples`]

### OpenAPI-specific [CODE: examples]
Since before [BOLD:JSON Schema] supported [CODE: examples] OpenAPI had support for a different field also called [CODE: examples].
This [BOLD:OpenAPI-specific] [CODE: examples] goes in another section in the OpenAPI specification. It goes in the *[ITALIC:details for each ]path operation[ITALIC:[BOLD:, not inside each JSON Schema.
And Swagger UI has supported this particular [CODE: examples] field for a while. So, you can use it to ]show[BOLD: different ]examples in the docs UI[BOLD:.
The shape of this OpenAPI-specific field [CODE: examples] is a [CODE: dict] with ]multiple examples[BOLD: (instead of a [CODE: list]), each with extra information that will be added to ]OpenAPI][ITALIC: too.
This doesn't go inside of each JSON Schema contained in OpenAPI, this goes outside, in the ]path operation* directly.

metadata.breadcrumb: Declare Request Example Data > `examples` in JSON Schema - OpenAPI > OpenAPI-specific `examples`
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/schema-extra-example.md

page_content:
[Context: Declare Request Example Data > `examples` in JSON Schema - OpenAPI > Using the `openapi_examples` Parameter]

### Using the [CODE: openapi[ITALIC:examples] Parameter
You can declare the OpenAPI-specific [CODE: examples] in FastAPI with the parameter [CODE: openapi]examples] for:
[ITALIC: [CODE: Path()]
] [CODE: Query()]
[ITALIC: [CODE: Header()]
] [CODE: Cookie()]
[ITALIC: [CODE: Body()]
] [CODE: Form()]
[ITALIC: [CODE: File()]
The keys of the [CODE: dict] identify each example, and each value is another [CODE: dict].
Each specific example [CODE: dict] in the [CODE: examples] can contain:
] [CODE: summary]: Short description for the example.
[ITALIC: [CODE: description]: A long description that can contain Markdown text.
] [CODE: value]: This is the actual example shown, e.g. a [CODE: dict].
- [CODE: externalValue]: alternative to [CODE: value], a URL pointing to the example. Although this might not be supported by as many tools as [CODE: value].
You can use it like this:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: openapi_examples={ "normal": { "summary": "A norma...]

[ITALIC: [CODE: description]: A long description that can contain Markdown text.
] [CODE: value]: This is the actual example shown, e.g. a [CODE: dict].
- [CODE: externalValue]: alternative to [CODE: value], a URL pointing to the example. Although this might not be supported by as many tools as [CODE: value].
You can use it like this:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: openapi_examples={ "normal": { "summary": "A norma...]

page_content:
[Context: Declare Request Example Data > Technical Details > JSON Schema's `examples` field]

### JSON Schema's [CODE: examples] field
But then JSON Schema added an [[CODE: examples]](https://json-schema.org/draft/2019-09/json-schema-validation.html#rfc.section.9.5) field to a new version of the specification.
And then the new OpenAPI 3.1.0 was based on the latest version (JSON Schema 2020-12) that included this new field [CODE: examples].
And now this new [CODE: examples] field takes precedence over the old single (and custom) [CODE: example] field, that is now deprecated.
This new [CODE: examples] field in JSON Schema is [BOLD:just a [CODE: list]] of examples, not a dict with extra metadata as in the other places in OpenAPI (described above).
/// info
Even after OpenAPI 3.1.0 was released with this new simpler integration with JSON Schema, for a while, Swagger UI, the tool that provides the automatic docs, didn't support OpenAPI 3.1.0 (it does since version 5.0.0 ).
Because of that, versions of FastAPI previous to 0.99.0 still used versions of OpenAPI lower than 3.1.0.
///

metadata.breadcrumb: Declare Request Example Data > Technical Details > JSON Schema's `examples` field
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/schema-extra-example.md

page_content:
[Context: Declare Request Example Data > Technical Details > JSON Schema's `examples` field]

### JSON Schema's [CODE: examples] field
But then JSON Schema added an [[CODE: examples]](https://json-schema.org/draft/2019-09/json-schema-validation.html#rfc.section.9.5) field to a new version of the specification.
And then the new OpenAPI 3.1.0 was based on the latest version (JSON Schema 2020-12) that included this new field [CODE: examples].
And now this new [CODE: examples] field takes precedence over the old single (and custom) [CODE: example] field, that is now deprecated.
This new [CODE: examples] field in JSON Schema is [BOLD:just a [CODE: list]] of examples, not a dict with extra metadata as in the other places in OpenAPI (described above).
/// info
Even after OpenAPI 3.1.0 was released with this new simpler integration with JSON Schema, for a while, Swagger UI, the tool that provides the automatic docs, didn't support OpenAPI 3.1.0 (it does since version 5.0.0 ).
Because of that, versions of FastAPI previous to 0.99.0 still used versions of OpenAPI lower than 3.1.0.
///

metadata.breadcrumb: Declare Request Example Data > Technical Details > JSON Schema's `examples` field
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/schema-extra-example.md

page_content:
[Context: Declare Request Example Data > Technical Details > Pydantic and FastAPI `examples`]

### Pydantic and FastAPI [CODE: examples]
When you add [CODE: examples] inside a Pydantic model, using [CODE: schema_extra] or [CODE: Field(examples=["something"])] that example is added to the [BOLD:JSON Schema] for that Pydantic model.
And that [BOLD:JSON Schema] of the Pydantic model is included in the [BOLD:OpenAPI] of your API, and then it's used in the docs UI.
In versions of FastAPI before 0.99.0 (0.99.0 and above use the newer OpenAPI 3.1.0) when you used [CODE: example] or [CODE: examples] with any of the other utilities ([CODE: Query()], [CODE: Body()], etc.) those examples were not added to the JSON Schema that describes that data (not even to OpenAPI's own version of JSON Schema), they were added directly to the [ITALIC:path operation] declaration in OpenAPI (outside the parts of OpenAPI that use JSON Schema).
But now that FastAPI 0.99.0 and above uses OpenAPI 3.1.0, that uses JSON Schema 2020-12, and Swagger UI 5.0.0 and above, everything is more consistent and the examples are included in JSON Schema.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/schema-extra-example.md

In versions of FastAPI before 0.99.0 (0.99.0 and above use the newer OpenAPI 3.1.0) when you used [CODE: example] or [CODE: examples] with any of the other utilities ([CODE: Query()], [CODE: Body()], etc.) those examples were not added to the JSON Schema that describes that data (not even to OpenAPI's own version of JSON Schema), they were added directly to the [ITALIC:path operation] declaration in OpenAPI (outside the parts of OpenAPI that use JSON Schema).
But now that FastAPI 0.99.0 and above uses OpenAPI 3.1.0, that uses JSON Schema 2020-12, and Swagger UI 5.0.0 and above, everything is more consistent and the examples are included in JSON Schema.

metadata.breadcrumb: Declare Request Example Data > Technical Details > Pydantic and FastAPI `examples`

page_content:
[Context: Declare Request Example Data > Technical Details > Swagger UI and OpenAPI-specific `examples`]

### Swagger UI and OpenAPI-specific [CODE: examples]
Now, as Swagger UI didn't support multiple JSON Schema examples (as of 2023-08-26), users didn't have a way to show multiple examples in the docs.
To solve that, FastAPI [CODE: 0.103.0] [BOLD:added support] for declaring the same old [BOLD:OpenAPI-specific] [CODE: examples] field with the new parameter [CODE: openapi_examples]. 🤓

page_content:
[Context: Declare Request Example Data > Technical Details > Summary]

### Summary
I used to say I didn't like history that much... and look at me now giving "tech history" lessons.
In short, [BOLD:upgrade to FastAPI 0.99.0 or above], and things are much [BOLD:simpler, consistent, and intuitive], and you don't have to know all these historic details.

page_content:
[Context: Server-Sent Events (SSE)]

# Server-Sent Events (SSE)
You can stream data to the client using [BOLD:Server-Sent Events] (SSE).
This is similar to Stream JSON Lines, but uses the [CODE: text/event-stream] format, which is supported natively by browsers with the [[CODE: EventSource] API](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/EventSource).
/// info
Added in FastAPI 0.135.0.
///

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/server-sent-events.md

page_content:
[Context: Server-Sent Events (SSE) > What are Server-Sent Events?]

## What are Server-Sent Events?
SSE is a standard for streaming data from the server to the client over HTTP.
Each event is a small text block with "fields" like [CODE: data], [CODE: event], [CODE: id], and [CODE: retry], separated by blank lines.
It looks like this:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: data: {"name": "Portal Gun", "price": 999.99} data...]
SSE is commonly used for AI chat streaming, live notifications, logs and observability, and other cases where the server pushes updates to the client.
/// tip
If you want to stream binary data, for example video or audio, check the advanced guide: Stream Data.
///

metadata.breadcrumb: Server-Sent Events (SSE) > What are Server-Sent Events?
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/server-sent-events.md

page_content:
[Context: Server-Sent Events (SSE) > Stream SSE with FastAPI]

## Stream SSE with FastAPI
To stream SSE with FastAPI, use [CODE: yield] in your [ITALIC:path operation function] and set [CODE: response[ITALIC:class=EventSourceResponse].
Import [CODE: EventSourceResponse] from [CODE: fastapi.sse]:
```python
from fastapi.sse import EventSourceResponse
from pydantic import BaseModel
@app.get("/items/stream", response]class=EventSourceResponse)
async def sse[ITALIC:items() -> AsyncIterable[Item]:
```
Each yielded item is encoded as JSON and sent in the [CODE: data:] field of an SSE event.
If you declare the return type as [CODE: AsyncIterable[Item]], FastAPI will use it to [BOLD:validate], [BOLD:document], and [BOLD:serialize] the data using Pydantic.
```python
class Item(BaseModel):
name: str
description: str | None
async def sse]items() -> AsyncIterable[Item]:
for item in items:
```
/// tip
As Pydantic will serialize it in the [BOLD:Rust] side, you will get much higher [BOLD:performance] than if you don't declare a return type.
///

metadata.breadcrumb: Server-Sent Events (SSE) > Stream SSE with FastAPI
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/server-sent-events.md

page_content:
[Context: Server-Sent Events (SSE) > Stream SSE with FastAPI]

## Stream SSE with FastAPI
To stream SSE with FastAPI, use [CODE: yield] in your [ITALIC:path operation function] and set [CODE: response[ITALIC:class=EventSourceResponse].
Import [CODE: EventSourceResponse] from [CODE: fastapi.sse]:
```python
from fastapi.sse import EventSourceResponse
from pydantic import BaseModel
@app.get("/items/stream", response]class=EventSourceResponse)
async def sse[ITALIC:items() -> AsyncIterable[Item]:
```
Each yielded item is encoded as JSON and sent in the [CODE: data:] field of an SSE event.
If you declare the return type as [CODE: AsyncIterable[Item]], FastAPI will use it to [BOLD:validate], [BOLD:document], and [BOLD:serialize] the data using Pydantic.
```python
class Item(BaseModel):
name: str
description: str | None
async def sse]items() -> AsyncIterable[Item]:
for item in items:
```
/// tip
As Pydantic will serialize it in the [BOLD:Rust] side, you will get much higher [BOLD:performance] than if you don't declare a return type.
///

metadata.breadcrumb: Server-Sent Events (SSE) > Stream SSE with FastAPI
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/server-sent-events.md

page_content:
[Context: Server-Sent Events (SSE) > Stream SSE with FastAPI > Non-async *path operation functions*]

### Non-async [ITALIC:path operation functions]
You can also use regular [CODE: def] functions (without [CODE: async]), and use [CODE: yield] the same way.
FastAPI will make sure it's run correctly so that it doesn't block the event loop.
As in this case the function is not async, the right return type would be [CODE: Iterable[Item]]:
```python
def sse[ITALIC:items]no_async() -> Iterable[Item]:
for item in items:
```

metadata.breadcrumb: Server-Sent Events (SSE) > Stream SSE with FastAPI > Non-async *path operation functions*
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/server-sent-events.md

page_content:
[Context: Server-Sent Events (SSE) > Stream SSE with FastAPI > No Return Type]

### No Return Type
You can also omit the return type. FastAPI will use the [[CODE: jsonable[ITALIC:encoder]](./encoder.md) to convert the data and send it.
```python
async def sse]items[ITALIC:no]annotation():
for item in items:
```

metadata.breadcrumb: Server-Sent Events (SSE) > Stream SSE with FastAPI > No Return Type
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/server-sent-events.md

page_content:
[Context: Server-Sent Events (SSE) > `ServerSentEvent`]

## [CODE: ServerSentEvent]
If you need to set SSE fields like [CODE: event], [CODE: id], [CODE: retry], or [CODE: comment], you can yield [CODE: ServerSentEvent] objects instead of plain data.
Import [CODE: ServerSentEvent] from [CODE: fastapi.sse]:
```python
from fastapi.sse import EventSourceResponse, ServerSentEvent
from pydantic import BaseModel
yield ServerSentEvent(data=item, event="item_update", id=str(i + 1), retry=5000)
```
The [CODE: data] field is always encoded as JSON. You can pass any value that can be serialized as JSON, including Pydantic models.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/server-sent-events.md

page_content:
[Context: Server-Sent Events (SSE) > Raw Data]

## Raw Data
If you need to send data [BOLD:without] JSON encoding, use [CODE: raw[ITALIC:data] instead of [CODE: data].
This is useful for sending pre-formatted text, log lines, or special "sentinel" values like [CODE: [DONE]].
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:0: yield ServerSentEvent(raw]data=log[ITALIC:line)...]
/// note
[CODE: data] and [CODE: raw]data] are mutually exclusive. You can only set one of them on each [CODE: ServerSentEvent].
///

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/server-sent-events.md

page_content:
[Context: Server-Sent Events (SSE) > Resuming with `Last-Event-ID`]

## Resuming with [CODE: Last-Event-ID]
When a browser reconnects after a connection drop, it sends the last received [CODE: id] in the [CODE: Last-Event-ID] header.
You can read it as a header parameter and use it to resume the stream from where the client left off:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: last[ITALIC:event]id: Annotated[int | None, Header()] = N...]

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/server-sent-events.md

page_content:
[Context: Server-Sent Events (SSE) > SSE with POST]

## SSE with POST
SSE works with [BOLD:any HTTP method], not just [CODE: GET].
This is useful for protocols like MCP that stream SSE over [CODE: POST]:
```python
@app.post("/chat/stream", response[ITALIC:class=EventSourceResponse)
async def stream]chat(prompt: Prompt) -> AsyncIterable[ServerSentEvent]:
```

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/server-sent-events.md

page_content:
[Context: Server-Sent Events (SSE) > Technical Details]

## Technical Details
FastAPI implements some SSE best practices out of the box.
[ITALIC: Send a [BOLD:"keep alive" [CODE: ping] comment] every 15 seconds when there hasn't been any message, to prevent some proxies from closing the connection, as suggested in the HTML specification: Server-Sent Events.
] Set the [CODE: Cache-Control: no-cache] header to [BOLD:prevent caching] of the stream.
- Set a special header [CODE: X-Accel-Buffering: no] to [BOLD:prevent buffering] in some proxies like Nginx.
You don't have to do anything about it, it works out of the box. 🤓

page_content:
[Context: SQL (Relational) Databases > Install `SQLModel`]

## Install [CODE: SQLModel]
First, make sure you create your virtual environment, activate it, and then install [CODE: sqlmodel]:

[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: $ pip install sqlmodel ---> 100%...]

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/sql-databases.md

page_content:
[Context: SQL (Relational) Databases > Create the App with a Single Model]

## Create the App with a Single Model
We'll create the simplest first version of the app with a single [BOLD:SQLModel] model first.
Later we'll improve it increasing security and versatility with [BOLD:multiple models] below. 🤓

hierarchy: Create the App with a Single Model
metadata.breadcrumb: SQL (Relational) Databases > Create the App with a Single Model
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/sql-databases.md

page_content:
[Context: SQL (Relational) Databases > Create the App with a Single Model > Create Models]

### Create Models
Import [CODE: SQLModel] and create a database model:
```python
class Hero(SQLModel, table=True):
id: int | None = Field(default=None, primary[ITALIC:key=True)
name: str = Field(index=True)
age: int | None = Field(default=None, index=True)
secret]name: str
```
The [CODE: Hero] class is very similar to a Pydantic model (in fact, underneath, it actually [ITALIC:is a Pydantic model]).
There are a few differences:
[ITALIC: [CODE: table=True] tells SQLModel that this is a ]table model[ITALIC:, it should represent a [BOLD:table] in the SQL database, it's not just a ]data model[ITALIC: (as would be any other regular Pydantic class).
] [CODE: Field(primary_key=True)] tells SQLModel that the [CODE: id] is the [BOLD:primary key] in the SQL database (you can learn more about SQL primary keys in the SQLModel docs).
[BOLD:Note:] We use [CODE: int | None] for the primary key field so that in Python code we can [ITALIC:create an object without an [CODE: id]] ([CODE: id=None]), assuming the database will [ITALIC:generate it when saving]. SQLModel understands that the database will provide the [CODE: id] and [ITALIC:defines the column as a non-null [CODE: INTEGER]] in the database schema. See SQLModel docs on primary keys for details.
- [CODE: Field(index=True)] tells SQLModel that it should create a [BOLD:SQL index] for this column, that would allow faster lookups in the database when reading data filtered by this column.
SQLModel will know that something declared as [CODE: str] will be a SQL column of type [CODE: TEXT] (or [CODE: VARCHAR], depending on the database).

hierarchy: Create the App with a Single Model
metadata.breadcrumb: SQL (Relational) Databases > Create the App with a Single Model > Create Models

[Context: SQL (Relational) Databases > Create the App with a Single Model > Create Models]

### Create Models
Import [CODE: SQLModel] and create a database model:
```python
class Hero(SQLModel, table=True):
id: int | None = Field(default=None, primary[ITALIC:key=True)
name: str = Field(index=True)
age: int | None = Field(default=None, index=True)
secret]name: str
```
The [CODE: Hero] class is very similar to a Pydantic model (in fact, underneath, it actually [ITALIC:is a Pydantic model]).
There are a few differences:
[ITALIC: [CODE: table=True] tells SQLModel that this is a ]table model[ITALIC:, it should represent a [BOLD:table] in the SQL database, it's not just a ]data model[ITALIC: (as would be any other regular Pydantic class).
] [CODE: Field(primary_key=True)] tells SQLModel that the [CODE: id] is the [BOLD:primary key] in the SQL database (you can learn more about SQL primary keys in the SQLModel docs).
[BOLD:Note:] We use [CODE: int | None] for the primary key field so that in Python code we can [ITALIC:create an object without an [CODE: id]] ([CODE: id=None]), assuming the database will [ITALIC:generate it when saving]. SQLModel understands that the database will provide the [CODE: id] and [ITALIC:defines the column as a non-null [CODE: INTEGER]] in the database schema. See SQLModel docs on primary keys for details.
- [CODE: Field(index=True)] tells SQLModel that it should create a [BOLD:SQL index] for this column, that would allow faster lookups in the database when reading data filtered by this column.
SQLModel will know that something declared as [CODE: str] will be a SQL column of type [CODE: TEXT] (or [CODE: VARCHAR], depending on the database).

page_content:
[Context: SQL (Relational) Databases > Create the App with a Single Model > Create an Engine]

### Create an Engine
A SQLModel [CODE: engine] (underneath it's actually a SQLAlchemy [CODE: engine]) is what [BOLD:holds the connections] to the database.
You would have [BOLD:one single [CODE: engine] object] for all your code to connect to the same database.
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: sqlite[ITALIC:file]name = "database.db" sqlite[ITALIC:url = f"sq...]
Using [CODE: check]same[ITALIC:thread=False] allows FastAPI to use the same SQLite database in different threads. This is necessary as [BOLD:one single request] could use [BOLD:more than one thread] (for example in dependencies).
Don't worry, with the way the code is structured, we'll make sure we use *[ITALIC:a single SQLModel ]session[ITALIC: per request]* later, this is actually what the [CODE: check]same_thread] is trying to achieve.

hierarchy: Create the App with a Single Model
metadata.breadcrumb: SQL (Relational) Databases > Create the App with a Single Model > Create an Engine
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/sql-databases.md

page_content:
[Context: SQL (Relational) Databases > Create the App with a Single Model > Create the Tables]

### Create the Tables
We then add a function that uses [CODE: SQLModel.metadata.create[ITALIC:all(engine)] to [BOLD:create the tables] for all the [ITALIC:table models].
```python
def create]db[ITALIC:and]tables():
SQLModel.metadata.create_all(engine)
```

hierarchy: Create the App with a Single Model
metadata.breadcrumb: SQL (Relational) Databases > Create the App with a Single Model > Create the Tables
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/sql-databases.md

page_content:
[Context: SQL (Relational) Databases > Create the App with a Single Model > Create a Session Dependency]

### Create a Session Dependency
A [BOLD:[CODE: Session]] is what stores the [BOLD:objects in memory] and keeps track of any changes needed in the data, then it [BOLD:uses the [CODE: engine]] to communicate with the database.
We will create a FastAPI [BOLD:dependency] with [CODE: yield] that will provide a new [CODE: Session] for each request. This is what ensures that we use a single session per request. 🤓
Then we create an [CODE: Annotated] dependency [CODE: SessionDep] to simplify the rest of the code that will use this dependency.
```python
def get[ITALIC:session():
with Session(engine) as session:
yield session
SessionDep = Annotated[Session, Depends(get]session)]

```

hierarchy: Create the App with a Single Model
metadata.breadcrumb: SQL (Relational) Databases > Create the App with a Single Model > Create a Session Dependency

page_content:
[Context: SQL (Relational) Databases > Create the App with a Single Model > Create a Hero]

### Create a Hero
Because each SQLModel model is also a Pydantic model, you can use it in the same [BOLD:type annotations] that you could use Pydantic models.
For example, if you declare a parameter of type [CODE: Hero], it will be read from the [BOLD:JSON body].
The same way, you can declare it as the function's [BOLD:return type], and then the shape of the data will show up in the automatic API docs UI.
```python
@app.post("/heroes/")
def create_hero(hero: Hero, session: SessionDep) -> Hero:
session.add(hero)
session.commit()
session.refresh(hero)
return hero
```
Here we use the [CODE: SessionDep] dependency (a [CODE: Session]) to add the new [CODE: Hero] to the [CODE: Session] instance, commit the changes to the database, refresh the data in the [CODE: hero], and then return it.

[Context: SQL (Relational) Databases > Update the App with Multiple Models]

## Update the App with Multiple Models
Now let's [BOLD:refactor] this app a bit to increase [BOLD:security] and [BOLD:versatility].
If you check the previous app, in the UI you can see that, up to now, it lets the client decide the [CODE: id] of the [CODE: Hero] to create.
We shouldn't let that happen, they could overwrite an [CODE: id] we already have assigned in the DB. Deciding the [CODE: id] should be done by the [BOLD:backend] or the [BOLD:database], [BOLD:not by the client].
Additionally, we create a [CODE: secret_name] for the hero, but so far, we are returning it everywhere, that's not very [BOLD:secret]...
We'll fix these things by adding a few [BOLD:extra models]. Here's where SQLModel will shine.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/sql-databases.md

page_content:
[Context: SQL (Relational) Databases > Update the App with Multiple Models > Create Multiple Models]

### Create Multiple Models
In [BOLD:SQLModel], any model class that has [CODE: table=True] is a [BOLD:table model].
And any model class that doesn't have [CODE: table=True] is a [BOLD:data model], these ones are actually just Pydantic models (with a couple of small extra features). 🤓
With SQLModel, we can use [BOLD:inheritance] to [BOLD:avoid duplicating] all the fields in all the cases.
#### [CODE: HeroBase] - the base class
Let's start with a [CODE: HeroBase] model that has all the [BOLD:fields that are shared] by all the models:
[ITALIC: [CODE: name]
] [CODE: age]
```python
class HeroBase(SQLModel):
name: str = Field(index=True)
age: int | None = Field(default=None, index=True)
```
#### [CODE: Hero] - the [ITALIC:table model]
Then let's create [CODE: Hero], the actual [ITALIC:table model], with the [BOLD:extra fields] that are not always in the other models:
[ITALIC: [CODE: id]
] [CODE: secret[ITALIC:name]
Because [CODE: Hero] inherits form [CODE: HeroBase], it [BOLD:also] has the [BOLD:fields] declared in [CODE: HeroBase], so all the fields for [CODE: Hero] are:
[ITALIC: [CODE: id]
] [CODE: name]
[ITALIC: [CODE: age]
] [CODE: secret]name]
```python
class Hero(HeroBase, table=True):
id: int | None = Field(default=None, primary[ITALIC:key=True)
secret]name: str
```
#### [CODE: HeroPublic] - the public [ITALIC:data model]
Next, we create a [CODE: HeroPublic] model, this is the one that will be [BOLD:returned] to the clients of the API.
It has the same fields as [CODE: HeroBase], so it won't include [CODE: secret[ITALIC:name].
Finally, the identity of our heroes is protected! 🥷
It also re-declares [CODE: id: int]. By doing this, we are making a [BOLD:contract] with the API clients, so that they can always expect the [CODE: id] to be there and to be an [CODE: int] (it will never be [CODE: None]).
/// tip
Having the return model ensure that a value is always available and always [CODE: int] (not [CODE: None]) is very useful for the API clients, they can write much simpler code having this certainty.
Also, [BOLD:automatically generated clients] will have simpler interfaces, so that the developers communicating with your API can have a much better time working with your API.
///
All the fields in [CODE: HeroPublic] are the same as in [CODE: HeroBase], with [CODE: id] declared as [CODE: int] (not [CODE: None]):
[ITALIC: [CODE: id]
] [CODE: name]
[ITALIC: [CODE: age]
```python
class HeroPublic(HeroBase):
id: int
```
#### [CODE: HeroCreate] - the ]data model[ITALIC: to create a hero
Now we create a [CODE: HeroCreate] model, this is the one that will [BOLD:validate] the data from the clients.
It has the same fields as [CODE: HeroBase], and it also has [CODE: secret]name].
Now, when the clients [BOLD:create a new hero], they will send the [CODE: secret[ITALIC:name], it will be stored in the database, but those secret names won't be returned in the API to the clients.
/// tip
This is how you would handle [BOLD:passwords]. Receive them, but don't return them in the API.
You would also [BOLD:hash] the values of the passwords before storing them, [BOLD:never store them in plain text].
///
The fields of [CODE: HeroCreate] are:
] [CODE: name]
[ITALIC: [CODE: age]
] [CODE: secret]name]
```python
class HeroCreate(HeroBase):
secret[ITALIC:name: str
```
#### [CODE: HeroUpdate] - the [ITALIC:data model] to update a hero
We didn't have a way to [BOLD:update a hero] in the previous version of the app, but now with [BOLD:multiple models], we can do it.
The [CODE: HeroUpdate] [ITALIC:data model] is somewhat special, it has [BOLD:all the same fields] that would be needed to create a new hero, but all the fields are [BOLD:optional] (they all have a default value). This way, when you update a hero, you can send just the fields that you want to update.
Because all the [BOLD:fields actually change] (the type now includes [CODE: None] and they now have a default value of [CODE: None]), we need to [BOLD:re-declare] them.
We don't really need to inherit from [CODE: HeroBase] because we are re-declaring all the fields. I'll leave it inheriting just for consistency, but this is not necessary. It's more a matter of personal taste. 🤷
The fields of [CODE: HeroUpdate] are:
[ITALIC: [CODE: name]
] [CODE: age]
- [CODE: secret]name]
```python
class HeroUpdate(HeroBase):
name: str | None = None
age: int | None = None
secret_name: str | None = None
```

] [CODE: secret]name]
```python
class Hero(HeroBase, table=True):
id: int | None = Field(default=None, primary[ITALIC:key=True)
secret]name: str
```
#### [CODE: HeroPublic] - the public [ITALIC:data model]
Next, we create a [CODE: HeroPublic] model, this is the one that will be [BOLD:returned] to the clients of the API.
It has the same fields as [CODE: HeroBase], so it won't include [CODE: secret[ITALIC:name].
Finally, the identity of our heroes is protected! 🥷
It also re-declares [CODE: id: int]. By doing this, we are making a [BOLD:contract] with the API clients, so that they can always expect the [CODE: id] to be there and to be an [CODE: int] (it will never be [CODE: None]).
/// tip
Having the return model ensure that a value is always available and always [CODE: int] (not [CODE: None]) is very useful for the API clients, they can write much simpler code having this certainty.
Also, [BOLD:automatically generated clients] will have simpler interfaces, so that the developers communicating with your API can have a much better time working with your API.
///
All the fields in [CODE: HeroPublic] are the same as in [CODE: HeroBase], with [CODE: id] declared as [CODE: int] (not [CODE: None]):
[ITALIC: [CODE: id]
] [CODE: name]
[ITALIC: [CODE: age]
```python
class HeroPublic(HeroBase):
id: int
```
#### [CODE: HeroCreate] - the ]data model[ITALIC: to create a hero
Now we create a [CODE: HeroCreate] model, this is the one that will [BOLD:validate] the data from the clients.
It has the same fields as [CODE: HeroBase], and it also has [CODE: secret]name].
Now, when the clients [BOLD:create a new hero], they will send the [CODE: secret[ITALIC:name], it will be stored in the database, but those secret names won't be returned in the API to the clients.
/// tip
This is how you would handle [BOLD:passwords]. Receive them, but don't return them in the API.
You would also [BOLD:hash] the values of the passwords before storing them, [BOLD:never store them in plain text].
///
The fields of [CODE: HeroCreate] are:
] [CODE: name]
[ITALIC: [CODE: age]
] [CODE: secret]name]
```python
class HeroCreate(HeroBase):
secret[ITALIC:name: str
```
#### [CODE: HeroUpdate] - the [ITALIC:data model] to update a hero
We didn't have a way to [BOLD:update a hero] in the previous version of the app, but now with [BOLD:multiple models], we can do it.
The [CODE: HeroUpdate] [ITALIC:data model] is somewhat special, it has [BOLD:all the same fields] that would be needed to create a new hero, but all the fields are [BOLD:optional] (they all have a default value). This way, when you update a hero, you can send just the fields that you want to update.
Because all the [BOLD:fields actually change] (the type now includes [CODE: None] and they now have a default value of [CODE: None]), we need to [BOLD:re-declare] them.
We don't really need to inherit from [CODE: HeroBase] because we are re-declaring all the fields. I'll leave it inheriting just for consistency, but this is not necessary. It's more a matter of personal taste. 🤷
The fields of [CODE: HeroUpdate] are:
[ITALIC: [CODE: name]
] [CODE: age]
- [CODE: secret]name]
```python
class HeroUpdate(HeroBase):
name: str | None = None
age: int | None = None
secret_name: str | None = None
```

page_content:
[Context: SQL (Relational) Databases > Update the App with Multiple Models > Create with `HeroCreate` and return a `HeroPublic`]

### Create with [CODE: HeroCreate] and return a [CODE: HeroPublic]
Now that we have [BOLD:multiple models], we can update the parts of the app that use them.
We receive in the request a [CODE: HeroCreate] [ITALIC:data model], and from it, we create a [CODE: Hero] [ITALIC:table model].
This new [ITALIC:table model] [CODE: Hero] will have the fields sent by the client, and will also have an [CODE: id] generated by the database.
Then we return the same [ITALIC:table model] [CODE: Hero] as is from the function. But as we declare the [CODE: response[ITALIC:model] with the [CODE: HeroPublic] [ITALIC:data model], [BOLD:FastAPI] will use [CODE: HeroPublic] to validate and serialize the data.
```python
@app.post("/heroes/", response]model=HeroPublic)
def create[ITALIC:hero(hero: HeroCreate, session: SessionDep):
db]hero = Hero.model[ITALIC:validate(hero)
```
/// tip
Now we use [CODE: response]model=HeroPublic] instead of the [BOLD:return type annotation] [CODE: -> HeroPublic] because the value that we are returning is actually [ITALIC:not] a [CODE: HeroPublic].
If we had declared [CODE: -> HeroPublic], your editor and linter would complain (rightfully so) that you are returning a [CODE: Hero] instead of a [CODE: HeroPublic].
By declaring it in [CODE: response_model] we are telling [BOLD:FastAPI] to do its thing, without interfering with the type annotations and the help from your editor and other tools.
///

### Create with [CODE: HeroCreate] and return a [CODE: HeroPublic]
Now that we have [BOLD:multiple models], we can update the parts of the app that use them.
We receive in the request a [CODE: HeroCreate] [ITALIC:data model], and from it, we create a [CODE: Hero] [ITALIC:table model].
This new [ITALIC:table model] [CODE: Hero] will have the fields sent by the client, and will also have an [CODE: id] generated by the database.
Then we return the same [ITALIC:table model] [CODE: Hero] as is from the function. But as we declare the [CODE: response[ITALIC:model] with the [CODE: HeroPublic] [ITALIC:data model], [BOLD:FastAPI] will use [CODE: HeroPublic] to validate and serialize the data.
```python
@app.post("/heroes/", response]model=HeroPublic)
def create[ITALIC:hero(hero: HeroCreate, session: SessionDep):
db]hero = Hero.model[ITALIC:validate(hero)
```
/// tip
Now we use [CODE: response]model=HeroPublic] instead of the [BOLD:return type annotation] [CODE: -> HeroPublic] because the value that we are returning is actually [ITALIC:not] a [CODE: HeroPublic].
If we had declared [CODE: -> HeroPublic], your editor and linter would complain (rightfully so) that you are returning a [CODE: Hero] instead of a [CODE: HeroPublic].
By declaring it in [CODE: response_model] we are telling [BOLD:FastAPI] to do its thing, without interfering with the type annotations and the help from your editor and other tools.
///

### Update a Hero with [CODE: HeroUpdate]
We can [BOLD:update a hero]. For this we use an HTTP [CODE: PATCH] operation.
And in the code, we get a [CODE: dict] with all the data sent by the client, [BOLD:only the data sent by the client], excluding any values that would be there just for being the default values. To do it we use [CODE: exclude[ITALIC:unset=True]. This is the main trick. 🪄
Then we use [CODE: hero]db.sqlmodel[ITALIC:update(hero]data)] to update the [CODE: hero[ITALIC:db] with the data from [CODE: hero]data].
```python
@app.patch("/heroes/{hero[ITALIC:id}", response]model=HeroPublic)
def update[ITALIC:hero(hero]id: int, hero: HeroUpdate, session: SessionDep):
hero[ITALIC:data = hero.model]dump(exclude[ITALIC:unset=True)
hero]db.sqlmodel[ITALIC:update(hero]data)
```

[Context: Static Files]

# Static Files
You can serve static files automatically from a directory using [CODE: StaticFiles].

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/static-files.md

page_content:
[Context: Static Files > Use `StaticFiles`]

## Use [CODE: StaticFiles]
[ITALIC: Import [CODE: StaticFiles].
] "Mount" a [CODE: StaticFiles()] instance in a specific path.
```python
from fastapi.staticfiles import StaticFiles

app.mount("/static", StaticFiles(directory="static"), name="static")
```
/// note | Technical Details
You could also use [CODE: from starlette.staticfiles import StaticFiles].
[BOLD:FastAPI] provides the same [CODE: starlette.staticfiles] as [CODE: fastapi.staticfiles] just as a convenience for you, the developer. But it actually comes directly from Starlette.
///

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/static-files.md

page_content:
[Context: Static Files > Use `StaticFiles` > What is "Mounting"]

### What is "Mounting"
"Mounting" means adding a complete "independent" application in a specific path, that then takes care of handling all the sub-paths.
This is different from using an [CODE: APIRouter] as a mounted application is completely independent. The OpenAPI and docs from your main application won't include anything from the mounted application, etc.
You can read more about this in the Advanced User Guide.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/static-files.md

page_content:
[Context: Static Files > Details]

## Details
The first [CODE: "/static"] refers to the sub-path this "sub-application" will be "mounted" on. So, any path that starts with [CODE: "/static"] will be handled by it.
The [CODE: directory="static"] refers to the name of the directory that contains your static files.
The [CODE: name="static"] gives it a name that can be used internally by [BOLD:FastAPI].
All these parameters can be different than "[CODE: static]", adjust them with the needs and specific details of your own application.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/static-files.md

page_content:
[Context: Static Files > More info]

## More info
For more details and options check Starlette's docs about Static Files.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/static-files.md
Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/stream-json-lines.md

page_content:
[Context: Stream JSON Lines > What is a Stream?]

## What is a Stream?
"[BOLD:Streaming]" data means that your app will start sending data items to the client without waiting for the entire sequence of items to be ready.
So, it will send the first item, the client will receive and start processing it, and you might still be producing the next item.
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: sequenceDiagram participant App participant Client...]
It could even be an infinite stream, where you keep sending data.

metadata.breadcrumb: Stream JSON Lines > What is a Stream?
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/stream-json-lines.md
Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/stream-json-lines.md
Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/stream-json-lines.md
Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/stream-json-lines.md

page_content:
[Context: Stream JSON Lines > Stream JSON Lines with FastAPI]

## Stream JSON Lines with FastAPI
To stream JSON Lines with FastAPI you can, instead of using [CODE: return] in your [ITALIC:path operation function], use [CODE: yield] to produce each item in turn.
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: yield item...]
If each JSON item you want to send back is of type [CODE: Item] (a Pydantic model) and it's an async function, you can declare the return type as [CODE: AsyncIterable[Item]]:
```python
class Item(BaseModel):
name: str
description: str | None
async def stream_items() -> AsyncIterable[Item]:
for item in items:
```
If you declare the return type, FastAPI will use it to [BOLD:validate] the data, [BOLD:document] it in OpenAPI, [BOLD:filter] it, and [BOLD:serialize] it using Pydantic.
/// tip
As Pydantic will serialize it in the [BOLD:Rust] side, you will get much higher [BOLD:performance] than if you don't declare a return type.
///

metadata.breadcrumb: Stream JSON Lines > Stream JSON Lines with FastAPI
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/stream-json-lines.md

page_content:
[Context: Stream JSON Lines > Stream JSON Lines with FastAPI > Non-async *path operation functions*]

### Non-async [ITALIC:path operation functions]
You can also use regular [CODE: def] functions (without [CODE: async]), and use [CODE: yield] the same way.
FastAPI will make sure it's run correctly so that it doesn't block the event loop.
As in this case the function is not async, the right return type would be [CODE: Iterable[Item]]:
```python
def stream[ITALIC:items]no_async() -> Iterable[Item]:
for item in items:
```

metadata.breadcrumb: Stream JSON Lines > Stream JSON Lines with FastAPI > Non-async *path operation functions*
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/stream-json-lines.md

page_content:
[Context: Stream JSON Lines > Stream JSON Lines with FastAPI > No Return Type]

### No Return Type
You can also omit the return type. FastAPI will then use the [[CODE: jsonable[ITALIC:encoder]](./encoder.md) to convert the data to something that can be serialized to JSON and then send it as JSON Lines.
```python
async def stream]items[ITALIC:no]annotation():
for item in items:
```

metadata.breadcrumb: Stream JSON Lines > Stream JSON Lines with FastAPI > No Return Type
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/stream-json-lines.md

page_content:
[Context: Stream JSON Lines > Server-Sent Events (SSE)]

## Server-Sent Events (SSE)
FastAPI also has first-class support for Server-Sent Events (SSE), which are quite similar but with a couple of extra details. You can learn about them in the next chapter: Server-Sent Events (SSE). 🤓
