# Testing
Thanks to Starlette, testing [BOLD:FastAPI] applications is easy and enjoyable.
It is based on HTTPX, which in turn is designed based on Requests, so it's very familiar and intuitive.
With it, you can use pytest directly with [BOLD:FastAPI].

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/testing.md
Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/testing.md
Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/testing.md

page_content:
[Context: Testing > Separating tests]

## Separating tests
In a real application, you probably would have your tests in a different file.
And your [BOLD:FastAPI] application might also be composed of several files/modules, etc.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/testing.md

page_content:
[Context: Testing > Separating tests > **FastAPI** app file]

### [BOLD:FastAPI] app file
Let's say you have a file structure as described in Bigger Applications:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: . app [BOLD:init].py main.py...]
In the file [CODE: main.py] you have your [BOLD:FastAPI] app:
{[ITALIC: ../../docs[ITALIC:src/app]testing/app[ITALIC:a]py310/main.py ]}

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/testing.md

page_content:
[Context: Testing > Separating tests > Testing file]

### Testing file
Then you could have a file [CODE: test[ITALIC:main.py] with your tests. It could live on the same Python package (the same directory with a [CODE: [BOLD:init].py] file):
``` hl]lines="5"
.
app
[BOLD:init].py
main.py
test_main.py
```
Because this file is in the same package, you can use relative imports to import the object [CODE: app] from the [CODE: main] module ([CODE: main.py]):
```python
from .main import app

```
...and have the code for the tests just like before.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/testing.md

[Context: Testing > Testing: extended example]

## Testing: extended example
Now let's extend this example and add more details to see how to test different parts.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/testing.md

page_content:
[Context: Testing > Testing: extended example > Extended **FastAPI** app file]

### Extended [BOLD:FastAPI] app file
Let's continue with the same file structure as before:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: . app [BOLD:init].py main.py te...]
Let's say that now the file [CODE: main.py] with your [BOLD:FastAPI] app has some other [BOLD:path operations].
It has a [CODE: GET] operation that could return an error.
It has a [CODE: POST] operation that could return several errors.
Both [ITALIC:path operations] require an [CODE: X-Token] header.
{[ITALIC: ../../docs[ITALIC:src/app]testing/app[ITALIC:b]an_py310/main.py ]}

metadata.breadcrumb: Testing > Testing: extended example > Extended **FastAPI** app file
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > tutorial/testing.md

page_content:
[Context: Testing > Testing: extended example > Extended testing file]

### Extended testing file
You could then update [CODE: test[ITALIC:main.py] with the extended tests:
{[ITALIC: ../../docs]src/app[ITALIC:testing/app]b[ITALIC:an]py310/test[ITALIC:main.py ]}
Whenever you need the client to pass information in the request and you don't know how to, you can search (Google) how to do it in [CODE: httpx], or even how to do it with [CODE: requests], as HTTPX's design is based on Requests' design.
Then you just do the same in your tests.
E.g.:
[ITALIC: To pass a ]path[ITALIC: or ]query[ITALIC: parameter, add it to the URL itself.
] To pass a JSON body, pass a Python object (e.g. a [CODE: dict]) to the parameter [CODE: json].
[ITALIC: If you need to send ]Form Data[ITALIC: instead of JSON, use the [CODE: data] parameter instead.
] To pass [ITALIC:headers], use a [CODE: dict] in the [CODE: headers] parameter.
[ITALIC: For ]cookies*, a [CODE: dict] in the [CODE: cookies] parameter.
For more information about how to pass data to the backend (using [CODE: httpx] or the [CODE: TestClient]) check the HTTPX documentation.
/// info
Note that the [CODE: TestClient] receives data that can be converted to JSON, not Pydantic models.
If you have a Pydantic model in your test and you want to send its data to the application during testing, you can use the [CODE: jsonable]encoder] described in JSON Compatible Encoder.
///

{[ITALIC: ../../docs]src/app[ITALIC:testing/app]b[ITALIC:an]py310/test[ITALIC:main.py ]}
Whenever you need the client to pass information in the request and you don't know how to, you can search (Google) how to do it in [CODE: httpx], or even how to do it with [CODE: requests], as HTTPX's design is based on Requests' design.
Then you just do the same in your tests.
E.g.:
[ITALIC: To pass a ]path[ITALIC: or ]query[ITALIC: parameter, add it to the URL itself.
] To pass a JSON body, pass a Python object (e.g. a [CODE: dict]) to the parameter [CODE: json].
[ITALIC: If you need to send ]Form Data[ITALIC: instead of JSON, use the [CODE: data] parameter instead.
] To pass [ITALIC:headers], use a [CODE: dict] in the [CODE: headers] parameter.
[ITALIC: For ]cookies*, a [CODE: dict] in the [CODE: cookies] parameter.
For more information about how to pass data to the backend (using [CODE: httpx] or the [CODE: TestClient]) check the HTTPX documentation.
/// info
Note that the [CODE: TestClient] receives data that can be converted to JSON, not Pydantic models.
If you have a Pydantic model in your test and you want to send its data to the application during testing, you can use the [CODE: jsonable]encoder] described in JSON Compatible Encoder.

page_content:
[Context: Classes as Dependencies]

# Classes as Dependencies
Before diving deeper into the [BOLD:Dependency Injection] system, let's upgrade the previous example.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Dependencies > tutorial/dependencies/classes-as-dependencies.md

page_content:
[Context: Classes as Dependencies > A `dict` from the previous example]

## A [CODE: dict] from the previous example
In the previous example, we were returning a [CODE: dict] from our dependency ("dependable"):
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: return {"q": q, "skip": skip, "limit": limit}...]
But then we get a [CODE: dict] in the parameter [CODE: commons] of the [ITALIC:path operation function].
And we know that editors can't provide a lot of support (like completion) for [CODE: dict]s, because they can't know their keys and value types.
We can do better...

metadata.breadcrumb: Classes as Dependencies > A `dict` from the previous example
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Dependencies > tutorial/dependencies/classes-as-dependencies.md

page_content:
[Context: Classes as Dependencies > What makes a dependency]

## What makes a dependency
Up to now you have seen dependencies declared as functions.
But that's not the only way to declare dependencies (although it would probably be the more common).
The key factor is that a dependency should be a "callable".
A "[BOLD:callable]" in Python is anything that Python can "call" like a function.
So, if you have an object [CODE: something] (that might [ITALIC:not] be a function) and you can "call" it (execute it) like:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: something()...]
or
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]1: something(some[ITALIC:argument, some]keyword_argument="fo...]
then it is a "callable".

metadata.breadcrumb: Classes as Dependencies > What makes a dependency
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Dependencies > tutorial/dependencies/classes-as-dependencies.md

page_content:
[Context: Classes as Dependencies > Classes as dependencies]

## Classes as dependencies
You might notice that to create an instance of a Python class, you use that same syntax.
For example:
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:0: class Cat: def [BOLD:init](self, name: str): self.nam...]
In this case, [CODE: fluffy] is an instance of the class [CODE: Cat].
And to create [CODE: fluffy], you are "calling" [CODE: Cat].
So, a Python class is also a [BOLD:callable].
Then, in [BOLD:FastAPI], you could use a Python class as a dependency.
What FastAPI actually checks is that it is a "callable" (function, class or anything else) and the parameters defined.
If you pass a "callable" as a dependency in [BOLD:FastAPI], it will analyze the parameters for that "callable", and process them in the same way as the parameters for a [ITALIC:path operation function]. Including sub-dependencies.
That also applies to callables with no parameters at all. The same as it would be for [ITALIC:path operation functions] with no parameters.
Then, we can change the dependency "dependable" [CODE: common]parameters] from above to the class [CODE: CommonQueryParams]:
```python
class CommonQueryParams:
def [BOLD:init](self, q: str | None = None, skip: int = 0, limit: int = 100):
self.q = q
self.skip = skip
self.limit = limit
```
Pay attention to the [CODE: [BOLD:init]] method used to create the instance of the class:
```python
def [BOLD:init](self, q: str | None = None, skip: int = 0, limit: int = 100):
self.q = q
```
...it has the same parameters as our previous [CODE: common[ITALIC:parameters]:
```python
async def common]parameters(q: str | None = None, skip: int = 0, limit: int = 100):
return {"q": q, "skip": skip, "limit": limit}
```
Those parameters are what [BOLD:FastAPI] will use to "solve" the dependency.
In both cases, it will have:
[ITALIC: An optional [CODE: q] query parameter that is a [CODE: str].
] A [CODE: skip] query parameter that is an [CODE: int], with a default of [CODE: 0].
- A [CODE: limit] query parameter that is an [CODE: int], with a default of [CODE: 100].
In both cases the data will be converted, validated, documented on the OpenAPI schema, etc.

page_content:
[Context: Classes as Dependencies > Classes as dependencies]

## Classes as dependencies
You might notice that to create an instance of a Python class, you use that same syntax.
For example:
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:0: class Cat: def [BOLD:init](self, name: str): self.nam...]
In this case, [CODE: fluffy] is an instance of the class [CODE: Cat].
And to create [CODE: fluffy], you are "calling" [CODE: Cat].
So, a Python class is also a [BOLD:callable].
Then, in [BOLD:FastAPI], you could use a Python class as a dependency.
What FastAPI actually checks is that it is a "callable" (function, class or anything else) and the parameters defined.
If you pass a "callable" as a dependency in [BOLD:FastAPI], it will analyze the parameters for that "callable", and process them in the same way as the parameters for a [ITALIC:path operation function]. Including sub-dependencies.
That also applies to callables with no parameters at all. The same as it would be for [ITALIC:path operation functions] with no parameters.
Then, we can change the dependency "dependable" [CODE: common]parameters] from above to the class [CODE: CommonQueryParams]:
```python
class CommonQueryParams:
def [BOLD:init](self, q: str | None = None, skip: int = 0, limit: int = 100):
self.q = q
self.skip = skip
self.limit = limit
```
Pay attention to the [CODE: [BOLD:init]] method used to create the instance of the class:
```python
def [BOLD:init](self, q: str | None = None, skip: int = 0, limit: int = 100):
self.q = q
```
...it has the same parameters as our previous [CODE: common[ITALIC:parameters]:
```python
async def common]parameters(q: str | None = None, skip: int = 0, limit: int = 100):
return {"q": q, "skip": skip, "limit": limit}
```
Those parameters are what [BOLD:FastAPI] will use to "solve" the dependency.
In both cases, it will have:
[ITALIC: An optional [CODE: q] query parameter that is a [CODE: str].
] A [CODE: skip] query parameter that is an [CODE: int], with a default of [CODE: 0].
- A [CODE: limit] query parameter that is an [CODE: int], with a default of [CODE: 100].
In both cases the data will be converted, validated, documented on the OpenAPI schema, etc.

## Use it
Now you can declare your dependency using this class.
```python
async def read_items(commons: Annotated[CommonQueryParams, Depends(CommonQueryParams)]):
response = {}
```
[BOLD:FastAPI] calls the [CODE: CommonQueryParams] class. This creates an "instance" of that class and the instance will be passed as the parameter [CODE: commons] to your function.

page_content:
[Context: Dependencies in path operation decorators]

# Dependencies in path operation decorators
In some cases you don't really need the return value of a dependency inside your [ITALIC:path operation function].
Or the dependency doesn't return a value.
But you still need it to be executed/solved.
For those cases, instead of declaring a [ITALIC:path operation function] parameter with [CODE: Depends], you can add a [CODE: list] of [CODE: dependencies] to the [ITALIC:path operation decorator].

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Dependencies > tutorial/dependencies/dependencies-in-path-operation-decorators.md

page_content:
[Context: Dependencies in path operation decorators > Add `dependencies` to the *path operation decorator*]

## Add [CODE: dependencies] to the [ITALIC:path operation decorator]
The [ITALIC:path operation decorator] receives an optional argument [CODE: dependencies].
It should be a [CODE: list] of [CODE: Depends()]:
```python
@app.get("/items/", dependencies=[Depends(verify[ITALIC:token), Depends(verify]key)])
async def read_items():
```
These dependencies will be executed/solved the same way as normal dependencies. But their value (if they return any) won't be passed to your [ITALIC:path operation function].
/// tip
Some editors check for unused function parameters, and show them as errors.
Using these [CODE: dependencies] in the [ITALIC:path operation decorator] you can make sure they are executed while avoiding editor/tooling errors.
It might also help avoid confusion for new developers that see an unused parameter in your code and could think it's unnecessary.
///
/// info
In this example we use invented custom headers [CODE: X-Key] and [CODE: X-Token].
But in real cases, when implementing security, you would get more benefits from using the integrated Security utilities (the next chapter).
///

## Dependencies errors and return values
You can use the same dependency [ITALIC:functions] you use normally.

page_content:
[Context: Dependencies in path operation decorators > Dependencies errors and return values > Dependency requirements]

### Dependency requirements
They can declare request requirements (like headers) or other sub-dependencies:
```python
async def verify[ITALIC:token(x]token: Annotated[str, Header()]):
if x[ITALIC:token != "fake-super-secret-token":
async def verify]key(x[ITALIC:key: Annotated[str, Header()]):
if x]key != "fake-super-secret-key":
```

### Raise exceptions
These dependencies can [CODE: raise] exceptions, the same as normal dependencies:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: raise HTTPException(status_code=400, detail="X-Tok...]

### Return values
And they can return values or not, the values won't be used.
So, you can reuse a normal dependency (that returns a value) you already use somewhere else, and even though the value won't be used, the dependency will be executed:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: return x_key...]

page_content:
[Context: Dependencies with yield > A database dependency with `yield`]

## A database dependency with [CODE: yield]
For example, you could use this to create a database session and close it after finishing.
Only the code prior to and including the [CODE: yield] statement is executed before creating a response:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: db = DBSession() try: yield db...]
The yielded value is what is injected into [ITALIC:path operations] and other dependencies:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]1: yield db finally:...]
The code following the [CODE: yield] statement is executed after the response:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]2: finally: db.close()...]
/// tip
You can use [CODE: async] or regular functions.
[BOLD:FastAPI] will do the right thing with each, the same as with normal dependencies.
///

metadata.breadcrumb: Dependencies with yield > A database dependency with `yield`
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Dependencies > tutorial/dependencies/dependencies-with-yield.md

page_content:
[Context: Dependencies with yield > A dependency with `yield` and `try`]

## A dependency with [CODE: yield] and [CODE: try]
If you use a [CODE: try] block in a dependency with [CODE: yield], you'll receive any exception that was thrown when using the dependency.
For example, if some code at some point in the middle, in another dependency or in a [ITALIC:path operation], made a database transaction "rollback" or created any other exception, you would receive the exception in your dependency.
So, you can look for that specific exception inside the dependency with [CODE: except SomeException].
In the same way, you can use [CODE: finally] to make sure the exit steps are executed, no matter if there was an exception or not.
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: try: yield db finally: db.close()...]

metadata.breadcrumb: Dependencies with yield > A dependency with `yield` and `try`
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Dependencies > tutorial/dependencies/dependencies-with-yield.md

page_content:
[Context: Dependencies with yield > Sub-dependencies with `yield`]

## Sub-dependencies with [CODE: yield]
You can have sub-dependencies and "trees" of sub-dependencies of any size and shape, and any or all of them can use [CODE: yield].
[BOLD:FastAPI] will make sure that the "exit code" in each dependency with [CODE: yield] is run in the correct order.
For example, [CODE: dependency[ITALIC:c] can have a dependency on [CODE: dependency]b], and [CODE: dependency[ITALIC:b] on [CODE: dependency]a]:
```python
async def dependency[ITALIC:a():
dep]a = generate[ITALIC:dep]a()
async def dependency[ITALIC:b(dep]a: Annotated[DepA, Depends(dependency[ITALIC:a)]):
dep]b = generate[ITALIC:dep]b()
async def dependency[ITALIC:c(dep]b: Annotated[DepB, Depends(dependency[ITALIC:b)]):
dep]c = generate[ITALIC:dep]c()
```
And all of them can use [CODE: yield].
In this case [CODE: dependency[ITALIC:c], to execute its exit code, needs the value from [CODE: dependency]b] (here named [CODE: dep[ITALIC:b]) to still be available.
And, in turn, [CODE: dependency]b] needs the value from [CODE: dependency[ITALIC:a] (here named [CODE: dep]a]) to be available for its exit code.
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: finally: dep[ITALIC:b.close(dep]a) finally: dep_c.close(d...]
The same way, you could have some dependencies with [CODE: yield] and some other dependencies with [CODE: return], and have some of those depend on some of the others.
And you could have a single dependency that requires several other dependencies with [CODE: yield], etc.
You can have any combinations of dependencies that you want.
[BOLD:FastAPI] will make sure everything is run in the correct order.
/// note | Technical Details
This works thanks to Python's Context Managers.
[BOLD:FastAPI] uses them internally to achieve this.
///

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Dependencies > tutorial/dependencies/dependencies-with-yield.md

page_content:
[Context: Dependencies with yield > Dependencies with `yield` and `HTTPException`]

## Dependencies with [CODE: yield] and [CODE: HTTPException]
You saw that you can use dependencies with [CODE: yield] and have [CODE: try] blocks that try to execute some code and then run some exit code after [CODE: finally].
You can also use [CODE: except] to catch the exception that was raised and do something with it.
For example, you can raise a different exception, like [CODE: HTTPException].
/// tip
This is a somewhat advanced technique, and in most of the cases you won't really need it, as you can raise exceptions (including [CODE: HTTPException]) from inside of the rest of your application code, for example, in the [ITALIC:path operation function].
But it's there for you if you need it. 🤓
///
```python
def get[ITALIC:username():
try:
yield "Rick"
except OwnerError as e:
raise HTTPException(status]code=400, detail=f"Owner error: {e}")
raise OwnerError(username)
return item
```
If you want to catch exceptions and create a custom response based on that, create a Custom Exception Handler.

metadata.breadcrumb: Dependencies with yield > Dependencies with `yield` and `HTTPException`
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Dependencies > tutorial/dependencies/dependencies-with-yield.md

page_content:
[Context: Dependencies with yield > Dependencies with `yield` and `HTTPException`]

## Dependencies with [CODE: yield] and [CODE: HTTPException]
You saw that you can use dependencies with [CODE: yield] and have [CODE: try] blocks that try to execute some code and then run some exit code after [CODE: finally].
You can also use [CODE: except] to catch the exception that was raised and do something with it.
For example, you can raise a different exception, like [CODE: HTTPException].
/// tip
This is a somewhat advanced technique, and in most of the cases you won't really need it, as you can raise exceptions (including [CODE: HTTPException]) from inside of the rest of your application code, for example, in the [ITALIC:path operation function].
But it's there for you if you need it. 🤓
///
```python
def get[ITALIC:username():
try:
yield "Rick"
except OwnerError as e:
raise HTTPException(status]code=400, detail=f"Owner error: {e}")
raise OwnerError(username)
return item
```
If you want to catch exceptions and create a custom response based on that, create a Custom Exception Handler.

metadata.breadcrumb: Dependencies with yield > Dependencies with `yield` and `HTTPException`
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Dependencies > tutorial/dependencies/dependencies-with-yield.md

page_content:
[Context: Dependencies with yield > Dependencies with `yield` and `except`]

## Dependencies with [CODE: yield] and [CODE: except]
If you catch an exception using [CODE: except] in a dependency with [CODE: yield] and you don't raise it again (or raise a new exception), FastAPI won't be able to notice there was an exception, the same way that would happen with regular Python:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: except InternalError: print("Oops, we didn't raise...]
In this case, the client will see an [ITALIC:HTTP 500 Internal Server Error] response as it should, given that we are not raising an [CODE: HTTPException] or similar, but the server will [BOLD:not have any logs] or any other indication of what was the error.

metadata.breadcrumb: Dependencies with yield > Dependencies with `yield` and `except`
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Dependencies > tutorial/dependencies/dependencies-with-yield.md

page_content:
[Context: Dependencies with yield > Dependencies with `yield` and `except` > Always `raise` in Dependencies with `yield` and `except`]

### Always [CODE: raise] in Dependencies with [CODE: yield] and [CODE: except]
If you catch an exception in a dependency with [CODE: yield], unless you are raising another [CODE: HTTPException] or similar, [BOLD:you should re-raise the original exception].
You can re-raise the same exception using [CODE: raise]:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: raise...]
Now the client will get the same [ITALIC:HTTP 500 Internal Server Error] response, but the server will have our custom [CODE: InternalError] in the logs.

metadata.breadcrumb: Dependencies with yield > Dependencies with `yield` and `except` > Always `raise` in Dependencies with `yield` and `except`
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Dependencies > tutorial/dependencies/dependencies-with-yield.md

metadata.breadcrumb: Dependencies with yield > Execution of dependencies with `yield`
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Dependencies > tutorial/dependencies/dependencies-with-yield.md

page_content:
[Context: Dependencies with yield > Early exit and `scope`]

## Early exit and [CODE: scope]
Normally the exit code of dependencies with [CODE: yield] is executed [BOLD:after the response] is sent to the client.
But if you know that you won't need to use the dependency after returning from the [ITALIC:path operation function], you can use [CODE: Depends(scope="function")] to tell FastAPI that it should close the dependency after the [ITALIC:path operation function] returns, but [BOLD:before] the [BOLD:response is sent].
```python
print("Cleanup up before response is sent")

def get[ITALIC:user]me(username: Annotated[str, Depends(get_username, scope="function")]):
return username
```
[CODE: Depends()] receives a [CODE: scope] parameter that can be:
[ITALIC: [CODE: "function"]: start the dependency before the ]path operation function[ITALIC: that handles the request, end the dependency after the ]path operation function[ITALIC: ends, but [BOLD:before] the response is sent back to the client. So, the dependency function will be executed [BOLD:around] the ]path operation [BOLD:function][ITALIC:.
] [CODE: "request"]: start the dependency before the [ITALIC:path operation function] that handles the request (similar to when using [CODE: "function"]), but end [BOLD:after] the response is sent back to the client. So, the dependency function will be executed [BOLD:around] the [BOLD:request] and response cycle.
If not specified and the dependency has [CODE: yield], it will have a [CODE: scope] of [CODE: "request"] by default.

metadata.breadcrumb: Dependencies with yield > Early exit and `scope`
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Dependencies > tutorial/dependencies/dependencies-with-yield.md

page_content:
[Context: Dependencies with yield > Early exit and `scope`]

## Early exit and [CODE: scope]
Normally the exit code of dependencies with [CODE: yield] is executed [BOLD:after the response] is sent to the client.
But if you know that you won't need to use the dependency after returning from the [ITALIC:path operation function], you can use [CODE: Depends(scope="function")] to tell FastAPI that it should close the dependency after the [ITALIC:path operation function] returns, but [BOLD:before] the [BOLD:response is sent].
```python
print("Cleanup up before response is sent")

def get[ITALIC:user]me(username: Annotated[str, Depends(get_username, scope="function")]):
return username
```
[CODE: Depends()] receives a [CODE: scope] parameter that can be:
[ITALIC: [CODE: "function"]: start the dependency before the ]path operation function[ITALIC: that handles the request, end the dependency after the ]path operation function[ITALIC: ends, but [BOLD:before] the response is sent back to the client. So, the dependency function will be executed [BOLD:around] the ]path operation [BOLD:function][ITALIC:.
] [CODE: "request"]: start the dependency before the [ITALIC:path operation function] that handles the request (similar to when using [CODE: "function"]), but end [BOLD:after] the response is sent back to the client. So, the dependency function will be executed [BOLD:around] the [BOLD:request] and response cycle.
If not specified and the dependency has [CODE: yield], it will have a [CODE: scope] of [CODE: "request"] by default.

metadata.breadcrumb: Dependencies with yield > Early exit and `scope`
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Dependencies > tutorial/dependencies/dependencies-with-yield.md

page_content:
[Context: Dependencies with yield > Early exit and `scope` > `scope` for sub-dependencies]

### [CODE: scope] for sub-dependencies
When you declare a dependency with a [CODE: scope="request"] (the default), any sub-dependency needs to also have a [CODE: scope] of [CODE: "request"].
But a dependency with [CODE: scope] of [CODE: "function"] can have dependencies with [CODE: scope] of [CODE: "function"] and [CODE: scope] of [CODE: "request"].
This is because any dependency needs to be able to run its exit code before the sub-dependencies, as it might need to still use them during its exit code.
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: sequenceDiagram participant client as Client parti...]

metadata.breadcrumb: Dependencies with yield > Early exit and `scope` > `scope` for sub-dependencies

page_content:
[Context: Dependencies with yield > Dependencies with `yield`, `HTTPException`, `except` and Background Tasks]

## Dependencies with [CODE: yield], [CODE: HTTPException], [CODE: except] and Background Tasks
Dependencies with [CODE: yield] have evolved over time to cover different use cases and fix some issues.
If you want to see what has changed in different versions of FastAPI, you can read more about it in the advanced guide, in [Advanced Dependencies - Dependencies with [CODE: yield], [CODE: HTTPException], [CODE: except] and Background Tasks](../../advanced/advanced-dependencies.md#dependencies-with-yield-httpexception-except-and-background-tasks).

metadata.breadcrumb: Dependencies with yield > Dependencies with `yield`, `HTTPException`, `except` and Background Tasks
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Dependencies > tutorial/dependencies/dependencies-with-yield.md

page_content:
[Context: Dependencies with yield > Context Managers > What are "Context Managers"]

## Context Managers
### What are "Context Managers"
"Context Managers" are any of those Python objects that you can use in a [CODE: with] statement.
For example, [you can use [CODE: with] to read a file](https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/inputoutput.html#reading-and-writing-files):
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: with open("./somefile.txt") as f: contents = f.rea...]
Underneath, the [CODE: open("./somefile.txt")] creates an object that is called a "Context Manager".
When the [CODE: with] block finishes, it makes sure to close the file, even if there were exceptions.
When you create a dependency with [CODE: yield], [BOLD:FastAPI] will internally create a context manager for it, and combine it with some other related tools.

metadata.breadcrumb: Dependencies with yield > Context Managers > What are "Context Managers"

page_content:
[Context: Global Dependencies]

# Global Dependencies
For some types of applications you might want to add dependencies to the whole application.
Similar to the way you can [add [CODE: dependencies] to the [ITALIC:path operation decorators]](dependencies-in-path-operation-decorators.md), you can add them to the [CODE: FastAPI] application.
In that case, they will be applied to all the [ITALIC:path operations] in the application:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: app = FastAPI(dependencies=[Depends(verify_token),...]
And all the ideas in the section about [adding [CODE: dependencies] to the [ITALIC:path operation decorators]](dependencies-in-path-operation-decorators.md) still apply, but in this case, to all of the [ITALIC:path operations] in the app.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Dependencies > tutorial/dependencies/global-dependencies.md

page_content:
[Context: Global Dependencies > Dependencies for groups of *path operations*]

## Dependencies for groups of [ITALIC:path operations]
Later, when reading about how to structure bigger applications (Bigger Applications - Multiple Files), possibly with multiple files, you will learn how to declare a single [CODE: dependencies] parameter for a group of [ITALIC:path operations].

metadata.breadcrumb: Global Dependencies > Dependencies for groups of *path operations*
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Dependencies > tutorial/dependencies/global-dependencies.md

page_content:
[Context: Dependencies]

# Dependencies
[BOLD:FastAPI] has a very powerful but intuitive [BOLD:Dependency Injection] system.
It is designed to be very simple to use, and to make it very easy for any developer to integrate other components with [BOLD:FastAPI].

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Dependencies > tutorial/dependencies/index.md

page_content:
[Context: Dependencies > What is "Dependency Injection"]

## What is "Dependency Injection"
[BOLD:"Dependency Injection"] means, in programming, that there is a way for your code (in this case, your [ITALIC:path operation functions]) to declare things that it requires to work and use: "dependencies".
And then, that system (in this case [BOLD:FastAPI]) will take care of doing whatever is needed to provide your code with those needed dependencies ("inject" the dependencies).
This is very useful when you need to:
[ITALIC: Have shared logic (the same code logic again and again).
] Share database connections.
[ITALIC: Enforce security, authentication, role requirements, etc.
] And many other things...
All these, while minimizing code repetition.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Dependencies > tutorial/dependencies/index.md

page_content:
[Context: Dependencies > First Steps]

## First Steps
Let's see a very simple example. It will be so simple that it is not very useful, for now.
But this way we can focus on how the [BOLD:Dependency Injection] system works.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Dependencies > tutorial/dependencies/index.md

page_content:
[Context: Dependencies > First Steps > Create a dependency, or "dependable"]

### Create a dependency, or "dependable"
Let's first focus on the dependency.
It is just a function that can take all the same parameters that a [ITALIC:path operation function] can take:
```python
async def common_parameters(q: str | None = None, skip: int = 0, limit: int = 100):
return {"q": q, "skip": skip, "limit": limit}
```
That's it.
[BOLD:2 lines].
And it has the same shape and structure that all your [ITALIC:path operation functions] have.
You can think of it as a [ITALIC:path operation function] without the "decorator" (without the [CODE: @app.get("/some-path")]).
And it can return anything you want.
In this case, this dependency expects:
[ITALIC: An optional query parameter [CODE: q] that is a [CODE: str].
] An optional query parameter [CODE: skip] that is an [CODE: int], and by default is [CODE: 0].
- An optional query parameter [CODE: limit] that is an [CODE: int], and by default is [CODE: 100].
And then it just returns a [CODE: dict] containing those values.
/// info
FastAPI added support for [CODE: Annotated] (and started recommending it) in version 0.95.0.
If you have an older version, you would get errors when trying to use [CODE: Annotated].
Make sure you Upgrade the FastAPI version to at least 0.95.1 before using [CODE: Annotated].
///

metadata.breadcrumb: Dependencies > First Steps > Create a dependency, or "dependable"
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Dependencies > tutorial/dependencies/index.md

page_content:
[Context: Dependencies > First Steps > Create a dependency, or "dependable"]

### Create a dependency, or "dependable"
Let's first focus on the dependency.
It is just a function that can take all the same parameters that a [ITALIC:path operation function] can take:
```python
async def common_parameters(q: str | None = None, skip: int = 0, limit: int = 100):
return {"q": q, "skip": skip, "limit": limit}
```
That's it.
[BOLD:2 lines].
And it has the same shape and structure that all your [ITALIC:path operation functions] have.
You can think of it as a [ITALIC:path operation function] without the "decorator" (without the [CODE: @app.get("/some-path")]).
And it can return anything you want.
In this case, this dependency expects:
[ITALIC: An optional query parameter [CODE: q] that is a [CODE: str].
] An optional query parameter [CODE: skip] that is an [CODE: int], and by default is [CODE: 0].
- An optional query parameter [CODE: limit] that is an [CODE: int], and by default is [CODE: 100].
And then it just returns a [CODE: dict] containing those values.
/// info
FastAPI added support for [CODE: Annotated] (and started recommending it) in version 0.95.0.
If you have an older version, you would get errors when trying to use [CODE: Annotated].
Make sure you Upgrade the FastAPI version to at least 0.95.1 before using [CODE: Annotated].
///

metadata.breadcrumb: Dependencies > First Steps > Create a dependency, or "dependable"
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Dependencies > tutorial/dependencies/index.md

page_content:
[Context: Dependencies > First Steps > Import `Depends`]

### Import [CODE: Depends]
```python
from fastapi import Depends, FastAPI

```

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Dependencies > tutorial/dependencies/index.md

page_content:
[Context: Dependencies > First Steps > Declare the dependency, in the "dependant"]

### Declare the dependency, in the "dependant"
The same way you use [CODE: Body], [CODE: Query], etc. with your [ITALIC:path operation function] parameters, use [CODE: Depends] with a new parameter:
```python
async def read[ITALIC:items(commons: Annotated[dict, Depends(common]parameters)]):
return commons
async def read[ITALIC:users(commons: Annotated[dict, Depends(common]parameters)]):
return commons
```
Although you use [CODE: Depends] in the parameters of your function the same way you use [CODE: Body], [CODE: Query], etc, [CODE: Depends] works a bit differently.
You only give [CODE: Depends] a single parameter.
This parameter must be something like a function.
You [BOLD:don't call it] directly (don't add the parenthesis at the end), you just pass it as a parameter to [CODE: Depends()].
And that function takes parameters in the same way that [ITALIC:path operation functions] do.
/// tip
You'll see what other "things", apart from functions, can be used as dependencies in the next chapter.
///
Whenever a new request arrives, [BOLD:FastAPI] will take care of:
[ITALIC: Calling your dependency ("dependable") function with the correct parameters.
] Get the result from your function.
[ITALIC: Assign that result to the parameter in your ]path operation function[ITALIC:.
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: graph TB common[ITALIC:parameters(["common]parameters"]) ...]
This way you write shared code once and [BOLD:FastAPI] takes care of calling it for your ]path operations*.
/// check
Notice that you don't have to create a special class and pass it somewhere to [BOLD:FastAPI] to "register" it or anything similar.
You just pass it to [CODE: Depends] and [BOLD:FastAPI] knows how to do the rest.
///

metadata.breadcrumb: Dependencies > First Steps > Declare the dependency, in the "dependant"
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Dependencies > tutorial/dependencies/index.md

page_content:
[Context: Dependencies > First Steps > Declare the dependency, in the "dependant"]

### Declare the dependency, in the "dependant"
The same way you use [CODE: Body], [CODE: Query], etc. with your [ITALIC:path operation function] parameters, use [CODE: Depends] with a new parameter:
```python
async def read[ITALIC:items(commons: Annotated[dict, Depends(common]parameters)]):
return commons
async def read[ITALIC:users(commons: Annotated[dict, Depends(common]parameters)]):
return commons
```
Although you use [CODE: Depends] in the parameters of your function the same way you use [CODE: Body], [CODE: Query], etc, [CODE: Depends] works a bit differently.
You only give [CODE: Depends] a single parameter.
This parameter must be something like a function.
You [BOLD:don't call it] directly (don't add the parenthesis at the end), you just pass it as a parameter to [CODE: Depends()].
And that function takes parameters in the same way that [ITALIC:path operation functions] do.
/// tip
You'll see what other "things", apart from functions, can be used as dependencies in the next chapter.
///
Whenever a new request arrives, [BOLD:FastAPI] will take care of:
[ITALIC: Calling your dependency ("dependable") function with the correct parameters.
] Get the result from your function.
[ITALIC: Assign that result to the parameter in your ]path operation function[ITALIC:.
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: graph TB common[ITALIC:parameters(["common]parameters"]) ...]
This way you write shared code once and [BOLD:FastAPI] takes care of calling it for your ]path operations*.
/// check
Notice that you don't have to create a special class and pass it somewhere to [BOLD:FastAPI] to "register" it or anything similar.
You just pass it to [CODE: Depends] and [BOLD:FastAPI] knows how to do the rest.
///

page_content:
[Context: Dependencies > Share `Annotated` dependencies]

## Share [CODE: Annotated] dependencies
In the examples above, you see that there's a tiny bit of [BOLD:code duplication].
When you need to use the [CODE: common[ITALIC:parameters()] dependency, you have to write the whole parameter with the type annotation and [CODE: Depends()]:
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:0: commons: Annotated[dict, Depends(common]parameters...]
But because we are using [CODE: Annotated], we can store that [CODE: Annotated] value in a variable and use it in multiple places:
```python
CommonsDep = Annotated[dict, Depends(common[ITALIC:parameters)]

async def read]items(commons: CommonsDep):
return commons
async def read_users(commons: CommonsDep):
return commons
```
/// tip
This is just standard Python, it's called a "type alias", it's actually not specific to [BOLD:FastAPI].
But because [BOLD:FastAPI] is based on the Python standards, including [CODE: Annotated], you can use this trick in your code.
///
The dependencies will keep working as expected, and the [BOLD:best part] is that the [BOLD:type information will be preserved], which means that your editor will be able to keep providing you with [BOLD:autocompletion], [BOLD:inline errors], etc. The same for other tools like [CODE: mypy].
This will be especially useful when you use it in a [BOLD:large code base] where you use [BOLD:the same dependencies] over and over again in *[ITALIC:many ]path operations***.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Dependencies > tutorial/dependencies/index.md

page_content:
[Context: Dependencies > Share `Annotated` dependencies]

## Share [CODE: Annotated] dependencies
In the examples above, you see that there's a tiny bit of [BOLD:code duplication].
When you need to use the [CODE: common[ITALIC:parameters()] dependency, you have to write the whole parameter with the type annotation and [CODE: Depends()]:
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:0: commons: Annotated[dict, Depends(common]parameters...]
But because we are using [CODE: Annotated], we can store that [CODE: Annotated] value in a variable and use it in multiple places:
```python
CommonsDep = Annotated[dict, Depends(common[ITALIC:parameters)]

async def read]items(commons: CommonsDep):
return commons
async def read_users(commons: CommonsDep):
return commons
```
/// tip
This is just standard Python, it's called a "type alias", it's actually not specific to [BOLD:FastAPI].
But because [BOLD:FastAPI] is based on the Python standards, including [CODE: Annotated], you can use this trick in your code.
///
The dependencies will keep working as expected, and the [BOLD:best part] is that the [BOLD:type information will be preserved], which means that your editor will be able to keep providing you with [BOLD:autocompletion], [BOLD:inline errors], etc. The same for other tools like [CODE: mypy].
This will be especially useful when you use it in a [BOLD:large code base] where you use [BOLD:the same dependencies] over and over again in *[ITALIC:many ]path operations***.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Dependencies > tutorial/dependencies/index.md

page_content:
[Context: Dependencies > To `async` or not to `async`]

## To [CODE: async] or not to [CODE: async]
As dependencies will also be called by [BOLD:FastAPI] (the same as your [ITALIC:path operation functions]), the same rules apply while defining your functions.
You can use [CODE: async def] or normal [CODE: def].
And you can declare dependencies with [CODE: async def] inside of normal [CODE: def] [ITALIC:path operation functions], or [CODE: def] dependencies inside of [CODE: async def] [ITALIC:path operation functions], etc.
It doesn't matter. [BOLD:FastAPI] will know what to do.
/// note
If you don't know, check the Async: [ITALIC:"In a hurry?"] section about [CODE: async] and [CODE: await] in the docs.
///

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Dependencies > tutorial/dependencies/index.md

page_content:
[Context: Dependencies > Integrated with OpenAPI]

## Integrated with OpenAPI
All the request declarations, validations and requirements of your dependencies (and sub-dependencies) will be integrated in the same OpenAPI schema.
So, the interactive docs will have all the information from these dependencies too:

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Dependencies > tutorial/dependencies/index.md

page_content:
[Context: Dependencies > Simple usage]

## Simple usage
If you look at it, [ITALIC:path operation functions] are declared to be used whenever a [ITALIC:path] and [ITALIC:operation] matches, and then [BOLD:FastAPI] takes care of calling the function with the correct parameters, extracting the data from the request.
Actually, all (or most) of the web frameworks work in this same way.
You never call those functions directly. They are called by your framework (in this case, [BOLD:FastAPI]).
With the Dependency Injection system, you can also tell [BOLD:FastAPI] that your [ITALIC:path operation function] also "depends" on something else that should be executed before your [ITALIC:path operation function], and [BOLD:FastAPI] will take care of executing it and "injecting" the results.
Other common terms for this same idea of "dependency injection" are:
[ITALIC: resources
] providers
[ITALIC: services
] injectables
- components

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Dependencies > tutorial/dependencies/index.md

page_content:
[Context: Dependencies > **FastAPI** plug-ins]

## [BOLD:FastAPI] plug-ins
Integrations and "plug-ins" can be built using the [BOLD:Dependency Injection] system. But in fact, there is actually [BOLD:no need to create "plug-ins"], as by using dependencies it's possible to declare an infinite number of integrations and interactions that become available to your [ITALIC:path operation functions].
And dependencies can be created in a very simple and intuitive way that allows you to just import the Python packages you need, and integrate them with your API functions in a couple of lines of code, [ITALIC:literally].
You will see examples of this in the next chapters, about relational and NoSQL databases, security, etc.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Dependencies > tutorial/dependencies/index.md
Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Dependencies > tutorial/dependencies/index.md

page_content:
[Context: Dependencies > Simple and Powerful]

## Simple and Powerful
Although the hierarchical dependency injection system is very simple to define and use, it's still very powerful.
You can define dependencies that in turn can define dependencies themselves.
In the end, a hierarchical tree of dependencies is built, and the [BOLD:Dependency Injection] system takes care of solving all these dependencies for you (and their sub-dependencies) and providing (injecting) the results at each step.
For example, let's say you have 4 API endpoints ([ITALIC:path operations]):
[ITALIC: [CODE: /items/public/]
] [CODE: /items/private/]
[ITALIC: [CODE: /users/{user[ITALIC:id}/activate]
] [CODE: /items/pro/]
then you could add different permission requirements for each of them just with dependencies and sub-dependencies:
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:0: graph TB current]user(["current[ITALIC:user"]) active]use...]

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Dependencies > tutorial/dependencies/index.md

page_content:
[Context: Dependencies > Integrated with **OpenAPI**]

## Integrated with [BOLD:OpenAPI]
All these dependencies, while declaring their requirements, also add parameters, validations, etc. to your [ITALIC:path operations].
[BOLD:FastAPI] will take care of adding it all to the OpenAPI schema, so that it is shown in the interactive documentation systems.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Dependencies > tutorial/dependencies/index.md

page_content:
[Context: Sub-dependencies]

# Sub-dependencies
You can create dependencies that have [BOLD:sub-dependencies].
They can be as [BOLD:deep] as you need them to be.
[BOLD:FastAPI] will take care of solving them.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Dependencies > tutorial/dependencies/sub-dependencies.md

page_content:
[Context: Sub-dependencies > First dependency "dependable"]

## First dependency "dependable"
You could create a first dependency ("dependable") like:
```python
def query_extractor(q: str | None = None):
return q
```
It declares an optional query parameter [CODE: q] as a [CODE: str], and then it just returns it.
This is quite simple (not very useful), but will help us focus on how the sub-dependencies work.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Dependencies > tutorial/dependencies/sub-dependencies.md

page_content:
[Context: Sub-dependencies > Second dependency, "dependable" and "dependant"]

## Second dependency, "dependable" and "dependant"
Then you can create another dependency function (a "dependable") that at the same time declares a dependency of its own (so it is a "dependant" too):
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: q: Annotated[str, Depends(query[ITALIC:extractor)], last]...]
Let's focus on the parameters declared:
[ITALIC: Even though this function is a dependency ("dependable") itself, it also declares another dependency (it "depends" on something else).
] It depends on the [CODE: query[ITALIC:extractor], and assigns the value returned by it to the parameter [CODE: q].
[ITALIC: It also declares an optional [CODE: last]query] cookie, as a [CODE: str].
] If the user didn't provide any query [CODE: q], we use the last query used, which we saved to a cookie before.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Dependencies > tutorial/dependencies/sub-dependencies.md

page_content:
[Context: Sub-dependencies > Use the dependency]

## Use the dependency
Then we can use the dependency with:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: query[ITALIC:or]default: Annotated[str, Depends(query[ITALIC:or]...]
/// info
Notice that we are only declaring one dependency in the [ITALIC:path operation function], the [CODE: query[ITALIC:or]cookie[ITALIC:extractor].
But [BOLD:FastAPI] will know that it has to solve [CODE: query]extractor] first, to pass the results of that to [CODE: query[ITALIC:or]cookie[ITALIC:extractor] while calling it.
///
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:1: graph TB query]extractor(["query_extractor"]) quer...]

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Dependencies > tutorial/dependencies/sub-dependencies.md

hierarchy: Using the same dependency multiple times
metadata.breadcrumb: Sub-dependencies > Using the same dependency multiple times
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Dependencies > tutorial/dependencies/sub-dependencies.md

hierarchy: Using the same dependency multiple times
metadata.breadcrumb: Sub-dependencies > Using the same dependency multiple times
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Dependencies > tutorial/dependencies/sub-dependencies.md

page_content:
[Context: Sub-dependencies > Recap]

## Recap
Apart from all the fancy words used here, the [BOLD:Dependency Injection] system is quite simple.
Just functions that look the same as the [ITALIC:path operation functions].
But still, it is very powerful, and allows you to declare arbitrarily deeply nested dependency "graphs" (trees).
/// tip
All this might not seem as useful with these simple examples.
But you will see how useful it is in the chapters about [BOLD:security].
And you will also see the amounts of code it will save you.
///

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Dependencies > tutorial/dependencies/sub-dependencies.md

page_content:
[Context: Security - First Steps]

# Security - First Steps
Let's imagine that you have your [BOLD:backend] API in some domain.
And you have a [BOLD:frontend] in another domain or in a different path of the same domain (or in a mobile application).
And you want to have a way for the frontend to authenticate with the backend, using a [BOLD:username] and [BOLD:password].
We can use [BOLD:OAuth2] to build that with [BOLD:FastAPI].
But let's save you the time of reading the full long specification just to find those little pieces of information you need.
Let's use the tools provided by [BOLD:FastAPI] to handle security.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Security > tutorial/security/first-steps.md

page_content:
[Context: Security - First Steps > How it looks]

## How it looks
Let's first just use the code and see how it works, and then we'll come back to understand what's happening.

metadata.breadcrumb: Security - First Steps > How it looks
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Security > tutorial/security/first-steps.md

page_content:
[Context: Security - First Steps > Create `main.py`]

## Create [CODE: main.py]
Copy the example in a file [CODE: main.py]:
{[ITALIC: ../../docs[ITALIC:src/security/tutorial001]an_py310.py ]}

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Security > tutorial/security/first-steps.md

page_content:
[Context: Security - First Steps > Run it]

## Run it
/// info
The [[CODE: python-multipart]](https://github.com/Kludex/python-multipart) package is automatically installed with [BOLD:FastAPI] when you run the [CODE: pip install "fastapi[standard]"] command.
However, if you use the [CODE: pip install fastapi] command, the [CODE: python-multipart] package is not included by default.
To install it manually, make sure you create a virtual environment, activate it, and then install it with:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: $ pip install python-multipart...]
This is because [BOLD:OAuth2] uses "form data" for sending the [CODE: username] and [CODE: password].
///
Run the example with:

[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]1: $ fastapi dev INFO

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Security > tutorial/security/first-steps.md

page_content:
[Context: Security - First Steps > Check it]

## Check it
Go to the interactive docs at: http://127.0.0.1:8000/docs.
You will see something like this:

/// check | Authorize button!
You already have a shiny new "Authorize" button.
And your [ITALIC:path operation] has a little lock in the top-right corner that you can click.
///
And if you click it, you have a little authorization form to type a [CODE: username] and [CODE: password] (and other optional fields):

/// note
It doesn't matter what you type in the form, it won't work yet. But we'll get there.
///
This is of course not the frontend for the final users, but it's a great automatic tool to document interactively all your API.
It can be used by the frontend team (that can also be yourself).
It can be used by third party applications and systems.
And it can also be used by yourself, to debug, check and test the same application.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Security > tutorial/security/first-steps.md
Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Security > tutorial/security/first-steps.md
Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Security > tutorial/security/first-steps.md

page_content:
[Context: Security - First Steps > **FastAPI**'s `OAuth2PasswordBearer`]

## [BOLD:FastAPI]'s [CODE: OAuth2PasswordBearer]
[BOLD:FastAPI] provides several tools, at different levels of abstraction, to implement these security features.
In this example we are going to use [BOLD:OAuth2], with the [BOLD:Password] flow, using a [BOLD:Bearer] token. We do that using the [CODE: OAuth2PasswordBearer] class.
/// info
A "bearer" token is not the only option.
But it's the best one for our use case.
And it might be the best for most use cases, unless you are an OAuth2 expert and know exactly why there's another option that better suits your needs.
In that case, [BOLD:FastAPI] also provides you with the tools to build it.
///
When we create an instance of the [CODE: OAuth2PasswordBearer] class we pass in the [CODE: tokenUrl] parameter. This parameter contains the URL that the client (the frontend running in the user's browser) will use to send the [CODE: username] and [CODE: password] in order to get a token.
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: oauth2[ITALIC:scheme = OAuth2PasswordBearer(tokenUrl="tok...]
/// tip
Here [CODE: tokenUrl="token"] refers to a relative URL [CODE: token] that we haven't created yet. As it's a relative URL, it's equivalent to [CODE: ./token].
Because we are using a relative URL, if your API was located at [CODE: https://example.com/], then it would refer to [CODE: https://example.com/token]. But if your API was located at [CODE: https://example.com/api/v1/], then it would refer to [CODE: https://example.com/api/v1/token].
Using a relative URL is important to make sure your application keeps working even in an advanced use case like Behind a Proxy.
///
This parameter doesn't create that endpoint / [ITALIC:path operation], but declares that the URL [CODE: /token] will be the one that the client should use to get the token. That information is used in OpenAPI, and then in the interactive API documentation systems.
We will soon also create the actual path operation.
/// info
If you are a very strict "Pythonista" you might dislike the style of the parameter name [CODE: tokenUrl] instead of [CODE: token]url].
That's because it is using the same name as in the OpenAPI spec. So that if you need to investigate more about any of these security schemes you can just copy and paste it to find more information about it.
///
The [CODE: oauth2[ITALIC:scheme] variable is an instance of [CODE: OAuth2PasswordBearer], but it is also a "callable".
It could be called as:
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:1: oauth2]scheme(some, parameters)...]
So, it can be used with [CODE: Depends].

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Security > tutorial/security/first-steps.md

page_content:
[Context: Security - First Steps > **FastAPI**'s `OAuth2PasswordBearer` > Use it]

### Use it
Now you can pass that [CODE: oauth2[ITALIC:scheme] in a dependency with [CODE: Depends].
```python
async def read]items(token: Annotated[str, Depends(oauth2_scheme)]):
return {"token": token}
```
This dependency will provide a [CODE: str] that is assigned to the parameter [CODE: token] of the [ITALIC:path operation function].
[BOLD:FastAPI] will know that it can use this dependency to define a "security scheme" in the OpenAPI schema (and the automatic API docs).
/// info | Technical Details
[BOLD:FastAPI] will know that it can use the class [CODE: OAuth2PasswordBearer] (declared in a dependency) to define the security scheme in OpenAPI because it inherits from [CODE: fastapi.security.oauth2.OAuth2], which in turn inherits from [CODE: fastapi.security.base.SecurityBase].
All the security utilities that integrate with OpenAPI (and the automatic API docs) inherit from [CODE: SecurityBase], that's how [BOLD:FastAPI] can know how to integrate them in OpenAPI.
///

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Security > tutorial/security/first-steps.md

page_content:
[Context: Security - First Steps > **FastAPI**'s `OAuth2PasswordBearer` > Use it]

### Use it
Now you can pass that [CODE: oauth2[ITALIC:scheme] in a dependency with [CODE: Depends].
```python
async def read]items(token: Annotated[str, Depends(oauth2_scheme)]):
return {"token": token}
```
This dependency will provide a [CODE: str] that is assigned to the parameter [CODE: token] of the [ITALIC:path operation function].
[BOLD:FastAPI] will know that it can use this dependency to define a "security scheme" in the OpenAPI schema (and the automatic API docs).
/// info | Technical Details
[BOLD:FastAPI] will know that it can use the class [CODE: OAuth2PasswordBearer] (declared in a dependency) to define the security scheme in OpenAPI because it inherits from [CODE: fastapi.security.oauth2.OAuth2], which in turn inherits from [CODE: fastapi.security.base.SecurityBase].
All the security utilities that integrate with OpenAPI (and the automatic API docs) inherit from [CODE: SecurityBase], that's how [BOLD:FastAPI] can know how to integrate them in OpenAPI.
///

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Security > tutorial/security/first-steps.md

page_content:
[Context: Security - First Steps > What it does]

## What it does
It will go and look in the request for that [CODE: Authorization] header, check if the value is [CODE: Bearer ] plus some token, and will return the token as a [CODE: str].
If it doesn't see an [CODE: Authorization] header, or the value doesn't have a [CODE: Bearer ] token, it will respond with a 401 status code error ([CODE: UNAUTHORIZED]) directly.
You don't even have to check if the token exists to return an error. You can be sure that if your function is executed, it will have a [CODE: str] in that token.
You can try it already in the interactive docs:

We are not verifying the validity of the token yet, but that's a start already.

metadata.breadcrumb: Security - First Steps > What it does
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Security > tutorial/security/first-steps.md

page_content:
[Context: Security - First Steps > Recap]

## Recap
So, in just 3 or 4 extra lines, you already have some primitive form of security.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Security > tutorial/security/first-steps.md

page_content:
[Context: Get Current User]

# Get Current User
In the previous chapter the security system (which is based on the dependency injection system) was giving the [ITALIC:path operation function] a [CODE: token] as a [CODE: str]:
```python
async def read[ITALIC:items(token: Annotated[str, Depends(oauth2]scheme)]):
return {"token": token}
```
But that is still not that useful.
Let's make it give us the current user.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Security > tutorial/security/get-current-user.md

page_content:
[Context: Get Current User > Create a user model]

## Create a user model
First, let's create a Pydantic user model.
The same way we use Pydantic to declare bodies, we can use it anywhere else:
```python
from pydantic import BaseModel

```

metadata.breadcrumb: Get Current User > Create a user model
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Security > tutorial/security/get-current-user.md

page_content:
[Context: Get Current User > Create a `get_current_user` dependency]

## Create a [CODE: get[ITALIC:current]user] dependency
Let's create a dependency [CODE: get[ITALIC:current]user].
Remember that dependencies can have sub-dependencies?
[CODE: get[ITALIC:current]user] will have a dependency with the same [CODE: oauth2[ITALIC:scheme] we created before.
The same as we were doing before in the [ITALIC:path operation] directly, our new dependency [CODE: get]current[ITALIC:user] will receive a [CODE: token] as a [CODE: str] from the sub-dependency [CODE: oauth2]scheme]:
```python
async def get[ITALIC:current]user(token: Annotated[str, Depends(oauth2[ITALIC:scheme)]):
user = fake]decode_token(token)
```

metadata.breadcrumb: Get Current User > Create a `get_current_user` dependency
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Security > tutorial/security/get-current-user.md

page_content:
[Context: Get Current User > Get the user]

## Get the user
[CODE: get[ITALIC:current]user] will use a (fake) utility function we created, that takes a token as a [CODE: str] and returns our Pydantic [CODE: User] model:
```python
def fake[ITALIC:decode]token(token):
return User(
username=token + "fakedecoded", email="john@example.com", full[ITALIC:name="John Doe"
)
user = fake]decode_token(token)
return user
```

metadata.breadcrumb: Get Current User > Get the user
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Security > tutorial/security/get-current-user.md

page_content:
[Context: Get Current User > Inject the current user]

## Inject the current user
So now we can use the same [CODE: Depends] with our [CODE: get[ITALIC:current]user] in the [ITALIC:path operation]:
```python
async def read[ITALIC:users]me(current[ITALIC:user: Annotated[User, Depends(get]current[ITALIC:user)]):
return current]user
```
Notice that we declare the type of [CODE: current_user] as the Pydantic model [CODE: User].
This will help us inside of the function with all the completion and type checks.
/// tip
You might remember that request bodies are also declared with Pydantic models.
Here [BOLD:FastAPI] won't get confused because you are using [CODE: Depends].
///
/// check
The way this dependency system is designed allows us to have different dependencies (different "dependables") that all return a [CODE: User] model.
We are not restricted to having only one dependency that can return that type of data.
///

metadata.breadcrumb: Get Current User > Inject the current user
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Security > tutorial/security/get-current-user.md

page_content:
[Context: Get Current User > Other models]

## Other models
You can now get the current user directly in the [ITALIC:path operation functions] and deal with the security mechanisms at the [BOLD:Dependency Injection] level, using [CODE: Depends].
And you can use any model or data for the security requirements (in this case, a Pydantic model [CODE: User]).
But you are not restricted to using some specific data model, class or type.
Do you want to have an [CODE: id] and [CODE: email] and not have any [CODE: username] in your model? Sure. You can use these same tools.
Do you want to just have a [CODE: str]? Or just a [CODE: dict]? Or a database class model instance directly? It all works the same way.
You actually don't have users that log in to your application but robots, bots, or other systems, that have just an access token? Again, it all works the same.
Just use any kind of model, any kind of class, any kind of database that you need for your application. [BOLD:FastAPI] has you covered with the dependency injection system.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Security > tutorial/security/get-current-user.md

page_content:
[Context: Get Current User > Code size]

## Code size
This example might seem verbose. Keep in mind that we are mixing security, data models, utility functions and [ITALIC:path operations] in the same file.
But here's the key point.
The security and dependency injection stuff is written once.
And you can make it as complex as you want. And still, have it written only once, in a single place. With all the flexibility.
But you can have thousands of endpoints ([ITALIC:path operations]) using the same security system.
And all of them (or any portion of them that you want) can take advantage of re-using these dependencies or any other dependencies you create.
And all these thousands of [ITALIC:path operations] can be as small as 3 lines:
```python
@app.get("/users/me")
async def read[ITALIC:users]me(current[ITALIC:user: Annotated[User, Depends(get]current[ITALIC:user)]):
return current]user
```

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Security > tutorial/security/get-current-user.md

page_content:
[Context: Get Current User > Recap]

## Recap
You can now get the current user directly in your [ITALIC:path operation function].
We are already halfway there.
We just need to add a [ITALIC:path operation] for the user/client to actually send the [CODE: username] and [CODE: password].
That comes next.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Security > tutorial/security/get-current-user.md

page_content:
[Context: Security]

# Security
There are many ways to handle security, authentication and authorization.
And it normally is a complex and "difficult" topic.
In many frameworks and systems just handling security and authentication takes a big amount of effort and code (in many cases it can be 50% or more of all the code written).
[BOLD:FastAPI] provides several tools to help you deal with [BOLD:Security] easily, rapidly, in a standard way, without having to study and learn all the security specifications.
But first, let's check some small concepts.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Security > tutorial/security/index.md

page_content:
[Context: Security > In a hurry?]

## In a hurry?
If you don't care about any of these terms and you just need to add security with authentication based on username and password [ITALIC:right now], skip to the next chapters.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Security > tutorial/security/index.md

page_content:
[Context: Security > OAuth2]

## OAuth2
OAuth2 is a specification that defines several ways to handle authentication and authorization.
It is quite an extensive specification and covers several complex use cases.
It includes ways to authenticate using a "third party".
That's what all the systems with "login with Facebook, Google, X (Twitter), GitHub" use underneath.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Security > tutorial/security/index.md

page_content:
[Context: Security > OAuth2 > OAuth 1]

### OAuth 1
There was an OAuth 1, which is very different from OAuth2, and more complex, as it included direct specifications on how to encrypt the communication.
It is not very popular or used nowadays.
OAuth2 doesn't specify how to encrypt the communication, it expects you to have your application served with HTTPS.
/// tip
In the section about [BOLD:deployment] you will see how to set up HTTPS for free, using Traefik and Let's Encrypt.
///

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Security > tutorial/security/index.md

page_content:
[Context: Security > OpenID Connect]

## OpenID Connect
OpenID Connect is another specification, based on [BOLD:OAuth2].
It just extends OAuth2 specifying some things that are relatively ambiguous in OAuth2, to try to make it more interoperable.
For example, Google login uses OpenID Connect (which underneath uses OAuth2).
But Facebook login doesn't support OpenID Connect. It has its own flavor of OAuth2.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Security > tutorial/security/index.md

page_content:
[Context: Security > OpenID Connect > OpenID (not "OpenID Connect")]

### OpenID (not "OpenID Connect")
There was also an "OpenID" specification. That tried to solve the same thing as [BOLD:OpenID Connect], but was not based on OAuth2.
So, it was a complete additional system.
It is not very popular or used nowadays.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Security > tutorial/security/index.md
Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Security > tutorial/security/index.md
Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Security > tutorial/security/index.md

page_content:
[Context: Security > **FastAPI** utilities]

## [BOLD:FastAPI] utilities
FastAPI provides several tools for each of these security schemes in the [CODE: fastapi.security] module that simplify using these security mechanisms.
In the next chapters you will see how to add security to your API using those tools provided by [BOLD:FastAPI].
And you will also see how it gets automatically integrated into the interactive documentation system.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Security > tutorial/security/index.md

page_content:
[Context: OAuth2 with Password (and hashing), Bearer with JWT tokens]

# OAuth2 with Password (and hashing), Bearer with JWT tokens
Now that we have all the security flow, let's make the application actually secure, using JWT tokens and secure password hashing.
This code is something you can actually use in your application, save the password hashes in your database, etc.
We are going to start from where we left in the previous chapter and increment it.

hierarchy: OAuth2 with Password (and hashing), Bearer with JWT tokens
metadata.breadcrumb: OAuth2 with Password (and hashing), Bearer with JWT tokens
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Security > tutorial/security/oauth2-jwt.md

page_content:
[Context: OAuth2 with Password (and hashing), Bearer with JWT tokens > About JWT]

## About JWT
JWT means "JSON Web Tokens".
It's a standard to codify a JSON object in a long dense string without spaces. It looks like this:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9.eyJzdWIiOiIxM...]
It is not encrypted, so, anyone could recover the information from the contents.
But it's signed. So, when you receive a token that you emitted, you can verify that you actually emitted it.
That way, you can create a token with an expiration of, let's say, 1 week. And then when the user comes back the next day with the token, you know that user is still logged in to your system.
After a week, the token will be expired and the user will not be authorized and will have to sign in again to get a new token. And if the user (or a third party) tried to modify the token to change the expiration, you would be able to discover it, because the signatures would not match.
If you want to play with JWT tokens and see how they work, check https://jwt.io.

hierarchy: OAuth2 with Password (and hashing), Bearer with JWT tokens
metadata.breadcrumb: OAuth2 with Password (and hashing), Bearer with JWT tokens > About JWT
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Security > tutorial/security/oauth2-jwt.md

page_content:
[Context: OAuth2 with Password (and hashing), Bearer with JWT tokens > About JWT]

## About JWT
JWT means "JSON Web Tokens".
It's a standard to codify a JSON object in a long dense string without spaces. It looks like this:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9.eyJzdWIiOiIxM...]
It is not encrypted, so, anyone could recover the information from the contents.
But it's signed. So, when you receive a token that you emitted, you can verify that you actually emitted it.
That way, you can create a token with an expiration of, let's say, 1 week. And then when the user comes back the next day with the token, you know that user is still logged in to your system.
After a week, the token will be expired and the user will not be authorized and will have to sign in again to get a new token. And if the user (or a third party) tried to modify the token to change the expiration, you would be able to discover it, because the signatures would not match.
If you want to play with JWT tokens and see how they work, check https://jwt.io.

hierarchy: OAuth2 with Password (and hashing), Bearer with JWT tokens
metadata.breadcrumb: OAuth2 with Password (and hashing), Bearer with JWT tokens > About JWT
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Security > tutorial/security/oauth2-jwt.md

page_content:
[Context: OAuth2 with Password (and hashing), Bearer with JWT tokens > Install `PyJWT`]

## Install [CODE: PyJWT]
We need to install [CODE: PyJWT] to generate and verify the JWT tokens in Python.
Make sure you create a virtual environment, activate it, and then install [CODE: pyjwt]:

[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: $ pip install pyjwt ---> 100%...]

/// info
If you are planning to use digital signature algorithms like RSA or ECDSA, you should install the cryptography library dependency [CODE: pyjwt[crypto]].
You can read more about it in the PyJWT Installation docs.
///

hierarchy: OAuth2 with Password (and hashing), Bearer with JWT tokens
metadata.breadcrumb: OAuth2 with Password (and hashing), Bearer with JWT tokens > Install `PyJWT`
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Security > tutorial/security/oauth2-jwt.md

page_content:
[Context: OAuth2 with Password (and hashing), Bearer with JWT tokens > Password hashing]

## Password hashing
"Hashing" means converting some content (a password in this case) into a sequence of bytes (just a string) that looks like gibberish.
Whenever you pass exactly the same content (exactly the same password) you get exactly the same gibberish.
But you cannot convert from the gibberish back to the password.

hierarchy: OAuth2 with Password (and hashing), Bearer with JWT tokens
metadata.breadcrumb: OAuth2 with Password (and hashing), Bearer with JWT tokens > Password hashing
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Security > tutorial/security/oauth2-jwt.md

page_content:
[Context: OAuth2 with Password (and hashing), Bearer with JWT tokens > Password hashing > Why use password hashing]

### Why use password hashing
If your database is stolen, the thief won't have your users' plaintext passwords, only the hashes.
So, the thief won't be able to try to use that password in another system (as many users use the same password everywhere, this would be dangerous).

hierarchy: OAuth2 with Password (and hashing), Bearer with JWT tokens
metadata.breadcrumb: OAuth2 with Password (and hashing), Bearer with JWT tokens > Password hashing > Why use password hashing
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Security > tutorial/security/oauth2-jwt.md

page_content:
[Context: OAuth2 with Password (and hashing), Bearer with JWT tokens > Install `pwdlib`]

## Install [CODE: pwdlib]
pwdlib is a great Python package to handle password hashes.
It supports many secure hashing algorithms and utilities to work with them.
The recommended algorithm is "Argon2".
Make sure you create a virtual environment, activate it, and then install pwdlib with Argon2:

[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: $ pip install "pwdlib[argon2]" ---> 100%...]

/// tip
With [CODE: pwdlib], you could even configure it to be able to read passwords created by [BOLD:Django], a [BOLD:Flask] security plug-in or many others.
So, you would be able to, for example, share the same data from a Django application in a database with a FastAPI application. Or gradually migrate a Django application using the same database.
And your users would be able to login from your Django app or from your [BOLD:FastAPI] app, at the same time.
///
