page_content:
[Context: OAuth2 with Password (and hashing), Bearer with JWT tokens > Handle JWT tokens]

## Handle JWT tokens
Import the modules installed.
Create a random secret key that will be used to sign the JWT tokens.
To generate a secure random secret key use the command:

[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: $ openssl rand -hex 32 09d25e094faa6ca2556c818166b...]

And copy the output to the variable [CODE: SECRET[ITALIC:KEY] (don't use the one in the example).
Create a variable [CODE: ALGORITHM] with the algorithm used to sign the JWT token and set it to [CODE: "HS256"].
Create a variable for the expiration of the token.
Define a Pydantic Model that will be used in the token endpoint for the response.
Create a utility function to generate a new access token.
```python
import jwt
from fastapi import Depends, FastAPI, HTTPException, status
from jwt.exceptions import InvalidTokenError
from pwdlib import PasswordHash
SECRET]KEY = "09d25e094faa6ca2556c818166b7a9563b93f7099f6f0f4caa6cf63b88e8d3e7"
ALGORITHM = "HS256"
ACCESS[ITALIC:TOKEN]EXPIRE[ITALIC:MINUTES = 30
class Token(BaseModel):
access]token: str
token[ITALIC:type: str
def create]access[ITALIC:token(data: dict, expires]delta: timedelta | None = None):
to[ITALIC:encode = data.copy()
if expires]delta:
expire = datetime.now(timezone.utc) + expires[ITALIC:delta
else:
expire = datetime.now(timezone.utc) + timedelta(minutes=15)
to]encode.update({"exp": expire})
encoded[ITALIC:jwt = jwt.encode(to]encode, SECRET[ITALIC:KEY, algorithm=ALGORITHM)
return encoded]jwt
```

[Context: OAuth2 with Password (and hashing), Bearer with JWT tokens > Update the dependencies]

## Update the dependencies
Update [CODE: get[ITALIC:current]user] to receive the same token as before, but this time, using JWT tokens.
Decode the received token, verify it, and return the current user.
If the token is invalid, return an HTTP error right away.
```python
async def get[ITALIC:current]user(token: Annotated[str, Depends(oauth2[ITALIC:scheme)]):
credentials]exception = HTTPException(
status[ITALIC:code=status.HTTP]401[ITALIC:UNAUTHORIZED,
detail="Could not validate credentials",
headers={"WWW-Authenticate": "Bearer"},
)
try:
payload = jwt.decode(token, SECRET]KEY, algorithms=[ALGORITHM])
username = payload.get("sub")
if username is None:
raise credentials[ITALIC:exception
token]data = TokenData(username=username)
except InvalidTokenError:
raise credentials[ITALIC:exception
user = get]user(fake[ITALIC:users]db, username=token[ITALIC:data.username)
if user is None:
raise credentials]exception
return user
```

## Update the [CODE: /token] [ITALIC:path operation]
Create a [CODE: timedelta] with the expiration time of the token.
Create a real JWT access token and return it.
```python
@app.post("/token")
async def login[ITALIC:for]access[ITALIC:token(
form]data: Annotated[OAuth2PasswordRequestForm, Depends()],
) -> Token:
user = authenticate[ITALIC:user(fake]users[ITALIC:db, form]data.username, form[ITALIC:data.password)
if not user:
raise HTTPException(
status]code=status.HTTP[ITALIC:401]UNAUTHORIZED,
detail="Incorrect username or password",
headers={"WWW-Authenticate": "Bearer"},
)
access[ITALIC:token]expires = timedelta(minutes=ACCESS[ITALIC:TOKEN]EXPIRE[ITALIC:MINUTES)
access]token = create[ITALIC:access]token(
data={"sub": user.username}, expires[ITALIC:delta=access]token[ITALIC:expires
)
return Token(access]token=access[ITALIC:token, token]type="bearer")
```

page_content:
[Context: OAuth2 with Password (and hashing), Bearer with JWT tokens > Update the `/token` *path operation* > Technical details about the JWT "subject" `sub`]

### Technical details about the JWT "subject" [CODE: sub]
The JWT specification says that there's a key [CODE: sub], with the subject of the token.
It's optional to use it, but that's where you would put the user's identification, so we are using it here.
JWT might be used for other things apart from identifying a user and allowing them to perform operations directly on your API.
For example, you could identify a "car" or a "blog post".
Then you could add permissions about that entity, like "drive" (for the car) or "edit" (for the blog).
And then, you could give that JWT token to a user (or bot), and they could use it to perform those actions (drive the car, or edit the blog post) without even needing to have an account, just with the JWT token your API generated for that.
Using these ideas, JWT can be used for way more sophisticated scenarios.
In those cases, several of those entities could have the same ID, let's say [CODE: foo] (a user [CODE: foo], a car [CODE: foo], and a blog post [CODE: foo]).
So, to avoid ID collisions, when creating the JWT token for the user, you could prefix the value of the [CODE: sub] key, e.g. with [CODE: username:]. So, in this example, the value of [CODE: sub] could have been: [CODE: username:johndoe].
The important thing to keep in mind is that the [CODE: sub] key should have a unique identifier across the entire application, and it should be a string.

page_content:
[Context: OAuth2 with Password (and hashing), Bearer with JWT tokens > Check it]

## Check it
Run the server and go to the docs: http://127.0.0.1:8000/docs.
You'll see the user interface like:

Authorize the application the same way as before.
Using the credentials:
Username: [CODE: johndoe]
Password: [CODE: secret]
/// check
Notice that nowhere in the code is the plaintext password "[CODE: secret]", we only have the hashed version.
///

Call the endpoint [CODE: /users/me/], you will get the response as:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: { "username": "johndoe", "email": "johndoe@example...]

If you open the developer tools, you could see how the data sent only includes the token, the password is only sent in the first request to authenticate the user and get that access token, but not afterwards:

/// note
Notice the header [CODE: Authorization], with a value that starts with [CODE: Bearer ].
///

page_content:
[Context: OAuth2 with Password (and hashing), Bearer with JWT tokens > Advanced usage with `scopes`]

## Advanced usage with [CODE: scopes]
OAuth2 has the notion of "scopes".
You can use them to add a specific set of permissions to a JWT token.
Then you can give this token to a user directly or a third party, to interact with your API with a set of restrictions.
You can learn how to use them and how they are integrated into [BOLD:FastAPI] later in the [BOLD:Advanced User Guide].

page_content:
[Context: OAuth2 with Password (and hashing), Bearer with JWT tokens > Recap]

## Recap
With what you have seen up to now, you can set up a secure [BOLD:FastAPI] application using standards like OAuth2 and JWT.
In almost any framework handling the security becomes a rather complex subject quite quickly.
Many packages that simplify it a lot have to make many compromises with the data model, database, and available features. And some of these packages that simplify things too much actually have security flaws underneath.
---
[BOLD:FastAPI] doesn't make any compromise with any database, data model or tool.
It gives you all the flexibility to choose the ones that fit your project the best.
And you can use directly many well maintained and widely used packages like [CODE: pwdlib] and [CODE: PyJWT], because [BOLD:FastAPI] doesn't require any complex mechanisms to integrate external packages.
But it provides you the tools to simplify the process as much as possible without compromising flexibility, robustness, or security.
And you can use and implement secure, standard protocols, like OAuth2 in a relatively simple way.
You can learn more in the [BOLD:Advanced User Guide] about how to use OAuth2 "scopes", for a more fine-grained permission system, following these same standards. OAuth2 with scopes is the mechanism used by many big authentication providers, like Facebook, Google, GitHub, Microsoft, X (Twitter), etc. to authorize third party applications to interact with their APIs on behalf of their users.

page_content:
[Context: Simple OAuth2 with Password and Bearer]

# Simple OAuth2 with Password and Bearer
Now let's build from the previous chapter and add the missing parts to have a complete security flow.

hierarchy: Simple OAuth2 with Password and Bearer
metadata.breadcrumb: Simple OAuth2 with Password and Bearer
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Security > tutorial/security/simple-oauth2.md

page_content:
[Context: Simple OAuth2 with Password and Bearer > Get the `username` and `password`]

## Get the [CODE: username] and [CODE: password]
We are going to use [BOLD:FastAPI] security utilities to get the [CODE: username] and [CODE: password].
OAuth2 specifies that when using the "password flow" (that we are using) the client/user must send a [CODE: username] and [CODE: password] fields as form data.
And the spec says that the fields have to be named like that. So [CODE: user-name] or [CODE: email] wouldn't work.
But don't worry, you can show it as you wish to your final users in the frontend.
And your database models can use any other names you want.
But for the login [ITALIC:path operation], we need to use these names to be compatible with the spec (and be able to, for example, use the integrated API documentation system).
The spec also states that the [CODE: username] and [CODE: password] must be sent as form data (so, no JSON here).

hierarchy: Simple OAuth2 with Password and Bearer
metadata.breadcrumb: Simple OAuth2 with Password and Bearer > Get the `username` and `password`
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Security > tutorial/security/simple-oauth2.md

page_content:
[Context: Simple OAuth2 with Password and Bearer > Get the `username` and `password` > `scope`]

### [CODE: scope]
The spec also says that the client can send another form field "[CODE: scope]".
The form field name is [CODE: scope] (in singular), but it is actually a long string with "scopes" separated by spaces.
Each "scope" is just a string (without spaces).
They are normally used to declare specific security permissions, for example:
[ITALIC: [CODE: users:read] or [CODE: users:write] are common examples.
] [CODE: instagram_basic] is used by Facebook / Instagram.
- [CODE: https://www.googleapis.com/auth/drive] is used by Google.
/// info
In OAuth2 a "scope" is just a string that declares a specific permission required.
It doesn't matter if it has other characters like [CODE: :] or if it is a URL.
Those details are implementation specific.
For OAuth2 they are just strings.
///

hierarchy: Simple OAuth2 with Password and Bearer

page_content:
[Context: Simple OAuth2 with Password and Bearer > Code to get the `username` and `password`]

## Code to get the [CODE: username] and [CODE: password]
Now let's use the utilities provided by [BOLD:FastAPI] to handle this.

hierarchy: Simple OAuth2 with Password and Bearer
metadata.breadcrumb: Simple OAuth2 with Password and Bearer > Code to get the `username` and `password`
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Tutorial - User Guide > Root > Security > tutorial/security/simple-oauth2.md

page_content:
[Context: Simple OAuth2 with Password and Bearer > Code to get the `username` and `password` > `OAuth2PasswordRequestForm`]

### [CODE: OAuth2PasswordRequestForm]
First, import [CODE: OAuth2PasswordRequestForm], and use it as a dependency with [CODE: Depends] in the [ITALIC:path operation] for [CODE: /token]:
```python
from fastapi.security import OAuth2PasswordBearer, OAuth2PasswordRequestForm
from pydantic import BaseModel
async def login(form[ITALIC:data: Annotated[OAuth2PasswordRequestForm, Depends()]):
user]dict = fake[ITALIC:users]db.get(form[ITALIC:data.username)
```
[CODE: OAuth2PasswordRequestForm] is a class dependency that declares a form body with:
[ITALIC: The [CODE: username].
] The [CODE: password].
[ITALIC: An optional [CODE: scope] field as a big string, composed of strings separated by spaces.
] An optional [CODE: grant]type].
/// tip
The OAuth2 spec actually [ITALIC:requires] a field [CODE: grant[ITALIC:type] with a fixed value of [CODE: password], but [CODE: OAuth2PasswordRequestForm] doesn't enforce it.
If you need to enforce it, use [CODE: OAuth2PasswordRequestFormStrict] instead of [CODE: OAuth2PasswordRequestForm].
///
[ITALIC: An optional [CODE: client]id] (we don't need it for our example).
] An optional [CODE: client_secret] (we don't need it for our example).
/// info
The [CODE: OAuth2PasswordRequestForm] is not a special class for [BOLD:FastAPI] as is [CODE: OAuth2PasswordBearer].
[CODE: OAuth2PasswordBearer] makes [BOLD:FastAPI] know that it is a security scheme. So it is added that way to OpenAPI.
But [CODE: OAuth2PasswordRequestForm] is just a class dependency that you could have written yourself, or you could have declared [CODE: Form] parameters directly.
But as it's a common use case, it is provided by [BOLD:FastAPI] directly, just to make it easier.
///

page_content:
[Context: Simple OAuth2 with Password and Bearer > Code to get the `username` and `password` > Use the form data]

### Use the form data
/// tip
The instance of the dependency class [CODE: OAuth2PasswordRequestForm] won't have an attribute [CODE: scope] with the long string separated by spaces, instead, it will have a [CODE: scopes] attribute with the actual list of strings for each scope sent.
We are not using [CODE: scopes] in this example, but the functionality is there if you need it.
///
Now, get the user data from the (fake) database, using the [CODE: username] from the form field.
If there is no such user, we return an error saying "Incorrect username or password".
For the error, we use the exception [CODE: HTTPException]:
```python
from fastapi import Depends, FastAPI, HTTPException, status
from fastapi.security import OAuth2PasswordBearer, OAuth2PasswordRequestForm
user[ITALIC:dict = fake]users[ITALIC:db.get(form]data.username)
if not user[ITALIC:dict:
raise HTTPException(status]code=400, detail="Incorrect username or password")
```

/// tip
The instance of the dependency class [CODE: OAuth2PasswordRequestForm] won't have an attribute [CODE: scope] with the long string separated by spaces, instead, it will have a [CODE: scopes] attribute with the actual list of strings for each scope sent.
We are not using [CODE: scopes] in this example, but the functionality is there if you need it.
///
Now, get the user data from the (fake) database, using the [CODE: username] from the form field.
If there is no such user, we return an error saying "Incorrect username or password".
For the error, we use the exception [CODE: HTTPException]:
```python
from fastapi import Depends, FastAPI, HTTPException, status
from fastapi.security import OAuth2PasswordBearer, OAuth2PasswordRequestForm
user[ITALIC:dict = fake]users[ITALIC:db.get(form]data.username)
if not user[ITALIC:dict:
raise HTTPException(status]code=400, detail="Incorrect username or password")
```

page_content:
[Context: Simple OAuth2 with Password and Bearer > Code to get the `username` and `password` > Check the password]

### Check the password
At this point we have the user data from our database, but we haven't checked the password.
Let's put that data in the Pydantic [CODE: UserInDB] model first.
You should never save plaintext passwords, so, we'll use the (fake) password hashing system.
If the passwords don't match, we return the same error.
#### Password hashing
"Hashing" means: converting some content (a password in this case) into a sequence of bytes (just a string) that looks like gibberish.
Whenever you pass exactly the same content (exactly the same password) you get exactly the same gibberish.
But you cannot convert from the gibberish back to the password.
##### Why use password hashing
If your database is stolen, the thief won't have your users' plaintext passwords, only the hashes.
So, the thief won't be able to try to use those same passwords in another system (as many users use the same password everywhere, this would be dangerous).
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: user = UserInDB([BOLD:user[ITALIC:dict) hashed]password = fak...]
#### About [CODE: ]user[ITALIC:dict]
[CODE: UserInDB(*[ITALIC:user]dict)] means:
]Pass the keys and values of the [CODE: user[ITALIC:dict] directly as key-value arguments, equivalent to:[ITALIC:
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:1: UserInDB( username = user]dict["username"], email ...]
/// info
For a more complete explanation of [CODE: [BOLD:user_dict] check back in the documentation for ]Extra Models]*.
///

page_content:
[Context: Simple OAuth2 with Password and Bearer > Return the token]

## Return the token
The response of the [CODE: token] endpoint must be a JSON object.
It should have a [CODE: token[ITALIC:type]. In our case, as we are using "Bearer" tokens, the token type should be "[CODE: bearer]".
And it should have an [CODE: access]token], with a string containing our access token.
For this simple example, we are going to just be completely insecure and return the same [CODE: username] as the token.
/// tip
In the next chapter, you will see a real secure implementation, with password hashing and JWT tokens.
But for now, let's focus on the specific details we need.
///
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: return {"access[ITALIC:token": user.username, "token]type...]
/// tip
By the spec, you should return a JSON with an [CODE: access[ITALIC:token] and a [CODE: token]type], the same as in this example.
This is something that you have to do yourself in your code, and make sure you use those JSON keys.
It's almost the only thing that you have to remember to do correctly yourself, to be compliant with the specifications.
For the rest, [BOLD:FastAPI] handles it for you.
///

page_content:
[Context: Simple OAuth2 with Password and Bearer > Update the dependencies]

## Update the dependencies
Now we are going to update our dependencies.
We want to get the [CODE: current[ITALIC:user] [ITALIC:only] if this user is active.
So, we create an additional dependency [CODE: get]current[ITALIC:active]user] that in turn uses [CODE: get[ITALIC:current]user] as a dependency.
Both of these dependencies will just return an HTTP error if the user doesn't exist, or if is inactive.
So, in our endpoint, we will only get a user if the user exists, was correctly authenticated, and is active:
```python
async def get[ITALIC:current]user(token: Annotated[str, Depends(oauth2[ITALIC:scheme)]):
user = fake]decode[ITALIC:token(token)
if not user:
raise HTTPException(
status]code=status.HTTP[ITALIC:401]UNAUTHORIZED,
detail="Not authenticated",
headers={"WWW-Authenticate": "Bearer"},
)
return user
async def get[ITALIC:current]active[ITALIC:user(
current]user: Annotated[User, Depends(get[ITALIC:current]user)],
):
if current[ITALIC:user.disabled:
raise HTTPException(status]code=400, detail="Inactive user")
return current[ITALIC:user
return current]user
```
/// info
The additional header [CODE: WWW-Authenticate] with value [CODE: Bearer] we are returning here is also part of the spec.
Any HTTP (error) status code 401 "UNAUTHORIZED" is supposed to also return a [CODE: WWW-Authenticate] header.
In the case of bearer tokens (our case), the value of that header should be [CODE: Bearer].
You can actually skip that extra header and it would still work.
But it's provided here to be compliant with the specifications.
Also, there might be tools that expect and use it (now or in the future) and that might be useful for you or your users, now or in the future.
That's the benefit of standards...
///

page_content:
[Context: Simple OAuth2 with Password and Bearer > Recap]

## Recap
You now have the tools to implement a complete security system based on [CODE: username] and [CODE: password] for your API.
Using these tools, you can make the security system compatible with any database and with any user or data model.
The only detail missing is that it is not actually "secure" yet.
In the next chapter you'll see how to use a secure password hashing library and JWT tokens.

[Context: Security Tools]

# Security Tools
When you need to declare dependencies with OAuth2 scopes you use [CODE: Security()].
But you still need to define what is the dependable, the callable that you pass as a parameter to [CODE: Depends()] or [CODE: Security()].
There are multiple tools that you can use to create those dependables, and they get integrated into OpenAPI so they are shown in the automatic docs UI, they can be used by automatically generated clients and SDKs, etc.
You can import them from [CODE: fastapi.security]:
```python
from fastapi.security import (
APIKeyCookie,
APIKeyHeader,
APIKeyQuery,
HTTPAuthorizationCredentials,
HTTPBasic,
HTTPBasicCredentials,
HTTPBearer,
HTTPDigest,
OAuth2,
OAuth2AuthorizationCodeBearer,
OAuth2PasswordBearer,
OAuth2PasswordRequestForm,
OAuth2PasswordRequestFormStrict,
OpenIdConnect,
SecurityScopes,
)
```
Read more about them in the FastAPI docs about Security.

[Context: Security Tools > OpenID Connect]

## OpenID Connect
::: fastapi.security.OpenIdConnect

[Context: HTTP Basic Auth]

# HTTP Basic Auth
For the simplest cases, you can use HTTP Basic Auth.
In HTTP Basic Auth, the application expects a header that contains a username and a password.
If it doesn't receive it, it returns an HTTP 401 "Unauthorized" error.
And returns a header [CODE: WWW-Authenticate] with a value of [CODE: Basic], and an optional [CODE: realm] parameter.
That tells the browser to show the integrated prompt for a username and password.
Then, when you type that username and password, the browser sends them in the header automatically.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > Root > Advanced Security > advanced/security/http-basic-auth.md

page_content:
[Context: HTTP Basic Auth > Simple HTTP Basic Auth]

## Simple HTTP Basic Auth
[ITALIC: Import [CODE: HTTPBasic] and [CODE: HTTPBasicCredentials].
] Create a "[CODE: security] scheme" using [CODE: HTTPBasic].
[ITALIC: Use that [CODE: security] with a dependency in your ]path operation[ITALIC:.
] It returns an object of type [CODE: HTTPBasicCredentials]:
- It contains the [CODE: username] and [CODE: password] sent.
```python
from fastapi.security import HTTPBasic, HTTPBasicCredentials

security = HTTPBasic()

def read[ITALIC:current]user(credentials: Annotated[HTTPBasicCredentials, Depends(security)]):
return {"username": credentials.username, "password": credentials.password}
```
When you try to open the URL for the first time (or click the "Execute" button in the docs) the browser will ask you for your username and password:

metadata.breadcrumb: HTTP Basic Auth > Simple HTTP Basic Auth
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > Root > Advanced Security > advanced/security/http-basic-auth.md

page_content:
[Context: HTTP Basic Auth > Check the username]

## Check the username
Here's a more complete example.
Use a dependency to check if the username and password are correct.
For this, use the Python standard module [[CODE: secrets]](https://docs.python.org/3/library/secrets.html) to check the username and password.
[CODE: secrets.compare[ITALIC:digest()] needs to take [CODE: bytes] or a [CODE: str] that only contains ASCII characters (the ones in English), this means it wouldn't work with characters like [CODE: á], as in [CODE: Sebastián].
To handle that, we first convert the [CODE: username] and [CODE: password] to [CODE: bytes] encoding them with UTF-8.
Then we can use [CODE: secrets.compare]digest()] to ensure that [CODE: credentials.username] is [CODE: "stanleyjobson"], and that [CODE: credentials.password] is [CODE: "swordfish"].
```python
import secrets
from typing import Annotated
def get[ITALIC:current]username(
credentials: Annotated[HTTPBasicCredentials, Depends(security)],
):
current[ITALIC:username]bytes = credentials.username.encode("utf8")
correct[ITALIC:username]bytes = b"stanleyjobson"
is[ITALIC:correct]username = secrets.compare[ITALIC:digest(
current]username[ITALIC:bytes, correct]username[ITALIC:bytes
)
current]password[ITALIC:bytes = credentials.password.encode("utf8")
correct]password[ITALIC:bytes = b"swordfish"
is]correct[ITALIC:password = secrets.compare]digest(
current[ITALIC:password]bytes, correct[ITALIC:password]bytes
)
```
This would be similar to:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: if not (credentials.username == "stanleyjobson") o...]
But by using the [CODE: secrets.compare_digest()] it will be secure against a type of attacks called "timing attacks".

metadata.breadcrumb: HTTP Basic Auth > Check the username
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > Root > Advanced Security > advanced/security/http-basic-auth.md

page_content:
[Context: HTTP Basic Auth > Check the username]

## Check the username
Here's a more complete example.
Use a dependency to check if the username and password are correct.
For this, use the Python standard module [[CODE: secrets]](https://docs.python.org/3/library/secrets.html) to check the username and password.
[CODE: secrets.compare[ITALIC:digest()] needs to take [CODE: bytes] or a [CODE: str] that only contains ASCII characters (the ones in English), this means it wouldn't work with characters like [CODE: á], as in [CODE: Sebastián].
To handle that, we first convert the [CODE: username] and [CODE: password] to [CODE: bytes] encoding them with UTF-8.
Then we can use [CODE: secrets.compare]digest()] to ensure that [CODE: credentials.username] is [CODE: "stanleyjobson"], and that [CODE: credentials.password] is [CODE: "swordfish"].
```python
import secrets
from typing import Annotated
def get[ITALIC:current]username(
credentials: Annotated[HTTPBasicCredentials, Depends(security)],
):
current[ITALIC:username]bytes = credentials.username.encode("utf8")
correct[ITALIC:username]bytes = b"stanleyjobson"
is[ITALIC:correct]username = secrets.compare[ITALIC:digest(
current]username[ITALIC:bytes, correct]username[ITALIC:bytes
)
current]password[ITALIC:bytes = credentials.password.encode("utf8")
correct]password[ITALIC:bytes = b"swordfish"
is]correct[ITALIC:password = secrets.compare]digest(
current[ITALIC:password]bytes, correct[ITALIC:password]bytes
)
```
This would be similar to:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: if not (credentials.username == "stanleyjobson") o...]
But by using the [CODE: secrets.compare_digest()] it will be secure against a type of attacks called "timing attacks".

metadata.breadcrumb: HTTP Basic Auth > Check the username
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > Root > Advanced Security > advanced/security/http-basic-auth.md

page_content:
[Context: HTTP Basic Auth > Check the username > Timing Attacks]

### Timing Attacks
But what's a "timing attack"?
Let's imagine some attackers are trying to guess the username and password.
And they send a request with a username [CODE: johndoe] and a password [CODE: love123].
Then the Python code in your application would be equivalent to something like:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: if "johndoe" == "stanleyjobson" and "love123" == "...]
But right at the moment Python compares the first [CODE: j] in [CODE: johndoe] to the first [CODE: s] in [CODE: stanleyjobson], it will return [CODE: False], because it already knows that those two strings are not the same, thinking that "there's no need to waste more computation comparing the rest of the letters". And your application will say "Incorrect username or password".
But then the attackers try with username [CODE: stanleyjobsox] and password [CODE: love123].
And your application code does something like:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]1: if "stanleyjobsox" == "stanleyjobson" and "love123...]
Python will have to compare the whole [CODE: stanleyjobso] in both [CODE: stanleyjobsox] and [CODE: stanleyjobson] before realizing that both strings are not the same. So it will take some extra microseconds to reply back "Incorrect username or password".
#### The time to answer helps the attackers
At that point, by noticing that the server took some microseconds longer to send the "Incorrect username or password" response, the attackers will know that they got [ITALIC:something] right, some of the initial letters were right.
And then they can try again knowing that it's probably something more similar to [CODE: stanleyjobsox] than to [CODE: johndoe].
#### A "professional" attack
Of course, the attackers would not try all this by hand, they would write a program to do it, possibly with thousands or millions of tests per second. And they would get just one extra correct letter at a time.
But doing that, in some minutes or hours the attackers would have guessed the correct username and password, with the "help" of our application, just using the time taken to answer.
#### Fix it with [CODE: secrets.compare[ITALIC:digest()]
But in our code we are actually using [CODE: secrets.compare]digest()].
In short, it will take the same time to compare [CODE: stanleyjobsox] to [CODE: stanleyjobson] than it takes to compare [CODE: johndoe] to [CODE: stanleyjobson]. And the same for the password.
That way, using [CODE: secrets.compare_digest()] in your application code, it will be safe against this whole range of security attacks.

page_content:
[Context: HTTP Basic Auth > Check the username > Timing Attacks]

### Timing Attacks
But what's a "timing attack"?
Let's imagine some attackers are trying to guess the username and password.
And they send a request with a username [CODE: johndoe] and a password [CODE: love123].
Then the Python code in your application would be equivalent to something like:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: if "johndoe" == "stanleyjobson" and "love123" == "...]
But right at the moment Python compares the first [CODE: j] in [CODE: johndoe] to the first [CODE: s] in [CODE: stanleyjobson], it will return [CODE: False], because it already knows that those two strings are not the same, thinking that "there's no need to waste more computation comparing the rest of the letters". And your application will say "Incorrect username or password".
But then the attackers try with username [CODE: stanleyjobsox] and password [CODE: love123].
And your application code does something like:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]1: if "stanleyjobsox" == "stanleyjobson" and "love123...]
Python will have to compare the whole [CODE: stanleyjobso] in both [CODE: stanleyjobsox] and [CODE: stanleyjobson] before realizing that both strings are not the same. So it will take some extra microseconds to reply back "Incorrect username or password".
#### The time to answer helps the attackers
At that point, by noticing that the server took some microseconds longer to send the "Incorrect username or password" response, the attackers will know that they got [ITALIC:something] right, some of the initial letters were right.
And then they can try again knowing that it's probably something more similar to [CODE: stanleyjobsox] than to [CODE: johndoe].
#### A "professional" attack
Of course, the attackers would not try all this by hand, they would write a program to do it, possibly with thousands or millions of tests per second. And they would get just one extra correct letter at a time.
But doing that, in some minutes or hours the attackers would have guessed the correct username and password, with the "help" of our application, just using the time taken to answer.
#### Fix it with [CODE: secrets.compare[ITALIC:digest()]
But in our code we are actually using [CODE: secrets.compare]digest()].
In short, it will take the same time to compare [CODE: stanleyjobsox] to [CODE: stanleyjobson] than it takes to compare [CODE: johndoe] to [CODE: stanleyjobson]. And the same for the password.
That way, using [CODE: secrets.compare_digest()] in your application code, it will be safe against this whole range of security attacks.

page_content:
[Context: OAuth2 scopes]

# OAuth2 scopes
You can use OAuth2 scopes directly with [BOLD:FastAPI], they are integrated to work seamlessly.
This would allow you to have a more fine-grained permission system, following the OAuth2 standard, integrated into your OpenAPI application (and the API docs).
OAuth2 with scopes is the mechanism used by many big authentication providers, like Facebook, Google, GitHub, Microsoft, X (Twitter), etc. They use it to provide specific permissions to users and applications.
Every time you "log in with" Facebook, Google, GitHub, Microsoft, X (Twitter), that application is using OAuth2 with scopes.
In this section you will see how to manage authentication and authorization with the same OAuth2 with scopes in your [BOLD:FastAPI] application.
/// warning
This is a more or less advanced section. If you are just starting, you can skip it.
You don't necessarily need OAuth2 scopes, and you can handle authentication and authorization however you want.
But OAuth2 with scopes can be nicely integrated into your API (with OpenAPI) and your API docs.
Nevertheless, you still enforce those scopes, or any other security/authorization requirement, however you need, in your code.
In many cases, OAuth2 with scopes can be an overkill.
But if you know you need it, or you are curious, keep reading.
///

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > Root > Advanced Security > advanced/security/oauth2-scopes.md

page_content:
[Context: OAuth2 scopes > OAuth2 scopes and OpenAPI]

## OAuth2 scopes and OpenAPI
The OAuth2 specification defines "scopes" as a list of strings separated by spaces.
The content of each of these strings can have any format, but should not contain spaces.
These scopes represent "permissions".
In OpenAPI (e.g. the API docs), you can define "security schemes".
When one of these security schemes uses OAuth2, you can also declare and use scopes.
Each "scope" is just a string (without spaces).
They are normally used to declare specific security permissions, for example:
[ITALIC: [CODE: users:read] or [CODE: users:write] are common examples.
] [CODE: instagram_basic] is used by Facebook / Instagram.
- [CODE: https://www.googleapis.com/auth/drive] is used by Google.
/// info
In OAuth2 a "scope" is just a string that declares a specific permission required.
It doesn't matter if it has other characters like [CODE: :] or if it is a URL.
Those details are implementation specific.
For OAuth2 they are just strings.
///

metadata.breadcrumb: OAuth2 scopes > OAuth2 scopes and OpenAPI
metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > Root > Advanced Security > advanced/security/oauth2-scopes.md

page_content:
[Context: OAuth2 scopes > Global view]

## Global view
First, let's quickly see the parts that change from the examples in the main [BOLD:Tutorial - User Guide] for OAuth2 with Password (and hashing), Bearer with JWT tokens. Now using OAuth2 scopes:
```python
from fastapi import Depends, FastAPI, HTTPException, Security, status
from fastapi.security import (
SecurityScopes,
)
from pydantic import BaseModel, ValidationError

scopes: list[str] = []

oauth2[ITALIC:scheme = OAuth2PasswordBearer(
tokenUrl="token",

async def get]current[ITALIC:user(
security]scopes: SecurityScopes, token: Annotated[str, Depends(oauth2[ITALIC:scheme)]
):
if security]scopes.scopes:
authenticate[ITALIC:value = f'Bearer scope="{security]scopes.scope[ITALIC:str}"'
else:
authenticate]value = "Bearer"
credentials[ITALIC:exception = HTTPException(
status]code=status.HTTP[ITALIC:401]UNAUTHORIZED,
username = payload.get("sub")
if username is None:
raise credentials[ITALIC:exception
scope: str = payload.get("scope", "")
token]scopes = scope.split(" ")
user = get[ITALIC:user(fake]users[ITALIC:db, username=token]data.username)
if user is None:
raise credentials[ITALIC:exception
for scope in security]scopes.scopes:
if scope not in token[ITALIC:data.scopes:
raise HTTPException(
status]code=status.HTTP[ITALIC:401]UNAUTHORIZED,

raise HTTPException(status[ITALIC:code=400, detail="Incorrect username or password")
access]token[ITALIC:expires = timedelta(minutes=ACCESS]TOKEN[ITALIC:EXPIRE]MINUTES)
```
Now let's review those changes step by step.

page_content:
[Context: OAuth2 scopes > OAuth2 Security scheme]

## OAuth2 Security scheme
The first change is that now we are declaring the OAuth2 security scheme with two available scopes, [CODE: me] and [CODE: items].
The [CODE: scopes] parameter receives a [CODE: dict] with each scope as a key and the description as the value:
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: DUMMY[ITALIC:HASH = password]hash.hash("dummypassword") o...]
Because we are now declaring those scopes, they will show up in the API docs when you log-in/authorize.
And you will be able to select which scopes you want to give access to: [CODE: me] and [CODE: items].
This is the same mechanism used when you give permissions while logging in with Facebook, Google, GitHub, etc:

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > Root > Advanced Security > advanced/security/oauth2-scopes.md

page_content:
[Context: OAuth2 scopes > JWT token with scopes]

## JWT token with scopes
Now, modify the token [ITALIC:path operation] to return the scopes requested.
We are still using the same [CODE: OAuth2PasswordRequestForm]. It includes a property [CODE: scopes] with a [CODE: list] of [CODE: str], with each scope it received in the request.
And we return the scopes as part of the JWT token.
/// danger
For simplicity, here we are just adding the scopes received directly to the token.
But in your application, for security, you should make sure you only add the scopes that the user is actually able to have, or the ones you have predefined.
///
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: raise HTTPException(status_code=400, detail="Incor...]

page_content:
[Context: OAuth2 scopes > Declare scopes in *path operations* and dependencies]

## Declare scopes in [ITALIC:path operations] and dependencies
Now we declare that the [ITALIC:path operation] for [CODE: /users/me/items/] requires the scope [CODE: items].
For this, we import and use [CODE: Security] from [CODE: fastapi].
You can use [CODE: Security] to declare dependencies (just like [CODE: Depends]), but [CODE: Security] also receives a parameter [CODE: scopes] with a list of scopes (strings).
In this case, we pass a dependency function [CODE: get[ITALIC:current]active[ITALIC:user] to [CODE: Security] (the same way we would do with [CODE: Depends]).
But we also pass a [CODE: list] of scopes, in this case with just one scope: [CODE: items] (it could have more).
And the dependency function [CODE: get]current[ITALIC:active]user] can also declare sub-dependencies, not only with [CODE: Depends] but also with [CODE: Security]. Declaring its own sub-dependency function ([CODE: get[ITALIC:current]user]), and more scope requirements.
In this case, it requires the scope [CODE: me] (it could require more than one scope).
/// note
You don't necessarily need to add different scopes in different places.
We are doing it here to demonstrate how [BOLD:FastAPI] handles scopes declared at different levels.
///
```python
from fastapi import Depends, FastAPI, HTTPException, Security, status
from fastapi.security import (

@app.get("/users/me/items/")
```
/// info | Technical Details
[CODE: Security] is actually a subclass of [CODE: Depends], and it has just one extra parameter that we'll see later.
But by using [CODE: Security] instead of [CODE: Depends], [BOLD:FastAPI] will know that it can declare security scopes, use them internally, and document the API with OpenAPI.
But when you import [CODE: Query], [CODE: Path], [CODE: Depends], [CODE: Security] and others from [CODE: fastapi], those are actually functions that return special classes.
///

page_content:
[Context: OAuth2 scopes > Use `SecurityScopes`]

## Use [CODE: SecurityScopes]
Now update the dependency [CODE: get[ITALIC:current]user].
This is the one used by the dependencies above.
Here's where we are using the same OAuth2 scheme we created before, declaring it as a dependency: [CODE: oauth2[ITALIC:scheme].
Because this dependency function doesn't have any scope requirements itself, we can use [CODE: Depends] with [CODE: oauth2]scheme], we don't have to use [CODE: Security] when we don't need to specify security scopes.
We also declare a special parameter of type [CODE: SecurityScopes], imported from [CODE: fastapi.security].
This [CODE: SecurityScopes] class is similar to [CODE: Request] ([CODE: Request] was used to get the request object directly).
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: SecurityScopes, )...]

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > Root > Advanced Security > advanced/security/oauth2-scopes.md

page_content:
[Context: OAuth2 scopes > Use the `scopes`]

## Use the [CODE: scopes]
The parameter [CODE: security[ITALIC:scopes] will be of type [CODE: SecurityScopes].
It will have a property [CODE: scopes] with a list containing all the scopes required by itself and all the dependencies that use this as a sub-dependency. That means, all the "dependants"... this might sound confusing, it is explained again later below.
The [CODE: security]scopes] object (of class [CODE: SecurityScopes]) also provides a [CODE: scope[ITALIC:str] attribute with a single string, containing those scopes separated by spaces (we are going to use it).
We create an [CODE: HTTPException] that we can reuse ([CODE: raise]) later at several points.
In this exception, we include the scopes required (if any) as a string separated by spaces (using [CODE: scope]str]). We put that string containing the scopes in the [CODE: WWW-Authenticate] header (this is part of the spec).
```python

async def get[ITALIC:current]user(
security[ITALIC:scopes: SecurityScopes, token: Annotated[str, Depends(oauth2]scheme)]
):
if security[ITALIC:scopes.scopes:
authenticate]value = f'Bearer scope="{security[ITALIC:scopes.scope]str}"'
else:
authenticate[ITALIC:value = "Bearer"
credentials]exception = HTTPException(
status[ITALIC:code=status.HTTP]401_UNAUTHORIZED,
```

page_content:
[Context: OAuth2 scopes > Verify the `username` and data shape]

## Verify the [CODE: username] and data shape
We verify that we get a [CODE: username], and extract the scopes.
And then we validate that data with the Pydantic model (catching the [CODE: ValidationError] exception), and if we get an error reading the JWT token or validating the data with Pydantic, we raise the [CODE: HTTPException] we created before.
For that, we update the Pydantic model [CODE: TokenData] with a new property [CODE: scopes].
By validating the data with Pydantic we can make sure that we have, for example, exactly a [CODE: list] of [CODE: str] with the scopes and a [CODE: str] with the [CODE: username].
Instead of, for example, a [CODE: dict], or something else, as it could break the application at some point later, making it a security risk.
We also verify that we have a user with that username, and if not, we raise that same exception we created before.
[CODE[ITALIC:BLOCK]0: scopes: list[str] = [] detail="Could not validate ...]

page_content:
[Context: OAuth2 scopes > Verify the `scopes`]

## Verify the [CODE: scopes]
We now verify that all the scopes required, by this dependency and all the dependants (including [ITALIC:path operations]), are included in the scopes provided in the token received, otherwise raise an [CODE: HTTPException].
For this, we use [CODE: security[ITALIC:scopes.scopes], that contains a [CODE: list] with all these scopes as [CODE: str].
[CODE]BLOCK[ITALIC:0: user = get]user(fake[ITALIC:users]db, username=token_data...]

page_content:
[Context: OAuth2 scopes > More details about `SecurityScopes`]

## More details about [CODE: SecurityScopes]
You can use [CODE: SecurityScopes] at any point, and in multiple places, it doesn't have to be at the "root" dependency.
It will always have the security scopes declared in the current [CODE: Security] dependencies and all the dependants for [BOLD:that specific] [ITALIC:path operation] and [BOLD:that specific] dependency tree.
Because the [CODE: SecurityScopes] will have all the scopes declared by dependants, you can use it to verify that a token has the required scopes in a central dependency function, and then declare different scope requirements in different [ITALIC:path operations].
They will be checked independently for each [ITALIC:path operation].

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > Root > Advanced Security > advanced/security/oauth2-scopes.md

page_content:
[Context: OAuth2 scopes > Check it]

## Check it
If you open the API docs, you can authenticate and specify which scopes you want to authorize.

If you don't select any scope, you will be "authenticated", but when you try to access [CODE: /users/me/] or [CODE: /users/me/items/] you will get an error saying that you don't have enough permissions. You will still be able to access [CODE: /status/].
And if you select the scope [CODE: me] but not the scope [CODE: items], you will be able to access [CODE: /users/me/] but not [CODE: /users/me/items/].
That's what would happen to a third party application that tried to access one of these [ITALIC:path operations] with a token provided by a user, depending on how many permissions the user gave the application.

metadata.navigation_path: Root > Learn > Root > Advanced User Guide > Root > Advanced Security > advanced/security/oauth2-scopes.md

page_content:
[Context: OAuth2 scopes > About third party integrations]

## About third party integrations
In this example we are using the OAuth2 "password" flow.
This is appropriate when we are logging in to our own application, probably with our own frontend.
Because we can trust it to receive the [CODE: username] and [CODE: password], as we control it.
But if you are building an OAuth2 application that others would connect to (i.e., if you are building an authentication provider equivalent to Facebook, Google, GitHub, etc.) you should use one of the other flows.
The most common is the implicit flow.
The most secure is the code flow, but it's more complex to implement as it requires more steps. As it is more complex, many providers end up suggesting the implicit flow.
/// note
It's common that each authentication provider names their flows in a different way, to make it part of their brand.
But in the end, they are implementing the same OAuth2 standard.
///
[BOLD:FastAPI] includes utilities for all these OAuth2 authentication flows in [CODE: fastapi.security.oauth2].
